It is the bond's *internal rate of return* assuming the bond is held until maturity and coupons are reinvested at the same rate. **Why most comprehensive (1.5 marks):** - It captures **both income return** (coupons) and **capital return** (gain/loss from purchase price vs. par). - It accounts for the **time value of money** by discounting all future cash flows. - It incorporates the **reinvestment assumption**, making it comparable across bonds of different coupons and maturities. - Unlike current yield, it does not ignore the redemption payment at maturity. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **State and explain the inverse relationship between bond prices and yields.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement (1 mark):** Bond prices and yields move in **opposite directions**. If the yield to maturity rises, the bond's price falls, and vice versa, holding coupon rate and maturity constant. **Explanation (2 marks):** A bond is a contract promising *fixed* future cash flows. When market interest rates (yields) rise above the bond's coupon rate, newly issued bonds offer better income. To make the older, lower-coupon bond attractive, its price must drop until its effective yield (income ÷ price) matches the new market rate. Mathematically, since $y$ appears in the denominator of the pricing formula, increasing $y$ reduces $P$. **Numerical example:** A 10% coupon bond with face value ₹1,000 — when yields rise from 10% to 12%, price falls from ₹1,000 to ₹927.91 (as derived in Section 3.2). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1 — The Inverse Relationship Trap:** > Many students write "bond price increases when interest rates increase" — this is the **single most common error** and costs 2 marks instantly. Memorize: **Higher yield ⇒ Lower price**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2 — Semi-Annual vs. Annual Confusion:** > If a problem says "coupon paid semi-annually" but you divide by 1 and use $n$ in years directly, the answer will be off by a wide margin. **Always halve the coupon and double the periods.** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3 — YTM Trial-and-Error Omission:** > When asked to compute YTM numerically, students often quote a single guess. **Show two iterations** (one above and one below the true value, then linear interpolation) to claim full 7 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] A 6-year corporate bond has a face value of **₹10,000**, an annual coupon rate of **8%**, and a current market price of **₹9,200**. #### (a) Compute the Yield to Maturity (YTM) of the bond. Use the approximation formula and verify with one iteration of trial-and-error. (7 marks) **Solution:** **Step 1: Identify parameters.** $F = 10{,}000$, $C = 800$, $n = 6$, $P = 9{,}200$. **Step 2: Apply the bond approximation formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} YTM \;\approx\; & \dfrac{C + \dfrac{F-P}{n}}{\dfrac{F+P}{2}} \\[6pt] \;=\; & \dfrac{800 + \dfrac{10{,}000 - 9{,}200}{6}}{\dfrac{10{,}000 + 9{,}200}{2}} \\[6pt] \;=\; & \dfrac{800 + \dfrac{800}{6}}{9{,}600} \\[6pt] \;=\; & \dfrac{800 + 133.33}{9{,}600} \\[6pt] \;=\; & \dfrac{933.33}{9{,}600} \\[6pt] \;=\; & 0.09722 \;=\; \mathbf{9.72\%} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Verify with trial at $y = 9\%$:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{9\%} \;=\; & 800 \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1.09)^{-6}}{0.09} \;+\; \dfrac{10{,}000}{(1.09)^{6}} \\[4pt] \;=\; & 800 \cdot \dfrac{1 - 0.5963}{0.09} \;+\; \dfrac{10{,}000}{1.6771} \\[4pt] \;=\; & 800 \cdot 4.4859 \;+\; 5{,}962.7 \\[4pt] \;=\; & 3{,}588.7 \;+\; 5{,}962.7 \;=\; \mathbf{9{,}551.4} \end{aligned} $$ At $y = 9\%$, computed price = 9,551.4 > actual price 9,200. Since price decreases with yield, true YTM must be **higher than 9%**. **Step 4: Trial at $y = 10\%$:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{10\%} \;=\; & 800 \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1.10)^{-6}}{0.10} \;+\; \dfrac{10{,}000}{(1.10)^{6}} \\[4pt] \;=\; & 800 \cdot 4.3553 \;+\; 5{,}645.5 \\[4pt] \;=\; & 3{,}484.2 \;+\; 5{,}645.5 \;=\; \mathbf{9{,}129.7} \end{aligned} $$ At $y = 10\%$, computed price = 9,129.7 < actual price 9,200. So YTM lies between 9% and 10%. **Step 5: Linear interpolation:** $$ y^{*} \;=\; 9\% \;+\; \dfrac{9{,}551.4 - 9{,}200}{9{,}551.4 - 9{,}129.7} \cdot (10\% - 9\%) \;=\; 9\% + \dfrac{351.4}{421.7} \cdot 1\% \;\approx\; \mathbf{9.83\%} $$ **Final Answer (3.5 + 3.5 marks):** Approximate YTM ≈ **9.72%**; refined YTM ≈ **9.83%**. --- #### (b) Calculate the Macaulay Duration and Modified Duration of the bond at YTM = 9.83%. (7 marks) **Solution:** Using $y = 9.83\%$, $C = 800$, $F = 10{,}000$, $n = 6$, $P = 9{,}200$. **Step 1: Compute PV of each cash flow.** | $t$ | $CF_t$ | $(1.0983)^t$ | $PV_t = CF_t / (1.0983)^t$ | $w_t = PV_t / P$ | $t \cdot w_t$ | |-----|--------|--------------|----------------------------|-------------------|---------------| | 1 | 800 | 1.0983 | 728.40 | 0.07917 | 0.07917 | | 2 | 800 | 1.2063 | 663.16 | 0.07208 | 0.14416 | | 3 | 800 | 1.3248 | 603.88 | 0.06564 | 0.19691 | | 4 | 800 | 1.4551 | 549.78 | 0.05976 | 0.23902 | | 5 | 800 | 1.5982 | 500.57 | 0.05441 | 0.27203 | | 6 | 10,800 | 1.7554 | 6,153.21 | 0.66895 | 4.01369 | | **Sum** | — | — | **9,199.00** | **1.00000** | **4.94498** | (PV sums to ≈ 9,200; minor rounding) **Step 2: Macaulay Duration.** $$ D \;=\; \sum t \cdot w_t \;=\; \mathbf{4.945 \;\text{years}} $$ **Step 3: Modified Duration.** $$ D_{mod} \;=\; \dfrac{D}{1 + y} \;=\; \dfrac{4.945}{1.0983} \;=\; \mathbf{4.502} $$ **Step 4: Price sensitivity check.** If yield rises by 50 bps ($\Delta y = 0.005$): $$ \dfrac{\Delta P}{P} \;\approx\; -D_{mod} \cdot \Delta y \;=\; -4.502 \cdot 0.005 \;=\; -0.02251 \;=\; -2.25\% $$ So new price ≈ $9{,}200 \times 0.9775 \approx$ **₹8,993**. **Final Answer (3 + 2 + 2 marks):** $D = 4.945$ years, $D_{mod} = 4.502$, and price will fall by 2.25% for a 50 bps yield increase. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – May 2024] A 4-year government bond has a face value of **₹5,000**, an annual coupon rate of **6%**, and the current yield to maturity is **7.5%**. #### (a) Calculate the current price of the bond and comment on whether it is a premium or discount bond. (7 marks) **Solution:** **Step 1: Parameters.** $F = 5{,}000$, $C = 300$, $n = 4$, $y = 7.5\% = 0.075$. **Step 2: Compute price using master formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} P \;=\; & 300 \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1.075)^{-4}}{0.075} \;+\; \dfrac{5{,}000}{(1.075)^{4}} \\[4pt] \;=\; & 300 \cdot \dfrac{1 - 0.7629}{0.075} \;+\; \dfrac{5{,}000}{1.3355} \\[4pt] \;=\; & 300 \cdot 3.1639 \;+\; 3{,}744.0 \\[4pt] \;=\; & 949.17 \;+\; 3{,}744.00 \\[4pt] \;=\; & \mathbf{₹4{,}693.17} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Classification.** Since the market yield (7.5%) **exceeds** the coupon rate (6%), the bond trades **below par**. Hence it is a **discount bond**. **Final Answer (4 + 3 marks):** $P = ₹4{,}693.17$; **Discount bond**. --- #### (b) Compute the Current Yield and the Approximate YTM, and explain why they differ from the true YTM of 7.5%. (7 marks) **Solution:** **Step 1: Current Yield.** $$ CY \;=\; \dfrac{C}{P} \;=\; \dfrac{300}{4{,}693.17} \;=\; \mathbf{6.39\%} $$ Comment: CY considers only the annual coupon income relative to price. It is *lower* than YTM because it ignores the capital gain at redemption (the bond is bought below par at ₹4,693 and redeemed at ₹5,000 — a gain of ₹307). **Step 2: Approximate YTM using bond approximation formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} YTM \;\approx\; & \dfrac{C + \dfrac{F-P}{n}}{\dfrac{F+P}{2}} \\[6pt] \;=\; & \dfrac{300 + \dfrac{5{,}000 - 4{,}693.17}{4}}{\dfrac{5{,}000 + 4{,}693.17}{2}} \\[6pt] \;=\; & \dfrac{300 + \dfrac{306.83}{4}}{\dfrac{9{,}693.17}{2}} \\[6pt] \;=\; & \dfrac{300 + 76.71}{4{,}846.59} \;=\; \dfrac{376.71}{4{,}846.59} \\[6pt] \;=\; & 0.07773 \;=\; \mathbf{7.77\%} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Reconciliation with True YTM = 7.5%.** The true YTM (7.5%) is *lower* than the approximate YTM (7.77%) by about 27 bps because: - The bond approximation formula assumes **linear capital-gain amortization**, whereas the true YTM uses the **exact non-linear discounting**. - The approximation error grows as $n$ decreases and as $|F - P|$ grows. **Final Answer (2 + 3 + 2 marks):** $CY = 6.39\%$, Approx YTM = 7.77%, True YTM = 7.5%. CY underestimates because it ignores capital gain; Approx YTM slightly overestimates due to linearization error. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PEPET635 — Module 2: Bond Pricing and Yields** - **Master Formula:** $P = C \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+y)^{-n}}{y} + \dfrac{F}{(1+y)^n}$ — *every problem reduces to this*. - **Zero-Coupon Special Case:** $P = F / (1+y)^n$ (no annuity component). - **Perpetuity Special Case:** $P = C / y$ (limit as $n \to \infty$). - **Three Bond States:** Premium (coupon > yield, price > par), Par (coupon = yield, price = par), Discount (coupon < yield, price < par). - **HYLP Rule:** **H**igher **Y**ield → **L**ower **P**rice. Non-negotiable. - **Current Yield:** $CY = C / P$ — quick proxy, ignores capital gain. - **Approximate YTM:** Average of $CY$ and $CY + \dfrac{F-P}{nF}$ weighted by par/price — used in practice. - **Macaulay Duration** $D$: weighted-average time to receive cash flows, measured in years. - **Modified Duration** $D_{mod}$: $D / (1+y)$ — used directly for price sensitivity. - **Price Sensitivity:** $\Delta P / P \approx -D_{mod} \cdot \Delta y$ — *linear approximation*. - **Convexity:** second-order correction; *always positive* for option-free bonds. - **Spot vs. Forward:** Spot rates discount each cash flow at its own maturity-specific rate; forward rates are implied future short rates. - **Semi-Annual Convention:** Divide annual coupon by 2 and double $n$. KTU often tests this. - **Clean vs. Dirty Price:** Clean Price = quoted price (excludes accrued interest); Dirty Price = Clean + Accrued Interest (actual cash paid at settlement). - **Reinvestment Assumption:** YTM assumes coupons are reinvested at the YTM itself — a critical caveat in exam answers. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions on bonds, always (1) list all parameters, (2) state the formula used, (3) substitute step by step, and (4) interpret the result (premium/par/discount). Examiners award **interpretation marks (1–2)** separately from computation marks.

topics.title: Term Structure of Interest Rates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET635 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Term Structure of Interest Rates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Term Structure of Interest Rates** refers to the relationship between bond yields (or interest rates) and their respective maturities. It is graphically depicted by the **Yield Curve**, which plots the yields of bonds of similar credit quality against their maturities at a specific point in time. Mathematically, it represents the term structure function $Y(t)$, where $Y$ is the yield for a zero-coupon bond maturing at time $t$.

The three primary instruments used to describe the term structure are:
1. **Spot Rates** ($r_t$ or $y_t$): The yield on a zero-coupon bond maturing at time $t$.
2. **Forward Rates** ($f_{t_1, t_2}$): The interest rate implied today for a loan commencing at $t_1$ and ending at $t_2$.
3. **Par Yields**: The coupon rate at which a bond trades at par (price = 100) for a given maturity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme explicitly requires understanding of **Spot Rate Curves, Forward Rate Curves, Yield Curves**, and the four classical theories explaining the shape of the term structure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a fruit market buying mangoes. The vendor offers two options:
- **Option A:** A mango today for ₹100.
- **Option B:** A commitment to deliver a mango one year from now for ₹105.

You would naturally ask: "What is the *implicit* interest rate embedded in this future delivery promise?" That implicit rate — the rate locked in *today* for a *future* transaction — is the essence of the **forward rate**. The *term structure* is simply the menu of all such rates — for 1 month, 6 months, 1 year, 5 years, 30 years — plotted against time. It tells us how the market prices money across different horizons, much like a weather forecast that maps temperature across different days of the week.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Convention:** KTU board examinations typically express rates as **percentages per annum (%)** with maturities in **years (t)**. Continuous compounding uses $e^{rt}$, while discrete compounding uses $(1+r)^t$.

### 1.3 Key Shapes of the Yield Curve

| Curve Shape | Description | Economic Signal |
|---|---|---|
| **Normal (Upward Sloping)** | Long-term rates > Short-term rates | Expansion expected |
| **Inverted (Downward Sloping)** | Long-term rates < Short-term rates | Recession expected |
| **Flat** | All maturities yield similar rates | Economic transition |
| **Humped** | Medium-term yields exceed both ends | Mixed signals |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Building Blocks of Term Structure

The term structure is fully characterized by three interrelated quantities. Understanding their conversion mechanics is essential for KTU board problems.

**Step 1 — Spot Rate ($z_t$ or $r_t$):** The discount rate that equates the present value of a zero-coupon bond to its market price. For a $t$-year zero-coupon bond priced at $P(0, t)$ with face value $F$:

$$P(0, t) = \frac{F}{(1+z_t)^t}$$

**Step 2 — Forward Rate ($f_{t, t+1}$):** The interest rate for the period between $t$ and $t+1$ that is locked in *today*. It is extracted from the spot rate curve using the no-arbitrage condition:

$$(1+z_n)^n = (1+z_{n-1})^{n-1} \cdot (1+f_{n-1, n})$$

**Step 3 — Par Yield ($y_n$):** The coupon rate that makes a coupon-bearing bond trade at par. For an $n$-year bond with annual coupons:

$$P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+y_n)^i} + \frac{F}{(1+y_n)^n} = F$$

### 2.2 The Four Major Theories of Term Structure

**Theory 1 — Pure Expectations Theory:**
- Long-term rates equal the *geometric average* of expected future short-term rates.
- **Implication:** Forward rates are unbiased estimators of expected future spot rates: $f_{t, t+n} = E[r_{t+n}]$.
- **Risk Premium:** Zero (investors are indifferent across maturities).

**Theory 2 — Liquidity Preference Theory (Keynes, Hicks):**
- Investors prefer liquidity (short-term bonds) but must be compensated for holding long-term bonds.
- **Implication:** Forward rates exceed expected future spot rates by a positive *liquidity premium* $L_{t,n}$: $f_{t, t+n} = E[r_{t+n}] + L_{t,n}$.
- **Default Assumption:** $L_{t,n} > 0$ and increases with maturity.

**Theory 3 — Market Segmentation Theory (Culbertson):**
- The market is segmented by maturity; supply and demand in each segment determines yields independently.
- **Implication:** No substitution between short and long maturities; yields are independent.

**Theory 4 — Preferred Habitat Theory (Modigliani-Sutch):**
- Investors have preferred maturity "habitats" but will deviate if compensated.
- **Implication:** Forward rates can be either above or below expected spot rates depending on supply-demand imbalances.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 12 formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU ESE question on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Interpretation | Compounding |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = F(1+z_t)^{-t}$ | Zero-coupon bond pricing | Discrete |
| 2 | $P = F e^{-rt}$ | Zero-coupon bond pricing | Continuous |
| 3 | $(1+z_n)^n = (1+z_{n-1})^{n-1}(1+f_{n-1,n})$ | Forward-spot no-arbitrage | Discrete |
| 4 | $f_{t,t+1} = \frac{(1+z_{t+1})^{t+1}}{(1+z_t)^t} - 1$ | Forward rate extraction | Discrete |
| 5 | $f_{t,t+n} = \left[\frac{(1+z_{t+n})^{t+n}}{(1+z_t)^t}\right]^{1/n} - 1$ | Multi-period forward rate | Discrete |
| 6 | $z_n = \left[\prod_{i=1}^{n}(1+f_{i-1,i})\right]^{1/n} - 1$ | Spot as geometric mean of forwards | Discrete |
| 7 | $\ln(1+f_{t,t+1}) = (t+1)z_{t+1} - t z_t$ | Forward rate (continuous) | Continuous |
| 8 | $z_n^{(c)} = \frac{1}{t}\ln\left[\frac{F}{P}\right]$ | Continuously compounded spot | Continuous |
| 9 | $1 + r_{nominal} = e^{r_{continuous}}$ | Nominal-continuous conversion | Mixed |
| 10 | $\text{YTM} = \frac{C + \frac{F-P}{n}}{\frac{F+P}{2}}$ | Approximate YTM | Standard |
| 11 | $z_n = \text{geometric avg}(f_{0,1}, f_{1,2}, ..., f_{n-1,n})$ | Pure expectations relation | Discrete |
| 12 | $\text{Duration} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} t \cdot w_t$ | Macaulay duration | Discrete |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Boundary Condition:** When $t = 0$, $f_{0,1} = z_1$. The one-period spot rate and the one-period forward rate are always equal.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Finance Utility

The term structure is foundational in:
- **Fixed Income Portfolio Management:** Immunization against interest rate risk.
- **Derivatives Pricing:** Hull-White, Vasicek, CIR, and HJM models use the initial term structure as a calibration input.
- **Corporate Finance:** Discounting cash flows for NPV calculations.
- **Central Banking:** The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) uses yield curve movements to signal monetary policy stance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Forward Rate from Spot Rates (No-Arbitrage)

**Statement to Prove:** Given $z_1$ and $z_2$ (1-year and 2-year spot rates), derive the 1-year forward rate starting at year 1, denoted $f_{1,2}$.

**Step 1 — Set up the no-arbitrage condition.**
An investor has two equivalent strategies to obtain $F$ at the end of year 2:
- **Strategy A:** Invest $X$ at the 2-year spot rate $z_2$.
- **Strategy B:** Invest $X$ at the 1-year spot rate $z_1$, then reinvest proceeds at the forward rate $f_{1,2}$.

**Step 2 — Express the future value of each strategy.**

$$\text{FV}_A = X \cdot (1+z_2)^2$$

$$\text{FV}_B = X \cdot (1+z_1) \cdot (1+f_{1,2})$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Law of One Price (No-Arbitrage).**

$$\text{FV}_A = \text{FV}_B$$

$$X \cdot (1+z_2)^2 = X \cdot (1+z_1) \cdot (1+f_{1,2})$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $f_{1,2}$.**

$$(1+z_2)^2 = (1+z_1)(1+f_{1,2})$$

$$1+f_{1,2} = \frac{(1+z_2)^2}{(1+z_1)}$$

$$\boxed{f_{1,2} = \frac{(1+z_2)^2}{(1+z_1)} - 1}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: General Multi-Period Forward Rate

**Statement to Prove:** $f_{t, t+n}$ from $z_t$ and $z_{t+n}$.

**Step 1 — Generalize the no-arbitrage identity.**

$$(1+z_{t+n})^{t+n} = (1+z_t)^t \cdot (1+f_{t,t+n})^n$$

The left side is the gross return over $t+n$ years at the long spot rate. The right side decomposes this into $t$ years at spot $z_t$ followed by $n$ years at forward rate $f_{t,t+n}$.

**Step 2 — Isolate $(1+f_{t,t+n})^n$.**

$$(1+f_{t,t+n})^n = \frac{(1+z_{t+n})^{t+n}}{(1+z_t)^t}$$

**Step 3 — Take the $n$-th root.**

$$\boxed{f_{t, t+n} = \left[\frac{(1+z_{t+n})^{t+n}}{(1+z_t)^t}\right]^{1/n} - 1}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Spot Rate as Geometric Mean of Forward Rates

**Statement to Prove:** $z_n = \left[\prod_{i=1}^{n}(1+f_{i-1,i})\right]^{1/n} - 1$

**Step 1 — Telescope the no-arbitrage identity.**

$$(1+z_n)^n = (1+z_1)^1 \cdot (1+f_{1,2})(1+f_{2,3})\cdots(1+f_{n-1,n})$$

Since $z_1 = f_{0,1}$ (boundary condition), the first factor can be written as $(1+f_{0,1})$:

$$(1+z_n)^n = \prod_{i=0}^{n-1}(1+f_{i,i+1})$$

**Step 2 — Take the $n$-th root.**

$$1+z_n = \left[\prod_{i=0}^{n-1}(1+f_{i,i+1})\right]^{1/n}$$

**Step 3 — Final result.**

$$z_n = \left[\prod_{i=0}^{n-1}(1+f_{i,i+1})\right]^{1/n} - 1$$

This proves that the spot rate is the **geometric mean** of forward rates minus one — the core identity of the **Pure Expectations Theory**.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** Given that $z_1 = 5\%$, $z_2 = 6\%$, $z_3 = 6.5\%$. Find $f_{1,2}$, $f_{2,3}$, and $f_{1,3}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $f_{1,2}$.**

$$f_{1,2} = \frac{(1.06)^2}{1.05} - 1 = \frac{1.1236}{1.05} - 1 = 1.07010 - 1 = 7.010\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute $f_{2,3}$.**

$$f_{2,3} = \frac{(1.065)^3}{(1.06)^2} - 1 = \frac{1.207949}{1.1236} - 1 = 1.075041 - 1 = 7.504\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute $f_{1,3}$.**

$$f_{1,3} = \left[\frac{(1.065)^3}{1.05}\right]^{1/2} - 1 = \left[\frac{1.207949}{1.05}\right]^{1/2} - 1$$

$$= \left[1.150428\right]^{1/2} - 1 = 1.072580 - 1 = 7.258\%$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check using geometric mean property.**

$$1+z_3 = \left[(1+z_1)(1+f_{1,2})(1+f_{2,3})\right]^{1/3}$$

$$= \left[1.05 \times 1.07010 \times 1.07504\right]^{1/3} = \left[1.207949\right]^{1/3} = 1.0650 \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern Recognition:** Since $z_1 < z_2 < z_3$, the forward rates $f_{1,2} > z_2 > z_1$ and $f_{2,3} > z_3 > z_2$. This is the mathematical signature of an **upward-sloping yield curve**.

### 3.5 Continuous Compounding Derivation

When rates are continuously compounded, the relationships take the simpler logarithmic form.

**Step 1 — Recall continuous compounding identity.**

For a zero-coupon bond: $P = F e^{-rt}$, so $e^{rt} = F/P$.

**Step 2 — Apply no-arbitrage over two horizons.**

$$e^{r_1 \cdot 1} \cdot e^{f_{1,2}^{c} \cdot 1} = e^{r_2 \cdot 2}$$

$$r_1 + f_{1,2}^{c} = 2r_2$$

**Step 3 — Solve for forward rate.**

$$\boxed{f_{1,2}^{c} = 2r_2 - r_1}$$

**Generalization:** $f_{t, t+n}^{c} = \frac{(t+n)r_{t+n} - t r_t}{n}$

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Decision Framework for KTU Problems

| Given Data | Required Output | Direct Formula |
|---|---|---|
| $z_t, z_{t+n}$ | $f_{t, t+n}$ (discrete) | $f = \left[\frac{(1+z_{t+n})^{t+n}}{(1+z_t)^t}\right]^{1/n} - 1$ |
| $z_t, z_{t+n}$ | $f_{t, t+n}$ (continuous) | $f^c = \frac{(t+n)r_{t+n} - t r_t}{n}$ |
| Series of $f_{i-1,i}$ | $z_n$ | $z_n = \left[\prod (1+f)\right]^{1/n} - 1$ |
| Bond prices | Spot rates | $z_t = (F/P)^{1/t} - 1$ |
| Forward rates | Par yields | Iterative bootstrapping |
| Coupon bond prices | Yield to Maturity | YTM equation (trial-and-error) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Term Structure Architecture: From Spot to Forward

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Market Data: Bond Prices] --> B[Step 1: Compute Spot Rates z_t]
    B --> C[Step 2: Derive Forward Rates f_t_t+1]
    C --> D[Step 3: Construct Yield Curve]
    D --> E[Step 4: Apply Term Structure Theory]
    E --> F{Shape Analysis}
    F --> G[Normal Curve: Expectations Theory]
    F --> H[Inverted Curve: Recession Signal]
    F --> I[Humped Curve: Liquidity Premium]
    F --> J[Flat Curve: Market Segmentation]
    G --> K[Output: Valuation and Risk Decisions]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 No-Arbitrage Forward Rate Derivation Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Spot Rate z_2 for 2 years] --> C[Compound at z_2 for 2 years]
    B[Spot Rate z_1 for 1 year] --> D[Compound at z_1 for 1 year]
    D --> E[Reinvest for 1 more year]
    C --> F[Apply Law of One Price]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Solve for f_1,2]
    G --> H[Forward Rate Output]
```

### 4.3 The Four Theories Compared — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Term Structure Theories] --> B[Pure Expectations Theory]
    A --> C[Liquidity Preference Theory]
    A --> D[Market Segmentation Theory]
    A --> E[Preferred Habitat Theory]
    B --> B1[f_t = E z_t + 0]
    C --> C1[f_t = E z_t + L_t where L_t greater than 0]
    D --> D1[Independent supply/demand by maturity]
    E --> E1[f_t = E z_t plus or minus Risk Premium]
    B1 --> F[Yield Curve Shape Implied]
    C1 --> F
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F
    F --> G[Upward Sloping Generally]
```

### 4.4 Bootstrapping Process — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 0: Obtain 1 year bond price] --> B[Solve for z_1 directly]
    B --> C[Step 1: Obtain 2 year bond price]
    C --> D[Use z_1 and bond price to solve for z_2]
    D --> E[Step 2: Obtain 3 year bond price]
    E --> F[Use z_1 z_2 and bond price to solve for z_3]
    F --> G[Continue iteratively]
    G --> H[Output: Complete Spot Curve z_1 z_2 z_3 ... z_n]
    H --> I[Compute Forward Rates from Spots]
```

### 4.5 Yield Curve Shape vs. Economic Regime

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Economic State] --> B[Recession Expected]
    A --> C[Expansion Expected]
    A --> D[Stable Growth]
    B --> E[Inverted Curve: Long rates less than Short rates]
    C --> F[Normal Curve: Long rates greater than Short rates]
    D --> G[Flat Curve: All maturities similar]
    E --> H[Investor Strategy: Lock in long term yields]
    F --> I[Investor Strategy: Stay short for reinvestment]
    G --> J[Investor Strategy: Barbell or laddered portfolio]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define the **term structure of interest rates**. Distinguish between spot rates and forward rates with suitable examples.

**Model Answer:**

The term structure of interest rates is the relationship between the yields on bonds of similar credit quality and their maturities at a given point in time, depicted graphically by the **yield curve**.

**Spot Rate ($z_t$):** The yield on a zero-coupon bond maturing at time $t$. Example: The 3-year spot rate $z_3 = 6\%$ means ₹100 invested today in a 3-year zero-coupon bond grows to ₹100 × (1.06)³ = ₹119.10.

**Forward Rate ($f_{t,t+1}$):** The interest rate agreed *today* for a loan extending from time $t$ to $t+1$. Example: If $z_1 = 5\%$ and $z_2 = 6\%$, then the 1-year forward rate starting at year 1 is:

$$f_{1,2} = \frac{(1.06)^2}{1.05} - 1 = 7.01\%$$

**Key Difference:** Spot rates apply to investments starting today; forward rates apply to investments starting in the future, but both are determined *today*.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

State and explain the **Liquidity Preference Theory** of the term structure.

**Model Answer:**

The Liquidity Preference Theory, proposed by **J.M. Keynes** and **J.R. Hicks**, states that:

> Forward rates equal expected future spot rates plus a positive liquidity premium that increases with maturity.

Mathematically:

$$f_{t, t+n} = E[z_{t+n}] + L_{t,n}, \quad L_{t,n} > 0, \quad \frac{\partial L_{t,n}}{\partial n} > 0$$

**Reasoning:**
1. Investors prefer liquidity, i.e., short-term bonds that can be readily converted to cash.
2. Long-term bonds carry price risk and reinvestment risk.
3. To induce investors to hold long-term bonds, issuers must offer a **liquidity premium** that grows with maturity.

**Implication:** The yield curve normally slopes upward, even when future short rates are expected to remain constant, because the liquidity premium dominates.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Choice Option)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**Q3(a). [7 Marks]** Given the following spot rates: $z_1 = 4.5\%$, $z_2 = 5.2\%$, $z_3 = 5.8\%$. Calculate:
(i) The 1-year forward rate starting at the end of year 1, i.e., $f_{1,2}$.
(ii) The 1-year forward rate starting at the end of year 2, i.e., $f_{2,3}$.
(iii) The 2-year forward rate starting at the end of year 1, i.e., $f_{1,3}$.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Calculation of $f_{1,2}$:**

Apply the no-arbitrage identity:

$$(1+z_2)^2 = (1+z_1)(1+f_{1,2})$$

Substitute values:

$$(1.052)^2 = (1.045)(1+f_{1,2})$$

$$1.106704 = 1.045 \cdot (1+f_{1,2})$$

$$1+f_{1,2} = \frac{1.106704}{1.045} = 1.059048$$

$$\boxed{f_{1,2} = 5.905\%}$$

**(ii) Calculation of $f_{2,3}$:**

$$(1+z_3)^3 = (1+z_2)^2 \cdot (1+f_{2,3})$$

$$(1.058)^3 = (1.052)^2 \cdot (1+f_{2,3})$$

$$1.184662 = 1.106704 \cdot (1+f_{2,3})$$

$$1+f_{2,3} = \frac{1.184662}{1.106704} = 1.070472$$

$$\boxed{f_{2,3} = 7.047\%}$$

**(iii) Calculation of $f_{1,3}$:**

Apply the 2-year forward rate formula:

$$(1+f_{1,3})^2 = \frac{(1+z_3)^3}{(1+z_1)} = \frac{1.184662}{1.045} = 1.133648$$

$$1+f_{1,3} = \sqrt{1.133648} = 1.064731$$

$$\boxed{f_{1,3} = 6.473\%}$$

**Cross-check:** The geometric mean property requires:

$$(1+z_3)^3 = (1+z_1)(1+f_{1,2})(1+f_{2,3}) = 1.045 \times 1.059048 \times 1.070472$$

$$= 1.045 \times 1.133644 \approx 1.184658 \approx (1.058)^3 \checkmark$$

---

**Q3(b). [7 Marks]** Explain the **Pure Expectations Theory** of the term structure. Under this theory, what should be the shape of the yield curve if the market expects future short-term rates to fall by 1% each year for the next 3 years, given that the current 1-year rate is 6%?

**Model Solution:**

**Explanation of Pure Expectations Theory:**

The Pure Expectations Theory, attributed to **Irving Fisher**, asserts that:
- Long-term spot rates are the **geometric averages** of expected future short-term rates.
- Forward rates are **unbiased estimators** of expected future spot rates.
- There is **no liquidity premium** or risk premium in bond yields.

Mathematical formulation:

$$1+z_n = \left[\prod_{i=0}^{n-1}E[(1+z_{i+1})]\right]^{1/n}$$

$$f_{t, t+n} = E[z_{t+n}]$$

**Computation for the given scenario:**

Expected future 1-year rates:
- $E[z_1] = 6\%$
- $E[z_2] = 5\%$
- $E[z_3] = 4\%$

**1-year spot rate:** $z_1 = 6\%$

**2-year spot rate:**

$$1+z_2 = \left[(1.06)(1.05)\right]^{1/2} = \left[1.113\right]^{1/2} = 1.05503$$

$$z_2 = 5.50\%$$

**3-year spot rate:**

$$1+z_3 = \left[(1.06)(1.05)(1.04)\right]^{1/3} = \left[1.15752\right]^{1/3} = 1.04996$$

$$z_3 = 4.996\% \approx 5.00\%$$

**Yield Curve:** $z_1 = 6\% > z_2 = 5.50\% > z_3 = 5.00\%$

This is a **downward-sloping (inverted) yield curve**, indicating the market expects declining future short-term rates. Under Pure Expectations Theory, an inverted curve is a reliable signal of anticipated economic slowdown.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**Q4(a). [7 Marks]** The prices (per ₹100 face value) of three Treasury zero-coupon bonds are given below:

| Maturity (Years) | Price (₹) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 95.24 |
| 2 | 89.00 |
| 3 | 82.00 |

Calculate:
(i) The 1-year, 2-year, and 3-year spot rates.
(ii) The 1-year forward rate for the second year, $f_{1,2}$.
(iii) The 1-year forward rate for the third year, $f_{2,3}$.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Spot Rates:**

For $t = 1$:

$$P_1 = \frac{100}{1+z_1} \Rightarrow 95.24 = \frac{100}{1+z_1}$$

$$1+z_1 = \frac{100}{95.24} = 1.05 \Rightarrow \boxed{z_1 = 5.00\%}$$

For $t = 2$:

$$P_2 = \frac{100}{(1+z_2)^2} \Rightarrow 89.00 = \frac{100}{(1+z_2)^2}$$

$$(1+z_2)^2 = \frac{100}{89.00} = 1.12360$$

$$1+z_2 = \sqrt{1.12360} = 1.06 \Rightarrow \boxed{z_2 = 6.00\%}$$

For $t = 3$:

$$P_3 = \frac{100}{(1+z_3)^3} \Rightarrow 82.00 = \frac{100}{(1+z_3)^3}$$

$$(1+z_3)^3 = \frac{100}{82.00} = 1.21951$$

$$1+z_3 = (1.21951)^{1/3} = 1.06860 \Rightarrow \boxed{z_3 = 6.86\%}$$

**(ii) Forward Rate $f_{1,2}$:**

$$f_{1,2} = \frac{(1+z_2)^2}{(1+z_1)} - 1 = \frac{1.06^2}{1.05} - 1 = \frac{1.1236}{1.05} - 1$$

$$= 1.07010 - 1 = \boxed{7.01\%}$$

**(iii) Forward Rate $f_{2,3}$:**

$$f_{2,3} = \frac{(1+z_3)^3}{(1+z_2)^2} - 1 = \frac{1.21951}{1.1236} - 1 = 1.08536 - 1$$

$$\boxed{f_{2,3} = 8.54\%}$$

**Interpretation:** Since forward rates exceed spot rates at each horizon, the market expects rising future interest rates — a classic **upward-sloping yield curve** signaling economic expansion.

---

**Q4(b). [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the **Market Segmentation Theory** and the **Preferred Habitat Theory** of the term structure.

**Model Solution:**

| Dimension | Market Segmentation Theory | Preferred Habitat Theory |
|---|---|---|
| **Proponent** | J.M. Culbertson (1957) | F. Modigliani and R. Sutch (1966) |
| **Core Assumption** | Market is rigidly segmented by maturity | Market has preferred habitats but allows substitution |
| **Investor Behavior** | No switching between maturities | Investors will switch if compensated |
| **Yield Determinants** | Independent supply/demand in each segment | Supply/demand with risk premium adjustments |
| **Forward Rates** | May be above or below expected spot rates | May be above or below expected spot rates |
| **Substitutability** | Zero (perfectly inelastic) | Partial (responsive to yields) |
| **Empirical Validity** | Weak — does not explain curve smoothing | Stronger — explains gradual curve transitions |

**Key Similarities:**
1. Both reject the pure expectations view that forward rates are unbiased predictors.
2. Both allow the yield curve to deviate from expected future spot rates.
3. Both emphasize the role of institutional investors (banks, insurance companies, pension funds) with maturity-specific liabilities.

**Key Differences:**
1. The Market Segmentation Theory assumes **zero substitutability**, while the Preferred Habitat Theory allows **limited substitutability**.
2. The Market Segmentation Theory predicts **disconnected** yield curves across maturities, while the Preferred Habitat Theory predicts **smoothly connected** curves with risk premiums bridging habitats.
3. The Preferred Habitat Theory introduces a **risk premium** term to capture compensation for deviating from preferred maturity.

**Practical Implication:** The Preferred Habitat Theory is widely accepted in modern fixed-income analysis because it accommodates empirical regularities such as the consistent upward slope of the yield curve and the role of central banks in anchoring short rates.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Spot and Forward Rates:** Students frequently write $z_n = f_{n-1, n}$. This is only true for $n=1$. For $n > 1$, the spot rate is the geometric mean, NOT the single-period forward rate.
>
> 2. **Ignoring Compounding Frequency:** A problem stated in continuous compounding must use logarithmic identities, while discrete compounding uses algebraic powers. Mixing the two leads to wrong answers and zero marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert Percentages:** When substituting into $(1+r)^t$, ensure $r$ is in decimal (e.g., 5% → 0.05). Using 5 directly inflates the result by 100x.
>
> 4. **Misapplying Geometric Mean:** The relationship $z_n = [\prod(1+f)]^{1/n} - 1$ uses product, NOT average. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Verification Step:** Always cross-check answers using a different formula (e.g., verify $f_{1,3}$ using both the direct formula and the product identity).
>
> 6. **Theory Questions Without Numerical Examples:** When asked to "explain" a theory, KTU examiners award 2 marks for numerical illustration. Include at least one example in your answer.

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Term Structure of Interest Rates**

- **Spot Rate ($z_t$):** Yield on a zero-coupon bond maturing at time $t$; derived from current bond prices.
- **Forward Rate ($f_{t,t+n}$):** Rate locked in *today* for borrowing/lending between $t$ and $t+n$.
- **Key Identity:** $(1+z_n)^n = (1+z_{n-1})^{n-1}(1+f_{n-1,n})$ — the no-arbitage backbone.
- **Boundary Condition:** $f_{0,1} = z_1$. Always.
- **Pure Expectations Theory:** Forward rate equals expected future spot rate. No premium.
- **Liquidity Preference Theory:** Forward rate = Expected spot rate + Positive liquidity premium.
- **Market Segmentation Theory:** Each maturity segment is independent; no substitution.
- **Preferred Habitat Theory:** Limited substitution with maturity-specific risk premiums.
- **Continuous Compounding Form:** $f_{t,t+n}^c = \frac{(t+n)r_{t+n} - t r_t}{n}$.
- **Yield Curve Shapes:** Normal (upward), Inverted (downward), Flat, Humped.
- **Bootstrapping:** Sequential method to extract spot rates from coupon-bearing bond prices.
- **Geometric Mean Property:** $z_n$ is the geometric mean of forward rates.
- **Real-World Use:** Central bank policy signaling, bond portfolio immunization, derivatives calibration.
- **KTU Exam Weightage:** Expect 1–2 questions (7–14 marks combined) in any Module 2 examination.
- **Most-Tested Formula:** $f_{t,t+n} = \left[\frac{(1+z_{t+n})^{t+n}}{(1+z_t)^t}\right]^{1/n} - 1$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Duration and Convexity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET635 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Duration and Convexity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Duration** is the weighted average maturity of a bond's cash flows, where the weights are the present values of each cash flow as a fraction of the bond's total price. It is measured in **years** and serves as the primary measure of a bond's interest rate risk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convexity** is the second-order measure of bond price sensitivity to changes in yield. It captures the curvature of the **price-yield relationship** and corrects the linear approximation error introduced by duration alone.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, three principal forms of duration are tested:

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Macaulay Duration** | The cash-flow-weighted average time to receive the bond's payments, measured in years. |
| **Modified Duration** | Macaulay Duration scaled by $\frac{1}{1+y}$; gives the percentage price change per unit change in yield. |
| **Effective Duration** | Used for bonds with embedded options; computed by repricing under parallel yield shifts of $\pm \Delta y$. |

The standard yield shift convention in KTU numerical problems is **$\Delta y = 0.0001$ (1 basis point)** for DV01 (Dollar Value of a 01 bp) calculations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are balancing a **seesaw** where each cash flow of the bond is a child sitting at a distance equal to the time until that cash flow is received (year $t$). The bond's price is the **total weight** of all children. **Macaulay Duration is the balance point (fulcrum)** — the single distance at which the seesaw would be in equilibrium.

- A bond that returns all money at year 10 → Duration ≈ 10.
- A bond that returns money steadily over 10 years → Duration < 10 (centre of mass shifts earlier).
- A **zero-coupon bond** has Duration **exactly equal to its maturity** because there is only one cash flow.

**Convexity** is the *bulge* in the price-yield curve. If you plot bond price (Y-axis) versus yield (X-axis), the curve is **bowed upward (convex)**. Duration tells you the **slope** of this curve; convexity tells you **how curved** it is.

> [!NOTE]
> A positive convexity is a *friend* to the bondholder: for equal upward and downward yield shifts, the price gain from a yield drop is **larger** than the price loss from a yield rise.

---

## 1.3 Physical / Market Constants Used

- **Risk-free yield (y)**: quoted as a decimal, e.g., $y = 0.08$ for 8%.
- **1 basis point (bp)** = $0.0001 = 0.01\%$.
- **DV01**: dollar price change per 1 bp move in yield.
- Modified duration is **dimensionless** (percentage price change per unit yield).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Architecture of Bond Risk Measurement

### Step 1 — Bond Pricing Foundation
A bond's price is the present value of all future cash flows:

$$P = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+y)^t}$$

where $C_t$ = cash flow at time $t$ (coupon + principal), $y$ = yield-to-maturity, $n$ = number of periods.

### Step 2 — First Derivative: Macaulay & Modified Duration
Differentiating $P$ with respect to $y$ gives the slope of the price-yield curve. Normalizing by $P$ produces Modified Duration.

### Step 3 — Second Derivative: Convexity
Differentiating **twice** with respect to $y$ gives the curvature (convexity) of the price-yield curve.

### Step 4 — Taylor Series Approximation
Using a second-order Taylor expansion, the percentage price change of a bond for a yield change $\Delta y$ is:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D_{mod} \cdot \Delta y + \frac{1}{2} \cdot C \cdot (\Delta y)^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **first term** is the **linear (Duration)** approximation. The **second term** is the **convexity correction**. Higher $\vert \Delta y \vert$ ⇒ larger the linear error ⇒ convexity matters more.

### Step 5 — Properties Governing Duration & Convexity

- **Higher coupon** → lower duration (cash flows arrive sooner).
- **Higher yield** → lower duration (discounts distant cash flows more).
- **Longer maturity** → higher duration (in general).
- **Higher convexity** is preferred for **non-callable** bonds. Callable bonds exhibit **negative convexity** at low yields.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table. Every numerical problem in Module 2 reduces to one or more of these identities.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Bond Price | $P = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+y)^t}$ | Currency (₹ / $) |
| 2 | Macaulay Duration | $D = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} t \cdot \dfrac{C_t}{(1+y)^t}}{P}$ | Years |
| 3 | Modified Duration | $D_{mod} = \dfrac{D}{1+y}$ | Dimensionless (% per unit y) |
| 4 | % Price Change (Duration only) | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D_{mod} \cdot \Delta y$ | Decimal |
| 5 | Convexity | $C = \dfrac{1}{P} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{t(t+1) \cdot C_t}{(1+y)^{t+2}}$ | Years² |
| 6 | % Price Change (Duration + Convexity) | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D_{mod} \cdot \Delta y + \dfrac{1}{2} C (\Delta y)^2$ | Decimal |
| 7 | Dollar Duration | $D_{\$} = D_{mod} \cdot P$ | Currency per unit y |
| 8 | DV01 (PV01) | $\text{DV01} = D_{mod} \cdot P \cdot 0.0001$ | Currency per 1 bp |
| 9 | Effective Duration | $D_{eff} = \dfrac{P_{-} - P_{+}}{2 \cdot P_0 \cdot \Delta y}$ | Used with embedded options |
| 10 | Effective Convexity | $C_{eff} = \dfrac{P_{-} + P_{+} - 2 P_0}{P_0 \cdot (\Delta y)^2}$ | Used with embedded options |

> [!NOTE]
> In Table row 4 and 6, $\Delta y$ is taken as the **change in yield in decimal form**. Always keep this consistent — examiners deduct marks when the answer is "off by a factor of 100".

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering / Finance Utility

- **Bank Treasury & ALM (Asset-Liability Management)**: Banks immunise their net interest margin by matching the **duration** of assets to the duration of liabilities.
- **Pension Fund Management**: Liability-driven investment (LDI) strategies use duration matching to ensure funded status is insulated from rate shocks.
- **Fixed-Income Portfolio Management**: Portfolio duration is the **value-weighted average** of constituent bond durations — enables hedging with interest-rate futures.
- **Risk Reporting (Basel III / IFRS 7)**: IRRBB (Interest Rate Risk in the Banking Book) uses **Effective Duration** and **Effective Convexity** for EVE and NII sensitivity reporting.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Macaulay Duration

**Start** from the bond price equation:

$$P = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+y)^t}$$

Define the **present value of the $t$-th cash flow** as $PV_t = \dfrac{C_t}{(1+y)^t}$.

The weight of cash flow $t$ in the total price is $w_t = \dfrac{PV_t}{P}$, with $\sum w_t = 1$.

**Define Macaulay Duration** as the weighted average time:

$$D = \sum_{t=1}^{n} t \cdot w_t = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} t \cdot \dfrac{C_t}{(1+y)^t}}{P}$$

**Why "weighted average"?** Because cash flows received sooner are worth more (less discounting) and contribute more to total price — duration captures this centre of mass in time.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Modified Duration

Differentiate $P$ with respect to $y$:

$$\frac{dP}{dy} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} C_t \cdot \frac{d}{dy}\left[(1+y)^{-t}\right] = \sum_{t=1}^{n} C_t \cdot \left[ -t (1+y)^{-t-1} \right]$$

Re-arrange:

$$\frac{dP}{dy} = -\frac{1}{1+y} \sum_{t=1}^{n} t \cdot \frac{C_t}{(1+y)^t} = -\frac{1}{1+y} \cdot D \cdot P$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{1}{P} \frac{dP}{dy} = -\frac{D}{1+y} = -D_{mod}$$

So Modified Duration is **the percentage price change per unit change in yield**, and:

$$D_{mod} = \frac{D}{1+y}$$

The first-order price approximation is therefore:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D_{mod} \cdot \Delta y$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Convexity

Differentiate $P$ a second time with respect to $y$:

$$\frac{d^2 P}{dy^2} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} C_t \cdot t(t+1)(1+y)^{-t-2}$$

Normalise by price $P$:

$$\frac{1}{P} \frac{d^2 P}{dy^2} = \frac{1}{P} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{t(t+1) \cdot C_t}{(1+y)^{t+2}} \equiv C$$

This is the **convexity** of the bond. The full second-order Taylor approximation of price change is:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D_{mod} \cdot \Delta y + \frac{1}{2} \cdot C \cdot (\Delta y)^2$$

**Economic interpretation**:
- The first term is **linear** (tangent line approximation — underestimates for drops, overestimates for rises).
- The second term is **always positive** for vanilla bonds — it adds gain for $\vert \Delta y \vert$ large and equal in either direction.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Pattern

> **Problem**: A 3-year bond pays annual coupons of ₹100. Par = ₹1000. Yield = 10% p.a. Compute Macaulay Duration, Modified Duration, Convexity, and the approximate % price change for a yield drop of 50 bps.

**Step 1 — Cash Flows**: $C_1 = 100$, $C_2 = 100$, $C_3 = 1100$.

**Step 2 — Discount Factors**: $\frac{1}{1.10} = 0.9091$, $\frac{1}{1.21} = 0.8264$, $\frac{1}{1.331} = 0.7513$.

**Step 3 — Present Values of Each Cash Flow**:

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 100 \times 0.9091 = 90.91 \\
PV_2 &= 100 \times 0.8264 = 82.64 \\
PV_3 &= 1100 \times 0.7513 = 826.45 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Bond Price**:

$$P = 90.91 + 82.64 + 826.45 = 1000.00 \;\text{(par, as expected)}$$

**Step 5 — Macaulay Duration**:

$$D = \frac{1(90.91) + 2(82.64) + 3(826.45)}{1000.00} = \frac{90.91 + 165.28 + 2479.35}{1000} = 2.7355 \;\text{years}$$

**Step 6 — Modified Duration**:

$$D_{mod} = \frac{2.7355}{1.10} = 2.4869$$

**Step 7 — Convexity**:

$$C = \frac{1}{1000}\left[\frac{1(2)(100)}{1.10^{3}} + \frac{2(3)(100)}{1.10^{4}} + \frac{3(4)(1100)}{1.10^{5}}\right]$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{1}{1000}\left[\frac{200}{1.331} + \frac{600}{1.4641} + \frac{13200}{1.61051}\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{1000}\left[150.26 + 409.81 + 8196.20\right] \\
&= \frac{8756.27}{1000} = 8.7563 \;\text{years}^{2} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — % Price Change for $\Delta y = -0.005$**:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -(2.4869)(-0.005) + \frac{1}{2}(8.7563)(-0.005)^2$$

$$= 0.012435 + 0.0001095 = 0.012544 \;\text{or}\; 1.2544\%$$

**Step 9 — Predicted New Price**: $P_{new} = 1000 \times 1.012544 = 1012.54$.

**Step 10 — Comparison with True Price**: Re-pricing gives $P_{new,true} = 1013.49$. **Convexity correction added ₹0.95** in accuracy — exactly the purpose of convexity.

---

## 3.5 Bond Duration vs. Bond Maturity — Standard Result Table

| Bond Type | Coupon | Yield | Maturity (yrs) | Macaulay Duration (yrs) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Zero-coupon | 0 | 10% | 5 | 5.000 |
| Zero-coupon | 0 | 10% | 10 | 10.000 |
| 5% Coupon | 5% | 10% | 5 | 4.560 |
| 10% Coupon | 10% | 10% | 5 | 4.250 |
| 5% Coupon | 5% | 10% | 10 | 7.570 |
| 10% Coupon | 10% | 10% | 10 | 6.660 |

**Reading**: Duration is always **less than maturity** for coupon-paying bonds, and the gap widens with higher coupons.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Price–Yield Curve Showing Convexity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisX["Yield Axis increasing left to right"]
    axisY["Price Axis increasing bottom to top"]
    axisX --- axisY
    curve["Convex Curve bows upward to the left as yield drops and price rises"]
    tangent["Tangent line at current yield shows Duration slope"]
    noteA["Drop in yield gives LARGER price gain due to convexity"]
    noteB["Rise in yield gives SMALLER price loss due to convexity"]
    curve --- tangent
    curve --- noteA
    curve --- noteB
```

**Interpretation**: The curve is **convex to the origin**. The straight tangent line **overestimates** the price drop when yield rises and **underestimates** the price rise when yield falls. Convexity adjusts for this asymmetry.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bond Risk Measurement Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA["Bond Cash Flow Stream C1 to Cn"] --> stepA["Compute Present Values PVt"]
    stepA --> stepB["Sum to obtain Bond Price P"]
    stepB --> stepC["Apply Macaulay Duration Formula D equals sum of t times PVt divided by P"]
    stepC --> stepD["Compute Modified Duration Dmod equals D divided by 1 plus y"]
    stepB --> stepE["Compute Convexity C using second derivative formula"]
    stepD --> stepF["First Order Approximation dP by P equals minus Dmod times dy"]
    stepE --> stepG["Convexity Correction term half C times dy squared"]
    stepF --> stepH["Combined Duration plus Convexity Price Change Estimate"]
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> outputX["Final Price Estimate Pnew equals P times 1 plus combined change"]
```

**Reading**: This is the standard **engineering pipeline** for bond risk analytics. Feed cash flows in at the top, and the system outputs a Taylor-series-accurate price estimate for any yield shock.

---

## 4.3 Duration-Matching Immunisation Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    asset["Asset Portfolio with Duration DA and Convexity CA"] --> match{"Match DA equals DL"}
    liab["Liability Stream with Duration DL and Convexity CL"] --> match
    match --> immunised["Immunised Portfolio insulated from parallel yield shifts"]
    note["Convexity gap CA greater than CL provides additional cushion against large rate moves"]
    immunised --- note
```

**Reading**: Classical **immunisation theory** (Redington, 1952) requires both $D_A = D_L$ and $C_A > C_L$ for a robust hedge against large parallel rate shocks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between Macaulay Duration and Modified Duration. Why is Modified Duration more useful for measuring interest rate risk?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Macaulay Duration** is the **weighted average time** to receive a bond's cash flows, measured in years. It is computed as $D = \frac{\sum t \cdot PV(C_t)}{P}$.
- **Modified Duration** is Macaulay Duration divided by $(1+y)$, i.e., $D_{mod} = \frac{D}{1+y}$. It is dimensionless.
- Macaulay Duration tells you the *time-centre* of cash flows, but does **not directly** measure price sensitivity.
- Modified Duration is **the percentage change in bond price for a unit (decimal) change in yield**: $\frac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D_{mod} \cdot \Delta y$.
- Hence, Modified Duration is the direct **risk measure** used in hedging, DV01, and ALM applications.
- **(1 mark each for definition; 1 mark for utility explanation)**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** What is Convexity? Why is a bond with higher convexity preferred over one with lower convexity?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Convexity** is the **second derivative** of the bond price with respect to yield, normalised by price: $C = \frac{1}{P}\frac{d^2 P}{dy^2}$.
- It measures the **curvature** of the price-yield relationship and corrects the linear approximation error of duration.
- For two bonds with identical duration and yield, the one with **higher convexity** has:
  - **Greater price appreciation** when yields fall.
  - **Smaller price depreciation** when yields rise.
- Hence, higher convexity is **preferred by bondholders** (asymmetric upside). Callable bonds exhibit negative convexity at low yields, which penalises holders.
- **(1 mark definition; 2 marks for preference reasoning with asymmetric gain/loss explanation)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** A 4-year bond with face value ₹1000 pays an annual coupon of 8%. The current yield-to-maturity is 10%. Compute the Macaulay Duration, Modified Duration, and Convexity of the bond. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the duration-only and duration-plus-convexity approximations, estimate the new bond price if the yield drops by 75 basis points. Compare with the actual re-priced bond price. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

**Cash flows**: $C_1 = 80$, $C_2 = 80$, $C_3 = 80$, $C_4 = 1080$.

**Discount factors** at $y = 0.10$: $\frac{1}{1.1^t}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
t=1: & \quad 0.9091 \\
t=2: & \quad 0.8264 \\
t=3: & \quad 0.7513 \\
t=4: & \quad 0.6830 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Present Values**:

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 80 \times 0.9091 = 72.73 \\
PV_2 &= 80 \times 0.8264 = 66.12 \\
PV_3 &= 80 \times 0.7513 = 60.11 \\
PV_4 &= 1080 \times 0.6830 = 737.62 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Bond Price**:

$$P = 72.73 + 66.12 + 60.11 + 737.62 = 936.58$$

**Macaulay Duration**:

$$D = \frac{1(72.73) + 2(66.12) + 3(60.11) + 4(737.62)}{936.58}$$

$$D = \frac{72.73 + 132.24 + 180.33 + 2950.48}{936.58} = \frac{3335.78}{936.58} = 3.5616 \;\text{years}$$

**Modified Duration**:

$$D_{mod} = \frac{3.5616}{1.10} = 3.2378$$

**Convexity**:

$$C = \frac{1}{936.58}\left[\frac{1(2)(80)}{1.1^3} + \frac{2(3)(80)}{1.1^4} + \frac{3(4)(80)}{1.1^5} + \frac{4(5)(1080)}{1.1^6}\right]$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{1}{936.58}\left[\frac{160}{1.331} + \frac{480}{1.4641} + \frac{960}{1.61051} + \frac{21600}{1.77156}\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{936.58}\left[120.21 + 327.85 + 596.07 + 12192.06\right] \\
&= \frac{13236.19}{936.58} = 14.1328 \;\text{years}^2 \\
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**Yield drop**: $\Delta y = -0.0075$.

**Duration-only approximation**:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D_{mod} \cdot \Delta y = -(3.2378)(-0.0075) = 0.024284 = 2.4284\%$$

Predicted price (duration only): $P_{dur} = 936.58 \times 1.024284 = 959.32$.

**Duration + Convexity approximation**:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D_{mod} \cdot \Delta y + \frac{1}{2} C (\Delta y)^2$$

$$= 0.024284 + \frac{1}{2}(14.1328)(0.0075)^2$$

$$= 0.024284 + \frac{1}{2}(14.1328)(0.00005625)$$

$$= 0.024284 + 0.000397 = 0.024681 = 2.4681\%$$

Predicted price (duration + convexity): $P_{d+c} = 936.58 \times 1.024681 = 959.69$.

**Actual re-priced bond price** at $y = 0.0925$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{actual} &= \frac{80}{1.0925} + \frac{80}{1.0925^2} + \frac{80}{1.0925^3} + \frac{1080}{1.0925^4} \\
&= 73.23 + 67.04 + 61.36 + 757.93 = 959.56 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Comparison**:

| Method | Price (₹) | Error vs. Actual |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Duration only | 959.32 | -0.24 |
| Duration + Convexity | 959.69 | +0.13 |

**Conclusion**: Adding the convexity correction **reduces the estimation error** by approximately 46%, demonstrating that convexity materially improves price-change forecasting accuracy for yield shocks beyond a few basis points.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define Effective Duration and Effective Convexity. Why are these preferred over standard (Macaulay-based) measures for bonds with embedded options? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A callable bond has a current price of ₹980. When yields are shocked up by 50 bps, the price rises to ₹990; when shocked down by 50 bps, the price falls to ₹968. Compute Effective Duration and Effective Convexity. Comment on whether the convexity is positive or negative. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

- **Effective Duration** measures the percentage price change for a parallel yield shock, **without** assuming the bond's cash flows remain unchanged. It is defined as:

$$D_{eff} = \frac{P_{-} - P_{+}}{2 \cdot P_0 \cdot \Delta y}$$

- **Effective Convexity** is the second-difference measure:

$$C_{eff} = \frac{P_{-} + P_{+} - 2 P_0}{P_0 \cdot (\Delta y)^2}$$

- For **option-free** bonds, Effective Duration equals Modified Duration and Effective Convexity equals analytical Convexity.
- For **bonds with embedded options** (callable, putable, mortgage-backed), the cash flows **change** with yield because the option may or may not be exercised.
- Macaulay/Modified Duration **assumes fixed cash flows**, leading to **biased** risk estimates for these securities.
- Effective Duration captures the **actual** empirical price response, including the option exercise behaviour, and is the standard measure for ABS, MBS, and callable corporate bonds.
- **(3 marks for definitions; 4 marks for the embedded-option rationale)**.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Given**: $P_0 = 980$, $P_{+} = 990$ (yield +50 bps), $P_{-} = 968$ (yield -50 bps), $\Delta y = 0.005$.

**Effective Duration**:

$$D_{eff} = \frac{P_{-} - P_{+}}{2 \cdot P_0 \cdot \Delta y} = \frac{968 - 990}{2 \cdot 980 \cdot 0.005}$$

$$D_{eff} = \frac{-22}{9.80} = -2.2449$$

**Effective Convexity**:

$$C_{eff} = \frac{P_{-} + P_{+} - 2 P_0}{P_0 \cdot (\Delta y)^2} = \frac{968 + 990 - 2(980)}{980 \cdot (0.005)^2}$$

$$C_{eff} = \frac{1958 - 1960}{980 \cdot 0.000025} = \frac{-2}{0.0245} = -81.63 \;\text{years}^2$$

**Commentary**:

- The **Effective Duration is negative**, which is characteristic of deep-in-the-money callable bonds — the bond behaves like a short position in the embedded call option.
- The **Effective Convexity is negative**, confirming **negative convexity** at the current yield level.
- **Economic interpretation**: As yield rises, the call becomes less likely to be exercised, so the bond's price rises more than it would without the option (price compression from the call is relaxed). As yield falls, the call becomes more likely, capping the price appreciation. This asymmetric price response is the hallmark of negative convexity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Submit**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Macaulay & Modified Duration** — the most common deduction. If the question asks for "Duration measure of price sensitivity", answer with **Modified Duration**, not Macaulay.
> 2. **Sign error in $\Delta y$** — a yield *drop* gives a *positive* $\Delta P / P$ (bond price rises). Many students write the wrong sign.
> 3. **Convexity units** — convexity is in **years²**, not years. The Taylor series has $\frac{1}{2} C (\Delta y)^2$, where $(\Delta y)^2$ is dimensionless, so $C$ must carry year² units for dimensional consistency.
> 4. **DV01 vs. Modified Duration** — DV01 = $D_{mod} \cdot P \cdot 0.0001$. A common mistake is to use $D$ (Macaulay) instead of $D_{mod}$.
> 5. **Rounding** — KTU examiners allow intermediate rounding to 4 decimal places. Carry at least 4 dp in $D_{mod}$ and $C$ to avoid cumulative errors in the final price estimate.
> 6. **Effective vs. Analytical Convexity** — for option-free bonds they are numerically equal; for option-laden bonds, only the effective form is correct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

- ☐ Macaulay Duration = weighted average time of cash flows (units: years).
- ☐ Modified Duration = $\frac{D}{1+y}$ (dimensionless; sensitivity measure).
- ☐ **First-order price change**: $\Delta P / P \approx -D_{mod} \cdot \Delta y$.
- ☐ Convexity = $\frac{1}{P} \sum \frac{t(t+1) C_t}{(1+y)^{t+2}}$ (units: years²).
- ☐ **Second-order price change**: $\Delta P / P \approx -D_{mod} \cdot \Delta y + \frac{1}{2} C (\Delta y)^2$.
- ☐ DV01 = $D_{mod} \cdot P \cdot 0.0001$ — the dollar price change for a 1 bp yield move.
- ☐ **Effective Duration** and **Effective Convexity** are mandatory for option-embedded bonds (callable, MBS, ABS).
- ☐ **Higher convexity** is always beneficial for the holder of an option-free bond.
- ☐ **Callable bonds** display **negative convexity** at low yields (price is capped by call).
- ☐ Duration of a **zero-coupon bond = its maturity**.
- ☐ Duration of a **consol (perpetuity)** = $\frac{1+y}{y}$.
- ☐ **Immunisation** requires $D_A = D_L$ and $C_A > C_L$ for protection against parallel yield shifts.
- ☐ Portfolio Duration = market-value-weighted average of constituent bond durations.
- ☐ Portfolio Convexity = market-value-weighted average of constituent bond convexities (ignoring cross terms).
- ☐ **Always carry 4 decimal places** in Modified Duration and Convexity for KTU numerical accuracy.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Mortgage-Backed Securities. Risk Management Tools and Techniques: Types of Financial Risks - Market
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET635 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Mortgage-Backed Securities. Risk Management Tools and Techniques: Types of Financial Risks - Market

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS)

### Formal Definition
A **Mortgage-Backed Security (MBS)** is a type of asset-backed security (ABS) that is secured by a pool (or collection) of mortgage loans. The cash flow generated from the underlying mortgage pool — comprising monthly principal and interest payments from individual homebuyers (mortgagors) — is aggregated, structured, and redistributed to investors in the form of tradable securities. MBS thus transform a traditionally illiquid, locally held bank asset (a home loan) into a liquid, standardized, and tradable capital-market instrument.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 PEPET635 scheme, MBS is studied as a *securitization product* — a canonical example of how engineering mathematical models (cash-flow waterfalls, prepayment models) can be applied to restructure financial assets.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Mortgage Smoothie"
Imagine a bank holding 10,000 individual home loans. Each loan is a different size, has a different borrower, a different interest rate, and matures at a different time. Selling each loan separately to investors would be chaotic, expensive, and opaque. Instead, the bank **"blends"** all 10,000 mortgages into a single homogeneous financial product — just as a fruit vendor blends many fruits into a smoothie. This "smoothie" is sliced into standardized cups (called **tranches**) and sold to investors who now enjoy predictable, diversified exposure to the housing market. The blender is the **Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV)**, and the standardized cups are the **MBS tranches**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (Prepayment Convention):** Prepayment speeds in the U.S. MBS market are quoted using the **PSA (Public Securities Association / now PSA Prepayment Model)** standard benchmark. **100% PSA = 0.2% CPR in month 1, ramping linearly to 6% CPR in month 30, and remaining at 6% CPR thereafter.**

### Types of MBS
- **Pass-Through MBS (PT MBS):** All investors receive cash flow *pro rata* in proportion to their holding. The simplest structure.
- **Collateralized Mortgage Obligation (CMO):** A multi-class MBS where cash flows are distributed in a prescribed priority (senior tranches paid first, junior tranches absorb losses first).
- **Stripped MBS — Interest-Only (IO) and Principal-Only (PO) strips:** IO holders receive *only* the interest portion; PO holders receive *only* the principal portion. Highly sensitive to prepayment speeds.

---

## 1.2 Risk Management: Types of Financial Risks

### Formal Definition
A **financial risk** is the quantifiable possibility that the actual return on an investment or portfolio will differ from the expected return, resulting in a loss. Under the 2024 KTU syllabus, financial risks are classified into **Market, Credit, Operational, Liquidity, Legal, and Systemic** risks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 Focus:** This topic specifically addresses **Market Risk** — the risk of losses arising from movements in market prices such as equity prices, interest rates, foreign exchange rates, and commodity prices.

### The Four Pillars of Market Risk

| # | Risk Type | Description | Real-World Trigger |
|---|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Equity (Price) Risk** | Loss due to fall in stock/index prices | Stock market crash |
| 2 | **Interest Rate Risk** | Loss due to adverse movement in interest rates | Central bank rate hike |
| 3 | **Currency (FX) Risk** | Loss due to foreign exchange rate fluctuations | Rupee depreciation vs USD |
| 4 | **Commodity Risk** | Loss due to volatility in commodity prices | Oil price shock |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Ship Captain"
Picture a financial manager as a **ship captain** sailing across an ocean:
- **Equity Risk** = waves of stock-market tide lifting or sinking the ship.
- **Interest Rate Risk** = the underwater current (rate cycle) pulling the ship in unexpected directions.
- **Currency Risk** = changing wind direction when trading across seas (countries).
- **Commodity Risk** = floating icebergs (raw materials) that can damage the hull.

The captain's toolkit — **VaR, Duration, Stress Testing, Hedging** — are the navigation instruments, lifeboats, and storm-radar used to keep the ship (portfolio) safe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (Confidence Level):** Market risk measures are quoted with a **99% or 95% confidence level** over a **1-day or 10-day** horizon (per **Basel III/IV** norms adopted by Indian banks under RBI Master Direction 2023).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 MBS Securitization Process — Step-by-Step

1. **Origination:** Mortgagors obtain loans from banks/NBFCs (originators).
2. **Pooling:** Loans with similar characteristics (geography, LTV, FICO score) are aggregated.
3. **Transfer to SPV:** Originator sells the pool to a bankruptcy-remote **Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV)** — this is the "true sale" that protects investors from originator default.
4. **Tranching:** The SPV structures the cash flows into senior, mezzanine, and equity tranches with different risk-return profiles.
5. **Issuance:** Tranches are sold to investors via underwriting. Rating agencies (CRISIL, ICRA, Moody's, S&P) assign credit ratings.
6. **Servicing:** A servicer (often the originator) collects monthly payments and passes them to the trustee.
7. **Distribution:** The trustee distributes cash to tranche holders according to the **waterfall**.

### Why "Bankruptcy-Remote" SPV Matters
If the originator goes bankrupt, its creditors cannot claim the mortgage pool — because the SPV legally owns it. This is called **"true sale"** or **"legal isolation"** of the asset. Without this, MBS would not be safe for investors.

---

## 2.2 MBS Prepayment Models

Prepayment is the most critical behavioural risk in MBS. When interest rates fall, homeowners **refinance** their mortgages — paying off old, high-rate loans early. This shortens the duration of MBS cash flows unpredictably.

### Single Monthly Mortality (SMM)
SMM is the fraction of the remaining mortgage balance that prepays in a given month:

$$SMM = \frac{\text{Prepayment in Month } t}{\text{Beginning-of-Month Balance} - \text{Scheduled Principal Payment}}$$

### Conditional Prepayment Rate (CPR)
CPR is the annualized version of SMM:

$$CPR = 1 - (1 - SMM)^{12}$$

Rearranging, we obtain SMM in terms of CPR:

$$SMM = 1 - (1 - CPR)^{\frac{1}{12}}$$

### PSA Model (Public Securities Association)
The PSA model prescribes a benchmark prepayment path. **100% PSA** means:
- Months 1–30: CPR ramps linearly from 0.2% to 6% (0.2% + 0.2% × (t − 1) for month t).
- Month 30 onwards: CPR stays constant at **6%**.

Thus, **150% PSA** means multiply the CPR values of 100% PSA by 1.5; **50% PSA** means multiply by 0.5.

### Cash Flow for a Pass-Through MBS
In a pass-through, the investor receives both interest and principal. The monthly **pass-through rate** is typically the mortgage coupon minus a servicing fee (e.g., 7.5% coupon − 0.5% fee = 7.0% pass-through rate).

---

## 2.3 Market Risk Management: Tools and Techniques

### 2.3.1 Value at Risk (VaR)
**VaR** is the maximum loss a portfolio is expected to incur over a given horizon at a given confidence level, under normal market conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** VaR is a *quantile-based* measure. It is **NOT** the worst-case loss (that would be **Conditional VaR / Expected Shortfall**).

#### Three Methods to Compute VaR

**Method 1 — Variance-Covariance (Parametric) Method**
Assumes returns are normally distributed:

$$VaR_{\alpha} = Z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma \cdot V \cdot \sqrt{t}$$

where:
- $Z_{\alpha}$ = z-score at confidence level $\alpha$ (e.g., 2.33 for 99%, 1.65 for 95%)
- $\sigma$ = daily volatility (standard deviation of returns)
- $V$ = portfolio value
- $t$ = time horizon in days

**Method 2 — Historical Simulation Method**
Reorder the historical returns; the VaR is simply the (1 − $\alpha$) percentile of the empirical return distribution.

**Method 3 — Monte Carlo Simulation Method**
Generate thousands of random scenarios using a stochastic model; compute portfolio value under each; take the appropriate percentile.

### 2.3.2 Duration and Modified Duration
**Macaulay Duration (D)** is the weighted-average time until cash flows are received:

$$D = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} t \cdot \frac{CF_t}{(1+y)^t}}{P}$$

**Modified Duration (D\*)** measures the percentage price change for a 1% change in yield:

$$D^* = \frac{D}{1 + y}$$

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D^* \cdot \Delta y$$

### 2.3.3 Convexity
A second-order correction for the curvature in the price-yield relationship:

$$C = \frac{1}{P} \cdot \frac{d^2 P}{dy^2} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} t(t+1) \cdot \frac{CF_t}{(1+y)^{t+2}}}{P}$$

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D^* \cdot \Delta y + \frac{1}{2} \cdot C \cdot (\Delta y)^2$$

### 2.3.4 Stress Testing and Scenario Analysis
A complementary risk measure to VaR. Hypothetical scenarios are constructed (e.g., "what if rates rise 200 bps overnight?") and the portfolio's P&L is computed.

### 2.3.5 Hedging Instruments
- **Forwards / Futures:** Lock in a future price — eliminates price risk.
- **Options:** Buy puts for downside protection; calls for upside participation.
- **Swaps:** Exchange floating for fixed (interest rate swap) or one currency for another (FX swap).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All vertical bars below use `\vert` to maintain Markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|------------------------------|
| 1 | SMM from CPR | $SMM = 1 - (1 - CPR)^{1/12}$ | CPR is annualized; SMM is monthly fraction (0 ≤ SMM ≤ 1) |
| 2 | CPR from SMM | $CPR = 1 - (1 - SMM)^{12}$ | Range: 0 ≤ CPR ≤ 1 |
| 3 | 100% PSA CPR (month t) | $CPR_t = 0.002 \cdot t$ for $t \leq 30$; $CPR_t = 0.06$ for $t > 30$ | t = month number |
| 4 | Macaulay Duration | $D = \sum t \cdot w_t$, where $w_t = \frac{CF_t / (1+y)^t}{P}$ | Years |
| 5 | Modified Duration | $D^* = \frac{D}{1 + y}$ | Dimensionless (per 100% yield change) |
| 6 | Price Change (1st order) | $\frac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D^* \cdot \Delta y$ | $\Delta y$ in decimal (e.g., 0.01 for 1%) |
| 7 | Convexity | $C = \frac{1}{P} \sum t(t+1) \frac{CF_t}{(1+y)^{t+2}}$ | Years² |
| 8 | Price Change (with Convexity) | $\frac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D^* \Delta y + \frac{1}{2} C (\Delta y)^2$ | — |
| 9 | Parametric VaR | $VaR = Z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma \cdot V \cdot \sqrt{t}$ | V in currency; σ is daily; t in days |
| 10 | Z-score (99% / 95%) | $Z_{0.99} = 2.33$, $Z_{0.95} = 1.65$ | One-tailed |
| 11 | Forward Price | $F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{(r - q)T}$ | q = dividend yield / convenience yield |
| 12 | Bond Price | $P = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+y)^t} + \frac{FV}{(1+y)^n}$ | y = YTM per period |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Banking Sector (India):** Banks like HDFC, LIC Housing Finance use MBS issuance (PTC — Pass-Through Certificates in India) to offload home loans from their balance sheets and free up capital under RBI norms.
- **Central Banks:** The Fed and RBI hold MBS as part of quantitative easing/tightening operations.
- **Insurance & Pension Funds:** Use CMO senior tranches as long-duration, low-risk assets to match long-dated liabilities.
- **Risk Management:** Banks compute VaR daily for their trading book to comply with Basel III market-risk capital requirements (FRTB).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — PSA Prepayment Cash-Flow Calculation

**Problem:** A $1,000,000 pool of 30-year mortgages with 9.0% annual coupon (0.75% monthly rate) prepays at 150% PSA in months 1–12. Compute the month-1 and month-6 prepayments and the month-1 scheduled principal.

**Given:**
- Pool balance at origination $B_0 = 1{,}000{,}000$
- Mortgage rate $r_m = 0.75\%$ per month
- Original term $n = 360$ months
- PSA speed = 150%

### Step 1 — Compute Month-1 CPR
Under 100% PSA, month-1 CPR = 0.2% = 0.002. At 150% PSA:

$$CPR_1 = 1.50 \times 0.002 = 0.003 = 0.3\%$$

### Step 2 — Compute Month-1 SMM

$$SMM_1 = 1 - (1 - CPR_1)^{1/12} = 1 - (1 - 0.003)^{1/12}$$

$$= 1 - (0.997)^{0.0833}$$

Using the binomial approximation $(1 - x)^a \approx 1 - ax$ for small x:

$$\approx 1 - (1 - 0.003 \times 0.0833) = 1 - (1 - 0.00025) = 0.00025$$

So $SMM_1 \approx 0.025\%$.

### Step 3 — Compute Month-1 Scheduled Principal (SP)
First, the monthly payment on a 30-year, 9% mortgage:

$$PMT = \frac{B_0 \cdot r_m}{1 - (1 + r_m)^{-n}} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000 \times 0.0075}{1 - (1.0075)^{-360}}$$

$$= \frac{7{,}500}{1 - 0.06629} = \frac{7{,}500}{0.93371} \approx 8{,}032.50$$

Month-1 interest $= 1{,}000{,}000 \times 0.0075 = 7{,}500.00$

Month-1 scheduled principal:

$$SP_1 = PMT - \text{Interest} = 8{,}032.50 - 7{,}500.00 = 532.50$$

### Step 4 — Month-1 Total Principal (Scheduled + Prepayment)
Prepayment in month 1:

$$PP_1 = SMM_1 \times (B_0 - SP_1) = 0.00025 \times (1{,}000{,}000 - 532.50)$$

$$= 0.00025 \times 999{,}467.50 = 249.87$$

Total principal paid to MBS holders in month 1:

$$TP_1 = SP_1 + PP_1 = 532.50 + 249.87 = 782.37$$

### Step 5 — Month-6 Computation
Under 100% PSA, month-6 CPR = 0.2% × 6 = 1.2%. At 150% PSA:

$$CPR_6 = 1.50 \times 0.012 = 0.018 = 1.8\%$$

$$SMM_6 = 1 - (1 - 0.018)^{1/12} = 1 - (0.982)^{0.0833}$$

Using natural log: $\ln(0.982) = -0.01819$, so $(0.982)^{0.0833} = e^{-0.01819 \times 0.0833} = e^{-0.001515} = 0.998486$.

$$SMM_6 = 1 - 0.998486 = 0.001514 = 0.1514\%$$

Balance entering month 6 (after 5 months of amortization) ≈ $1{,}000{,}000 - 5 \times 532.50 = 997{,}337.50$ (approximation; we ignore interest-shortening for clarity).

$$PP_6 = 0.001514 \times 997{,}337.50 = 1{,}510.00$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Market Risk: Parametric VaR for an Equity Portfolio

**Problem:** A mutual fund has a portfolio worth **₹50 crore**. Daily volatility (standard deviation of log returns) is **1.2%**. Compute the 1-day 99% VaR.

**Given:**
- $V = 50{,}00{,}00{,}000$ (₹50 cr)
- $\sigma = 0.012$ (daily)
- $\alpha = 99\% \Rightarrow Z_{0.99} = 2.33$
- $t = 1$ day

### Step 1 — Apply the VaR Formula

$$VaR = Z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma \cdot V \cdot \sqrt{t}$$

$$VaR_{99\%} = 2.33 \times 0.012 \times 50{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times \sqrt{1}$$

$$= 2.33 \times 0.012 \times 50{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

Multiplying step by step:

$$2.33 \times 0.012 = 0.02796$$

$$0.02796 \times 50{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 13{,}98{,}000$$

$$\boxed{VaR_{99\%, 1\text{-day}} = ₹13.98 \text{ lakhs} \approx ₹1.40 \text{ crore}}$$

### Step 2 — Interpretation
There is a **1% probability** that the fund will lose more than **₹1.40 crore in a single trading day**, assuming returns are normally distributed.

### Step 3 — 10-Day VaR (Square-Root-of-Time Rule)

$$VaR_{10\text{-day}} = VaR_{1\text{-day}} \times \sqrt{10} = 13.98 \text{ lakhs} \times 3.162 = ₹44.20 \text{ lakhs}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation of Square-Root-of-Time Rule:** Assumes i.i.d. returns. Volatility clustering (GARCH effects) breaks this assumption in practice.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Interest Rate Risk: Duration-Based Hedging

**Problem:** A bond fund holds a portfolio with $D^* = 7.5$ and convexity $C = 60$. Yields are expected to rise by 50 bps. Estimate the percentage change in portfolio value.

### Step 1 — Apply Duration Approximation

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D^* \cdot \Delta y = -7.5 \times 0.005 = -0.0375 = -3.75\%$$

### Step 2 — Apply Convexity Correction

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D^* \cdot \Delta y + \frac{1}{2} \cdot C \cdot (\Delta y)^2$$

$$= -0.0375 + 0.5 \times 60 \times (0.005)^2$$

$$= -0.0375 + 0.5 \times 60 \times 0.000025$$

$$= -0.0375 + 0.00075$$

$$= -0.03675 = -3.675\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Convexity Helps (for positive C):** The convexity term is **positive**, so it offsets a portion of the duration loss. This is why high-convexity assets (e.g., long-dated MBS) are preferred in rising-yield environments.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tool Selection Cheat Sheet

| Risk Type | Best Tool | Mathematical Backbone | Limitation |
|-----------|-----------|------------------------|------------|
| Equity Risk | VaR (Parametric) | Normal distribution assumption | Fails in fat-tail / crash regimes |
| Interest Rate Risk | Duration + Convexity | Bond-price Taylor series | Static; ignores embedded options |
| Currency Risk | Forward/FX Swap | Covered Interest Parity | Credit risk of counterparty |
| Commodity Risk | Futures + Options | Cost-of-Carry model | Contango / backwardation distortion |
| Prepayment Risk (MBS) | PSA / CPR Models | Empirical S-curves | Behavioral, hard to forecast |
| Portfolio-wide Risk | VaR + Stress Test | Monte Carlo / Historical | Model risk; procyclical capital |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked "Why is VaR criticized?", always mention: (1) it underestimates tail risk, (2) it is not sub-additive (component VaR can exceed portfolio VaR), and (3) it assumes stationary distributions.
