# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MBS Securitization Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mortgagors / Home Buyers] --> B[Mortgage Originator - Bank or NBFC]
    B --> C[Pool of Mortgages - Segregated]
    C --> D[Special Purpose Vehicle - SPV]
    D --> E[True Sale - Legal Isolation Step]
    E --> F[Tranching - Senior Mezzanine Equity]
    F --> G[Tranche A - Senior Low Risk]
    F --> H[Tranche B - Mezzanine Medium Risk]
    F --> I[Tranche C - Equity High Risk]
    G --> J[Rating Agency - CRISIL Moodys SP]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Investors - Pension Insurance Banks]
    B --> L[Servicer - Collects EMI]
    L --> M[Trustee - Manages Waterfall]
    M --> K
    K --> N[Cash Flows - Monthly P and I]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The originator transfers the pool to the SPV, which issues tranched securities. The servicer (often the same originator) collects EMIs; the trustee enforces the payment waterfall to investors.

---

## 4.2 CMO Sequential-Pay Waterfall (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pool Cash Flow - Principal and Interest] --> A[Tranche A - Senior 200M]
    P --> B[Tranche B - Mezzanine 100M]
    P --> C[Tranche C - Junior 50M]
    A --> A1[Tranche A fully amortizes first]
    A1 --> B1[Tranche B begins amortizing]
    B1 --> C1[Tranche C receives principal last]
    C1 --> END[All tranches retired]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Sequential-Pay CMO Insight:** Tranche A has the **shortest** average life and **lowest** prepayment risk; Tranche C has the **longest** average life and acts as a "shock absorber" for prepayment volatility.

---

## 4.3 Market Risk Management Framework (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Identify Risk Exposures - Equity IR FX Commodity] --> ASSESS[Risk Measurement - VaR Stress Test]
    ASSESS --> LIMIT[Set Risk Limits - VaR Limit Stop Loss]
    LIMIT --> HEDGE[Hedging Strategy - Forwards Futures Options Swaps]
    HEDGE --> MONITOR[Continuous Monitoring - Backtest GARCH]
    MONITOR --> REPORT[Reporting to Risk Committee and RBI]
    REPORT --> IN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Closed-Loop Architecture:** Risk management is iterative. The output of monitoring feeds back into the identification stage, reflecting dynamic market conditions.

---

## 4.4 Prepayment Behaviour vs Interest Rates (Conceptual State Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Rates Stable] --> S2[Rates Fall 100 bps]
    S2 --> S3[Homeowners Refinance - Prepayment Surge]
    S3 --> S4[MBS Holder Loses Reinvestment Premium]
    S4 --> S5[SMM and CPR Spike]
    S5 --> S6[IO Tranches Lose Value]
    S5 --> S7[PO Tranches Gain Value]
    S1 --> T1[Rates Rise 100 bps]
    T1 --> T2[Refinancing Stops - Prepayment Slows]
    T2 --> T3[MBS Cash Flow Extends - Duration Rises]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** IO and PO strips move in *opposite* directions as rates change — a classic example of how a single cash-flow stream can be decomposed into two instruments with mirrored prepayment sensitivities.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define a Mortgage-Backed Security (MBS). Differentiate between a pass-through MBS and a Collateralized Mortgage Obligation (CMO).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** An **MBS** is an asset-backed security whose cash flows are derived from a pool of mortgage loans. Investors receive pro-rata shares of the principal and interest collected by the servicer.
- **(1 mark)** In a **pass-through MBS**, all investors receive cash flows *proportionally* and at the *same time*; there is no priority structure.
- **(1 mark)** In a **CMO**, the pool is divided into multiple *tranches* (classes) with a defined **payment priority** — senior tranches are paid first, providing protection to senior investors and subordination of risk.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**
**List the four main types of market risk and state one hedging instrument for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(0.5 mark each)**
  1. **Equity Risk** → Hedging: stock index *futures* or *put options*.
  2. **Interest Rate Risk** → Hedging: *interest rate swaps* or *bond futures*.
  3. **Currency (FX) Risk** → Hedging: *FX forwards* or *currency options*.
  4. **Commodity Risk** → Hedging: *commodity futures* or *commodity swaps*.

### Question A (14 Marks) — On Mortgage-Backed Securities

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** Explain the securitization process of mortgage loans with the help of a suitable block diagram. Discuss the role of the **Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV)** and the **servicer** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ₹10 crore pool of 20-year mortgages carries a 10% annual coupon. It prepays at **200% PSA**.
   (i) Compute the month-3 CPR and SMM. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) If the month-3 scheduled principal is ₹18,200, compute the total principal received by pass-through holders in month 3. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) Briefly explain the concept of **negative convexity** in MBS. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
- **(2 marks)** Describe the 7-step securitization process: Origination → Pooling → Transfer to SPV → Tranching → Issuance → Servicing → Distribution.
- **(2 marks)** Role of **SPV**: A bankruptcy-remote entity that purchases the mortgage pool from the originator. It issues the MBS and holds the assets in legal isolation. Without the SPV, MBS investors would be exposed to the originator's credit risk.
- **(2 marks)** Role of **Servicer**: Collects monthly EMIs from mortgagors, passes principal and interest to the trustee (after deducting a servicing fee, typically 25–50 bps), and handles defaults and foreclosures. Often the originator acts as servicer to preserve the borrower relationship.
- **(1 mark)** Conclude by stating that the **rating agency** assesses the credit quality of each tranche and that **tranching creates senior tranches with AAA/AA ratings from pools that are otherwise mid-investment-grade**.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Month-3 CPR and SMM (3 marks):**
Under 100% PSA, month-3 CPR = 0.2% × 3 = 0.6%. At 200% PSA:

$$CPR_3 = 2.00 \times 0.006 = 0.012 = 1.2\%$$

$$SMM_3 = 1 - (1 - CPR_3)^{1/12} = 1 - (1 - 0.012)^{1/12}$$

Using $\ln(1 - 0.012) = -0.01207$, then $(0.988)^{1/12} = e^{-0.01207/12} = e^{-0.001006} = 0.998995$.

$$SMM_3 = 1 - 0.998995 = 0.001005 \approx 0.1005\%$$

**(ii) Total Principal in Month 3 (2 marks):**
Balance entering month 3 ≈ ₹10,00,00,000 − 2 × 18,200 = ₹9,99,63,600.

$$PP_3 = SMM_3 \times (\text{Balance} - SP_3) = 0.001005 \times (9{,}99{,}63{,}600 - 18{,}200)$$

$$= 0.001005 \times 9{,}99{,}45{,}400 = ₹1{,}00{,}445.13$$

Total principal $= SP_3 + PP_3 = 18{,}200 + 1{,}00{,}445.13 = ₹1{,}18{,}645.13$

**(iii) Negative Convexity (2 marks):**
MBS exhibit **negative convexity** when interest rates fall: as rates decline, prepayment speeds accelerate, shortening the average life of the security. This means the price-yield curve is *concave* (curves downward) in the falling-rate region — the opposite of a straight (non-callable) bond's convexity. Consequence: MBS prices do not rise as much as a non-callable bond of equal duration when rates fall, and the holder is "called away" from favourable reinvestment opportunities.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — On Market Risk Management

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set A — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Define **Value at Risk (VaR)**. Explain the **Variance-Covariance (Parametric)** method and the **Historical Simulation** method with suitable formulas. List **two limitations** of VaR. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bank holds a ₹100 crore bond portfolio with modified duration 6.8 and convexity 55.
   (i) Estimate the percentage change in portfolio value if yields rise by 75 bps. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The bank wants to hedge using 10-year Government of India bonds with modified duration 7.2. Compute the **hedge ratio**. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) If the daily volatility of an equity position is 1.5% and the value is ₹25 crore, calculate the 1-day 99% VaR. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
- **(1.5 marks)** **Definition:** VaR is the maximum loss expected over a specified time horizon at a given confidence level, under normal market conditions. Formally, $P(L > VaR) = 1 - \alpha$.
- **(2 marks)** **Parametric Method:** Assumes returns are i.i.d. and normally distributed. $VaR = Z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma \cdot V \cdot \sqrt{t}$. Advantage: simple and fast. Disadvantage: underestimates tail risk.
- **(2 marks)** **Historical Simulation Method:** Uses actual past returns. Sort the historical returns; identify the percentile corresponding to $(1 - \alpha)$; multiply by portfolio value. Advantage: makes no distributional assumption. Disadvantage: assumes the past is representative of the future.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Limitations:**
  1. **Not sub-additive** — the VaR of a combined portfolio can exceed the sum of individual VaRs, falsely suggesting diversification fails.
  2. **Tail blindness** — VaR says nothing about the magnitude of losses *beyond* the VaR threshold.
  3. (Bonus) Procyclicality, model risk, sensitivity to estimation window.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Percentage Change in Portfolio Value (3 marks):**

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P} = -D^* \cdot \Delta y + \frac{1}{2} \cdot C \cdot (\Delta y)^2$$

$$= -6.8 \times 0.0075 + 0.5 \times 55 \times (0.0075)^2$$

$$= -0.0510 + 0.5 \times 55 \times 0.00005625$$

$$= -0.0510 + 0.001547 = -0.04945$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -4.945\%}$$

Absolute loss = 0.04945 × 100 crore = **₹4.945 crore**.

**(ii) Hedge Ratio (2 marks):**
Hedge ratio is the ratio of the duration of the position to be hedged to the duration of the hedging instrument:

$$HR = \frac{D^*_{\text{portfolio}}}{D^*_{\text{hedge}}} = \frac{6.8}{7.2} = 0.9444$$

To hedge ₹100 cr of portfolio, the bank should short-sell $0.9444 \times 100 = $ **₹94.44 crore** of the 10-year GoI bond.

**(iii) 1-Day 99% VaR (2 marks):**
$Z_{0.99} = 2.33$, $\sigma = 0.015$, $V = ₹25$ crore, $t = 1$.

$$VaR = 2.33 \times 0.015 \times 25 \times \sqrt{1} = 2.33 \times 0.375 = ₹0.874 \text{ crore}$$

$$\boxed{VaR_{99\%, 1\text{-day}} = ₹87.4 \text{ lakhs}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU PEPET635 Module 2 — High-Density Revision Checklist**

### Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS)
- **MBS = Pool of mortgages + SPV + Tranches + Pass-through cash flows.**
- **Three pillars of MBS structure:** (1) True sale to SPV (legal isolation), (2) Tranching (credit enhancement), (3) Servicing (cash-flow collection).
- **SMM is monthly; CPR is annualized.** $SMM = 1 - (1 - CPR)^{1/12}$.
- **100% PSA** = CPR ramps from 0.2% (month 1) to 6% (month 30), then 6% thereafter. **150% PSA = 1.5× the CPR path.**
- **Three MBS types:** Pass-through (pro-rata), CMO (tranched with waterfall), Stripped (IO and PO).
- **Negative convexity** is the defining feature of MBS — falling rates accelerate prepayments, capping price appreciation.
- **IO strips lose value when rates fall; PO strips gain value when rates fall.**
- **CMO sequential-pay waterfall** retires senior tranches first → shortest WAL for senior, longest for equity tranche.

### Market Risk
- **Four types:** Equity, Interest Rate, Currency, Commodity.
- **VaR is a *quantile* of the loss distribution**, not the worst-case loss.
- **Parametric VaR assumes normality:** $VaR = Z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma \cdot V \cdot \sqrt{t}$.
- **Historical Simulation VaR** is the $(1 - \alpha)$ percentile of the empirical return distribution.
- **Square-root-of-time rule** scales VaR across horizons: $VaR_T = VaR_1 \cdot \sqrt{T}$.
- **Duration** = first-order price sensitivity; **Convexity** = second-order curvature correction.
- **Negative convexity** = price-yield curve is concave (MBS, callable bonds).
- **Hedge ratio** = (Duration of exposure) / (Duration of hedge instrument).
- **Stress testing** complements VaR; it explores low-probability, high-impact scenarios.
- **Limitations of VaR:** Not sub-additive, tail-blind, procyclical, model-dependent.

### Key Z-Scores to Memorize
- **90%** → 1.28 | **95%** → 1.65 | **97.5%** → 1.96 | **99%** → 2.33 | **99.9%** → 3.09

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always re-read the question — "rate hike" means duration *loss*; "rate cut" means prepayment *acceleration* (negative convexity bites). Mixing these up is the #1 reason students lose marks on MBS questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Credit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET635 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Credit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Credit

In **Financial Engineering**, **Credit** is defined as the contractual arrangement in which a **borrower** receives present-day monetary value (principal) from a **lender**, with a binding promise to repay the principal along with an agreed cost of borrowing (**interest**) at a future date or over a series of scheduled installments. The terms of this exchange are codified in a **credit agreement**, which legally binds both counterparties to the stipulated repayment schedule.

In modern finance, credit extends beyond traditional bank lending to encompass **bonds, debentures, trade credit, credit derivatives**, and **interbank lending facilities**. The fundamental engineering of credit revolves around three pillars: **principal, maturity (tenor)**, and **credit premium (interest/spread)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Credit is a financial instrument representing the borrower's obligation to repay borrowed funds under predetermined terms, where the lender's expected return is contingent upon the borrower's **willingness** and **ability** to honor the debt obligation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **kirana (grocery) shop** in Kerala and ask the shopkeeper, *"Anna, can I take 1 kg of rice now and pay you ₹80 next Friday when I get my salary?"*

That arrangement — **goods now, money later** — is **credit in its purest form**.

Now extend this idea to a massive scale:
- A bank gives **₹10 lakh home loan** today to a software engineer → the engineer pays EMI for **20 years**.
- The Indian Government issues **₹1,000 crore bonds** to fund highway construction → investors get **coupon payments** every 6 months and the principal back at maturity.

In every case:
- Someone gives up **liquidity today**.
- In return, they get **future cash flows** + a **risk premium** (extra interest charged because repayment is uncertain).

**Credit Risk** is simply: *"Will the person/company who took money actually pay it back?"* If they don't pay, that's a **default**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Credit is fundamentally a function of **TIME + TRUST + PRICE**. The lender trusts the borrower's future ability to pay, charges a price (interest) for this trust, and waits through time for the cash flows to materialize.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Credit Engineering

The following standard metrics are universally applied in KTU-evaluated credit problems:

- **Loss Given Default (LGD)** — typically expressed as a **percentage (0–100%)**. Senior unsecured corporate bonds historically show LGD of approximately **40–60%**.
- **Probability of Default (PD)** — usually annual, ranging from **0.01% (AAA sovereign)** to **>20% (distressed issuers)**.
- **Exposure at Default (EAD)** — measured in **monetary units (₹, $, €)**.
- **Credit Spread** — quoted in **basis points (bps)**, where **1 bps = 0.01%**. Investment-grade spreads range **50–200 bps**; high-yield spreads exceed **400 bps**.
- **Recovery Rate (RR)** — typically **40% for senior unsecured, 70% for secured, 0% for equity**.

## 1.4 Distinction Between Credit and Other Forms of Financing

| Attribute | Credit (Debt) | Equity (Ownership) |
|---|---|---|
| **Claim Type** | Creditor (contractual) | Owner (residual) |
| **Cash Flow** | Fixed (coupon + principal) | Variable (dividends) |
| **Priority in Default** | Higher (paid first) | Lower (paid last) |
| **Voting Rights** | None | Yes |
| **Maturity** | Fixed | Perpetual |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Five C's of Credit — Framework for Credit Analysis

The **Five C's** form the qualitative backbone of credit assessment and are **direct KTU high-yield content**:

1. **Character** — Borrower's integrity, repayment history, willingness to pay. Evaluated through credit scores (CIBIL in India, FICO in USA).
2. **Capacity** — Borrower's ability to repay from operational cash flows. Measured via **Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR)**.
3. **Capital** — Borrower's net worth, equity cushion. Evaluated using **Debt-to-Equity (D/E) ratio**.
4. **Collateral** — Tangible assets pledged as security. Determines **recovery rate** in default.
5. **Conditions** — Macroeconomic environment, industry outlook, regulatory framework.

## 2.2 Quantitative Components of Credit Risk

Credit risk is decomposed into three stochastic variables that drive all pricing models:

### 2.2.1 Probability of Default (PD)
The likelihood that a borrower fails to make a required payment within a **one-year horizon** (or specified tenor). PD is **forward-looking** and estimated through:
- **Historical default databases** (Moody's Default Studies, S&P Global Ratings).
- **Structural models** (Merton Model — based on firm asset volatility).
- **Reduced-form models** (intensity-based, hazard rate approach).

### 2.2.2 Loss Given Default (LGD)
The proportion of exposure lost if default occurs. It is the complement of the recovery rate.

$$\text{LGD} = 1 - \text{Recovery Rate (RR)}$$

### 2.2.3 Exposure at Default (EAD)
The outstanding balance (principal + accrued interest) at the moment default occurs. For revolving facilities, **Credit Conversion Factors (CCF)** convert undrawn commitments into EAD.

## 2.3 Expected Loss (EL) — The Foundational Credit Equation

$$\boxed{\text{EL} = \text{PD} \times \text{LGD} \times \text{EAD}}$$

This is the **cornerstone formula** of credit risk management under **Basel II/III** regulatory frameworks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sudden Loss** = EAD − (Recovery Rate × EAD). If EAD = ₹100 crore and RR = 40%, Sudden Loss = ₹60 crore.

## 2.4 Credit Rating Framework

**Credit Rating Agencies (CRAs)** — Moody's, S&P Global, Fitch — assign letter grades reflecting the creditworthiness of issuers. KTU questions frequently test the **rating hierarchy**.

| Rating Tier | Moody's | S&P | Fitch | Indicative PD |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Investment Grade** | Aaa | AAA | AAA | ~0.01% |
| | Aa1, Aa2, Aa3 | AA+, AA, AA− | AA+, AA, AA− | ~0.05% |
| | A1, A2, A3 | A+, A, A− | A+, A, A− | ~0.10% |
| | Baa1, Baa2, Baa3 | BBB+, BBB, BBB− | BBB+, BBB, BBB− | ~0.30% |
| **Speculative (Junk)** | Ba1–Caa | BB+–CCC | BB+–CCC | 1%–>20% |
| **Default** | — | D | D | 100% |

## 2.5 Credit Spread and Yield Decomposition

The **credit spread** is the additional yield demanded by investors for bearing default risk above the **risk-free rate**. It is the single most observable market price of credit risk.

$$Y_{\text{corporate bond}} = R_f + \text{Credit Spread}$$

The credit spread compensates investors for **three risk components**:

$$\text{Credit Spread} = \text{Expected Loss} + \text{Risk Premium} + \text{Liquidity Premium}$$

In continuous compounding (rigorous KTU treatment):

$$Y = -\frac{1}{T}\ln\!\left(\frac{\text{PV of Cash Flows}}{\text{Face Value}}\right)$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every credit-related formula testable in KTU ESE. **Memorize these with units.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{EL} = \text{PD} \times \text{LGD} \times \text{EAD}$ | Expected Loss | ₹ / $ |
| 2 | $\text{LGD} = 1 - \text{RR}$ | Loss Given Default | Decimal (0–1) |
| 3 | $\text{DSCR} = \dfrac{\text{Net Operating Income}}{\text{Debt Service}}$ | Debt Service Coverage | Ratio |
| 4 | $\text{D/E} = \dfrac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{Total Equity}}$ | Debt-to-Equity | Ratio |
| 5 | $s = (1 - R) \cdot \lambda$ | Approximate Credit Spread (intensity model) | Decimal |
| 6 | $\text{Spread} \approx \text{PD} \times \text{LGD}$ | Simplified Spread Approximation | Decimal |
| 7 | $R_f + s = Y_c$ | Corporate Bond Yield Decomposition | Decimal |
| 8 | $V_D = \text{Max}(0, V_T - K)$ | Debt Payoff (Merton Model) | ₹ / $ |
| 9 | $\text{Equity} = \text{Max}(V_T - K, 0)$ | Equity Payoff (Call Option) | ₹ / $ |
| 10 | $\text{RAROC} = \dfrac{\text{Risk-Adjusted Return}}{\text{Capital}}$ | Risk-Adjusted Return on Capital | % |

> **Notation:** $\lambda$ = hazard rate (default intensity), $V_T$ = firm asset value at maturity $T$, $K$ = debt face value, $s$ = credit spread, $R_f$ = risk-free rate, $Y_c$ = corporate bond yield.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Credit engineering underpins:
- **Bank Loan Pricing** — corporate and retail lending decisions.
- **Bond Portfolio Management** — immunization against default cascades.
- **Basel III Capital Adequacy** — banks must hold capital = **EL + UL (Unexpected Loss)**.
- **Insurance Underwriting** — surety bonds, credit insurance.
- **Structured Finance** — Collateralized Debt Obligations (CDOs), Asset-Backed Securities (ABS).
- **Credit Derivatives** — Credit Default Swaps (CDS), Total Return Swaps.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Loss (EL) From First Principles

**Problem Setup:** A bank has extended a **₹5,00,00,000 (₹5 crore)** corporate term loan to M/s Kerala Steels Ltd. Internal rating models assign:
- Annual PD = **3%**
- Recovery Rate = **40%**
- Tenor = **5 years**

**Derive the Expected Loss (annual) and the cumulative 5-year EL.**

### Step 1 — Compute LGD

Since LGD is the complement of Recovery Rate:

$$\text{LGD} = 1 - \text{RR} = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60$$

This means **60% of the exposure is lost** if default occurs.

### Step 2 — Identify EAD

For a fully drawn term loan, EAD equals the outstanding balance:

$$\text{EAD} = ₹5,00,00,000$$

### Step 3 — Compute One-Year Expected Loss

Apply the foundational formula:

$$\text{EL}_{1\text{-yr}} = \text{PD} \times \text{LGD} \times \text{EAD}$$

Substituting values:

$$\text{EL}_{1\text{-yr}} = 0.03 \times 0.60 \times 5,00,00,000$$

$$\text{EL}_{1\text{-yr}} = 0.018 \times 5,00,00,000$$

$$\boxed{\text{EL}_{1\text{-yr}} = ₹9,00,000}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Cumulative 5-Year EL (assuming constant PD and no amortization)

Assuming a flat hazard rate, the cumulative default probability over $n$ years is:

$$\text{PD}_{n} = 1 - (1 - \text{PD})^n$$

For $n = 5$:

$$\text{PD}_{5} = 1 - (1 - 0.03)^5 = 1 - (0.97)^5$$

Compute $(0.97)^5$ step by step:

$$(0.97)^2 = 0.9409$$
$$(0.97)^3 = 0.9409 \times 0.97 = 0.912673$$
$$(0.97)^4 = 0.912673 \times 0.97 = 0.885293$$
$$(0.97)^5 = 0.885293 \times 0.97 = 0.858734$$

Therefore:

$$\text{PD}_{5} = 1 - 0.858734 = 0.141266$$

### Step 5 — Compute Cumulative Expected Loss

$$\text{EL}_{5\text{-yr}} = \text{PD}_{5} \times \text{LGD} \times \text{EAD}$$

$$\text{EL}_{5\text{-yr}} = 0.141266 \times 0.60 \times 5,00,00,000$$

$$\boxed{\text{EL}_{5\text{-yr}} = ₹42,37,980}$$

**Interpretation:** Over 5 years, the bank **statistically expects to lose ₹42.38 lakh** on this loan, which must be priced into the interest rate charged.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Merton Structural Model for Credit Risk

**Theoretical Foundation:** Robert Merton's 1974 model treats **equity as a call option** on the firm's underlying assets, with **debt face value $K$** as the strike price and **debt maturity $T$** as the time to expiry.

### Step 1 — Establish the Terminal Payoffs

At maturity $T$, the firm's asset value is $V_T$. The debt holders receive the **minimum of $V_T$ and the face value $K$**:

$$D_T = \text{Min}(V_T, K)$$

This can be decomposed as risk-free bond minus a put option:

$$D_T = K - \text{Max}(K - V_T, 0)$$

### Step 2 — Define Equity as a Call Option

Equity holders receive the residual after debt repayment:

$$E_T = \text{Max}(V_T - K, 0)$$

This is mathematically identical to a **European call option** with:
- Underlying = Firm Assets ($V_T$)
- Strike = Debt Face Value ($K$)
- Expiry = Debt Maturity ($T$)

### Step 3 — Apply Black-Scholes-Merton Formula

$$\boxed{E_0 = V_0 \, N(d_1) - K e^{-rT} N(d_2)}$$

Where:

$$d_1 = \frac{\ln(V_0/K) + (r + \sigma^2/2)T}{\sigma\sqrt{T}}$$

$$d_2 = d_1 - \sigma\sqrt{T}$$

### Step 4 — Extract Risk-Neutral Default Probability

Default occurs when $V_T < K$. Under risk-neutral measure $\mathbb{Q}$:

$$\text{PD}^{\mathbb{Q}} = N(-d_2)$$

The **credit spread** is then approximated as:

$$\boxed{s \approx \lambda \cdot \text{LGD} \approx \text{PD}^{\mathbb{Q}} \cdot \text{LGD}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Credit Spread Approximation from Bond Prices

**Problem:** A **5-year corporate bond** with face value ₹1,000 and **5% annual coupon** trades at **₹945**, while the equivalent **risk-free G-Sec yields 4.0%**. Compute the credit spread.

### Step 1 — Compute the Corporate Bond Yield $Y_c$

The bond's present value equation:

$$945 = \sum_{t=1}^{5} \frac{50}{(1+Y_c)^t} + \frac{1000}{(1+Y_c)^5}$$

### Step 2 — Solve Numerically (Iteration)

Testing $Y_c = 6\%$:

$$\text{PV} = \frac{50}{1.06} + \frac{50}{1.06^2} + \frac{50}{1.06^3} + \frac{50}{1.06^4} + \frac{1050}{1.06^5}$$

Compute each term:

$$\frac{50}{1.06} = 47.17$$
$$\frac{50}{1.06^2} = 44.50$$
$$\frac{50}{1.06^3} = 41.98$$
$$\frac{50}{1.06^4} = 39.60$$
$$\frac{1050}{1.06^5} = 786.18$$

Sum: $47.17 + 44.50 + 41.98 + 39.60 + 786.18 = 959.43$

Since ₹959.43 > ₹945, the yield must be slightly higher. Testing $Y_c = 6.5\%$:

$$\frac{50}{1.065} = 46.95$$
$$\frac{50}{1.065^2} = 44.08$$
$$\frac{50}{1.065^3} = 41.39$$
$$\frac{50}{1.065^4} = 38.86$$
$$\frac{1050}{1.065^5} = 760.42$$

Sum: $46.95 + 44.08 + 41.39 + 38.86 + 760.42 = 931.70$

Interpolating linearly:

$$Y_c \approx 6.0\% + \frac{959.43 - 945}{959.43 - 931.70} \times 0.5\%$$

$$Y_c \approx 6.0\% + \frac{14.43}{27.73} \times 0.5\% \approx 6.0\% + 0.26\%$$

$$Y_c \approx 6.26\%$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Credit Spread

$$\boxed{s = Y_c - R_f = 6.26\% - 4.00\% = 2.26\%}$$

**Interpretation:** Investors demand an extra **226 basis points** to bear the default risk of this corporate issuer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Credit Risk Analysis — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end credit risk engineering workflow** as it is applied in commercial banks and rating agencies. It captures data inputs, quantitative engines, and final outputs that determine loan pricing and capital allocation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Borrower Data Input] --> B[Five Cs of Credit Assessment]
    B --> C[Character Evaluation]
    B --> D[Capacity Measurement DSCR]
    B --> E[Capital Adequacy D E Ratio]
    B --> F[Collateral Valuation]
    B --> G[Macro Industry Conditions]
    C --> H[Credit Scoring Engine]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Probability of Default PD Model]
    I --> J[Structural Merton Model]
    I --> K[Reduced Form Intensity Model]
    J --> L[PD Estimate]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Loss Given Default LGD Module]
    M --> N[Recovery Rate Database]
    N --> O[LGD Estimate]
    O --> P[Exposure at Default EAD Calculator]
    P --> Q[Credit Conversion Factor CCF]
    Q --> R[Expected Loss Engine]
    R --> S[EL equals PD times LGD times EAD]
    S --> T[Unexpected Loss via VaR]
    T --> U[Basel III Capital Charge]
    S --> V[Loan Pricing Spread]
    U --> W[Final Loan Decision]
    V --> W
    W --> X[Approve Reject Price]
```

## 4.2 Credit Rating Hierarchy and Default Mapping

The following diagram depicts the **complete rating hierarchy** from AAA through Default, illustrating the **monotonic increase in default probability** as credit quality deteriorates.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AAA[AAA Aaa] --> AA[AA Aa]
    AA --> A[A]
    A --> BBB[BBB Baa]
    BBB --> JUMP[Speculative Grade]
    JUMP --> BB[BB Ba]
    BB --> B[B]
    B --> CCC[CCC Caa]
    CCC --> D[Default D]
    AAA -.-> PD1[PD approx 0.01 percent]
    AA -.-> PD2[PD approx 0.05 percent]
    A -.-> PD3[PD approx 0.10 percent]
    BBB -.-> PD4[PD approx 0.30 percent]
    BB -.-> PD5[PD approx 1.5 percent]
    B -.-> PD6[PD approx 6 percent]
    CCC -.-> PD7[PD above 20 percent]
    D -.-> PD8[PD equals 100 percent]
```

## 4.3 Credit Derivative — CDS Payoff Structure

A **Credit Default Swap (CDS)** is the most important credit derivative. The diagram below illustrates the cash flow mechanics between the **protection buyer** and **protection seller**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Protection Buyer] -->|Premium Leg Quarterly| P2[Protection Seller]
    P2 -->|Default Trigger Credit Event| P1
    P1 -.->|Physical Settlement| P3[Deliver Bond Face Value]
    P1 -.->|Cash Settlement| P4[Receive 1 minus RR times Notional]
    P2 -->|Until Maturity or Default| P5[Continuous Premium Stream]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "Credit" in the context of financial engineering. Distinguish between consumer credit and commercial credit.**

**Model Answer:**

**Credit** is a contractual financial arrangement in which a lender provides present monetary value to a borrower, who in turn promises to repay the principal along with a stipulated interest cost at a future date. It represents the **time-value exchange** between liquidity providers and liquidity seekers, governed by a legally binding credit agreement that specifies tenor, coupon, collateral, and default remedies.

**Consumer Credit** is credit extended to **individual households** for personal consumption (e.g., home loans, auto loans, credit cards). It is characterized by smaller ticket sizes, shorter tenors, and behavioral scoring models.

**Commercial Credit** is extended to **businesses, corporates, and institutions** for working capital, capex, or trade financing. It features larger exposures, longer maturities, balance-sheet based underwriting, and ratings-driven pricing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write generic definitions without distinguishing the **legal obligation** element of credit. Always mention the **contractual nature** and **future repayment obligation** explicitly.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**List and briefly explain the "Five C's of Credit" used by banks for evaluating loan proposals.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Five C's of Credit** are the qualitative framework used by lenders to assess borrower creditworthiness:

1. **Character** — The borrower's integrity and willingness to repay. Measured by past repayment track record and credit bureau scores (CIBIL, Experian).

2. **Capacity** — The borrower's ability to service debt from operating cash flows. Quantified using the **Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR)**.

3. **Capital** — The borrower's net worth and equity contribution. Evaluated through **Debt-to-Equity (D/E)** ratio.

4. **Collateral** — Specific assets pledged as security for the loan. Determines the **recovery rate** in case of default.

5. **Conditions** — The macroeconomic environment, industry cyclicality, and regulatory framework influencing repayment capacity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **Capacity** with **Capital**. Remember: Capacity = ability to pay (cash flow); Capital = net worth (balance sheet equity).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**A bank extends a ₹10 crore corporate term loan with the following parameters:**
- **Annual PD = 4%**
- **Recovery Rate = 35%**
- **Tenor = 3 years**
- **Risk-Free Rate = 6%**
- **Loan Rate Charged = 9.5%**

**(a)** Compute the **Expected Loss (EL)** for the **one-year horizon** and the **cumulative 3-year horizon**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Decompose the **loan interest rate (9.5%)** into its components (risk-free rate, expected loss margin, operating cost margin, profit margin). Comment on whether the loan is economically viable. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute LGD**

$$\text{LGD} = 1 - \text{RR} = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65$$

**Step 2: Identify EAD**

$$\text{EAD} = ₹10,00,00,000 \text{ (₹10 crore)}$$

**Step 3: One-Year Expected Loss**

$$\text{EL}_{1\text{-yr}} = \text{PD} \times \text{LGD} \times \text{EAD}$$

$$\text{EL}_{1\text{-yr}} = 0.04 \times 0.65 \times 10,00,00,000$$

$$\boxed{\text{EL}_{1\text{-yr}} = ₹26,00,000}$$

**Step 4: Cumulative 3-Year Default Probability**

$$\text{PD}_{3} = 1 - (1 - 0.04)^3 = 1 - (0.96)^3$$

Computing $(0.96)^3$:

$$(0.96)^2 = 0.9216$$
$$(0.96)^3 = 0.9216 \times 0.96 = 0.8847$$

Therefore:

$$\text{PD}_{3} = 1 - 0.8847 = 0.1153$$

**Step 5: Cumulative 3-Year Expected Loss**

$$\text{EL}_{3\text{-yr}} = 0.1153 \times 0.65 \times 10,00,00,000$$

$$\boxed{\text{EL}_{3\text{-yr}} = ₹74,94,500 \approx ₹74.95 \text{ lakh}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Identify Risk-Free Component**

$$R_f = 6.00\%$$

**Step 2: Compute Expected Loss Margin (as % of EAD)**

$$\text{EL Margin (1-yr)} = \frac{26,00,000}{10,00,00,000} = 0.026 = 2.60\%$$

**Step 3: Determine Remaining Spread for Operating Costs + Profit**

$$\text{Total Spread} = 9.5\% - 6.0\% = 3.5\%$$

$$\text{Remaining} = 3.5\% - 2.6\% = 0.9\%$$

**Step 4: Decomposition Table**

| Component | Rate |
|---|---|
| Risk-Free Rate | 6.00% |
| Expected Loss Margin | 2.60% |
| Operating Cost + Profit Margin | 0.90% |
| **Total Loan Rate** | **9.50%** |

**Step 5: Economic Viability Comment**

The 0.9% residual margin is **thin** for a ₹10 crore exposure. Typical bank operating cost ratios (cost-to-income) require at least **1.5–2.0%** net margin. Therefore, the loan is **marginally viable**; the bank should either:
- Negotiate a higher loan rate (≥ 10.0%), OR
- Demand collateral to boost recovery rate from 35% to ≥ 50%, OR
- Reduce exposure through syndication.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Merton Structural Model** of credit risk. Derive the equity-as-call-option analogy and compute the **risk-neutral probability of default** for a firm with $V_0 = ₹1200$, $K = ₹1000$, $r = 5\%$, $\sigma = 30\%$, $T = 2$ years. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Credit Default Swap (CDS)**. A bank buys 5-year CDS protection on a ₹50 crore notional bond with annual spread of **180 bps**. Compute the annual premium and explain the payoff structure on credit event trigger. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Theoretical Framework**

In the Merton model (1974), the firm's capital structure consists only of **equity** and a **single zero-coupon debt** maturing at $T$ with face value $K$. At maturity $T$:

- If asset value $V_T \geq K$: debt holders receive $K$, equity holders receive $V_T - K$.
- If $V_T < K$: default occurs; equity holders receive zero, debt holders receive $V_T$.

**Step 2: Equity Payoff as a Call Option**

$$E_T = \text{Max}(V_T - K, 0)$$

This is **mathematically identical to a European call option** on firm assets with strike $K$ and expiry $T$.

**Step 3: Apply Black-Scholes-Merton Formula**

$$E_0 = V_0 N(d_1) - K e^{-rT} N(d_2)$$

Where:

$$d_1 = \frac{\ln(V_0/K) + (r + \sigma^2/2)T}{\sigma\sqrt{T}}$$

$$d_2 = d_1 - \sigma\sqrt{T}$$

**Step 4: Compute $d_1$**

$$d_1 = \frac{\ln(1200/1000) + (0.05 + 0.30^2/2) \times 2}{0.30 \times \sqrt{2}}$$

$$\ln(1.2) = 0.1823$$
$$0.05 + 0.045 = 0.095$$
$$0.095 \times 2 = 0.190$$
$$0.1823 + 0.190 = 0.3723$$
$$0.30 \times 1.4142 = 0.4243$$

$$d_1 = \frac{0.3723}{0.4243} = 0.8775$$

**Step 5: Compute $d_2$**

$$d_2 = 0.8775 - 0.4243 = 0.4532$$

**Step 6: Look Up $N(-d_2)$**

$$-d_2 = -0.4532$$
$$N(-0.4532) = 1 - N(0.4532) = 1 - 0.6748 = 0.3252$$

$$\boxed{\text{PD}^{\mathbb{Q}} \approx 32.52\%}$$

**Interpretation:** Under risk-neutral measure, there is a **32.52% probability** that the firm's assets will fall below the debt face value at maturity.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Define CDS**

A **Credit Default Swap (CDS)** is an over-the-counter (OTC) bilateral derivative contract in which the **protection buyer** pays a periodic premium (quoted in bps) to the **protection seller** in exchange for a contingent payoff upon occurrence of a **credit event** (bankruptcy, failure to pay, restructuring) in the reference entity.

**Step 2: Compute Annual Premium**

$$\text{Annual Premium} = \text{Notional} \times \text{Spread}$$

$$\text{Annual Premium} = ₹50,00,00,000 \times 0.0180$$

$$\boxed{\text{Annual Premium} = ₹90,00,000 \text{ (₹90 lakh per year)}}$$

If paid quarterly: ₹22.5 lakh every three months.

**Step 3: Payoff on Credit Event Trigger**

If a credit event occurs during the contract life, the protection seller compensates the buyer through either:

- **Physical Settlement:** Buyer delivers the defaulted bond (face value ₹50 crore) and receives par value from seller.

- **Cash Settlement:** Buyer receives the loss amount:

$$\text{Payoff} = (1 - \text{Recovery Rate}) \times \text{Notional}$$

If recovery = 40%:

$$\text{Payoff} = 0.60 \times ₹50 \text{ crore} = ₹30 \text{ crore}$$

After the credit event, the **premium leg ceases** — the protection buyer stops paying further installments.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Credit**

- **Credit** = present value given now, future repayment with interest, under contractual obligation.
- The **Five C's** (Character, Capacity, Capital, Collateral, Conditions) are the qualitative underwriting pillars.
- The **Holy Trinity** of Credit Risk = **PD × LGD × EAD = EL**.
- **LGD = 1 − Recovery Rate**; senior secured RR ≈ 70%, senior unsecured RR ≈ 40%, subordinated RR ≈ 20%.
- **Cumulative PD** for $n$ years (constant hazard): $\text{PD}_n = 1 - (1-\text{PD})^n$.
- **Credit Spread** = $Y_c - R_f$, quoted in **basis points (bps)**.
- **Merton Model** treats equity as a **call option** on firm assets; default probability $= N(-d_2)$.
- **CDS** is the primary credit derivative; premium leg vs protection leg are the two cash flow streams.
- **Basel III** requires banks to hold capital = EL + Unexpected Loss (UL), where UL is computed via VaR at 99.9% confidence.
- **Investment Grade** = BBB−/Baa3 and above; below that = **Speculative Grade / Junk**.
- **Recovery Rate** is **collateral-dependent** — secured debt recovers more than unsecured.
- **Credit Conversion Factor (CCF)** converts off-balance-sheet exposures (undrawn lines) into on-balance-sheet EAD.
- **RAROC** (Risk-Adjusted Return on Capital) is the bank profitability metric for credit decisions.
- **Always** specify the **horizon** (1-year vs cumulative) when discussing PD and EL in KTU answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operational and Liquidity Risks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET635 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operational and Liquidity Risks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — OPERATIONAL AND LIQUIDITY RISKS

## 1.1 Operational Risk — Core Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Basel Committee on Banking Supervision, BCBS 2006):**
> *"Operational risk is the risk of loss resulting from inadequate or failed internal processes, people and systems, or from external events. This definition includes legal risk, but excludes strategic and reputational risk."*

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, **Operational Risk (OpRisk)** is classified as one of the three pillars of the Pillar 1 Minimum Capital Requirements under the **Basel III Accord**, alongside **Credit Risk** and **Market Risk**. The defining feature of OpRisk is that it is the residual risk that remains *after* market and credit risks have been neutralized — it is the "risk of doing business."

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model

Imagine a five-star restaurant. The chef (credit risk) ensures ingredients are top-grade. The market vendor (market risk) ensures prices don't fluctuate wildly. But the kitchen itself can still burn down (physical asset damage), the chef can fall sick (people risk), the billing system can crash (systems risk), or a customer can sue over food poisoning (legal/external event). **Operational Risk is everything that can go wrong inside the "kitchen" of the bank, even if the ingredients (assets) and market (economy) are perfectly fine.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants / Standards:**
> - **α (BIA Capital Factor) = 15%** (Basel prescribed gross income multiplier)
> - **OpRisk Capital Charge** must be ≥ 0 in all calculation approaches
> - **Holding period for OpRisk VaR** = 1 year (unlike 1-day/10-day for market risk)
> - **Confidence interval for AMA** = 99.9% (per Basel norms)

---

## 1.2 Liquidity Risk — Core Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (BCBS, Basel III: International Framework for Liquidity Risk Measurement, Standards and Monitoring, Jan 2013):**
> *"Liquidity risk is the risk that a financial institution cannot meet its financial obligations as they fall due, without incurring unacceptable losses. It arises from mismatches in the timing and value of cash inflows and outflows."*

Two major sub-types are recognized in the KTU syllabus:

1. **Funding Liquidity Risk** — The inability to raise new funds to meet liabilities (e.g., a bank facing a bank run).
2. **Market Liquidity Risk** — The inability to sell an asset quickly at a fair market price without a large price concession (e.g., during the 2008 crisis, securitized products became un-sellable).

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Tank" Model

A household has a water tank (assets) and multiple taps (liabilities). **Liquidity Risk** occurs when the water level in the tank is too low, the pipes are too narrow, or the tank is filled with ice cubes (illiquid assets) rather than liquid water. Even if the household *owns* the ice, it cannot drink from it instantly. The key metrics **LCR** and **NSFR** measure, respectively, whether the tank can withstand a 30-day drought and whether it has the right mix of water and ice for long-term survival.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Thresholds under Basel III:**
> - **LCR ≥ 100%** (HQLA / Net Cash Outflows over 30 days)
> - **NSFR ≥ 100%** (Available Stable Funding / Required Stable Funding)
> - **HQLA = Level 1 + Level 2A + Level 2B** (with regulatory haircuts)
> - **L2A haircut = 15%**, **L2B haircut = 25%–50%** (asset-specific)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Risk — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 The Seven Basel Event-Type Categories (Mandatory for KTU)

| # | Event Type (UE Code) | Description | Real-world Example |
|---|----------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Internal Fraud** | Intentional misappropriation, insider trading | Rogue trader (e.g., Barings, 1995) |
| 2 | **External Fraud** | Theft, forgery, hacking, vendor scams | Phishing attack on SWIFT |
| 3 | **Employment Practices** | Discrimination, compensation, HR disputes | Mass wrongful-termination suit |
| 4 | **Clients, Products & Business Practices** | Suitability, disclosure failures, market manipulation | Mis-selling of derivatives |
| 5 | **Business Disruption & System Failures** | Hardware/software failures, utility outages | Server crash during trading hours |
| 6 | **Damage to Physical Assets** | Natural disasters, terrorism, vandalism | Earthquake damaging HQ |
| 7 | **Execution, Delivery & Process Management** | Data entry errors, model errors, failed settlements | Fat-finger trade error |

### 2.1.2 The Three Capital Measurement Approaches

1. **Basic Indicator Approach (BIA)** — Simplest; all banks use 15% of average positive annual gross income.
2. **Standardized (TSA)** Approach — Banks map activities to 8 business lines; each line has its own β coefficient.
3. **Advanced Measurement Approach (AMA)** — Internal models using LDA, Scorecards, scenario analysis.

### 2.1.3 Loss Distribution Approach (LDA) — Internal Model

LDA assumes **operational losses follow a separate frequency distribution and a separate severity distribution**:

- **Frequency** $N$ ~ **Poisson** distribution
- **Severity** $X$ ~ **Lognormal** or **Pareto** distribution
- **Aggregate Annual Loss** $S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i$ — fitted using Monte Carlo simulation to derive the 99.9% VaR.

---

## 2.2 Liquidity Risk — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 The Two Pillars of Basel III Liquidity Framework

- **Pillar 1 — Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR):** Short-term (30-day) survival under a stress scenario.
- **Pillar 2 — Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR):** Long-term (1-year) structural balance sheet resilience.

### 2.2.2 Intraday Liquidity Risk

A specialized but increasingly tested concept: the ability to settle payment obligations on a real-time basis during the business day. **Intraday Liquidity Risk** gained prominence after Herstatt Risk (1974) and is monitored via:
- Real-time gross settlement (RTGS) queue lengths
- Maximum intraday liquidity usage caps

### 2.2.3 Liquidity-Adjusted Value at Risk (L-VaR)

Standard VaR assumes an asset can be sold at the mid-price in 1–10 days. **L-VaR** explicitly prices in liquidation costs:

$$L\text{-}VaR_{\alpha} = VaR_{\alpha} + \text{Liquidity Cost}$$

The liquidity cost is typically modelled as **half the bid-ask spread** times the position size.

---

## 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-critical equation bank** for Module 2. Memorize the boundary conditions, not just the formulas.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | **BIA Capital Charge** | $K_{BIA} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{3} GI_i \times \alpha}{3}$ | $\alpha = 15\%$, GI = positive annual gross income (last 3 years) |
| 2 | **TSA Capital Charge** | $K_{TSA} = \sum_{j=1}^{8} \beta_j \times GI_j$ | $\beta_j$ is business-line specific (4%–18%) |
| 3 | **LDA Aggregate Loss** | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i$ where $N \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)$, $X \sim \text{Lognormal}(\mu, \sigma)$ | Capital = $VaR_{99.9\%}(S)$ |
| 4 | **Liquidity Coverage Ratio** | $LCR = \dfrac{HQLA}{\text{Net Cash Outflows}_{30d}} \geq 100\%$ | Regulator: RBI / Fed / EBA |
| 5 | **Net Stable Funding Ratio** | $NSFR = \dfrac{ASF}{RSF} \geq 100\%$ | ASF = Available Stable Funding, RSF = Required Stable Funding |
| 6 | **HQLA** | $HQLA = L_1 + L_{2A} + L_{2B}$ | $L_1$ = unencumbered cash, gov securities |
| 7 | **HQLA with Haircuts** | $HQLA_{adj} = L_1 + 0.85 \cdot L_{2A} + 0.50 \text{–}0.75 \cdot L_{2B}$ | $L_{2A} \leq 40\%$ of HQLA; $L_{2B} \leq 15\%$ |
| 8 | **Bid-Ask Spread** | $s = Ask - Bid$ | Liquidity premium proxy |
| 9 | **Amihud Illiquidity Ratio** | $ILLIQ = \dfrac{\vert R_t \vert}{VOL_t}$ | Higher = more illiquid |
| 10 | **Liquidity-Adjusted VaR** | $L\text{-}VaR_{\alpha} = VaR_{\alpha} + \dfrac{s}{2} \cdot Q$ | $Q$ = position size |
| 11 | **Liquidity Gap** | $Gap_t = \text{Inflows}_t - \text{Outflows}_t$ | Negative gap = cumulative deficit |
| 12 | **Survival Horizon** | $H = \dfrac{\text{Liquid Assets}}{\text{Net Daily Outflows}}$ | Days of stress endurance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real banking practice, these formulas feed directly into the *Asset-Liability Management (ALM) desk*, the *Treasury function*, and *Regulatory Reporting*. For quant interviews, BIA and LCR are the most-tested.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS, NUMERICAL ANALYSIS & SOLVED PROBLEMS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Strategy Note:** For KTU ESE 14-mark questions, you must show **all intermediate steps**. Partial marks are awarded for every correct sub-step. Skip no step.

---

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Basic Indicator Approach (BIA) Capital Calculation

**Question (KTU pattern):** A bank reported the following Gross Incomes for the last three financial years:
- Year 1: ₹ 1,200 crore (positive)
- Year 2: ₹ (–) 400 crore (loss — negative)
- Year 3: ₹ 1,500 crore (positive)

Compute the Operational Risk Capital Charge under the BIA method, given $\alpha = 15\%$.

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Apply the positive-income filter.**

Per Basel rules, only **positive** gross income is considered. A negative year is **excluded** from both numerator and denominator.

$$
GI_{1} = +1200 \text{ cr}, \quad GI_{2} = \text{ignored} \left(\text{negative}\right), \quad GI_{3} = +1500 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 2: Sum of positive gross incomes over the last 3 years.**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{3} GI_i = 1200 + 1500 = 2700 \text{ cr}
$$

(The negative year contributes 0 to the sum.)

**Step 3: Apply the prescribed 15% multiplier.**

$$
\alpha \cdot \sum GI_i = 0.15 \times 2700 = 405 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 4: Divide by the fixed denominator 3 (averaging window).**

$$
K_{BIA} = \dfrac{405}{3} = 135 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 5: State the answer with units and regulatory context.**

$$
\boxed{K_{BIA} = \text{₹ } 135 \text{ crore}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why divide by 3?** The denominator 3 normalizes the 15% multiplier over a 3-year window, yielding an *average* annual capital charge. This smoothing prevents capital from fluctuating wildly with a single good or bad year.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) Computation

**Question:** A bank holds the following HQLA-eligible assets and faces the following 30-day cash flows:

**Level 1 Assets:**
- Cash: ₹ 500 cr
- Government securities (zero risk-weight): ₹ 1,500 cr

**Level 2A Assets:**
- Government-sponsored agency bonds (AA– rated): ₹ 400 cr

**Level 2B Assets:**
- Listed equities (in major index): ₹ 300 cr

**30-Day Net Cash Outflows (stress scenario):** ₹ 2,800 cr

Compute the **LCR** and comment on regulatory compliance.

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Compute unadjusted HQLA by tier.**

$$
L_1 = 500 + 1500 = 2000 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
L_{2A} = 400 \text{ cr}, \quad L_{2B} = 300 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 2: Apply regulatory haircuts.**

- $L_1$ → haircut = 0% (full value)
- $L_{2A}$ → haircut = 15% (retain 85%)

$$
L_{2A}^{adj} = 0.85 \times 400 = 340 \text{ cr}
$$

- $L_{2B}$ → haircut = 50% (retain 50%)

$$
L_{2B}^{adj} = 0.50 \times 300 = 150 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 3: Apply cap constraints (Basel III).**

- **Cap 1:** $L_{2A} + L_{2B} \leq 40\% \text{ of HQLA}$

$$
0.40 \times HQLA = 0.40 \times (L_1 + L_{2A}^{adj} + L_{2B}^{adj}) \text{ (post-cap)}
$$

Pre-cap HQLA = $2000 + 340 + 150 = 2490$ cr.

- **Cap 2:** $L_{2B} \leq 15\% \text{ of HQLA}$

$$
0.15 \times 2490 = 373.5 \text{ cr} \quad (\text{we have } 150 \text{ cr, so OK})
$$

$$
\boxed{HQLA_{adj} = 2490 \text{ crore}}
$$

**Step 4: Compute LCR.**

$$
LCR = \dfrac{HQLA_{adj}}{\text{Net Cash Outflows}} = \dfrac{2490}{2800} = 0.8893 = 88.93\%
$$

**Step 5: Regulatory verdict.**

Since $LCR = 88.93\% < 100\%$, the bank **fails the LCR test**. It is short of:

$$
\text{Shortfall} = 2800 - 2490 = 310 \text{ crore of HQLA}
$$

$$
\boxed{LCR = 88.93\% \quad (\text{Non-compliant; needs additional HQLA of } \text{₹ } 310 \text{ cr})}
$$

---

## 3.3 Problem 3 — Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR)

**Question:** A bank's Available Stable Funding (ASF) totals ₹ 8,000 cr, and its Required Stable Funding (RSF) totals ₹ 9,200 cr. Compute NSFR and interpret.

### Solution

**Step 1: Apply the formula directly.**

$$
NSFR = \dfrac{ASF}{RSF} = \dfrac{8000}{9200} = 0.8696 = 86.96\%
$$

**Step 2: Verdict.**

Since $NSFR < 100\%$, the bank has a **structural funding shortfall** and is non-compliant. It must either:

- Increase ASF (raise more long-term, stable liabilities like 1-year+ deposits or equity), OR
- Reduce RSF (replace illiquid long-term assets with less-RSF liquid ones).

$$
\boxed{NSFR = 86.96\% \quad (\text{Non-compliant; shortfall of } \text{₹ } 1200 \text{ cr})}
$$

---

## 3.4 Problem 4 — Liquidity-Adjusted Value at Risk (L-VaR)

**Question:** A portfolio has 1-day 99% VaR of ₹ 50 cr. The bid-ask spread on the assets is ₹ 0.80 per ₹ 100 face value, and the position size is 1,00,000 units of ₹ 100 each (i.e., ₹ 1 cr face). Compute L-VaR.

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute the half-spread cost (liquidation cost).**

The half-spread (per unit) = $0.80 / 2 = 0.40$ per ₹ 100 face.

Position value $Q = 1,00,000 \times 100 = \text{₹ } 1 \text{ crore}$.

**Step 2: Compute liquidation cost.**

$$
LC = \dfrac{0.40}{100} \times Q = 0.004 \times 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 40{,}000 = \text{₹ } 0.0004 \text{ cr}
$$

(For larger positions this scales linearly.)

**Step 3: Aggregate L-VaR.**

$$
L\text{-}VaR_{99\%} = VaR_{99\%} + LC = 50 + 0.0004 = \text{₹ } 50.0004 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 4: Engineering Insight.**

The incremental liquidity cost is small *here* because $Q$ is small. For a stressed portfolio with $Q = \text{₹ } 10,000 \text{ cr}$, the same spread would add ₹ 4 cr to the VaR — a non-trivial capital add-on.

$$
\boxed{L\text{-}VaR_{99\%} = \text{₹ } 50.0004 \text{ crore}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Problem 5 — Liquidity Gap & Survival Horizon

**Question:** A bank has Liquid Assets of ₹ 6,000 cr and Net Daily Outflows of ₹ 500 cr. Compute its **Survival Horizon** $H$ in days.

### Solution

**Step 1: Apply the survival horizon formula.**

$$
H = \dfrac{\text{Liquid Assets}}{\text{Net Daily Outflows}} = \dfrac{6000}{500} = 12 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 2: Interpretation.**

$$
\boxed{H = 12 \text{ days}}
$$

The bank can survive a 12-day deposit run at current outflow pace. This is *below* the LCR's 30-day stress horizon, signaling the bank needs to convert illiquid assets into HQLA or slow outflows.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Operational Risk — Event-Type & Capital Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Operational Risk Universe] --> B[Internal Drivers]
    A --> C[External Drivers]
    A --> D[Measurement Approaches]
    B --> B1[Internal Fraud]
    B --> B2[Employment Practices]
    B --> B3[Process Failures]
    B --> B4[System Failures]
    B --> B5[Physical Asset Damage]
    C --> C1[External Fraud]
    C --> C2[Legal Liability]
    C --> C3[Natural Disasters]
    D --> D1[BIA: 15 percent of GI]
    D --> D2[TSA: 8 Business Lines]
    D --> D3[AMA: Internal LDA Model]
    D1 --> E1[Capital Charge K1]
    D2 --> E2[Capital Charge K2]
    D3 --> E3[VaR 99.9 percent, 1 year]
    E1 --> F[Aggregate Pillar 1 Capital]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The graph shows the **three measurement approaches converging into a single Pillar 1 capital figure**. A KTU 14-mark question can be built around "Compare the three approaches and compute the capital under each."

---

## 4.2 Liquidity Risk — Management Framework (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC[Funding Sources] --> ASF[Available Stable Funding ASF]
    USE[Asset Deployment] --> RSF[Required Stable Funding RSF]
    HQLA[Liquid Asset Pool] --> LCR[LCR Computation]
    CASH[Cash Inflows Outflows] --> NET[Net Cash Outflows 30d]
    NET --> LCR
    ASF --> NSFR[NSFR Computation]
    RSF --> NSFR
    LCR --> STRESS[30 Day Stress Survival]
    NSFR --> STRUCT[1 Year Structural Balance]
    STRESS --> ALERT[Regulatory Alert if LCR below 100]
    STRUCT --> ALERT
    LCR --> GAP[Intraday Liquidity Monitoring]
    GAP --> TICK[Real Time Settlement Queue]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The block architecture shows that **LCR and NSFR are independent ratios with their own numerator/denominator pipelines**, but they both feed into a single **regulatory alert layer**. The leftmost column (HQLA, Cash Flows) is the *short-term* axis; the rightmost (ASF, RSF) is the *long-term* axis. This is exactly the KTU Module 2 syllabus structure.

---

## 4.3 Loss Distribution Approach (LDA) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    F[Frequency Model N] -->|Poisson lambda| AGG[Aggregate Loss S]
    SVR[Severity Model X] -->|Lognormal or Pareto| AGG
    AGG --> MC[Monte Carlo Simulation 100000 trials]
    MC --> DIST[Loss Distribution Fs]
    DIST --> VAR[VaR 99.9 percent]
    VAR --> CAP[OpRisk Capital Allocation]
    KRI[Key Risk Indicators] --> CAP
    SCEN[Scenario Analysis] --> CAP
    RCSA[Risk and Control Self Assessment] --> CAP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** LDA fuses **quantitative (Frequency × Severity × Monte Carlo)** and **qualitative (KRI, RCSA, Scenario)** inputs. In AMA, the bank combines them through Bayesian methods to arrive at the final capital figure. This is a high-weightage 14-mark topic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper] (3 Marks)

**Differentiate between Funding Liquidity Risk and Market Liquidity Risk with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Funding Liquidity Risk | Market Liquidity Risk |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Definition | Inability to *raise* new funds to meet obligations | Inability to *sell* an existing asset at a fair price |
| Origin | Liability side (mismatch in cash flows) | Asset side (absence of buyers, wide spreads) |
| Example | A bank unable to roll over short-term wholesale deposits | An investor holding a CDO tranche in 2008 with no buyers |
| Mitigation | Diversify funding sources, maintain HQLA buffer | Reduce concentrated positions, trade on liquid exchanges |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (3 Marks)

**List the seven event-type categories of operational risk as per the Basel framework.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Internal Fraud
2. External Fraud
3. Employment Practices & Workplace Safety
4. Clients, Products & Business Practices
5. Business Disruption & System Failures
6. Damage to Physical Assets
7. Execution, Delivery & Process Management

*(Mnemonic: **"I-E-C-B-D-E"** — Internal, External, Clients, Business, Damage, Execution + Employment)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ESE Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer **one** full question (either Q-A or Q-B) from the module. Each carries 14 marks and has internal choice.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Operational Risk Capital Under BIA & LDA

#### Part (a) — BIA Capital Charge Computation (7 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

A bank reports the following Gross Incomes over the last three years:
- Year 1: ₹ 800 crore (positive)
- Year 2: ₹ 1,200 crore (positive)
- Year 3: ₹ (–) 200 crore (negative)

Compute the **operational risk capital charge** under the Basic Indicator Approach. Comment on the impact of the negative year.

**Model Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1:** Identify positive gross income years (per Basel rule, negative GI is excluded).

$$
GI_1 = 800 \text{ cr}, \quad GI_2 = 1200 \text{ cr}, \quad GI_3 = \text{excluded}
$$

**Step 2:** Sum positive gross incomes.

$$
\sum GI = 800 + 1200 = 2000 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by 15% and divide by 3.

$$
K_{BIA} = \dfrac{0.15 \times 2000}{3} = \dfrac{300}{3} = 100 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 4:** Final answer with regulatory comment.

$$
\boxed{K_{BIA} = \text{₹ } 100 \text{ crore}}
$$

> The negative year is **dropped**, not netted. This is a key Basel rule that many students miss. The bank *benefits* from the negative year because excluding it keeps the capital charge lower than if the year had been marginally positive.

---

#### Part (b) — LDA Concept with Numerical Severity Example (7 Marks)

Explain the **Loss Distribution Approach (LDA)** for operational risk. If a bank estimates the **average frequency** of operational loss events as $\lambda = 4$ events/year, and the **average severity** of each event is ₹ 50 lakh, with severity standard deviation ₹ 20 lakh, compute the **expected annual aggregate loss** assuming severity is lognormal.

**Model Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: State the LDA framework.**

LDA decomposes aggregate loss into two independent components:
- Frequency $N$ ~ Poisson($\lambda$)
- Severity $X$ ~ Lognormal($\mu, \sigma$)

**Step 2: Expected Frequency.**

$$
E[N] = \lambda = 4 \text{ events/year}
$$

**Step 3: Expected Severity (lognormal mean).**

For a lognormal distribution with parameters $\mu, \sigma$, the mean is:

$$
E[X] = e^{\mu + \sigma^2 / 2} = 50 \text{ lakh}
$$

(The problem directly provides the mean; we use it as given.)

**Step 4: Expected Aggregate Annual Loss.**

$$
E[S] = E[N] \cdot E[X] = 4 \times 50 = 200 \text{ lakh} = \text{₹ } 2 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 5: Bank's capital implication.**

The expected loss $E[S] = \text{₹ } 2$ cr must be covered as **expected loss provision** (Pillar 1 operational capital covers the *unexpected* loss, while the 99.9% VaR — beyond the mean — is the actual regulatory capital).

$$
\boxed{E[S] = \text{₹ } 2 \text{ crore per year}}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Liquidity Risk: LCR & NSFR Full Computation

#### Part (a) — LCR with Multi-Tier HQLA (7 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Replica]**

A bank has the following liquid assets:
- **Cash & Reserves:** ₹ 1,000 cr
- **Government Securities (Level 1):** ₹ 4,000 cr
- **Level 2A (AA-rated covered bonds):** ₹ 1,200 cr
- **Level 2B (Listed equity in major index):** ₹ 600 cr

The 30-day stressed net cash outflow is **₹ 7,000 cr**.

(i) Compute the LCR.
(ii) State whether the bank is compliant.
(iii) If non-compliant, calculate the additional HQLA required to just meet the 100% threshold.

**Model Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: Compute unadjusted HQLA.**

$$
L_1 = 1000 + 4000 = 5000 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
L_{2A} = 1200 \text{ cr}, \quad L_{2B} = 600 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Basel III haircuts.**

$$
L_{2A}^{adj} = 0.85 \times 1200 = 1020 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
L_{2B}^{adj} = 0.50 \times 600 = 300 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 3: Total HQLA (pre-cap).**

$$
HQLA_{pre} = 5000 + 1020 + 300 = 6320 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 4: Cap checks.**
- $L_2 \leq 40\%$ of HQLA: $0.40 \times 6320 = 2528$; our $L_2 = 1320$, so **OK**.
- $L_{2B} \leq 15\%$ of HQLA: $0.15 \times 6320 = 948$; our $L_{2B} = 300$, so **OK**.

**Step 5: LCR.**

$$
LCR = \dfrac{6320}{7000} = 0.9029 = 90.29\%
$$

**Step 6: Compliance verdict — NON-COMPLIANT.**

**Step 7: Additional HQLA required.**

$$
HQLA_{req} = 7000 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
\Delta HQLA = 7000 - 6320 = 680 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
\boxed{LCR = 90.29\%, \quad \text{Non-compliant; needs additional HQLA of } \text{₹ } 680 \text{ cr}}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — NSFR Computation and Intraday Liquidity (7 Marks)

A bank has the following funding structure:

| Liability | Amount (₹ cr) | ASF Factor |
|-----------|---------------|------------|
| Capital & Reserves | 2,000 | 100% |
| Retail Deposits (Stable) | 3,000 | 95% |
| Wholesale Deposits (< 1 yr) | 2,500 | 50% |
| Short-term Borrowings | 1,500 | 0% |

| Asset | Amount (₹ cr) | RSF Factor |
|-------|---------------|------------|
| HQLA (Level 1) | 1,000 | 0% |
| Loans to Corporates (1 yr+) | 5,000 | 85% |
| Residential Mortgages | 2,000 | 65% |
| Other Assets | 1,000 | 100% |

Compute the NSFR and interpret the result. Briefly explain **intraday liquidity risk** in 2–3 lines.

**Model Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: Compute ASF.**

$$
ASF = (2000 \times 1.00) + (3000 \times 0.95) + (2500 \times 0.50) + (1500 \times 0)
$$

$$
ASF = 2000 + 2850 + 1250 + 0 = 6100 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 2: Compute RSF.**

$$
RSF = (1000 \times 0.00) + (5000 \times 0.85) + (2000 \times 0.65) + (1000 \times 1.00)
$$

$$
RSF = 0 + 4250 + 1300 + 1000 = 6550 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 3: NSFR.**

$$
NSFR = \dfrac{ASF}{RSF} = \dfrac{6100}{6550} = 0.9313 = 93.13\%
$$

**Step 4: Verdict.**

Since $NSFR < 100\%$, the bank is **structurally non-compliant**. The shortfall is:

$$
6550 - 6100 = 450 \text{ cr in additional ASF needed.}
$$

**Step 5: Intraday Liquidity Risk (2–3 lines).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Intraday liquidity risk** is the risk that a bank cannot meet its payment obligations on a *real-time, same-day* basis as they fall due during the business day, particularly in RTGS systems. It arises from delayed incoming payments, queuing in netting systems, and timing mismatches in correspondent banking.

$$
\boxed{NSFR = 93.13\%, \quad \text{Non-compliant; shortfall of } \text{₹ } 450 \text{ cr in ASF}}
$$

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks in Module 2 (Operational & Liquidity Risks):**
>
> 1. **BIA Numerator Error:** Forgetting to *exclude* negative gross income years instead of netting them. **Always exclude.**
> 2. **HQLA Cap Misapplication:** Computing HQLA without checking the **40% cap** on Level 2 assets and the **15% cap** on Level 2B. Many solutions add $L_1 + L_{2A} + L_{2B}$ without capping and lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **LCR vs NSFR Confusion:** LCR = *30-day* stress; NSFR = *1-year* structural. Mixing them up yields wrong framework.
> 4. **LDA Expected Loss vs Capital:** Many students confuse $E[S]$ (expected loss) with regulatory capital. Capital = $VaR_{99.9\%} - E[S]$, not $E[S]$.
> 5. **Liquidity-Adjusted VaR Formula:** Some students forget to add half the spread, not the full spread. The half-spread assumption comes from the *Roll* (1984) market microstructure model.
> 6. **Intraday vs Interday:** LCR measures **interday** (30-day) liquidity, not **intraday**. A common KTU trick question.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2**

### Operational Risk
- ☐ OpRisk = loss from **inadequate processes, people, systems, or external events** (Basel).
- ☐ Excludes **strategic and reputational** risk; includes **legal** risk.
- ☐ **7 Event Types:** Internal Fraud, External Fraud, Employment Practices, Clients/Products, Business Disruption, Physical Asset Damage, Execution/Delivery.
- ☐ **BIA Formula:** $K = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot \sum GI^{+}}{3}$ with $\alpha = 15\%$.
- ☐ **TSA Formula:** $K = \sum_{j=1}^{8} \beta_j \cdot GI_j$, where $\beta_j \in [4\%, 18\%]$ per business line.
- ☐ **AMA uses LDA:** $N \sim \text{Poisson}$, $X \sim \text{Lognormal}$, Capital = $VaR_{99.9\%, 1yr}$.
- ☐ Negative GI year → **excluded**, not netted.
- ☐ Operational VaR is held over **1 year** (vs 1–10 days for market risk).

### Liquidity Risk
- ☐ Two sub-types: **Funding Liquidity Risk** and **Market Liquidity Risk**.
- ☐ **LCR** = HQLA / Net Cash Outflows (30-day stress) ≥ **100%**.
- ☐ **NSFR** = ASF / RSF (1-year structural) ≥ **100%**.
- ☐ HQLA: $L_1$ (no haircut), $L_{2A}$ (15% haircut), $L_{2B}$ (50% haircut).
- ☐ Caps: $L_2 \leq 40\%$ of HQLA; $L_{2B} \leq 15\%$ of HQLA.
- ☐ **Liquidity-Adjusted VaR** = Standard VaR + $\frac{1}{2} \times$ bid-ask spread × position size.
- ☐ **Amihud Illiquidity** = $\vert R_t \vert / VOL_t$ — higher = more illiquid.
- ☐ **Survival Horizon** = Liquid Assets / Net Daily Outflows.
- ☐ Intraday liquidity = real-time, same-day settlement risk; **distinct from LCR**.
- ☐ Roll's (1984) model: half-spread is the equilibrium bid-ask cost.

### High-Yield Mnemonics
- **"BIA = 15, TSA = 8, AMA = 99.9"** — three numbers to remember.
- **"LCR = 30 days, NSFR = 1 year"** — short vs long axis.
- **"7 OpRisk Events"** = **I-E-C-B-D-E-P** (Internal, External, Clients, Business, Damage, Execution, Practices/People).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk - Variance and Covariance Matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET635 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Risk - Variance and Covariance Matrix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Risk in Financial Engineering
In the context of KTU's Financial Engineering module, **risk** is formally defined as the quantifiable uncertainty of investment outcomes, mathematically measured as the dispersion of returns around their expected value. The two foundational building blocks used to capture this risk mathematically are the **variance** and the **covariance**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> Risk is the variability of actual returns from expected returns. For a single asset, this is captured by **variance** (or its square root, standard deviation). For multiple assets, the joint variability — i.e., how assets move together — is captured by the **covariance**, and when organized in matrix form, it becomes the **covariance matrix (Σ)**.

### 1.2 Variance — The "Standalone" Risk Metric
**Variance** of a random return variable $R$ is the expected value of the squared deviation of $R$ from its mean (expected return) $\mu = E[R]$.

$$\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(R) = E\left[(R - \mu)^2\right]$$

For a discrete probability distribution (e.g., historical or scenario-based), the sample variance is:

$$\sigma^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i (r_i - \bar{r})^2$$

where $p_i$ is the probability of return $r_i$, and $\bar{r}$ is the mean return.

### 1.3 Covariance — The "Togetherness" Metric
**Covariance** between two assets $A$ and $B$ with returns $R_A$ and $R_B$ measures the **directional relationship** between their returns:

$$\text{Cov}(R_A, R_B) = E\left[(R_A - \mu_A)(R_B - \mu_B)\right]$$

The **sign** of covariance tells the story:
- $\text{Cov} > 0$: Both assets tend to move **together** (both rise or both fall).
- $\text{Cov} < 0$: The assets move in **opposite directions** (diversification friendly).
- $\text{Cov} = 0$: The asset returns are **linearly unrelated**.

### 1.4 Covariance Matrix (Σ) — The "Portfolio Risk Blueprint"
A **covariance matrix** is a square, symmetric matrix that consolidates the variances of all individual assets on its main diagonal and the pairwise covariances off the diagonal. For an $N$-asset portfolio, it is an $N \times N$ matrix:

$$\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1^2 & \text{Cov}(1,2) & \cdots & \text{Cov}(1,N) \\ \text{Cov}(2,1) & \sigma_2^2 & \cdots & \text{Cov}(2,N) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \text{Cov}(N,1) & \text{Cov}(N,2) & \cdots & \sigma_N^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine three friends — Anu, Binu, and Chinu — going to the market every day. Each day, the price of apple and orange changes.
> - **Variance** of apple's price = how "jumpy" the apple price is on its own.
> - **Covariance** of apple and orange = whether they jump *together* (positive) or *opposite* (negative).
> - **Covariance matrix** = the full ledger that records every fruit's jumpiness and every pair's co-jumpiness in one table. When Anu wants a "basket" of fruits, this ledger is what tells her the total basket-jumpiness.

### 1.5 KTU Highlight — Why These Two Metrics Matter
| Metric | Captures | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Variance** | Standalone risk of one asset | Standard deviation in CAPM, Sharpe ratio |
| **Covariance** | Pairwise co-movement | Diversification benefit calculation |
| **Covariance Matrix (Σ)** | Whole-portfolio risk structure | Mean-Variance Optimization (Markowitz) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Conceptual Flow
1. **Collect return data** for each asset (historical, scenario-based, or parametric).
2. **Compute expected return** $\mu_i = E[R_i]$ for each asset.
3. **Compute deviations** $d_i = R_i - \mu_i$ from the mean.
4. **For variance**: Square each asset's own deviation and take its expectation → $\text{Var}(R_i) = E[d_i^2]$.
5. **For covariance**: Multiply two assets' deviations and take the expectation → $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = E[d_i \cdot d_j]$.
6. **Assemble Σ**: Place variances on the diagonal, covariances off the diagonal.
7. **Apply Σ** to portfolio weights $\mathbf{w}$ to compute portfolio variance.

### 2.2 Why the "Why" — Engineering Intuition
- **Variance is non-negative** because it is an expectation of a *squared* quantity — mathematically it can never fall below zero.
- **Covariance is bounded** in magnitude by the geometric mean of the two variances: $\vert \text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) \vert \le \sigma_i \cdot \sigma_j$. This bound gives rise to the **correlation coefficient** $\rho_{ij} = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j)}{\sigma_i \sigma_j}$, which always lies in $[-1, +1]$.
- **Symmetry** of Σ: $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = \text{Cov}(R_j, R_i)$ because the expectation operator is symmetric. Hence $\Sigma = \Sigma^T$.
- **Positive semi-definiteness**: For any weight vector $\mathbf{w}$, $\mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w} \ge 0$. This is why Σ is mathematically valid for variance computations.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table (use \mid for absolute value to keep markdown valid):**

| # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Variance | $\sigma_i^2 = E[(R_i - \mu_i)^2] = E[R_i^2] - \mu_i^2$ | Standalone risk of asset $i$ |
| 2 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\text{Var}(R_i)}$ | Same as variance, but in return units |
| 3 | Covariance | $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = E[(R_i - \mu_i)(R_j - \mu_j)]$ | Co-movement between assets $i, j$ |
| 4 | Covariance Identity | $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = E[R_i R_j] - \mu_i \mu_j$ | Computable from raw expectations |
| 5 | Covariance Symmetry | $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = \text{Cov}(R_j, R_i)$ | Matrix is symmetric |
| 6 | Correlation | $\rho_{ij} = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j)}{\sigma_i \sigma_j}$ | Standardized covariance, $\rho \in [-1, +1]$ |
| 7 | Covariance Bound | $\mid \text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) \mid \le \sigma_i \sigma_j$ | From Cauchy-Schwarz inequality |
| 8 | Portfolio Variance (2 assets) | $\sigma_P^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \text{Cov}(1,2)$ | Special case of matrix formula |
| 9 | Portfolio Variance (matrix) | $\sigma_P^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w}$ | General N-asset formula |
| 10 | Portfolio Variance (sum form) | $\sigma_P^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_i w_j \text{Cov}(R_i, R_j)$ | Element-wise expansion of $\mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w}$ |
| 11 | Covariance of Sum | $\text{Var}\!\left(\sum w_i R_i\right) = \sum_i \sum_j w_i w_j \text{Cov}(R_i, R_j)$ | Variance of a linear combination |
| 12 | Covariance with Constant | $\text{Cov}(R, c) = 0$ | Constants do not co-vary |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Markowitz Mean-Variance Optimization**: The covariance matrix is the *core input* that determines the efficient frontier.
- **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**: $\beta_i = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_M)}{\sigma_M^2}$ — beta is nothing but a normalized covariance.
- **Risk Parity & Black-Litterman Models**: These modern portfolio strategies all rely on a reliable estimate of Σ.
- **Value at Risk (VaR)** and **Monte Carlo simulation** of large portfolios all consume Σ as the engine of correlated random return generation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Pitfall (KTU-style):** Students often confuse *correlation* with *covariance*. Remember: covariance is **not** bounded in absolute value, but correlation $\rho$ *is* bounded in $[-1, +1]$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Variance from First Principles (Expanded Form)

Starting from the formal definition:

$$\sigma^2 = E[(R - \mu)^2]$$

**Step 1:** Expand the squared term using the identity $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$:

$$E[(R - \mu)^2] = E[R^2 - 2 R \mu + \mu^2]$$

**Step 2:** Apply linearity of expectation (sum of expectations = expectation of sum):

$$E[R^2 - 2 R \mu + \mu^2] = E[R^2] - 2\mu E[R] + \mu^2$$

**Step 3:** Since $\mu = E[R]$ is a constant, $E[R] = \mu$, and $E[\mu^2] = \mu^2$:

$$= E[R^2] - 2\mu \cdot \mu + \mu^2$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:

$$= E[R^2] - 2\mu^2 + \mu^2 = E[R^2] - \mu^2$$

**Final Identity:**

$$\boxed{\sigma^2 = E[R^2] - (E[R])^2}$$

> This shortcut identity is the most used in KTU numericals — it avoids the explicit mean-deviation calculation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Covariance from First Principles

Starting from the formal definition:

$$\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = E[(R_i - \mu_i)(R_j - \mu_j)]$$

**Step 1:** Expand the product of the two bracketed terms:

$$(R_i - \mu_i)(R_j - \mu_j) = R_i R_j - R_i \mu_j - R_j \mu_i + \mu_i \mu_j$$

**Step 2:** Take expectation term by term (linearly):

$$= E[R_i R_j] - \mu_j E[R_i] - \mu_i E[R_j] + \mu_i \mu_j$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $E[R_i] = \mu_i$ and $E[R_j] = \mu_j$:

$$= E[R_i R_j] - \mu_i \mu_j - \mu_i \mu_j + \mu_i \mu_j$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:

$$= E[R_i R_j] - \mu_i \mu_j$$

**Final Identity:**

$$\boxed{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = E[R_i R_j] - E[R_i] \cdot E[R_j]}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Portfolio Variance (N-Asset, Matrix Form)

Let the portfolio contain $N$ assets with weight vector $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N]^T$ and return vector $\mathbf{R} = [R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_N]^T$.

**Step 1:** Portfolio return is a weighted sum:

$$R_P = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i R_i = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{R}$$

**Step 2:** Portfolio variance is the variance of this linear combination:

$$\sigma_P^2 = \text{Var}(R_P) = \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i R_i\right)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the general variance-of-sum identity:

$$\text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i R_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_i w_j \, \text{Cov}(R_i, R_j)$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the double sum as a matrix product. Define $\Sigma$ where $\Sigma_{ij} = \text{Cov}(R_i, R_j)$:

$$\sigma_P^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w}$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_P^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_i w_j \, \Sigma_{ij}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Trick:** When the problem gives the matrix Σ explicitly, the valuation step is: (a) form the column vector $\mathbf{w}$; (b) compute $\Sigma \mathbf{w}$; (c) dot-product with $\mathbf{w}^T$. No summation needs to be expanded manually.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU-Style 3-Asset Problem

**Problem Setup (typical KTU 2024 question):**
> A portfolio has three assets with weights $\mathbf{w} = [0.5, \; 0.3, \; 0.2]^T$. The covariance matrix (in $\%^2$) is:
> 
> $$\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 & -0.5 \\ 1 & 9 & 2 \\ -0.5 & 2 & 16 \end{bmatrix}$$
> 
> Calculate the portfolio standard deviation.

**Step 1:** Verify $\Sigma$ is symmetric: $\Sigma_{12} = 1 = \Sigma_{21}$, $\Sigma_{13} = -0.5 = \Sigma_{31}$, $\Sigma_{23} = 2 = \Sigma_{32}$. ✔

**Step 2:** Compute the matrix product $\Sigma \mathbf{w}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \Sigma \mathbf{w} &= \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 & -0.5 \\ 1 & 9 & 2 \\ -0.5 & 2 & 16 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] &= \begin{bmatrix} (4)(0.5) + (1)(0.3) + (-0.5)(0.2) \\ (1)(0.5) + (9)(0.3) + (2)(0.2) \\ (-0.5)(0.5) + (2)(0.3) + (16)(0.2) \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] &= \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 + 0.3 - 0.1 \\ 0.5 + 2.7 + 0.4 \\ -0.25 + 0.6 + 3.2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.2 \\ 3.6 \\ 3.55 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the dot product $\mathbf{w}^T (\Sigma \mathbf{w})$:
