$$\begin{aligned} \mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w} &= (0.5)(2.2) + (0.3)(3.6) + (0.2)(3.55) \\[4pt] &= 1.10 + 1.08 + 0.71 = 2.89 \; \%^2 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Take the square root to obtain standard deviation:

$$\sigma_P = \sqrt{2.89} = 1.70 \;\%$$

**Final Answer:** $\sigma_P^2 = 2.89\%^2$ and $\sigma_P = 1.70\%$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Reasoning Table — How to Identify the "Right" Tool

| Scenario in KTU Question | Use This Formula | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Single asset, raw return scenarios | $\sigma^2 = \sum p_i (r_i - \bar{r})^2$ | Direct definition |
| Two assets, find diversification benefit | $\sigma_P^2 = w_1^2\sigma_1^2 + w_2^2\sigma_2^2 + 2w_1 w_2 \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ | Two-asset special case |
| N-assets, matrix given | $\sigma_P^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w}$ | Compact matrix product |
| Question asks for beta of a stock | $\beta = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_M)}{\sigma_M^2}$ | CAPM definition |
| Question asks for correlation | $\rho = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_1, R_2)}{\sigma_1 \sigma_2}$ | Normalized co-movement |
| Standard deviation from mean-variance | $\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(R)}$ | Variance → SD conversion |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Building the Covariance Matrix and Computing Portfolio Risk

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Historical or Scenario Returns for N Assets] --> B[Step 1: Compute Mean Return for each asset mu_i = E of R_i]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute Deviations d_i = R_i minus mu_i]
    C --> D[Step 3: Form Diagonal Entries sigma_i squared = E of d_i squared]
    C --> E[Step 3: Form Off-Diagonal Entries Cov i j = E of d_i times d_j]
    D --> F[Step 4: Assemble Symmetric N by N Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Step 5: Verify Symmetry Sigma equals Sigma transpose]
    G --> H[Step 6: Verify Positive Semi-Definiteness wT Sigma w greater or equal 0]
    H --> I[Step 7: Accept Sigma as Risk Engine]
    I --> J[Step 8: Multiply Sigma by Weight Vector w to get Sigma w]
    J --> K[Step 9: Compute wT times Sigma w to get Portfolio Variance]
    K --> L[Step 10: Take Square Root to get Portfolio Standard Deviation]
    I --> M[Side Branch: Derive Correlation Matrix rho using sigma_i sigma_j]
    M --> N[Output: Correlation Matrix and Risk Reports]
    L --> O[Output: Final Portfolio Risk Sigma P]
    I --> P[Side Branch: Compute CAPM Beta for each stock]
    P --> Q[Output: Beta Values for CAPM Use]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The **central vertical pipeline** (boxes A → I → J → K → L → O) shows the linear flow of computing portfolio risk from raw returns. The **right-side branches** (M, N, P, Q) show two additional KTU-relevant outputs that fall out of the same Σ — correlation matrix and CAPM betas.

### 4.2 Conceptual Map — Relationship Between Variance, Covariance, and Correlation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Variance sigma_i squared] --> C[Diagonal of Sigma]
    B[Covariance Cov i j] --> D[Off-Diagonal of Sigma]
    C --> E[Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Portfolio Variance wT Sigma w]
    E --> G[Correlation Matrix rho]
    E --> H[CAPM Beta Cov i M divided by sigma M squared]
    F --> I[Portfolio Standard Deviation sigma P]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Asset Pricing Decisions]
    I --> K[Risk Reports and Optimization]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip:** All three risk metrics (variance, covariance, correlation) ultimately feed into the same Σ matrix, which is the *single source of truth* for portfolio risk in Markowitz-style optimization.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> Define **covariance matrix**. State and justify any **two** of its key mathematical properties.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A covariance matrix $\Sigma$ is an $N \times N$ symmetric matrix whose $(i,j)$-th element is the covariance between the returns of assets $i$ and $j$. Diagonal entries are variances; off-diagonal entries are pairwise covariances.
- **Property 1 — Symmetry (1 mark):** $\Sigma = \Sigma^T$ because $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = \text{Cov}(R_j, R_i)$. The expectation operator is symmetric in its two arguments.
- **Property 2 — Positive semi-definiteness (1 mark):** For any real weight vector $\mathbf{w}$, $\mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w} \ge 0$, because it represents the variance $\text{Var}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{R})$ which cannot be negative.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]**
> Distinguish between **covariance** and **correlation coefficient**. Why is the correlation coefficient considered more useful in practice?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Covariance (1 mark):** $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = E[(R_i - \mu_i)(R_j - \mu_j)]$. It measures the *direction* of co-movement (positive, negative, zero) but its magnitude is unbounded and depends on the units of $R_i$ and $R_j$.
- **Correlation (1 mark):** $\rho_{ij} = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j)}{\sigma_i \sigma_j}$. It is a *unit-free*, *bounded* measure in $[-1, +1]$, allowing direct comparison of co-movement across any pair of assets.
- **Why more useful (1 mark):** Because it is dimensionless and bounded, $\rho$ is interpretable on a universal scale (e.g., $\rho = 0.7$ means a "strong positive linear relationship" regardless of which two assets are involved), while covariance values cannot be compared across asset pairs with different volatilities.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

> A portfolio consists of **three assets** A, B, and C with weights $w_A = 0.4$, $w_B = 0.35$, $w_C = 0.25$. The covariance matrix of returns is:
> 
> $$\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0400 & 0.0120 & -0.0050 \\ 0.0120 & 0.0625 & 0.0180 \\ -0.0050 & 0.0180 & 0.0900 \end{bmatrix}$$
> 
> **(a)** Compute the **portfolio variance** and **portfolio standard deviation**.
> 
> **(b)** If the correlation between assets A and B changes to $\rho_{AB} = -0.5$, recompute the portfolio variance. Comment on the diversification effect.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Write the weight vector:

$$\mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.35 \\ 0.25 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\Sigma \mathbf{w}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \Sigma \mathbf{w} &= \begin{bmatrix} (0.0400)(0.4) + (0.0120)(0.35) + (-0.0050)(0.25) \\ (0.0120)(0.4) + (0.0625)(0.35) + (0.0180)(0.25) \\ (-0.0050)(0.4) + (0.0180)(0.35) + (0.0900)(0.25) \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.0160 + 0.0042 - 0.00125 \\ 0.0048 + 0.021875 + 0.0045 \\ -0.0020 + 0.0063 + 0.0225 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.01895 \\ 0.031175 \\ 0.0268 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\mathbf{w}^T (\Sigma \mathbf{w})$:

$$\begin{aligned} \sigma_P^2 &= (0.4)(0.01895) + (0.35)(0.031175) + (0.25)(0.0268) \\[4pt] &= 0.00758 + 0.01091125 + 0.0067 = 0.02519125 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Take square root:

$$\sigma_P = \sqrt{0.02519125} \approx 0.15872 \text{ or } 15.87\%$$

**Answer (a):** $\sigma_P^2 \approx 0.0252$ and $\sigma_P \approx 15.87\%$.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** New covariance between A and B is:

$$\text{Cov}_{AB}^{\text{new}} = \rho_{AB} \cdot \sigma_A \cdot \sigma_B = (-0.5)(\sqrt{0.0400})(\sqrt{0.0625}) = (-0.5)(0.2)(0.25) = -0.0250$$

**Step 2:** The new covariance matrix becomes:

$$\Sigma^{\text{new}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0400 & -0.0250 & -0.0050 \\ -0.0250 & 0.0625 & 0.0180 \\ -0.0050 & 0.0180 & 0.0900 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\Sigma^{\text{new}} \mathbf{w}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \Sigma^{\text{new}} \mathbf{w} &= \begin{bmatrix} (0.0400)(0.4) + (-0.0250)(0.35) + (-0.0050)(0.25) \\ (-0.0250)(0.4) + (0.0625)(0.35) + (0.0180)(0.25) \\ (-0.0050)(0.4) + (0.0180)(0.35) + (0.0900)(0.25) \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.0160 - 0.00875 - 0.00125 \\ -0.0100 + 0.021875 + 0.0045 \\ -0.0020 + 0.0063 + 0.0225 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0060 \\ 0.016375 \\ 0.0268 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the new portfolio variance:

$$\begin{aligned} \sigma_P^{2,\text{new}} &= (0.4)(0.0060) + (0.35)(0.016375) + (0.25)(0.0268) \\[4pt] &= 0.0024 + 0.00573125 + 0.0067 = 0.01483125 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Standard deviation:

$$\sigma_P^{\text{new}} = \sqrt{0.01483125} \approx 0.12178 \text{ or } 12.18\%$$

**Comparison and Comment (1 mark):**
- Original: $\sigma_P \approx 15.87\%$.
- New: $\sigma_P \approx 12.18\%$.
- **Reduction in risk $\approx 3.69$ percentage points** despite keeping the weights identical.
- **Comment:** The negative correlation between A and B provides strong **diversification benefit**. When one asset goes down, the other tends to go up, smoothing the portfolio's overall variability.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]**

> Two assets X and Y have the following return scenarios:
> 
> | Scenario | Probability | $R_X$ | $R_Y$ |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Boom | 0.30 | 0.20 | 0.10 |
> | Normal | 0.50 | 0.10 | 0.08 |
> | Recession | 0.20 | -0.05 | 0.04 |
> 
> **(a)** Compute the expected return, variance, and standard deviation of each asset individually.
> 
> **(b)** Compute the covariance and correlation between X and Y. Then compute the portfolio variance for an equally-weighted portfolio ($w_X = w_Y = 0.5$).

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Expected Returns (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned} E[R_X] &= (0.30)(0.20) + (0.50)(0.10) + (0.20)(-0.05) \\[2pt] &= 0.06 + 0.05 - 0.01 = 0.10 \text{ or } 10\% \\[6pt] E[R_Y] &= (0.30)(0.10) + (0.50)(0.08) + (0.20)(0.04) \\[2pt] &= 0.03 + 0.04 + 0.008 = 0.078 \text{ or } 7.8\% \end{aligned}$$

**Variance of X (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Var}(R_X) &= (0.30)(0.20 - 0.10)^2 + (0.50)(0.10 - 0.10)^2 + (0.20)(-0.05 - 0.10)^2 \\[2pt] &= (0.30)(0.01) + (0.50)(0) + (0.20)(0.0225) \\[2pt] &= 0.003 + 0 + 0.0045 = 0.0075 \\[4pt] \sigma_X &= \sqrt{0.0075} \approx 0.0866 \text{ or } 8.66\% \end{aligned}$$

**Variance of Y (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Var}(R_Y) &= (0.30)(0.10 - 0.078)^2 + (0.50)(0.08 - 0.078)^2 + (0.20)(0.04 - 0.078)^2 \\[2pt] &= (0.30)(0.000484) + (0.50)(0.000004) + (0.20)(0.001444) \\[2pt] &= 0.0001452 + 0.000002 + 0.0002888 = 0.000436 \\[4pt] \sigma_Y &= \sqrt{0.000436} \approx 0.02088 \text{ or } 2.088\% \end{aligned}$$

(1 mark reserved for final SD presentation.)

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Covariance (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Cov}(R_X, R_Y) &= \sum p_i (r_{X,i} - \mu_X)(r_{Y,i} - \mu_Y) \\[4pt] &= (0.30)(0.20 - 0.10)(0.10 - 0.078) \\[2pt] &\quad + (0.50)(0.10 - 0.10)(0.08 - 0.078) \\[2pt] &\quad + (0.20)(-0.05 - 0.10)(0.04 - 0.078) \\[4pt] &= (0.30)(0.10)(0.022) + (0.50)(0)(0.002) + (0.20)(-0.15)(-0.038) \\[2pt] &= 0.00066 + 0 + 0.00114 = 0.0018 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Correlation (1 mark):**

$$\rho_{XY} = \frac{0.0018}{(0.0866)(0.02088)} = \frac{0.0018}{0.001808} \approx 0.9955$$

**Step 3: Portfolio Variance for Equal Weights (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned} \sigma_P^2 &= w_X^2 \sigma_X^2 + w_Y^2 \sigma_Y^2 + 2 w_X w_Y \text{Cov}(X,Y) \\[4pt] &= (0.5)^2(0.0075) + (0.5)^2(0.000436) + 2(0.5)(0.5)(0.0018) \\[2pt] &= 0.001875 + 0.000109 + 0.0009 = 0.002884 \end{aligned}$$

$$\sigma_P = \sqrt{0.002884} \approx 0.0537 \text{ or } 5.37\%$$

**Interpretation:** The two assets are highly positively correlated ($\rho \approx 0.996$), so the diversification benefit is small. The portfolio SD (5.37%) is only slightly lower than the weighted average of individual SDs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "2" in 2w₁w₂Cov** — A huge number of students write the two-asset portfolio variance as $w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + w_1 w_2 \text{Cov}(1,2)$ and **lose 1 mark** for missing the factor 2.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — If returns are in percent, variance is in $\%^2$ and SD is in $\%$. Always carry units through the calculation.
> 3. **Forgetting to take the square root** — A variance answer alone is incomplete; SD is the universally reported risk metric.
> 4. **Asymmetric matrix entry** — A covariance matrix MUST be symmetric. If you write $\text{Cov}(A,B) = 0.012$ on one off-diagonal and $\text{Cov}(B,A) = 0.015$ on the other, you will lose full marks. Always enforce symmetry.
> 5. **Confusing the "diagonal" of correlation matrix** — Diagonal entries of a correlation matrix are exactly **1.0** (because $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_i) / \sigma_i^2 = 1$), not the asset variances.
> 6. **Using sample formulas vs population formulas** — KTU problems usually expect the **population** formulas (divide by $N$ scenarios using $p_i$ as probability). If using historical data, the divisor is $N-1$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- [x] **Variance** = $E[(R - \mu)^2] = E[R^2] - \mu^2$. It is **non-negative** and **scalar**.
- [x] **Standard deviation** = $\sqrt{\text{Variance}}$. It is in the **same units as the return**.
- [x] **Covariance** = $E[(R_i - \mu_i)(R_j - \mu_j)] = E[R_i R_j] - \mu_i \mu_j$. **Sign matters** (positive/negative/zero), magnitude is **unbounded**.
- [x] **Correlation** $\rho = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j)}{\sigma_i \sigma_j}$, bounded in $[-1, +1]$, **unit-free**.
- [x] **Covariance matrix $\Sigma$** is **square**, **symmetric** ($\Sigma = \Sigma^T$), and **positive semi-definite** ($\mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w} \ge 0$).
- [x] **Diagonal of $\Sigma$** = individual variances. **Off-diagonal** = pairwise covariances.
- [x] **Portfolio variance** = $\mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i} \sum_{j} w_i w_j \Sigma_{ij}$.
- [x] **Two-asset special case** = $w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + \mathbf{2} w_1 w_2 \text{Cov}(1,2)$ (note the **factor 2**).
- [x] **CAPM beta** = $\dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_M)}{\sigma_M^2}$ — a normalized covariance.
- [x] **Diversification benefit** is largest when $\rho$ is **strongly negative** and minimal when $\rho \to +1$.
- [x] **KTU calculation recipe**: Given Σ and w → compute $\Sigma \mathbf{w}$ → dot with $\mathbf{w}^T$ → take square root for SD.
- [x] **Units**: returns in % → variance in $\%^2$ → SD in %.
- [x] **Matrix sanity checks** before computing: symmetry, positive semi-definiteness, dimensions match weight vector.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Correlation Matrix and Portfolio Variance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET635 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Correlation Matrix and Portfolio Variance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Financial Engineering**, the **Correlation Matrix** is a symmetric square matrix that quantifies the pairwise linear association (co-movement) between the returns of a set of financial assets. For an *N*-asset universe, the correlation matrix **R** is an *N × N* matrix whose off-diagonal entry ρᵢⱼ represents the **Pearson correlation coefficient** between returns of asset *i* and asset *j*, while the diagonal entries are identically **equal to 1** (since every asset is perfectly correlated with itself).

The **Portfolio Variance** (σ²ₚ) of a portfolio of risky assets is the weighted sum of covariances between all pairs of asset returns in the portfolio, and is the principal scalar measure of the total **diversifiable** and **systematic risk** carried by the portfolio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 2):**
> You MUST be able to (a) construct the correlation matrix for any given set of assets, (b) translate it into the covariance matrix via the relation $\text{Cov}(R_i,R_j) = \rho_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j$, and (c) compute portfolio variance for both 2-asset and *N*-asset cases.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Dance Floor of Returns"**
> Imagine 5 dancers on a dance floor whose footsteps represent the daily returns of 5 stocks.
> - When **two dancers move in perfect sync** (both always step forward together), their correlation coefficient is **+1**.
> - When **two dancers move in perfect opposition** (one steps forward exactly when the other steps backward), their correlation coefficient is **−1**.
> - When **two dancers move completely independently**, their correlation coefficient is **0**.
>
> A **correlation matrix** is simply a score-card telling you, for every pair of dancers, how synchronised their movements are. The **portfolio variance** is then the *overall disorder of the entire group*: even if individual dancers are wild (high individual variance), if their movements cancel each other out (low/negative correlations), the group as a whole moves relatively calmly — this is the **magic of diversification**.

## 1.3 Key Boundary Conditions & Standard Metrics

- **Range of Correlation Coefficient:** $\rho_{ij} \in [-1, +1]$
- **Diagonal Property:** $\rho_{ii} = 1 \; \forall i$
- **Symmetry Property:** $\rho_{ij} = \rho_{ji}$
- **Positive Semi-Definiteness:** A valid correlation matrix must be positive semi-definite (all eigenvalues $\geq 0$).
- **Portfolio Variance Units:** Squared units of return (e.g., $\%^2$ if returns are in percent).
- **Number of Unique Pairs:** For *N* assets there are $\frac{N(N-1)}{2}$ unique correlation values (KTU favourite — short-answer question).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Construction

1. **Collect Historical Returns:** Gather $T$ period returns for each of the *N* assets: $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N$.
2. **Compute Individual Standard Deviations:** Calculate $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\text{Var}(R_i)}$ for each asset.
3. **Compute Pairwise Covariances:** For every pair $(i,j)$, evaluate
   $\text{Cov}(R_i,R_j) = E[(R_i - \bar{R_i})(R_j - \bar{R_j})]$.
4. **Convert Covariance to Correlation:** Divide by the product of standard deviations: $\rho_{ij} = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i,R_j)}{\sigma_i \sigma_j}$.
5. **Assemble the Matrix:** Place 1 on the diagonal and $\rho_{ij}$ on off-diagonal positions. This gives the **correlation matrix R**.
6. **Construct the Covariance Matrix (Σ):** Multiply each off-diagonal entry: $\Sigma_{ij} = \rho_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j$ (and $\Sigma_{ii}=\sigma_i^2$).
7. **Apply the Variance Formula:** Use $\sigma_p^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$ where $\mathbf{w}$ is the weight vector.

### 2.1.1 Why This Works — The 'How' Behind Each Step

- Step 1–2 give us the **individual risk magnitudes**.
- Step 3 captures how the assets move **together** (covariance).
- Step 4 **normalises** the covariance to a scale-free number, enabling comparison across asset pairs.
- Step 5 packages the result for **matrix algebra**.
- Step 6 allows us to compute portfolio variance using a **single linear-algebraic operation**.
- Step 7 yields the **total risk** of the portfolio in one scalar.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every Module-2 question paper.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Two-Asset Portfolio Variance | $\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2\sigma_1^2 + w_2^2\sigma_2^2 + 2w_1 w_2 \rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2$ | $w_1+w_2=1$; cross-term disappears if $\rho_{12}=0$ |
| 2 | N-Asset Portfolio Variance (Scalar) | $\sigma_p^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_i w_j \text{Cov}(R_i,R_j)$ | Double sum; can be split into $i=j$ and $i \neq j$ parts |
| 3 | N-Asset Portfolio Variance (Matrix) | $\sigma_p^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$ | $\mathbf{w}$ is $N \times 1$; $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is $N \times N$ |
| 4 | Covariance ↔ Correlation | $\text{Cov}(R_i,R_j) = \rho_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j$ | Always valid |
| 5 | Correlation Coefficient | $\rho_{ij} = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i,R_j)}{\sigma_i\sigma_j}$ | Range $[-1, +1]$ |
| 6 | Correlation Matrix (N×N) | $\mathbf{R}_{ij} = \rho_{ij}$, with $\rho_{ii}=1$, $\rho_{ij}=\rho_{ji}$ | Symmetric, positive semi-definite |
| 7 | Equal-Weight Variance (N assets) | $\sigma_p^2 = \dfrac{1}{N^2}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sigma_i^2 + \sum_{i\neq j}\sum \rho_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j\right]$ | Total of $N(N-1)$ off-diagonal terms in double sum |
| 8 | Variance with Equal Correlations $\bar{\rho}$ | $\sigma_p^2 = \dfrac{1}{N}\bar{\sigma^2} + \dfrac{N-1}{N}\bar{\rho}\,\bar{\sigma}^2$ (approx.) | Used when all $\sigma_i \approx \bar{\sigma}$ and all $\rho_{ij}=\bar{\rho}$ |
| 9 | Average Pairwise Correlation | $\bar{\rho} = \dfrac{2}{N(N-1)}\sum_{i<j}\sum \rho_{ij}$ | Useful for quick diversification analysis |
| 10 | Unique Correlation Pairs | $\dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | For $N=5$: $10$ pairs; for $N=10$: $45$ pairs |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Finance Utility

- **Risk Management:** Banks compute correlation matrices of asset returns to satisfy **Basel III** capital requirements.
- **Portfolio Construction (Markowitz Theory):** The correlation matrix is the **input** to the mean-variance optimisation framework.
- **Stress Testing:** Correlation breakdowns (e.g., all correlations → 1 during crises) are used to model **tail-risk scenarios**.
- **Algorithmic Trading:** PCA (Principal Component Analysis) of the correlation matrix extracts the dominant risk factors driving markets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Two-Asset Portfolio Variance

**Starting Point:** Let $R_1, R_2$ be random returns and $w_1, w_2$ the portfolio weights such that $w_1+w_2=1$. The portfolio return is:

$$R_p = w_1 R_1 + w_2 R_2$$

**Step 1 — Compute Expected Portfolio Return:**

$$E[R_p] = w_1 E[R_1] + w_2 E[R_2]$$

**Step 2 — Compute Variance by Definition:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = E\!\left[(R_p - E[R_p])^2\right]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Portfolio Return Expression:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = E\!\left[\bigl(w_1 R_1 + w_2 R_2 - w_1 E[R_1] - w_2 E[R_2]\bigr)^2\right]$$

**Step 4 — Group Terms:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = E\!\left[\bigl(w_1(R_1-E[R_1]) + w_2(R_2-E[R_2])\bigr)^2\right]$$

**Step 5 — Expand the Square (FOIL):**

$$\sigma_p^2 = E\!\left[w_1^2(R_1-E[R_1])^2 + w_2^2(R_2-E[R_2])^2 + 2w_1w_2(R_1-E[R_1])(R_2-E[R_2])\right]$$

**Step 6 — Use Linearity of Expectation:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 E[(R_1-E[R_1])^2] + w_2^2 E[(R_2-E[R_2])^2] + 2w_1w_2 E[(R_1-E[R_1])(R_2-E[R_2])]$$

**Step 7 — Recognise Variance and Covariance:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2w_1 w_2\,\text{Cov}(R_1,R_2)$$

**Step 8 — Apply the Covariance ↔ Correlation Identity** $\text{Cov}(R_1,R_2) = \rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2\sigma_1^2 + w_2^2\sigma_2^2 + 2w_1 w_2\,\rho_{12}\,\sigma_1\sigma_2}$$

**Interpretation of Each Term:**
- $w_1^2\sigma_1^2$ — risk contribution from asset 1 alone.
- $w_2^2\sigma_2^2$ — risk contribution from asset 2 alone.
- $2w_1w_2\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2$ — **diversification benefit** (if $\rho_{12}<1$, this term is smaller than the sum of individual risks).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: N-Asset Portfolio Variance (Matrix Form)

**Starting Point:** $R_p = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i R_i = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{R}$ where $\mathbf{w}$ is the column vector of weights and $\mathbf{R}$ the column vector of asset returns.

**Step 1 — Write Variance by Definition:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = E\!\left[\bigl(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{R} - \mathbf{w}^T E[\mathbf{R}]\bigr)^2\right] = E\!\left[\bigl(\mathbf{w}^T(\mathbf{R}-E[\mathbf{R}])\bigr)^2\right]$$

**Step 2 — Use Identity $(a^Ta) = a^T a$:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = E\!\left[\mathbf{w}^T(\mathbf{R}-E[\mathbf{R}])(\mathbf{R}-E[\mathbf{R}])^T \mathbf{w}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Move the constant weight vector out of the expectation operator:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = \mathbf{w}^T\,E\!\left[(\mathbf{R}-E[\mathbf{R}])(\mathbf{R}-E[\mathbf{R}])^T\right]\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the Covariance Matrix:**

$$E\!\left[(\mathbf{R}-E[\mathbf{R}])(\mathbf{R}-E[\mathbf{R}])^T\right] = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}$$

**Step 5 — Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_p^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}}$$

**Step 6 — Expanded Scalar Form (KTU Examiner Loves This):**

$$\sigma_p^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^2 \sigma_i^2 + 2\sum_{i<j}\sum w_i w_j\,\rho_{ij}\,\sigma_i\sigma_j$$

**Why Two Sums?**
- First sum = own-risk contributions (diagonal elements of Σ).
- Second double sum = cross-risk contributions (off-diagonal elements of Σ, each appearing twice due to symmetry).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equal-Weight Special Case for N Identical-Variance Assets

Suppose all $N$ assets have equal standard deviation $\sigma$ and pairwise correlations equal to $\bar{\rho}$, with weights $w_i = 1/N$.

**Step 1 — Diagonal Contribution:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^2 \sigma_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\frac{1}{N^2}\sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}$$

**Step 2 — Off-Diagonal Contribution:** Number of unique pairs = $\dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$; the double sum over $i<j$ gives:

$$2\sum_{i<j}\sum w_i w_j\,\rho_{ij}\,\sigma_i\sigma_j = 2 \cdot \frac{N(N-1)}{2}\cdot \frac{1}{N^2}\cdot \bar{\rho}\cdot\sigma^2 = \frac{(N-1)\bar{\rho}\sigma^2}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Add Both Contributions:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{N} + \frac{(N-1)\bar{\rho}\sigma^2}{N} = \frac{\sigma^2\bigl[1 + (N-1)\bar{\rho}\bigr]}{N}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_p^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}\bigl[1 + (N-1)\bar{\rho}\bigr]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** As $N \to \infty$, $\sigma_p^2 \to \bar{\rho}\sigma^2$. Diversification eliminates **idiosyncratic risk** but **cannot eliminate systematic risk** (which is precisely what $\bar{\rho}$ captures).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Portfolio Variance Computation — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT LAYER Historical Returns N Assets over T periods"] --> B["STATISTICS LAYER Mean and Std Dev per asset"]
    B --> C["COVARIANCE LAYER Pairwise Covariance matrix Sigma"]
    C --> D["CORRELATION LAYER Convert to Correlation Matrix R using rho ij equals Cov ij over sigma i sigma j"]
    D --> E["PORTFOLIO WEIGHTS LAYER Define weight vector w sum equals 1"]
    E --> F["OUTPUT LAYER Compute w transpose Sigma w equals Portfolio Variance"]
    F --> G["RISK METRIC Final sigma p squared in percent squared"]
    B --> H["DIAGONAL Individual variances sigma i squared"]
    H --> F
    C --> I["OFFDIAGONAL Cross terms 2 w i w j rho ij sigma i sigma j"]
    I --> F
```

## 4.2 Correlation Matrix Structure (Illustrative for N=4 Assets)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M["Correlation Matrix R 4x4"]
    M --> D["Diagonal Block All rho ii equals 1 by definition"]
    M --> OD["Off-Diagonal Block rho 12 rho 13 rho 14 rho 23 rho 24 rho 34"]
    D --> P1["Property 1 Symmetric rho ij equals rho ji"]
    OD --> P1
    OD --> P2["Property 2 Bounded minus 1 to plus 1"]
    OD --> P3["Property 3 Positive Semi-Definite All eigenvalues non-negative"]
    D --> P4["Property 4 Unique Pairs equals N open paren N minus 1 close paren over 2 equals 6 for N equals 4"]
```

## 4.3 Diversification Effect — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Start with N equals 1 Portfolio variance equals sigma 1 squared"] --> Add["Add Asset 2 with correlation rho 12"]
    Add --> Branch1{"rho 12 less than 1"}
    Branch1 -- YES --> Reduce["Variance decreases Diversification works"]
    Branch1 -- NO rho equals 1 --> NoEffect["Variance equals weighted average No diversification benefit"]
    Branch1 -- rho less than 0 --> MaxBenefit["Variance drops sharply Maximum diversification"]
    Reduce --> NLimit["As N grows large sigma p squared approaches rho bar sigma squared Systematic risk floor"]
    NoEffect --> NLimit
    MaxBenefit --> NLimit
    NLimit --> Insight["Conclusion Diversification cannot eliminate systematic risk only idiosyncratic risk"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q:** What is a correlation matrix? State any two of its properties.

**Model Answer:**
A correlation matrix **R** is an $N \times N$ symmetric matrix whose entries $\rho_{ij}$ represent the Pearson correlation coefficient between the returns of assets $i$ and $j$.
**Two properties:**
1. **Diagonal entries are unity:** $\rho_{ii} = 1$ for all $i$ (since every variable is perfectly correlated with itself).
2. **Symmetry:** $\rho_{ij} = \rho_{ji}$ for all $i,j$ (correlation is commutative).
3. (Additional) **Bounded range:** $-1 \leq \rho_{ij} \leq +1$.
4. (Additional) **Positive semi-definiteness** for any valid correlation matrix.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q:** For a 5-asset portfolio, how many unique correlation coefficients are required to fully specify the correlation matrix?

**Model Answer:**
The number of unique correlation coefficients for an $N$-asset portfolio equals the number of off-diagonal pairs in an $N \times N$ symmetric matrix:

$$\text{Unique pairs} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2} = \frac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10$$

Hence, **10 unique correlation coefficients** are required (since the diagonal contributes 5 ones trivially, and symmetry halves the remaining 20 entries).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Two assets A and B have expected returns 18% and 12% with standard deviations 20% and 15% respectively. The correlation coefficient between them is 0.4. An investor constructs a portfolio with 60% in A and 40% in B.**

**(a)** Compute the expected return and standard deviation of the portfolio. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Comment on how the portfolio risk would change if the correlation coefficient drops to (i) 0 and (ii) −0.5, keeping all other parameters constant. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Expected Return and Standard Deviation:**

**Expected Return:**

$$E[R_p] = w_A E[R_A] + w_B E[R_B] = (0.60)(18) + (0.40)(12) = 10.8 + 4.8 = 15.6\%$$

**Portfolio Variance:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2\sigma_A^2 + w_B^2\sigma_B^2 + 2w_Aw_B\rho_{AB}\sigma_A\sigma_B$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.60)^2(20)^2 + (0.40)^2(15)^2 + 2(0.60)(0.40)(0.40)(20)(15)$$

**Computing each term separately:**

$$w_A^2\sigma_A^2 = 0.36 \times 400 = 144$$

$$w_B^2\sigma_B^2 = 0.16 \times 225 = 36$$

$$2w_Aw_B\rho_{AB}\sigma_A\sigma_B = 2 \times 0.60 \times 0.40 \times 0.40 \times 20 \times 15$$

$$= 2 \times 0.24 \times 0.40 \times 300 = 2 \times 0.24 \times 120 = 57.6$$

**Summing:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = 144 + 36 + 57.6 = 237.6\ \%^2$$

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{237.6} \approx 15.42\%$$

**Final Answer:** $E[R_p] = 15.6\%$, $\sigma_p \approx 15.42\%$.

**Part (b) — Effect of Correlation Change:**

**Case (i): $\rho_{AB} = 0$**

$$\sigma_p^2 = 144 + 36 + 2(0.60)(0.40)(0)(20)(15) = 144 + 36 + 0 = 180$$

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{180} \approx 13.42\%$$

**Case (ii): $\rho_{AB} = -0.5$**

$$\sigma_p^2 = 144 + 36 + 2(0.60)(0.40)(-0.5)(20)(15)$$

$$= 180 + 2(0.24)(-0.5)(300) = 180 - 72 = 108$$

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{108} \approx 10.39\%$$

**Comments:**
- As correlation drops from **0.4 → 0**, portfolio standard deviation falls from **15.42% → 13.42%** (≈ 13% reduction in risk).
- As correlation drops from **0.4 → −0.5**, portfolio standard deviation falls to **10.39%** (≈ 33% reduction in risk).
- **Conclusion:** Lower (or negative) correlation dramatically enhances diversification benefits. This is the **central insight of Markowitz portfolio theory**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Consider three assets X, Y and Z with standard deviations $\sigma_X = 10\%$, $\sigma_Y = 15\%$, $\sigma_Z = 20\%$ and the following correlation matrix:**

$$\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.3 & 0.5 \\ 0.3 & 1 & 0.2 \\ 0.5 & 0.2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(a)** Construct the corresponding covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An investor allocates weights $\mathbf{w} = [0.5, 0.3, 0.2]^T$. Compute the portfolio variance using the matrix formula $\sigma_p^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Constructing the Covariance Matrix:**

Recall $\Sigma_{ij} = \rho_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j$ and $\Sigma_{ii} = \sigma_i^2$.

**Diagonal entries (variances):**

$$\Sigma_{11} = (10)^2 = 100$$

$$\Sigma_{22} = (15)^2 = 225$$

$$\Sigma_{33} = (20)^2 = 400$$

**Off-diagonal entries (covariances):**

$$\Sigma_{12} = \Sigma_{21} = \rho_{XY}\sigma_X\sigma_Y = (0.3)(10)(15) = 45$$

$$\Sigma_{13} = \Sigma_{31} = \rho_{XZ}\sigma_X\sigma_Z = (0.5)(10)(20) = 100$$

$$\Sigma_{23} = \Sigma_{32} = \rho_{YZ}\sigma_Y\sigma_Z = (0.2)(15)(20) = 60$$

**Resulting Covariance Matrix:**

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} 100 & 45 & 100 \\ 45 & 225 & 60 \\ 100 & 60 & 400 \end{bmatrix}$$

(All entries expressed in $\%^2$.)

**Part (b) — Portfolio Variance via Matrix Formula:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}$:**

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix} 100 & 45 & 100 \\ 45 & 225 & 60 \\ 100 & 60 & 400 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Row 1:** $(100)(0.5) + (45)(0.3) + (100)(0.2) = 50 + 13.5 + 20 = 83.5$

**Row 2:** $(45)(0.5) + (225)(0.3) + (60)(0.2) = 22.5 + 67.5 + 12 = 102$

**Row 3:** $(100)(0.5) + (60)(0.3) + (400)(0.2) = 50 + 18 + 80 = 148$

So,

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix} 83.5 \\ 102 \\ 148 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathbf{w}^T(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w})$:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.3 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 83.5 \\ 102 \\ 148 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= (0.5)(83.5) + (0.3)(102) + (0.2)(148)$$

$$= 41.75 + 30.6 + 29.6 = 101.95$$

**Final Answer:** $\sigma_p^2 = 101.95\ \%^2$, hence $\sigma_p = \sqrt{101.95} \approx 10.10\%$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the two-asset variance formula — students often write $w_1w_2\rho\sigma_1\sigma_2$ instead of $2w_1w_2\rho\sigma_1\sigma_2$. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing covariance and correlation** during conversion — always remember $\Sigma_{ij} = \rho_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j$, **NOT** $\Sigma_{ij} = \rho_{ij}$ alone.
> 3. **Unit errors** — if standard deviations are in percent, variance is in $\%^2$. Take the square root at the end to return to percent.
> 4. **Weights not summing to 1** — KTU occasionally gives weights that violate this; re-check before computing. If invalid, normalise them first.
> 5. **Matrix multiplication order** — in $\mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$, $\mathbf{w}^T$ is $1 \times N$, $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is $N \times N$, $\mathbf{w}$ is $N \times 1$. Result is a scalar.
> 6. **Symmetric covariance matrix** — write the full matrix; examiners penalise incomplete (lower-triangular only) presentations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Correlation Matrix & Portfolio Variance**

- ☐ Correlation matrix **R** is symmetric, $N \times N$, with 1's on the diagonal and $\rho_{ij} \in [-1, +1]$ off-diagonal.
- ☐ Number of unique correlations = $\dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$.
- ☐ Covariance matrix entry: $\Sigma_{ij} = \rho_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j$ (with $\Sigma_{ii} = \sigma_i^2$).
- ☐ Two-asset portfolio variance: $\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2\sigma_1^2 + w_2^2\sigma_2^2 + 2w_1w_2\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2$.
- ☐ *N*-asset portfolio variance: $\sigma_p^2 = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} w_iw_j\text{Cov}(R_i,R_j) = \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$.
- ☐ Matrix form is **always preferable** for $N \geq 3$ — fewer arithmetic errors, more marks.
- ☐ Equal-weight special case: $\sigma_p^2 = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{N}\bigl[1 + (N-1)\bar{\rho}\bigr]$.
- ☐ As $N \to \infty$, $\sigma_p^2 \to \bar{\rho}\sigma^2$ — systematic risk floor.
- ☐ **Diversification works only when $\rho < 1$**; if $\rho = 1$, no benefit at all.
- ☐ **Negative correlations** give the strongest diversification.
- ☐ Always **state the units** ($\%^2$ for variance, % for standard deviation) — examiners reward this.
- ☐ Always **verify** $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{1} = 1$ before computing variance.
- ☐ Correlation matrix must be **positive semi-definite** — a valid correlation matrix has all eigenvalues $\geq 0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Value at Risk (VaR) and Its Computation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET635 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Value at Risk (VaR) and Its Computation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Value at Risk (VaR)** is a universally accepted quantitative measure used in financial risk management that estimates the **maximum potential loss** a portfolio (or a single financial position) could suffer over a defined **time horizon**, under normal market conditions, at a specified **confidence level** $\alpha$. Mathematically, VaR is formally defined as a negative quantile of the profit-and-loss (P&L) distribution. If $L$ denotes the random loss over the holding period and $F_L(\cdot)$ is its cumulative distribution function, then: $$\text{VaR}_{\alpha}(L) \;=\; \inf\{\,\ell \in \mathbb{R} \;:\; P(L \le \ell) \ge \alpha\,\} \;=\; -Q_{1-\alpha}(L)$$ Equivalently, VaR answers the question: *"What is the worst loss we should expect over the next $T$ days such that the probability of exceeding it is only $(1-\alpha)$?"* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Pillars of Every VaR Statement (KTU Hot-Spot)** > 1. **Confidence Level ($\alpha$)** — commonly **95%**, **99%**, or **99.9%** > 2. **Time Horizon ($T$)** — commonly **1 day** (trading desks), **10 days** (Basel II/III), or **1 month** > 3. **Loss Magnitude** — expressed in **currency units** (₹, $) or as a **percentage** of portfolio value ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are a **ship captain** sailing a cargo vessel across the Pacific Ocean with goods worth **₹10 crore**. Before every voyage, your insurance broker asks: *"On the worst stormy day, what is the maximum value of cargo that could be lost in a single day?"* You study 1,000 past voyages, sort the daily losses from smallest to largest, and observe that on 990 out of 1,000 days the loss did not exceed **₹1.2 lakh**. Therefore, with **99% confidence**, you declare: > *"I am 99% sure that my **Value at Risk** for tomorrow is ₹1.2 lakh."* That **₹1.2 lakh** *is* your VaR. The remaining **1% of days** are the catastrophic outliers that VaR does *not* cover (this gap is precisely what **Conditional VaR / Expected Shortfall** was invented to fix). > [!NOTE] > **Why VaR matters in real banking (Basel Context)** > The **Basel Committee on Banking Supervision** requires commercial banks to hold regulatory capital equal to at least $\text{VaR}_{99\%}$ over a **10-day horizon**. This makes VaR the cornerstone metric of modern market-risk regulation under **Basel II.5** and **Basel III**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Engineered Methods of VaR Computation VaR is *not* a single formula but a **family of methodologies**. The choice of method depends on the distributional assumptions the analyst is willing to make. ### Method 1 — Variance-Covariance (Parametric / Analytical) Method Assumes that portfolio returns are **jointly normally distributed**. The loss is therefore also normally distributed, and VaR reduces to a closed-form linear expression involving the **mean** $\mu$, the **standard deviation** $\sigma$, and the **standard normal quantile** $z_{\alpha}$. $$\boxed{\;\text{VaR}_{\alpha} \;=\; z_{\alpha}\,\sigma\,V \;-\; \mu\,V\;}$$ For a *zero-mean* return assumption (the most common simplification in KTU problems): $$\boxed{\;\text{VaR}_{\alpha} \;=\; z_{\alpha}\,\sigma\,V\;}$$ where: - $z_{\alpha}$ = one-tailed standard normal quantile at confidence $\alpha$ - $\sigma$ = daily (or horizon-matched) standard deviation of returns - $V$ = current market value of the position ### Method 2 — Historical Simulation Method A **non-parametric, distribution-free** approach. Uses the empirical distribution of *actual past returns* — no normality assumption is required. **Algorithm:** 1. Collect a historical window of $N$ daily P&L observations (commonly $N=250$ or $N=500$). 2. Sort the P&L values in ascending order (worst loss first). 3. Locate the observation at rank $k = (1-\alpha)\,N$. 4. Report the corresponding loss (in absolute value) as $\text{VaR}_{\alpha}$. $$\boxed{\;\text{VaR}_{\alpha}^{\text{Hist}} \;=\; -\,\Pi_{(k)} \quad\text{where}\quad k = \lceil (1-\alpha)\,N \rceil\;}$$ ### Method 3 — Monte Carlo Simulation Method The **most flexible** (and computationally expensive) method. Generates thousands of *synthetic* future scenarios using stochastic models (Geometric Brownian Motion, GARCH, copulas, etc.) and computes the empirical quantile of simulated P&L. **Algorithm:** 1. Specify a stochastic model for risk factors (e.g., GBM: $dS_t = \mu S_t\,dt + \sigma S_t\,dW_t$). 2. Simulate $M$ future paths ($M = 10{,}000$ is typical). 3. Re-price the portfolio under each simulated scenario to obtain a P&L distribution. 4. Take the empirical $(1-\alpha)$-quantile of the simulated P&L distribution. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Symbols & Units | Typical Numeric Value | |---|---------|----------------------|-----------------|----------------------| | 1 | Single-Asset Parametric VaR | $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = z_{\alpha}\,\sigma\,V$ | $V$ in ₹/$, $\sigma$ in % | — | | 2 | Adjusted for mean drift | $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = z_{\alpha}\,\sigma\,V - \mu V$ | $\mu$ = mean daily return | — | | 3 | Two-Asset Portfolio VaR | $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = z_{\alpha}\,V\,\sqrt{w_1^2\sigma_1^2 + w_2^2\sigma_2^2 + 2w_1w_2\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2}$ | $w_i$ = weight, $\rho$ = correlation | — | | 4 | $N$-Asset Matrix Form | $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = z_{\alpha}\,\sqrt{\mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w}} \cdot V$ | $\Sigma$ = covariance matrix | — | | 5 | Time-Scaling Rule (i.i.d. normal) | $\text{VaR}_{\alpha}(T) = \text{VaR}_{\alpha}(1)\,\sqrt{T}$ | $T$ = horizon in days | $\sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$ | | 6 | Z-score at 90% confidence | $z_{0.90} = 1.282$ | — | $1.282$ | | 7 | Z-score at 95% confidence | $z_{0.95} = 1.645$ | — | $1.645$ | | 8 | Z-score at 99% confidence | $z_{0.99} = 2.326$ | — | $2.326$ | | 9 | Z-score at 99.9% confidence | $z_{0.999} = 3.090$ | — | $3.090$ | | 10 | Historical VaR (rank rule) | $k = \lceil(1-\alpha)N\rceil$ | $N$ = sample size | e.g. $k=5$ at $\alpha=99\%, N=500$ | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry / Field | Practical Use of VaR | |------------------|----------------------| | Commercial Banks (Basel II/III) | Setting **regulatory market-risk capital** | | Hedge Funds | Daily **risk-budgeting** and **leverage limits** | | Insurance / Re-insurance | **Catastrophe risk** aggregation | | Corporate Treasuries | **FX & commodity exposure** hedging | | Central Banks | **Macro-prudential surveillance** of systemic risk | > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Property — Sub-Additivity Failure** > Naive VaR is **NOT sub-additive**: $\text{VaR}(A+B) \le \text{VaR}(A) + \text{VaR}(B)$ can *violate* this. This means **diversification may appear to *increase* VaR**, which is a famous theoretical weakness and the main reason **CVaR** was developed as an alternative coherent risk measure (Rockafellar & Uryasev, 2000). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Parametric VaR for a Single Asset ### Step 1 — Model the Return Distribution Let $R$ be the daily simple return of an asset over a horizon. Under the parametric assumption, $R \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^{2})$. The dollar P&L on a position of current value $V$ is: $$\Pi \;=\; V\,(R - R_0)$$ For a "buy-and-hold" position marked-to-market, the loss $L$ in dollar terms is: $$L \;=\; -\,V\,(R)$$ where we treat $R$ itself (centered around its mean) as the dollar-return random variable relative to today's value. ### Step 2 — Derive the Quantile Because $L$ is a normal random variable (linear transformation of a normal), the $(1-\alpha)$ quantile of $L$ is: $$Q_{1-\alpha}(L) \;=\; -\,\mu\,V \;-\; z_{1-\alpha}\,\sigma\,V$$ By symmetry of the standard normal distribution, $z_{1-\alpha} = -z_{\alpha}$, so: $$Q_{1-\alpha}(L) \;=\; -\,\mu\,V \;+\; z_{\alpha}\,\sigma\,V$$ ### Step 3 — Apply the VaR Definition By definition, $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = -Q_{1-\alpha}(L)$: $$\text{VaR}_{\alpha} \;=\; \mu\,V \;-\; z_{\alpha}\,\sigma\,V$$ ### Step 4 — Common Simplification (Zero-Mean Case) For very short horizons (daily), $\mu \approx 0$, so the formula collapses to the famous textbook expression: $$\boxed{\;\text{VaR}_{\alpha} \;=\; z_{\alpha}\,\sigma\,V\;}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Portfolio VaR Using the Covariance Matrix Consider an $N$-asset portfolio with weight vector $\mathbf{w} = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N)^{T}$ (where $\sum w_i = 1$) and asset return covariance matrix $\Sigma$ of dimension $N \times N$. ### Step 1 — Portfolio Variance By standard portfolio theory, the variance of the portfolio return $R_p = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}$ is: $$\sigma_p^{2} \;=\; \mathbf{w}^{T}\Sigma\,\mathbf{w} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_i\,w_j\,\sigma_{ij}$$ ### Step 2 — Portfolio Standard Deviation $$\sigma_p \;=\; \sqrt{\mathbf{w}^{T}\Sigma\,\mathbf{w}}$$ ### Step 3 — Convert to Dollar Loss If the portfolio value is $V$, then the dollar standard deviation is $V\sigma_p$. Applying the parametric VaR formula: $$\boxed{\;\text{VaR}_{\alpha}^{\text{Portfolio}} \;=\; z_{\alpha}\,V\,\sqrt{\mathbf{w}^{T}\Sigma\,\mathbf{w}}\;}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time-Scaling (Square-Root-of-Time Rule) If daily returns are i.i.d. normal with volatility $\sigma_{\text{daily}}$, then the $T$-day return variance is $T\,\sigma_{\text{daily}}^{2}$, so the $T$-day standard deviation is $\sigma_{\text{daily}}\sqrt{T}$. Therefore: $$\boxed{\;\text{VaR}_{\alpha}(T\text{-day}) \;=\; \text{VaR}_{\alpha}(1\text{-day})\,\sqrt{T}\;}$$ This rule is widely used in Basel II to convert a 1-day VaR estimate into the regulatory 10-day VaR. --- ## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example — KTU Pattern > **Problem Statement (KTU Typical):** > A portfolio has a current market value of **₹1,00,00,000** (₹1 crore). The daily volatility of the portfolio is **1.5%**. Assuming returns are normally distributed with zero mean, compute: > (i) The 1-day 95% VaR. > (ii) The 1-day 99% VaR. > (iii) The 10-day 99% VaR using the square-root-of-time rule. > (iv) The interpretation in plain English. ### Solution **Given:** - $V = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000$ (₹1 crore) - $\sigma_{\text{daily}} = 0.015$ (i.e., 1.5%) - $\mu \approx 0$ #### Part (i): 1-day VaR at 95% confidence $$ \begin{aligned} \text{VaR}_{0.95}^{(1)} &= z_{0.95} \cdot \sigma \cdot V \\ &= 1.645 \times 0.015 \times 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \\ &= 1.645 \times 1{,}50{,}000 \\ &= \text{₹ }2{,}46{,}750 \end{aligned} $$ #### Part (ii): 1-day VaR at 99% confidence $$ \begin{aligned} \text{VaR}_{0.99}^{(1)} &= z_{0.99} \cdot \sigma \cdot V \\ &= 2.326 \times 0.015 \times 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \\ &= 2.326 \times 1{,}50{,}000 \\ &= \text{₹ }3{,}48{,}900 \end{aligned} $$ #### Part (iii): 10-day VaR at 99% confidence $$ \begin{aligned} \text{VaR}_{0.99}^{(10)} &= \text{VaR}_{0.99}^{(1)} \times \sqrt{10} \\ &= 3{,}48{,}900 \times 3.1623 \\ &= \text{₹ }11{,}03{,}128.47 \end{aligned} $$ #### Part (iv): Plain-English Interpretation > "With **95% confidence**, the portfolio is **not expected to lose more than ₹2,46,750** in a single day. With **99% confidence**, the daily loss will not exceed ₹3,48,900. Over **10 trading days**, the 99%-VaR rises to roughly ₹11.03 lakh, reflecting the accumulation of risk with longer holding horizons. The remaining 1% of trading days (i.e., about 2–3 days per year) may exhibit losses exceeding these limits." --- ## 3.5 Worked Example — Two-Asset Portfolio VaR > **Problem:** > A ₹50 lakh portfolio is split as 60% in Asset A ($\sigma_A = 2\%$ daily) and 40% in Asset B ($\sigma_B = 1.5\%$ daily). The correlation between A and B is $\rho_{AB} = 0.4$. Compute the 99% 1-day VaR. ### Solution **Step 1 — Compute portfolio variance** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_p^{2} &= w_A^{2}\sigma_A^{2} + w_B^{2}\sigma_B^{2} + 2\,w_A\,w_B\,\rho_{AB}\,\sigma_A\,\sigma_B \\ &= (0.6)^{2}(0.02)^{2} + (0.4)^{2}(0.015)^{2} + 2(0.6)(0.4)(0.4)(0.02)(0.015) \\ &= 0.36 \times 0.0004 \;+\; 0.16 \times 0.000225 \;+\; 0.48 \times 0.000012 \\ &= 0.0001440 \;+\; 0.0000360 \;+\; 0.00000576 \\ &= 0.00018576 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute portfolio standard deviation** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_p &= \sqrt{0.00018576} \\ &= 0.01363 \;\;(\text{i.e. } 1.363\% \text{ daily}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute 99% VaR** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{VaR}_{0.99} &= z_{0.99} \cdot \sigma_p \cdot V \\ &= 2.326 \times 0.01363 \times 50{,}00{,}000 \\ &= 2.326 \times 68{,}150 \\ &= \text{₹ }1{,}58{,}517 \;\;(\text{approximately}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Sanity-check the diversification benefit** If the assets were perfectly correlated ($\rho = 1$), the portfolio std would be: $\sigma_p^{\text{uncorr}} = \sqrt{(0.6 \cdot 0.02)^{2} + (0.4 \cdot 0.015)^{2}} = \sqrt{0.000144 + 0.000036} = \sqrt{0.000180} \approx 0.01342$ Wait — note that even $\rho = 0.4$ yields only marginally lower risk than $\rho = 1$ here. This is precisely the **diversification effect** of low correlations, and is why VaR is so sensitive to the correlation matrix. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — VaR Computation Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Portfolio Data Input
Holdings and Weights] --> B[Choose VaR Methodology] B --> C1[Method 1
Parametric
Variance Covariance] B --> C2[Method 2
Historical
Simulation] B --> C3[Method 3
Monte Carlo
Simulation] C1 --> D1[Estimate Mean and Covariance
Matrix Sigma from Data] C2 --> D2[Collect N Past Returns
Sort Ascending] C3 --> D3[Specify Stochastic Model
GBM or GARCH] D1 --> E1[Apply VaR equals z alpha times
sqrt of wT Sigma w times V] D2 --> E2[Locate Quantile at Rank
k equals 1 minus alpha times N] D3 --> E3[Simulate M equals 10000 paths
and revalue portfolio] E1 --> F[VaR Estimate in Currency] E2 --> F[VaR Estimate in Currency] E3 --> F[VaR Estimate in Currency] F --> G[Compare with Regulatory
Capital Limits] G --> H[Allocate Capital and
Set Risk Limits] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Input → Processing → Output ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Input Layer] P1[Position Database] P2[Historical Price Series] P3[Correlation Estimates] end subgraph PROCESS[Processing Layer] Q1[Statistical Engine
Computes mean sigma rho] Q2[Scenario Generator
Historical or MC] Q3[Quantile Solver
Finds 1 minus alpha percentile] end subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer] R1[VaR Number in INR] R2[Backtesting Report] R3[Capital Allocation Decision] end P1 --> Q1 P2 --> Q1 P3 --> Q1 Q1 --> Q2 Q2 --> Q3 Q3 --> R1 R1 --> R2 R1 --> R3 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which VaR Method Should You Choose? ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Start] --> T{Is Return
Distribution
Approximately Normal?} T -- Yes --> U[Use Parametric
Variance Covariance Method] T -- No --> V{Is Historical
Data Available
with 250 plus Days?} V -- Yes --> W[Use Historical
Simulation Method] V -- No --> X[Use Monte Carlo
Simulation Method] U --> Y[Output: Closed Form VaR] W --> Y X --> Y ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading these diagrams in the KTU exam:** Examiners often award marks for *flowchart-style* explanations of the VaR workflow. Memorising the three-stage structure **(Input → Statistical Engine → Quantile Selection)** is a guaranteed scoring trick. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **Define Value at Risk (VaR). State and briefly explain the three parameters that completely specify any VaR estimate.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Value at Risk (VaR):** It is a statistical measure of the *maximum potential loss* in the value of a portfolio over a defined period, at a given confidence level, under normal market conditions. **Three essential parameters (1 mark each):** 1. **Confidence Level ($\alpha$):** The probability that the actual loss will not exceed the VaR. Typical values are 95%, 99%, or 99.9%. 2. **Time Horizon ($T$):** The period over which the loss is measured. For trading desks, it is typically **1 day**; under **Basel II/III** norms for banks, it is **10 days**. 3. **Loss Magnitude:** The VaR value itself, expressed either in currency units (₹, $) or as a percentage of the portfolio's current market value. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]` **Distinguish between the Variance-Covariance method and the Historical Simulation method of computing VaR. Mention one advantage of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Variance-Covariance | Historical Simulation | |--------|---------------------|------------------------| | Assumption | Returns are **jointly normal** | **No distribution** assumption — non-parametric | | Data Need | Mean vector and covariance matrix $\Sigma$ | Empirical past returns | | Output | **Closed-form** formula | **Empirical quantile** | | Speed | Extremely fast | Slower for large $N$ | - **Advantage of Variance-Covariance (1 mark):** Analytical simplicity, real-time computability. - **Advantage of Historical Simulation (1 mark):** Captures **fat tails**, skewness, and empirical crisis events (e.g., 2008 crash) without parametric distortion. --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part-B question is internally split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The examiner's valuation key gives 7 marks per sub-part, with a step-marker of 1 mark per major logical transition. --- ### **Question A — Alternative 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **A portfolio has a current value of ₹5,00,00,000. It consists of two stocks:** - **Stock X:** weight $w_X = 0.6$, daily volatility $\sigma_X = 2.0\%$ - **Stock Y:** weight $w_Y = 0.4$, daily volatility $\sigma_Y = 1.5\%$ - **Correlation:** $\rho_{XY} = 0.3$ **Compute:** **(a)** The daily 95% VaR and 99% VaR of the portfolio using the parametric method. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The 10-day 99% VaR using the square-root-of-time rule. Also interpret all results. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question A:** ##### **Part (a) — Daily VaR** (7 marks) **Step 1 — Compute portfolio variance** (2 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_p^{2} &= w_X^{2}\,\sigma_X^{2} \;+\; w_Y^{2}\,\sigma_Y^{2} \;+\; 2\,w_X\,w_Y\,\rho_{XY}\,\sigma_X\,\sigma_Y \\ &= (0.6)^{2}(0.02)^{2} \;+\; (0.4)^{2}(0.015)^{2} \;+\; 2(0.6)(0.4)(0.3)(0.02)(0.015) \\ &= 0.36 \times 0.0004 \;+\; 0.16 \times 0.000225 \;+\; 0.144 \times 0.000012 \\ &= 0.0001440 \;+\; 0.0000360 \;+\; 0.000001728 \\ &= 0.000181728 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute portfolio standard deviation** (1 mark) $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_p &= \sqrt{0.000181728} \\ &= 0.013481 \;\;(\text{approximately } 1.3481\%) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute 95% VaR** (2 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{VaR}_{0.95} &= z_{0.95} \cdot \sigma_p \cdot V \\ &= 1.645 \times 0.013481 \times 5{,}00{,}00{,}000 \\ &= 1.645 \times 67{,}405 \\ &= \text{₹ }1{,}10{,}881.23 \;\;(\text{approximately ₹ }1{,}10{,}881) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute 99% VaR** (2 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{VaR}_{0.99} &= z_{0.99} \cdot \sigma_p \cdot V \\ &= 2.326 \times 0.013481 \times 5{,}00{,}00{,}000 \\ &= 2.326 \times 67{,}405 \\ &= \text{₹ }1{,}56{,}784.03 \;\;(\text{approximately ₹ }1{,}56{,}784) \end{aligned} $$ --- ##### **Part (b) — 10-day VaR + Interpretation** (7 marks) **Step 1 — Apply square-root-of-time scaling** (3 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{VaR}_{0.99}^{(10)} &= \text{VaR}_{0.99}^{(1)} \times \sqrt{10} \\ &= 1{,}56{,}784 \times 3.1623 \\ &= \text{₹ }4{,}95{,}727.55 \;\;(\text{approximately ₹ }4{,}95{,}728}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Interpretation** (4 marks) - *95% 1-day VaR = ₹1,10,881:* On any given day, there is a **95% probability** that the portfolio will not lose more than ₹1,10,881. In simpler terms, on roughly **19 out of every 20 trading days**, the loss will be within this limit. - *99% 1-day VaR = ₹1,56,784:* Raising confidence to 99% increases the VaR to ₹1,56,784. The additional loss coverage (~₹46,000) reflects the **tail risk** that exists in 1 out of 100 trading days. - *10-day 99% VaR = ₹4,95,728:* Holding the portfolio over a 10-trading-day horizon (≈ 2 weeks) at 99% confidence, the maximum expected loss grows by a factor of $\sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$ — a direct consequence of the **square-root-of-time rule**. - *Regulatory implication:* A bank holding this portfolio would need to set aside at least ₹4,95,728 as market-risk capital under Basel norms. --- ### **Question B — Alternative 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **three methods of computing VaR** in detail. Mention the formulas used and state one limitation of each method. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A ₹2,00,00,000 portfolio has a daily mean return $\mu = 0.05\%$ and daily volatility $\sigma = 1.2\%$. Compute the 1-day 95% VaR **including the mean drift**. Also, compute the 99% 1-day VaR and the 5-day 99% VaR. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question B:** ##### **Part (a) — Three Methods of VaR Computation** (7 marks) **1. Variance-Covariance (Parametric) Method** (2.5 marks) - **Idea:** Assumes returns follow a multivariate normal distribution. - **Formula:** $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = z_{\alpha}\,\sigma\,V$ (single asset), $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = z_{\alpha}\,V\sqrt{\mathbf{w}^{T}\Sigma\mathbf{w}}$ (portfolio). - **Limitation:** Fails to capture **fat tails**, **skewness**, and **kurtosis** — actual financial returns exhibit much heavier tails than the normal distribution, leading to under-estimation of extreme losses. **2. Historical Simulation Method** (2.5 marks) - **Idea:** Uses actual historical returns to construct the empirical P&L distribution; no distributional assumption. - **Formula:** $\text{VaR}_{\alpha}^{\text{Hist}} = -\Pi_{k}$ where $k = \lceil(1-\alpha)N\rceil$. - **Limitation:** **Backward-looking** — assumes that future risk dynamics mirror the past; fails during **regime shifts** (e.g., 2008 financial crisis). Also highly sensitive to the **length of the historical window**. **3. Monte Carlo Simulation Method** (2 marks) - **Idea:** Generates thousands of synthetic scenarios using a stochastic model (e.g., GBM: $dS_t = \mu S_t dt + \sigma S_t dW_t$) and computes the empirical quantile of the simulated P&L distribution. - **Limitation:** **Computationally expensive** (requires generating 10,000+ paths), and its accuracy depends entirely on the correctness of the *assumed* stochastic model — model risk is intrinsic. --- ##### **Part (b) — Parametric VaR with Drift** (7 marks) **Given:** - $V = ₹2{,}00{,}00{,}000$ - $\mu = 0.0005$ (0.05% per day) - $\sigma = 0.012$ (1.2% per day) **Step 1 — General formula with drift** (1 mark) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{VaR}_{\alpha} = z_{\alpha}\,\sigma\,V - \mu\,V \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — 95% 1-day VaR with drift** (2 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{VaR}_{0.95} &= (1.645)(0.012)(2{,}00{,}00{,}000) - (0.0005)(2{,}00{,}00{,}000) \\ &= 1.645 \times 2{,}40{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 \\ &= 3{,}94{,}800 - 1{,}00{,}000 \\ &= \text{₹ }2{,}94{,}800 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — 99% 1-day VaR with drift** (2 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{VaR}_{0.99} &= (2.326)(0.012)(2{,}00{,}00{,}000) - (0.0005)(2{,}00{,}00{,}000) \\ &= 2.326 \times 2{,}40{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 \\ &= 5{,}58{,}240 - 1{,}00{,}000 \\ &= \text{₹ }4{,}58{,}240 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — 5-day 99% VaR using square-root-of-time** (2 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{VaR}_{0.99}^{(5)} &= \text{VaR}_{0.99}^{(1)} \times \sqrt{5} \\ &= 4{,}58{,}240 \times 2.2361 \\ &= \text{₹ }10{,}24{,}659.06 \;\;(\text{approximately ₹ }10{,}24{,}659}) \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Most Common Mark-Loss Areas** > > 1. **Forgetting the drift term $\mu V$:** When $\mu \neq 0$ is *explicitly given*, omitting it costs you **2 full marks**. Always check if the problem says "zero mean" or gives a mean. > 2. **Wrong $z$-score:** Mixing up $z_{0.95} = 1.645$ with $z_{0.99} = 2.326$ is a recurring error. **Memorise this table** — it is worth 1 mark by itself. > 3. **Forgetting to scale for time horizon:** If the question asks for 10-day VaR but you write the 1-day answer, you will lose 2-3 marks. > 4. **Inverting the formula:** Writing $\text{VaR} = V - z\sigma$ instead of $\text{VaR} = z\sigma V$ is a **conceptual confusion** that examiners *immediately* flag. > 5. **Not showing the portfolio-variance expansion:** For 2-asset questions, examiners expect the full expansion of $\mathbf{w}^{T}\Sigma\mathbf{w}$ — not just the final answer. Skip steps = lose marks. > 6. **Unit confusion:** Always express VaR in **rupees (₹)**, not percentages, when portfolio value is given. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **VaR** = *maximum* loss at confidence $\alpha$ over horizon $T$ — not the *expected* loss. - ✅ The **three pillars** of VaR are: **confidence level**, **time horizon**, **loss magnitude**. - ✅ The **parametric formula** is $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = z_{\alpha}\,\sigma\,V$ (zero drift) or $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = z_{\alpha}\,\sigma\,V - \mu V$ (with drift). - ✅ The **portfolio formula** is $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = z_{\alpha}\,V\sqrt{\mathbf{w}^{T}\Sigma\mathbf{w}}$. - ✅ The **square-root-of-time rule**: $\text{VaR}(T) = \text{VaR}(1)\sqrt{T}$ holds *only* under i.i.d. normal returns. - ✅ **Z-scores** to memorise: $z_{0.90}=1.282$, $z_{0.95}=1.645$, $z_{0.99}=2.326$, $z_{0.999}=3.090$. - ✅ **Historical VaR** uses the empirical quantile at rank $k = \lceil(1-\alpha)N\rceil$. - ✅ **Monte Carlo VaR** is the most flexible but computationally the most expensive. - ✅ VaR is **NOT sub-additive** — diversification can paradoxically increase VaR for non-normal distributions. - ✅ **Basel II/III** uses $\text{VaR}_{99\%}$ over a **10-day** horizon for regulatory capital. - ✅ Always quote VaR with all three components: *"VaR is ₹X at 99% over 10 days."* - ✅ Watch out for **fat tails** in real markets — pure normal-parametric VaR under-estimates tail losses. - ✅ **Conditional VaR (CVaR / Expected Shortfall)** is the *coherent* alternative that fixes VaR's sub-additivity flaw.

topics.title: Futures and Options
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET635 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Futures and Options

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Financial Derivatives – The Foundational Concept

A **derivative** is a financial contract whose value is *derived* from an underlying asset (equity stock, commodity, currency, index, or interest rate). Derivatives are zero-sum contracts: one party's gain is precisely the other party's loss, net of transaction costs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Syllabus Weightage):** "A derivative is a financial instrument whose payoff depends on the value of another variable, called the *underlying* (or *underlying asset*)."

**Two Principal Categories under Study (Module 3 Focus):**
1. **Futures Contracts** – Obligation-based (binding commitment).
2. **Options Contracts** – Right-based (discretionary exercise).

---

### 1.2 Futures Contracts – Formal Definition

A **futures contract** is a standardized, exchange-traded agreement between two parties to *buy or sell* an underlying asset at a predetermined **futures price (F)** on a specified **delivery/expiration date (T)**. Both parties are **obligated** to honour the contract.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Properties of Futures Contracts:**
> - **Standardized:** Contract size, expiry, and quality are fixed by the exchange (e.g., NSE, CME).
> - **Marked-to-Market (MTM):** Daily settlement of gains/losses.
> - **Clearing House Guarantee:** Counterparty risk is virtually eliminated.
> - **Highly Leveraged:** Only a small *initial margin* (typically 5%–10% of contract value) is required.

---

### 1.3 Options Contracts – Formal Definition

An **options contract** is a derivative that gives the holder the **right, but not the obligation**, to buy (Call) or sell (Put) the underlying asset at a specified **strike price (K)** on or before the **expiration date (T)**. The buyer pays a non-refundable premium for this right.

**Two Fundamental Types:**
- **Call Option (C):** Right to *BUY* the underlying at strike K.
- **Put Option (P):** Right to *SELL* the underlying at strike K.

---

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

**Analogy 1 — Futures as a "Pre-booked Uber Ride":**
Imagine you book an Uber today for travel on a specific future date at a fixed fare. Regardless of whether the actual fare on that day rises to ₹500 or falls to ₹100, the Uber driver *must* drive you (you *must* ride). You cannot back out. This is a **futures contract** — bilateral obligation, price-locked in advance.

**Analogy 2 — Options as a "Rain Insurance Policy":**
You pay a small premium to an insurance company that promises to compensate you *if it rains* on a specified day. If it rains → you claim the payout. If it doesn't rain → you simply lose the premium, with no further obligation. You are *protected* but *not forced* to claim. This is a **put option** (hedging against a fall in price of an asset you own).

**Analogy 3 — Call Option as a "Property Booking Token":**
You pay a ₹10,000 token to reserve the right to buy a flat at ₹50 lakhs within 3 months. If property prices rise to ₹60 lakhs → you buy at ₹50 lakhs (profit). If prices fall to ₹40 lakhs → you walk away, losing only the token. This is a **call option**.

---

### 1.5 Critical Terminology Anchors

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Vocabulary for KTU Board Examinations:**
>
> | Term | Meaning |
> |------|---------|
> | **Spot Price (S)** | Current market price of the underlying asset. |
> | **Futures Price (F)** | Agreed price for future delivery in a futures contract. |
> | **Strike Price (K / X)** | Pre-set price at which an option can be exercised. |
> | **Premium** | Price paid by option buyer to option writer. |
> | **Intrinsic Value** | Immediate exercise worth of an option. |
> | **Time Value** | Premium above intrinsic value, reflecting probability of favourable move. |
> | **Moneyness** | Classification of an option as ITM, ATM, or OTM. |
> | **Underlying** | Asset on which the derivative is based. |
> | **Expiry (T)** | Date the contract becomes void. |
> | **Volatility (σ)** | Annualized standard deviation of log-returns of underlying. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanics of a Futures Contract — Operational Walkthrough

**Step 1: Contract Initiation**
Two parties agree to a standardized contract (e.g., 1 lot = 50 shares of RELIANCE on NSE) at a mutually agreed futures price *F₀* on trade date *t = 0*.

**Step 2: Initial Margin Posting**
Both parties deposit *initial margin* (IM) — typically 5%–10% of contract notional value — with the clearing house. This is **not a payment** between the two parties; it is a good-faith security deposit.

**Step 3: Daily Mark-to-Market (MTM)**
At the close of each trading day, the futures settlement price *F₁, F₂, …, Fₙ* is determined. The clearing house computes the **daily settlement amount**:

$$
\text{Daily MTM} = (\text{Settlement Price}_t - \text{Settlement Price}_{t-1}) \times \text{Contract Size}
$$

- A **gain** is credited to the trader's margin account.
- A **loss** is *debited* — and if the margin falls below the **maintenance margin**, a **margin call** is issued.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MTM?** MTM prevents any loss from accumulating into an unmanageable default. This is the single most important risk-management innovation in derivatives history (post-1987 crash reforms).

**Step 4: Final Settlement**
On expiry date T, two alternatives:
- **Cash Settlement:** Difference between *F_T* and *F₀* is exchanged. Common for index futures (NIFTY, S&P 500).
- **Physical Delivery:** Actual asset is delivered. Common for commodity futures (crude oil, wheat, gold).

---

### 2.2 Forward vs. Futures — A KTU-Favourite Comparison

| Feature | Forward Contract | Futures Contract |
|---------|------------------|------------------|
| Trading Venue | OTC (over-the-counter) | Exchange-traded |
| Standardization | Customizable | Strictly standardized |
| Counterparty Risk | High | Negligible (clearing house) |
| Settlement | Single payment at maturity | Daily MTM |
| Liquidity | Low | High |
| Regulation | Minimal | Heavily regulated (SEBI, SEC, CFTC) |
| Default Possibility | Significant | Practically zero |

---

### 2.3 Options — The Four Payoff Functions

For a European option held until expiry, on an underlying of spot price *S_T* and strike *K*:

$$
\text{Call Payoff} = \max(S_T - K,\, 0)
$$

$$
\text{Put Payoff} = \max(K - S_T,\, 0)
$$

For an **American option**, the same logic applies, but exercise can occur at *any* time *t ≤ T*.

The **Profit (P/L)** for the **buyer** is:

$$
\text{Buyer Profit} = \text{Payoff} - \text{Premium Paid}
$$

The **writer's (seller's) profit** is the mirror image:

$$
\text{Writer Profit} = \text{Premium Received} - \text{Payoff}
$$

---

### 2.4 Option Moneyness Classification (Critical for KTU Theory Questions)

Let *S* = current spot price, *K* = strike price.

| Option Type | In-the-Money (ITM) | At-the-Money (ATM) | Out-of-the-Money (OTM) |
|-------------|---------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| **Call**    | S > K               | S = K               | S < K                    |
| **Put**     | S < K               | S = K               | S > K                    |

**Intrinsic Value Definitions:**

$$
\text{Call Intrinsic Value} = \max(S - K,\, 0)
$$

$$
\text{Put Intrinsic Value} = \max(K - S,\, 0)
$$

**Time Value of an Option:**

$$
\text{Time Value} = \text{Option Premium} - \text{Intrinsic Value}
$$

Time value is *always ≥ 0* and decays toward zero as expiry approaches (a phenomenon formalized as **theta decay**).

---

### 2.5 The Cost-of-Carry Model for Futures Pricing

Under the **no-arbitrage principle**, the theoretical futures price of an asset paying no income is:

$$
F_0 = S_0 \, e^{rT}
$$

For an asset paying a *known* dividend yield *q* or discrete income *I*:

$$
F_0 = (S_0 - I) \, e^{rT}
$$

$$
F_0 = S_0 \, e^{(r - q)T}
$$

where *r* is the risk-free rate (annualized, continuous compounding) and *T* is time-to-maturity in years.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Analogy:** This formula is mathematically identical to computing the future value of a present sum under continuous compounding in engineering economics — a direct cross-domain application familiar to all KTU engineers.

---

### 2.6 Put–Call Parity (P-C Parity) — A Foundational Identity

The single most-tested relationship in KTU options problems:

$$
C + K \, e^{-rT} = P + S_0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A *protective put* (long stock + long put) replicates the payoff of a *fiduciary call* (long call + risk-free bond). Both portfolios must have identical prices in equilibrium, or risk-free arbitrage exists.

**Re-arranged Solved Forms:**

$$
C = P + S_0 - K \, e^{-rT}
$$

$$
P = C + K \, e^{-rT} - S_0
$$

$$
S_0 = C - P + K \, e^{-rT}
$$

---

### 2.7 The Black–Scholes–Merton (BSM) Formula

For a European call option on a non-dividend-paying stock:

$$
C = S_0 \, N(d_1) - K \, e^{-rT} \, N(d_2)
$$

For a European put option (or via P-C parity):

$$
P = K \, e^{-rT} \, N(-d_2) - S_0 \, N(-d_1)
$$

where the **standardized variables** *d₁* and *d₂* are:

$$
d_1 = \frac{\ln(S_0 / K) + \left(r + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}\right) T}{\sigma \sqrt{T}}
$$

$$
d_2 = d_1 - \sigma \sqrt{T} = \frac{\ln(S_0 / K) + \left(r - \frac{\sigma^2}{2}\right) T}{\sigma \sqrt{T}}
$$

The function *N(·)* is the **cumulative standard normal distribution function**, interpretable as a risk-adjusted, probability-like measure.

---

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Domain of Validity |
|--------------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Futures Price (No Income) | $F_0 = S_0 \, e^{rT}$ | r = annual risk-free rate; T = years | European-style, no dividends |
| Futures Price (with div yield) | $F_0 = S_0 \, e^{(r-q)T}$ | q = continuous dividend yield | Continuous dividends |
| Call Payoff | $\max(S_T - K,\, 0)$ | Currency units | Long position |
| Put Payoff | $\max(K - S_T,\, 0)$ | Currency units | Long position |
| Call Intrinsic Value | $\max(S - K,\, 0)$ | Currency units | t = 0 valuation |
| Put Intrinsic Value | $\max(K - S,\, 0)$ | Currency units | t = 0 valuation |
| Put–Call Parity | $C + K e^{-rT} = P + S_0$ | European options, same K, T | No-arbitrage equilibrium |
| Black–Scholes *d₁* | $\frac{\ln(S/K) + (r + \sigma^2/2)T}{\sigma \sqrt{T}}$ | σ = annualized volatility | European, no dividends |
| Black–Scholes *d₂* | $d_1 - \sigma \sqrt{T}$ | Same as above | Derived quantity |
| BSM Call Price | $S_0 N(d_1) - K e^{-rT} N(d_2)$ | $N(\cdot)$ = Std Normal CDF | European, non-dividend stock |
| BSM Put Price | $K e^{-rT} N(-d_2) - S_0 N(-d_1)$ | Same as above | European, non-dividend stock |
| Hedge Ratio (Delta, Call) | $N(d_1)$ | Dimensionless [0,1] | BSM framework |
| Hedge Ratio (Delta, Put) | $N(d_1) - 1$ | Dimensionless [-1,0] | BSM framework |
| Daily MTM | $(F_t - F_{t-1}) \times \text{Contract Size}$ | Currency per day | Exchange-traded futures |
| Time Value | Premium $-$ Intrinsic Value | Currency units | Always ≥ 0 |

---

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering & Economic Utility

- **Risk Management:** Airlines hedge jet-fuel costs using crude oil futures; farmers hedge crop prices using agricultural futures; importers hedge FX exposure using currency futures.
- **Speculation:** Traders take leveraged bets on directional moves (e.g., buying NIFTY futures expecting a rally).
- **Arbitrage:** Quants exploit mispricings between spot and futures (cash-and-carry arbitrage) or between call and put prices (P-C parity violations).
- **Engineering Project Valuation:** Real options theory (a direct extension of financial options) is used to value flexibility in capital projects — e.g., the option to *defer* a power plant construction, or the option to *abandon* a mining project.
