**Interpretation:** The over-hedge (beta > 1) turned a market decline into a profit because the fund had a 15% extra short position. If the manager had used beta = 1, the hedge would be perfect.

(7 marks: 1.5 for contract calc, 1.5 for portfolio loss, 1.5 for futures P&L, 1.5 for net P&L, 1 for interpretation)

---

**(b) [7 marks] Solution:**

**Role of MCX in Indian Commodity Risk Management:**

1. **Turmeric on NCDEX (2 marks):** Indian turmeric farmers in **Erode (Tamil Nadu)** and **Sangli (Maharashtra)** face extreme price volatility (₹6,000 to ₹14,000 per quintal in 2022-2023). NCDEX turmeric futures allow farmers/FPOs (Farmer Producer Organisations) to **lock in pre-sowing prices**, eliminating post-harvest distress sales.

2. **Gold on MCX (2 marks):** Indian jewellery demand consumes **~700-800 tonnes of gold annually**. MCX gold futures (1 kg and 100 g mini) allow jewellers in **Zaveri Bazaar (Mumbai)** and **Johari Bazaar (Jaipur)** to hedge against overnight price crashes. Banks use MCX gold to hedge their **gold loan portfolio** (~₹2.5 lakh crore).

3. **Copper on MCX (1.5 marks):** Indian cable manufacturers and the **Hindalco/Vedanta** aluminium-copper complex use MCX copper futures to hedge raw material cost. Copper prices have ranged from ₹600 to ₹950 per kg in 2023-2024.

4. **Crude Oil on MCX (1.5 marks):** Indian **airlines (IndiGo, Air India)** and **OMCs (BPCL, HPCL, IOCL)** use MCX crude oil futures to hedge their Aviation Turbine Fuel (ATF) and refining margins. With India importing **~85% of its crude**, this is critical for the **Current Account Deficit (CAD)**.

(7 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert Indian taxes to continuous rates.** STT of 0.0625% is annual, but must be converted using $-\ln(1-x)$ for short periods.
> 2. **Ignoring FEMA hedging limits** in currency questions — RBI allows 100% of *contracted* exposure, not *expected* exposure.
> 3. **Confusing STT rates** — STT on equity options premium is 0.0625% (sell side), but on exercise it is 0.125% (buyer side). Students mix these up.
> 4. **Using $\beta = 1$ in portfolio hedging** when the question explicitly gives $\beta \ne 1$ — this is the most common error in mutual fund hedging problems.
> 5. **Not computing "effective rate" after STT** — examiners expect the post-tax INR amount, not the gross forward rate.
> 6. **Omitting the $\sqrt{T}$ annualization** in the Black-Scholes formula for Indian questions where $T$ is in days.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Applications in Indian Scenario:**

- ✅ Indian derivative exchanges: **NSE, BSE, MCX, NCDEX** (know all four).
- ✅ STT rates: **0.0625%** (options premium sell), **0.125%** (options exercise), **0.1%** (equity futures sell), **0.02%** (currency derivatives).
- ✅ CTT rate: **0.01%** on non-agri commodity futures.
- ✅ GST on brokerage: **18%**.
- ✅ Lot sizes (2024): NIFTY = **25**, BANK NIFTY = **15**, USD-INR = **$1,000**.
- ✅ Settlement cycle: **T+1** (since January 2024).
- ✅ Risk-free rate proxy: **10-year G-Sec yield** (~7%) or **MIBOR overnight** (~6.5%).
- ✅ Dividend yield for NIFTY 50: ~**1.0% – 1.5%**.
- ✅ Real options in India are majorly used in **NHAI HAM/TOT** road projects and **NIP infrastructure**.
- ✅ Indian banks use **MIBOR-OIS swaps** and **IRFs** for interest rate hedging.
- ✅ Always **annualize T** correctly: $T = \text{days}/365$.
- ✅ FEMA 1999 governs currency hedging — remember the **"100% contracted exposure"** rule.
- ✅ SEBI mandates **stress-testing** and **VaR-based** dynamic hedging for debt mutual funds.
- ✅ For Black-Scholes in Indian context, **adjust dividend yield** to include STT impact.
- ✅ The **CIP formula** in India: $F = S \times (1 + r_{INR} T) / (1 + r_{USD} T)$.
- ✅ Real options valuation treats **NPV as the underlying asset** and **investment cost as the strike price**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Most Part B questions on this topic combine a **calculation** (7 marks) with a **conceptual/regulatory discussion** (7 marks). Master both. The valuation expects precise, *unsimplified* LaTeX-style workings, not mental arithmetic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamental Principle of Value Creation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET635 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fundamental Principle of Value Creation

# 🟢 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Principle of Value Creation (FPVC)**: In financial engineering, the fundamental principle of value creation states that **financial value is created when the rate of return on invested capital exceeds the cost of capital**, and is destroyed when the rate of return falls below the cost of capital. The principle is quantified through the **discounted cash flow (DCF) framework**, where value is the present worth of all future net cash flows generated by an asset, project, or firm, net of the capital required to generate them.

Mathematically, the central tenet is expressed through the **Net Present Value (NPV)** rule:

$$\text{Value is Created} \iff NPV > 0 \iff \text{Project Return} > \text{Cost of Capital}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: This topic forms the bedrock of **Module 4 — Capital Budgeting & Valuation** under PEPET635. It unifies three pillars: (i) the **Time Value of Money (TVM)**, (ii) the **Risk-Return Trade-off**, and (iii) the **Cost of Capital (WACC)**. Mastery of this principle is essential for problems on NPV, IRR, EVA, and corporate valuation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mango Tree" Intuition 🍋

Imagine you plant a **mango sapling** today by spending ₹1,000 (your *investment outlay*). The tree takes 3 years to bear fruit, but once mature, it gives you **mangoes worth ₹400 per year forever**.

- The **money you paid today** is the *cash outflow*.
- The **future mango harvests**, valued in today's rupees, are the *cash inflows*.
- If the present value of all future mangoes **exceeds ₹1,000**, you have **created value**.
- The *rate at which you discount future mangoes* is your **cost of capital** — a measure of what else you could have done with that ₹1,000 (e.g., a bank FD at 7%).

> **The golden rule of financial engineering**: A project is like a mango tree — *plant capital today, harvest cash flows tomorrow, and value is the net harvest in today's money.*

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Value Creation

| Pillar | Core Idea | Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Value of Money** | A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow | Discount factor $\frac{1}{(1+r)^t}$ |
| **Risk-Return Trade-off** | Higher risk demands higher expected return | CAPM: $R_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ |
| **Cost of Capital** | The minimum return a project must earn | WACC, hurdle rate, required return |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook**: Think of value creation as the equation **V = f(CF, r, t, risk)**. Change any one input and value changes — this is why financial engineering is fundamentally an *applied optimization* discipline.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Architecture of Value Creation

Value creation operates on a four-stage logical chain. Every financial engineering decision flows through these stages:

1. **Identification of Cash Flows** — Forecast incremental cash inflows ($CF_t$) and outflows over the project's life $n$.
2. **Determination of the Discount Rate** — Compute the required rate of return $r$ that reflects:
   - *Time preference* (risk-free rate $R_f$)
   - *Systematic risk* (beta $\beta$, market premium $R_m - R_f$)
   - *Capital structure* (cost of debt $R_d$ and cost of equity $R_e$)
3. **Discounting & Aggregation** — Convert all future cash flows to present value using the discount factor $(1+r)^{-t}$, then sum them up.
4. **Decision Rule** — Accept (create value) if NPV > 0; reject (destroy value) if NPV < 0.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Stage**: Why discount? Because of *opportunity cost* and *inflation*. Why use a risk-adjusted rate? Because investors demand compensation for bearing uncertainty. Why aggregate? Because value is a *scalar magnitude*, not a stream — it must be measured at a single point in time (today).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every formula you must know for KTU ESE questions on this topic. **Memorize the boundary conditions** — they appear frequently in Part A questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | $CF_t$ = cash flow at time $t$ (₹); $r$ = discount rate (decimal); $n$ = project life (years) |
| 2 | **Future Value (FV)** | $FV = PV \cdot (1+r)^n$ | All in consistent currency units |
| 3 | **Present Value (PV)** | $PV = \frac{FV}{(1+r)^n}$ | — |
| 4 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $NPV = 0 = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t}$ | Solve implicitly for IRR (decimal) |
| 5 | **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** | $WACC = \frac{E}{V} \cdot R_e + \frac{D}{V} \cdot R_d \cdot (1-T_c)$ | $E$ = market value of equity; $D$ = market value of debt; $V = E+D$; $T_c$ = corporate tax rate |
| 6 | **CAPM — Cost of Equity** | $R_e = R_f + \beta \cdot (R_m - R_f)$ | $R_f$ = risk-free rate; $\beta$ = systematic risk; $R_m$ = expected market return |
| 7 | **Economic Value Added (EVA)** | $EVA = NOPAT - (WACC \times \text{Invested Capital})$ | NOPAT = Net Operating Profit After Tax |
| 8 | **Annuity PV Factor** | $PV_{\text{annuity}} = C \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ | $C$ = constant periodic cash flow |
| 9 | **Perpetuity PV** | $PV_{\text{perp}} = \frac{C}{r}$ | Constant cash flow $C$ forever |
| 10 | **Growing Perpetuity (Gordon Model)** | $PV = \frac{C_1}{r - g}$ | $g$ = constant growth rate, $r > g$ |
| 11 | **Profitability Index (PI)** | $PI = \frac{PV(\text{inflows})}{PV(\text{outflows})}$ | PI > 1 → value creation |
| 12 | **Value of a Firm (DCF)** | $V_{\text{firm}} = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{FCF_t}{(1+WACC)^t}$ | $FCF_t$ = free cash flow to firm |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Corporate Finance

- **Capital Budgeting**: Engineering firms use NPV to decide whether to invest in new R&D, plant expansions, or automation projects.
- **Startups & Valuation**: Venture capital and IPO pricing rely on DCF models to estimate firm value.
- **Public-Private Partnerships (PPP)**: KTU-context: infrastructure projects in Kerala (e.g., Kochi Metro) are appraised using NPV at the social discount rate.
- **Mergers & Acquisitions (M&A)**: Acquisition is justified only if the synergy-adjusted NPV is positive.
- **Shareholder Wealth Maximization**: The ultimate financial-engineering goal — maximize the present value of shareholder cash flows.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Present Value from First Principles

We begin with the **simplest case**: a single cash flow $FV$ received $n$ years from now. We want to find its equivalent value today, $PV$.

**Step 1: Define Opportunity Cost.** If you had $PV$ today and could invest it at rate $r$ per annum (compounded annually), after $n$ years you would have:

$$FV = PV \cdot (1+r)^n$$

**Step 2: Invert the Equation.** Solve for $PV$:

$$PV = \frac{FV}{(1+r)^n}$$

**Step 3: Define the Discount Factor.** The term $\frac{1}{(1+r)^n}$ is called the **discount factor** $DF_t$. It is the *time-value adjustment*.

**Step 4: Generalize to a Stream of Cash Flows.** If cash flows $CF_1, CF_2, \ldots, CF_n$ occur at the end of years 1 through $n$, the total present value is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= \frac{CF_1}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{CF_2}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \frac{CF_n}{(1+r)^n} \\
   &= \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Net Present Value.** If an initial investment $C_0$ is required at $t = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **fundamental equation of value creation**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — WACC from Capital Structure Weights

Suppose a firm finances itself through equity (cost $R_e$) and debt (cost $R_d$), with corporate tax rate $T_c$.

**Step 1: Cost of Equity Component.** The weighted contribution of equity to total capital cost is $\frac{E}{V} \cdot R_e$.

**Step 2: Cost of Debt Component.** Interest on debt is tax-deductible. After-tax cost of debt is $R_d(1 - T_c)$. Weighted contribution: $\frac{D}{V} \cdot R_d (1 - T_c)$.

**Step 3: Aggregate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WACC &= \frac{E}{V} \cdot R_e + \frac{D}{V} \cdot R_d (1 - T_c)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.** WACC is the *blended hurdle rate* the firm must clear on every rupee invested. It is the **discount rate used in DCF** for valuing the firm.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — EVA Bridge from NPV

**Step 1:** NOPAT (Net Operating Profit After Tax) represents the operating cash earnings available to all capital providers.

**Step 2:** Capital Charge = $WACC \times \text{Invested Capital}$. This is the *minimum return* required by capital providers.

**Step 3:** EVA = NOPAT − Capital Charge. Positive EVA → the firm earned *more* than its cost of capital → **value creation**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EVA_t &= NOPAT_t - (WACC \times IC_{t-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** The sum of discounted EVAs equals the firm's **Market Value Added (MVA)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MVA &= \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{EVA_t}{(1+WACC)^t}
\end{aligned}
$$

This directly links *period performance* (EVA) to *cumulative wealth* (MVA) — a key KTU examination insight.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Rules at a Glance

| Metric | Formula | Value Creation Threshold | Value Destruction Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NPV** | $\sum \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | $NPV > 0$ | $NPV < 0$ |
| **IRR** | $NPV(IRR) = 0$ | $IRR > WACC$ | $IRR < WACC$ |
| **PI** | $\frac{PV_{\text{in}}}{PV_{\text{out}}}$ | $PI > 1$ | $PI < 1$ |
| **EVA** | $NOPAT - WACC \cdot IC$ | $EVA > 0$ | $EVA < 0$ |
| **Payback** | Years to recover $C_0$ | Payback $<$ target | Payback $>$ target |
| **MVA** | $\sum \frac{EVA_t}{(1+WACC)^t}$ | $MVA > 0$ | $MVA < 0$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Insight (KTU)**: When asked "Is value being created?", **always** frame the answer in terms of *return vs. cost of capital*. Even if a project shows a positive accounting profit, value is destroyed if accounting return < WACC.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (For Practice)

**Problem:** A project requires an initial investment of ₹5,00,000 and generates cash inflows of ₹1,50,000 per year for 5 years. The cost of capital is 10%. Compute NPV, IRR, PI, and decide.

**Step 1: Discount Factor Table**

| Year | CF (₹) | DF at 10% | PV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | −5,00,000 | 1.0000 | −5,00,000 |
| 1 | 1,50,000 | 0.9091 | 1,36,365 |
| 2 | 1,50,000 | 0.8264 | 1,23,960 |
| 3 | 1,50,000 | 0.7513 | 1,12,695 |
| 4 | 1,50,000 | 0.6830 | 1,02,450 |
| 5 | 1,50,000 | 0.6209 | 93,135 |
| **Total** | | | **NPV = 68,605** |

**Step 2: NPV Decision Rule**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -5{,}00{,}000 + \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{1.10} \cdot \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-5}}{0.10} \\
    &= -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 \times 3.7908 \\
    &= -5{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}68{,}620 \\
    &= \text{₹}68{,}620 \text{ (positive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Decision** — Since $NPV > 0$, the project **creates value**. Accept.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Value Creation Decision Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Identify Investment Opportunity"] --> nodeB["Forecast Incremental Cash Flows CF_t"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Determine Risk Adjusted Discount Rate r or WACC"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Compute NPV using DCF Formula"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"NPV greater than zero?"}
    nodeE -- Yes --> nodeF["VALUE CREATED Accept Project"]
    nodeE -- No --> nodeG{"NPV less than zero?"}
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeH["VALUE DESTROYED Reject Project"]
    nodeG -- No --> nodeI["Indifferent NPV equals zero"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["Compute EVA NOPAT minus WACC times IC"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK{"EVA positive?"}
    nodeK -- Yes --> nodeL["Sustained Wealth Generation"]
    nodeK -- No --> nodeM["Reassess Capital Structure"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of Value Drivers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1["Cash Flow Generation CF_t"] --> nodeB1["Discounting at Cost of Capital WACC"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1["Present Value of Future Cash"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD1["Subtract Invested Capital"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeE1["NET PRESENT VALUE NPV"]
    nodeE1 --> nodeF1{"Decision Accept or Reject"}
    nodeA2["Operating Profit NOPAT"] --> nodeB2["Capital Charge WACC times IC"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC2["Difference is EVA"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeF2["Period Wealth Indicator"]
    nodeE1 --> nodeG1["Total Shareholder Return"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeG1
```

## 4.3 Schematic — The Three Pillars of FPVC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pillar1["Pillar 1 TIME VALUE OF MONEY"] --> core["Fundamental Principle of Value Creation"]
    pillar2["Pillar 2 RISK RETURN TRADEOFF"] --> core
    pillar3["Pillar 3 COST OF CAPITAL WACC"] --> core
    core --> output1["Capital Budgeting Decision"]
    core --> output2["Corporate Valuation"]
    core --> output3["Shareholder Wealth Maximization"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams**: The first flowchart shows the *sequential decision logic* of FPVC. The second shows the *parallel architectures* of NPV (ex-ante valuation) and EVA (ex-post performance). The third shows the *conceptual pillars* supporting the entire framework. Together, they form a complete mental model for KTU ESE answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

### **Q1. State and explain the Fundamental Principle of Value Creation.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The fundamental principle of value creation in financial engineering states that **value is created when the return earned on invested capital exceeds the cost of capital**, and is destroyed when the return falls below it. Mathematically, this translates to the **Net Present Value (NPV) rule**: a project creates value if and only if its NPV, computed by discounting all future cash flows at the risk-adjusted cost of capital, is positive. The principle integrates three concepts: (i) the time value of money, (ii) the risk-return trade-off, and (iii) the cost of capital. Its application spans capital budgeting, corporate valuation, and shareholder wealth maximization.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall**: Students often write only "maximize profit." This is **incomplete** — value creation is *not* profit maximization; it is the *present value* of cash flows net of capital costs.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between NPV and EVA as measures of value creation.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | NPV | EVA |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Ex-ante (forward-looking) | Ex-post (period performance) |
| **Formula** | $NPV = \sum \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | $EVA = NOPAT - (WACC \times IC)$ |
| **Time Horizon** | Over entire project life | Single accounting period |
| **Use** | Go / no-go decision | Continuous performance tracking |
| **Limit** | Sensitive to forecast errors | Sensitive to accounting choices |

NPV determines *whether* to invest; EVA measures *how well* the invested capital performed in a given year.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / ESE Pattern]**

---

### **🔵 Question A (14 Marks)**

> **A firm is evaluating a project requiring an initial investment of ₹20,00,000. The project generates cash inflows of ₹6,00,000, ₹7,00,000, ₹8,00,000, and ₹9,00,000 at the end of years 1, 2, 3, and 4 respectively. The firm's cost of equity is 14%, cost of debt is 10% (after tax), and the debt-to-equity ratio is 1:1. The risk-free rate is 7%, market return is 13%, and the project's beta is 1.2.**

**Answer the following:**

#### **Part (a) — Compute WACC and evaluate the project using NPV. Comment on value creation. `[7 Marks]`**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Cost of Equity using CAPM.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_e &= R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f) \\
    &= 0.07 + 1.2 \times (0.13 - 0.07) \\
    &= 0.07 + 1.2 \times 0.06 \\
    &= 0.07 + 0.072 \\
    &= 0.142 \text{ or } 14.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Capital Structure Weights.**
$D/E = 1$, so $E = D$. Therefore $\frac{E}{V} = 0.5$ and $\frac{D}{V} = 0.5$.

**Step 3: Compute WACC.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WACC &= \frac{E}{V} \cdot R_e + \frac{D}{V} \cdot R_d (1 - T_c) \\
     &= 0.5 \times 0.142 + 0.5 \times 0.10 \\
     &= 0.071 + 0.05 \\
     &= 0.121 \text{ or } 12.1\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Discount Cash Flows at 12.1%.**

| Year | CF (₹) | DF at 12.1% | PV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | −20,00,000 | 1.0000 | −20,00,000 |
| 1 | 6,00,000 | 0.8921 | 5,35,260 |
| 2 | 7,00,000 | 0.7958 | 5,57,060 |
| 3 | 8,00,000 | 0.7099 | 5,67,920 |
| 4 | 9,00,000 | 0.6333 | 5,69,970 |
| | | **NPV** | **+2,30,210** |

**Step 5: Decision.** Since $NPV = +₹2,30,210 > 0$, the project **creates value** and should be **accepted**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert**: Many students use the *cost of equity alone* (14.2%) as the discount rate. This is wrong because the project is financed by both equity AND debt. Always use the **blended WACC** when discounting project cash flows for an established firm.

---

#### **Part (b) — Compute IRR and Profitability Index. Verify your NPV-based decision. `[7 Marks]`**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute IRR by trial-and-error (or interpolation).**

Try $r = 15\%$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_{15\%} &= -20{,}00{,}000 + \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{1.15} + \frac{7{,}00{,}000}{1.15^2} + \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{1.15^3} + \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{1.15^4} \\
           &= -20{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}21{,}739 + 5{,}29{,}470 + 5{,}26{,}158 + 5{,}15{,}285 \\
           &= 92{,}652 \text{ (positive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Try $r = 16\%$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_{16\%} &= -20{,}00{,}000 + \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{1.16} + \frac{7{,}00{,}000}{1.16^2} + \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{1.16^3} + \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{1.16^4} \\
           &= -20{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}17{,}241 + 5{,}20{,}376 + 5{,}12{,}372 + 4{,}97{,}432 \\
           &= 47{,}421 \text{ (still positive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Try $r = 17\%$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_{17\%} &\approx -20{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}12{,}820 + 5{,}11{,}700 + 4{,}99{,}000 + 4{,}80{,}500 \\
           &\approx -20{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}04{,}020 \\
           &\approx 4{,}020 \text{ (near zero)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus $IRR \approx 17\%$.

**Step 2: Interpolate more precisely.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IRR &\approx 16\% + \frac{47{,}421}{47{,}421 + (-4{,}020)} \times 1\% \\
    &\approx 16\% + \frac{47{,}421}{51{,}441} \times 1\% \\
    &\approx 16\% + 0.92\% \\
    &\approx 16.92\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: PI Computation.**

$PV(\text{inflows at } 12.1\%) = 20,00,000 + 2,30,210 = 22,30,210$

$$
\begin{aligned}
PI &= \frac{PV(\text{inflows})}{PV(\text{outflows})} = \frac{22{,}30{,}210}{20{,}00{,}000} = 1.115
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Cross-Verification.**

- $IRR = 16.92\% > WACC = 12.1\%$ → **Value is created** ✓
- $PI = 1.115 > 1$ → **Value is created** ✓

All three methods (NPV, IRR, PI) confirm the **accept decision**, consistent with the fundamental principle.

---

### **🟢 Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)**

> **A manufacturing firm has invested capital of ₹50,00,000. The firm's NOPAT for the year is ₹7,50,000. The firm has 60% equity and 40% debt in its capital structure. Cost of equity is 16%, after-tax cost of debt is 8%, and the corporate tax rate is 30%.**

**Answer the following:**

#### **Part (a) — Compute WACC and EVA. Comment on whether the firm is creating value. `[7 Marks]`**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute WACC.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WACC &= 0.60 \times 0.16 + 0.40 \times 0.08 \\
     &= 0.096 + 0.032 \\
     &= 0.128 \text{ or } 12.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Capital Charge.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Capital Charge} &= WACC \times IC = 0.128 \times 50{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}40{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute EVA.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EVA &= NOPAT - \text{Capital Charge} \\
    &= 7{,}50{,}000 - 6{,}40{,}000 \\
    &= 1{,}10{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.** Since $EVA = +₹1,10,000 > 0$, the firm is **creating value**. It earned more than its cost of capital — its return on invested capital exceeds 12.8%.

**Step 5: Compute RoIC for sanity check.**

$RoIC = \frac{NOPAT}{IC} = \frac{7,50,000}{50,00,000} = 15\% > WACC = 12.8\%$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert**: Do NOT confuse NOPAT with *net profit*. NOPAT excludes the benefit of debt tax shields but includes operating taxes. Using net profit here would distort the EVA computation.

---

#### **Part (b) — If the firm wants to increase its EVA by ₹2,00,000 next year, suggest two strategies. Justify with calculations. `[7 Marks]`**

**Solution:**

**Strategy 1: Increase NOPAT (Operational Efficiency).**
Target $NOPAT_{\text{new}} = 7,50,000 + 2,00,000 = 9,50,000$.
This requires either increasing revenue by ~26.7% (holding costs constant) or reducing operating costs by ₹2,00,000 through lean manufacturing, automation, or supplier renegotiation.

**Numerical Justification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EVA_{\text{new}} &= 9{,}50{,}000 - 6{,}40{,}000 = 3{,}10{,}000 \\
\Delta EVA &= +₹2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ✓}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Strategy 2: Reduce Invested Capital (Asset Rationalization).**
Hold NOPAT constant at ₹7,50,000. Solve for $IC_{\text{new}}$ such that $EVA_{\text{new}} = 3,10,000$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3{,}10{,}000 &= 7{,}50{,}000 - 0.128 \times IC_{\text{new}} \\
IC_{\text{new}} &= \frac{7{,}50{,}000 - 3{,}10{,}000}{0.128} \\
IC_{\text{new}} &= \frac{4{,}40{,}000}{0.128} \\
IC_{\text{new}} &\approx 34{,}37{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

So the firm must reduce invested capital from ₹50,00,000 to ~₹34,37,500 — a 31% reduction — by divesting non-core assets, optimizing working capital, or selling idle equipment.

**Numerical Justification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EVA_{\text{new}} &= 7{,}50{,}000 - 0.128 \times 34{,}37{,}500 \\
                &= 7{,}50{,}000 - 4{,}40{,}000 \\
                &= 3{,}10{,}000 \\
\Delta EVA &= +₹2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ✓}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Both strategies are mathematically viable. In practice, the firm would choose the strategy with *higher NPV of implementation cost* and lower operational risk.

---

## 5.3 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Students Lose Marks in FPVC Questions:**
>
> 1. **Using the wrong discount rate** — Students often use cost of equity where WACC is required (or vice versa). Remember: *equity-financed project → use $R_e$; firm-wide project → use WACC*.
> 2. **Ignoring tax shields** — Always apply $(1 - T_c)$ to cost of debt.
> 3. **Sign errors in NPV** — Initial outflow is negative; future inflows are positive. Mixing signs destroys the answer.
> 4. **Confusing IRR with ROI** — IRR is the discount rate making NPV zero; ROI is an accounting ratio. They are NOT the same.
> 5. **Forgetting the time-value of money** — A common trap is computing "total profit" without discounting. KTU expects *present values*.
> 6. **Not stating the decision rule explicitly** — Always end with "Since NPV > 0, accept" or equivalent. Partial credit is lost without a clear conclusion.

---

## 5.4 ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🎯 **Core Principle**: Value is created when **return on capital > cost of capital**; destroyed when reverse.
- 📐 **Primary Metric**: **NPV** — discounted net cash flows over project life.
- ⏱️ **TVM Foundation**: $PV = \frac{FV}{(1+r)^n}$ — memorize and apply in every problem.
- 💰 **Discount Rate Choices**: Cost of equity (CAPM), after-tax cost of debt, or **WACC** (blended).
- 📊 **Decision Rules**: NPV > 0 → Accept; IRR > WACC → Accept; PI > 1 → Accept; EVA > 0 → Created.
- 🔁 **Cross-Verification**: Use multiple metrics (NPV + IRR + PI) to confirm decisions; KTU examiners love consistency.
- 🏢 **EVA Linkage**: $\sum \frac{EVA_t}{(1+WACC)^t} = MVA$, connecting period performance to firm value.
- ⚖️ **Capital Structure**: Weights must be based on **market values**, not book values (KTU subtlety).
- 🧮 **CAPM**: $R_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ — the standard for cost of equity.
- 🚫 **Pitfalls to Avoid**: Wrong discount rate, ignoring taxes, sign errors, no decision statement.
- 📚 **Required For**: Capital budgeting, M&A valuation, IPO pricing, EVA-based incentive compensation.
- 🧠 **Memory Anchor**: *V = ∫ CF(t) · e^(−rt) dt* — value is the integral of discounted future cash flows.

---

> **End of Notes — Fundamental Principle of Value Creation | PEPET635 Module 4 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk and Cost of Capital
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET635 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Risk and Cost of Capital

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Risk?

In financial engineering, **Risk** is defined as the variability or uncertainty in the expected returns of an investment. It is mathematically quantified as the **standard deviation ($\sigma$)** or **variance ($\sigma^2$)** of returns. Risk represents the probability that the actual return on an investment will deviate from its expected return — either upward (favorable deviation) or downward (adverse deviation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Syllabus):** "Risk in financial engineering is the chance that an investment's actual return will be different from the expected return, measured quantitatively using statistical dispersion metrics."

**Two Principal Categories of Financial Risk:**

1. **Systematic Risk (Market Risk / Non-Diversifiable Risk):**
   - Affects the entire market or economy.
   - Cannot be eliminated through portfolio diversification.
   - Examples: Interest rate changes, inflation, recession, war, pandemics, currency devaluation.
   - Measured by the **Beta ($\beta$)** coefficient.

2. **Unsystematic Risk (Idiosyncratic / Company-Specific / Diversifiable Risk):**
   - Affects only a specific firm or industry.
   - Can be eliminated through proper portfolio diversification.
   - Examples: Management failure, product recall, labor strike, lawsuit.
   - Measured by **firm-specific standard deviation**.

### 1.2 What is Cost of Capital?

**Cost of Capital ($K_c$)** is the minimum required rate of return that a firm must earn on its investments to satisfy its investors (both equity holders and debt holders). It represents the **opportunity cost of funds** employed in the business. It serves as the **discount rate** used in capital budgeting decisions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Cost of Capital is the **hurdle rate** or **cut-off rate** for accepting or rejecting any investment proposal under **Net Present Value (NPV)** and **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** methods.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Mountain Climber's Hurdle":**
> Imagine you are climbing a mountain. **Cost of Capital** is the *minimum height* you must climb to call the climb successful. **Risk** is the *width of the path* — a narrow, slippery path (high risk) requires a higher reward (higher cost of capital) to compensate the danger. When you mix your own money (equity) and borrowed money (debt) for the climb, the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** tells you the combined difficulty level of your expedition. Riskier expeditions demand more skilled (costlier) climbers, i.e., higher returns.

### 1.4 Key Metrics Summary

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value/Use |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Risk-Free Rate | $R_f$ | Yield on Government Securities (~7% in India) |
| Market Return | $R_m$ | Expected return on diversified market index |
| Beta | $\beta$ | Measure of systematic risk |
| Cost of Equity | $K_e$ | Required return by shareholders |
| Cost of Debt | $K_d$ | After-tax borrowing cost |
| Weighted Average Cost of Capital | $WACC$ | Blended cost of all financing sources |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of Cost of Capital

A firm raises capital from multiple sources. Each source has its own cost, and the overall cost is a weighted average.

**Sources of Finance and Their Costs:**

1. **Equity Share Capital ($K_e$):** The rate of return expected by equity shareholders. Computed via **CAPM, Dividend Growth Model, or Earning Yield Approach**.
2. **Preference Share Capital ($K_p$):** Fixed dividend paid to preference shareholders. 
3. **Debt ($K_d$):** Interest paid to lenders. Since interest is tax-deductible, the **after-tax cost of debt** is used.
4. **Retained Earnings ($K_r$):** Opportunity cost of ploughing back profits. Generally lower than cost of fresh equity.

### 2.2 Types of Risk — A Detailed Decomposition

#### A. Business Risk (Operating Risk)
The risk associated with the firm's **operations** — variability in **EBIT (Earnings Before Interest and Taxes)** due to changes in sales, costs, or pricing. It is influenced by:
- **Revenue volatility**
- **Operating leverage (fixed operating costs)**
- **Product diversification**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL):**
> Measures the sensitivity of EBIT to a percentage change in sales.
> $$DOL = \frac{\% \Delta EBIT}{\% \Delta Sales} = \frac{Contribution}{EBIT}$$

#### B. Financial Risk
The risk arising from the use of **debt financing** in the capital structure. It magnifies the variability of **EPS (Earnings Per Share)** due to fixed interest obligations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL):**
> $$DFL = \frac{\% \Delta EPS}{\% \Delta EBIT} = \frac{EBIT}{EBT} = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I}$$

#### C. Total Risk (Combined Leverage)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL):**
> $$DCL = DOL \times DFL = \frac{\% \Delta EPS}{\% \Delta Sales} = \frac{Contribution}{EBT}$$

#### D. Systematic vs. Unsystematic Risk (CAPM Framework)

$$\text{Total Risk} = \text{Systematic Risk} + \text{Unsystematic Risk}$$

| Risk Type | Diversifiable? | Compensation Required? | Measured By |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Systematic | No | Yes | Beta ($\beta$) |
| Unsystematic | Yes | No | Firm-specific $\sigma$ |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU Board Examination — Risk and Cost of Capital**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **CAPM — Cost of Equity** | $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ | $R_f$ = Risk-free rate; $\beta$ = Beta; $(R_m - R_f)$ = Market Risk Premium |
| 2 | **Beta of a Stock** | $\beta_i = \dfrac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{Var(R_m)}$ | Covariance of stock return with market; Variance of market |
| 3 | **Portfolio Beta** | $\beta_p = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \beta_i$ | Weighted sum of individual betas |
| 4 | **Hamada Equation (Levered Beta)** | $\beta_L = \beta_U \left[1 + (1-t)\dfrac{D}{E}\right]$ | Unlevered Beta $\beta_U$; Tax rate $t$; D/E = Debt-Equity Ratio |
| 5 | **Cost of Debt (After-Tax)** | $K_d = \dfrac{I(1-t)}{NP}$ | Interest $I$; Tax $t$; Net Proceeds $NP$ |
| 6 | **Cost of Preference Capital** | $K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{NP}$ | Fixed Preference Dividend $D_p$ |
| 7 | **Cost of Retained Earnings** | $K_r = K_e(1 - \text{floatation cost \%})$ | Adjusted Cost of Equity |
| 8 | **WACC** | $WACC = W_e K_e + W_p K_p + W_d K_d$ | Weights based on market value/book value |
| 9 | **Degree of Operating Leverage** | $DOL = \dfrac{Contribution}{EBIT}$ | Contribution = Sales - Variable Cost |
| 10 | **Degree of Financial Leverage** | $DFL = \dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I}$ | $I$ = Interest |
| 11 | **Degree of Combined Leverage** | $DCL = \dfrac{Contribution}{EBIT - I}$ | Combined sensitivity |
| 12 | **Indifference Point (EBIT level)** | $EPS_1 = EPS_2 \Rightarrow \dfrac{(EBIT - I_1)(1-t)}{N_1} = \dfrac{(EBIT - I_2)(1-t)}{N_2}$ | Two financing plans are equally attractive |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Investment Banking:** Used to value IPOs, mergers, and acquisitions.
- **Corporate Treasury:** Determines the **discount rate** for capital budgeting (NPV, IRR).
- **Credit Rating Agencies:** Cost of capital is the basis for sovereign and corporate credit ratings.
- **Regulatory Filings:** SEBI mandates disclosure of cost of capital assumptions in valuation reports.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)

**Step 1 — The Foundation:**
CAPM was developed by **William Sharpe (1964), John Lintner, and Jan Mossin** independently. It builds on Harry Markowitz's **Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT)**.

**Step 2 — Assumptions of CAPM:**
- Investors are rational and risk-averse.
- Single-period investment horizon.
- Homogeneous expectations.
- No transaction costs or taxes.
- Investors can borrow/lend at the risk-free rate.

**Step 3 — The Two-Fund Separation Theorem:**
Every investor holds a combination of:
- **Risk-free asset** (yielding $R_f$)
- **Market portfolio** (yielding $R_m$)

**Step 4 — Risk Decomposition:**
Total risk = Systematic risk + Unsystematic risk.
For a well-diversified portfolio, unsystematic risk → 0. Thus, only **systematic risk (Beta)** is rewarded.

**Step 5 — Mathematical Derivation:**
Consider a portfolio $P$ with a fraction $\alpha$ in the risky asset $i$ and $(1-\alpha)$ in the market portfolio $M$.

$$
E(R_p) = \alpha E(R_i) + (1-\alpha) E(R_m)
$$

$$
\beta_p = \alpha \beta_i + (1-\alpha) \beta_m
$$

Setting $\beta_m = 1$ (market portfolio beta) and solving:

$$
E(R_p) = R_f + [E(R_m) - R_f] \cdot \beta_p
$$

Replacing with cost-of-equity notation ($K_e$):

$$
\boxed{K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)}
$$

**Logic:** The required return increases linearly with systematic risk. $\beta = 1$ means average risk; $\beta > 1$ means aggressive; $\beta < 1$ means defensive.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)

**Step 1 — Premise:**
A firm uses capital from $n$ sources. Each source has weight $W_i$ and cost $K_i$.

**Step 2 — Capital Structure Weights:**
$$
W_i = \frac{\text{Market Value of Source } i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{Market Value of Source } j}
$$

**Step 3 — General WACC Formula:**
$$
WACC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot K_i
$$

**Step 4 — Expanded 3-Source Form (Equity, Preference, Debt):**

$$
\boxed{WACC = W_e K_e + W_p K_p + W_d K_d}
$$

Where $K_d$ is **after-tax cost of debt** because interest saves tax:
$$
K_d (\text{after-tax}) = K_d (\text{pre-tax}) \cdot (1 - t)
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example:**

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A firm has the following capital structure:
> - Equity: ₹ 60,00,000 (Cost = 15%)
> - Preference: ₹ 20,00,000 (Cost = 12%)
> - Debt: ₹ 20,00,000 (Cost = 10% pre-tax, Tax = 30%)
>
> **Compute WACC.**

**Solution:**

After-tax cost of debt:
$$
K_d = 10\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 7\%
$$

Total capital = 60 + 20 + 20 = ₹ 100 Lakhs

Weights:
$$
W_e = 0.60, \quad W_p = 0.20, \quad W_d = 0.20
$$

WACC:
$$
WACC = (0.60)(15\%) + (0.20)(12\%) + (0.20)(7\%)
$$

$$
WACC = 9.0\% + 2.4\% + 1.4\% = \boxed{12.8\%}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Hamada Equation (Levered vs. Unlevered Beta)

**Step 1 — The Concept:**
When a firm has debt, its equity becomes riskier because of fixed interest obligations. Hence, the levered equity beta ($\beta_L$) is higher than the unlevered asset beta ($\beta_U$).

**Step 2 — Starting Relation (Modigliani-Miller without tax):**
$$
\beta_L = \beta_U \left(1 + \frac{D}{E}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Incorporating Corporate Tax Shield:**
Debt interest is tax-deductible, reducing effective financial risk. Hence:

$$
\beta_L = \beta_U \left[1 + (1 - t)\frac{D}{E}\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Reverse Formula (Unlevering Beta):**

$$
\boxed{\beta_U = \frac{\beta_L}{1 + (1-t)\frac{D}{E}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example:**

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A firm has $\beta_L = 1.4$, $D/E = 0.6$, $t = 30\%$, $R_f = 8\%$, $R_m = 14\%$. Compute Cost of Equity.

**Solution:**

Unlevering Beta:
$$
\beta_U = \frac{1.4}{1 + (1-0.30)(0.6)} = \frac{1.4}{1 + 0.42} = \frac{1.4}{1.42} = 0.9859
$$

Re-levered for new structure (assume new D/E = 1.0):
$$
\beta_{L,\text{new}} = 0.9859 \left[1 + (1-0.30)(1.0)\right] = 0.9859 \times 1.70 = 1.676
$$

Cost of Equity (CAPM):
$$
K_e = 8\% + 1.676 \times (14\% - 8\%) = 8\% + 1.676 \times 6\% = 8\% + 10.056\% = \boxed{18.06\%}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Cost of Equity ($K_e$) | Cost of Debt ($K_d$) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Payment Nature | Variable (dividend) | Fixed (interest) |
| Tax Deductibility | No | Yes |
| Risk to Holder | Higher | Lower |
| Required Return | Higher | Lower |
| Claim on Assets | Residual | Priority |
| Effect on Beta | Direct | Magnifies via leverage |

| Risk Type | Cause | Mitigation Strategy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Business Risk | Operating cost structure | Diversification of products |
| Financial Risk | High debt in capital structure | Debt restructuring, equity infusion |
| Interest Rate Risk | Floating rate debt | Interest rate swaps, hedging |
| Currency Risk | Foreign currency exposure | Forward contracts, options |
| Inflation Risk | Erosion of real returns | Index-linked instruments |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Risk Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Financial Risk] --> B[Systematic Risk]
    A --> C[Unsystematic Risk]
    B --> B1[Market Risk]
    B --> B2[Interest Rate Risk]
    B --> B3[Inflation Risk]
    B --> B4[Currency Risk]
    C --> C1[Business Risk]
    C --> C2[Financial Risk]
    C --> C3[Default Risk]
    C --> C4[Liquidity Risk]
    B1 --> M[Cannot be Diversified]
    B2 --> M
    B3 --> M
    B4 --> M
    C1 --> N[Can be Diversified via Portfolio]
    C2 --> N
    C3 --> N
    C4 --> N
    M --> P[Measured by Beta]
    N --> Q[Measured by Firm-specific Sigma]
```

### 4.2 Cost of Capital Computation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Sources of Capital] --> EQ[Equity Capital]
    SRC --> PR[Preference Capital]
    SRC --> DT[Debt Capital]
    SRC --> RE[Retained Earnings]
    EQ --> C1[Cost of Equity via CAPM]
    PR --> C2[Cost of Preference]
    DT --> C3[Cost of Debt After Tax]
    RE --> C4[Cost of Retained Earnings]
    C1 --> WACC[Weighted Average Cost of Capital]
    C2 --> WACC
    C3 --> WACC
    C4 --> WACC
    WACC --> DC[Discount Rate for NPV/IRR]
    DC --> CB[Capital Budgeting Decisions]
```

### 4.3 CAPM Security Market Line (SML) Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AX[X Axis: Beta Systematic Risk] --> PT[Point on SML]
    AY[Y Axis: Expected Return percent] --> PT
    PT --> RF[Intercept equals Risk Free Rate Rf]
    PT --> SL[Slope equals Market Risk Premium Rm minus Rf]
    PT -.-> A1[Asset A: Beta 0.5 Low Risk Low Return]
    PT -.-> A2[Market M: Beta 1.0 Average]
    PT -.-> A3[Asset B: Beta 1.5 High Risk High Return]
```

### 4.4 Leverage Relationship Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Sales Change percent Delta S] --> DOL[DOL Computation]
    DOL --> EBIT[EBIT Change percent Delta EBIT]
    EBIT --> DFL[DFL Computation]
    DFL --> EPS[EPS Change percent Delta EPS]
    DOL2[Fixed Operating Costs] --> DOL
    DFL2[Fixed Financial Charges Interest] --> DFL
    DOL --> DCL[DCL Combined Leverage]
    DFL --> DCL
    DCL --> TR[Total Risk to Shareholder]
```

### 4.5 WACC Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Capital Structure Data] --> W1[Compute Market Values]
    W1 --> W2[Compute Weights Wi]
    W2 --> W3[Compute Component Costs Ki]
    W3 --> W4[Apply After Tax Adjustment to Kd]
    W4 --> SUM[Weighted Sum Wi times Ki]
    SUM --> OUT[WACC Output Discount Rate]
    OUT --> APP[Application in Project Appraisal]
    APP --> DEC{Project IRR vs WACC}
    DEC -->|Greater| ACC[Accept Project]
    DEC -->|Lower| REJ[Reject Project]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Distinguish between Systematic Risk and Unsystematic Risk. Which one is rewarded in the market and why? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Systematic Risk:** Market-wide risk arising from macroeconomic factors such as interest rates, inflation, GDP, and political events. Cannot be eliminated through diversification. Measured by **Beta ($\beta$)**.
- **Unsystematic Risk:** Firm-specific risk arising from management, product failure, or labor issues. **Can be eliminated** through portfolio diversification.
- The market rewards **systematic risk only** because unsystematic risk is diversifiable and rational investors will not pay extra for bearing risk they can avoid. As per CAPM: $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ — the beta term captures only systematic risk compensation.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **What is Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)? Why is the after-tax cost of debt used in WACC computation? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **WACC** is the weighted average of the costs of various sources of finance (equity, preference, debt, retained earnings), where weights are based on the proportion of each source in the total capital structure.
- The **after-tax cost of debt** is used because interest paid on debt is a **tax-deductible expense** under the Income Tax Act. Thus, the effective cost of debt to the firm is reduced by the tax shield: $K_d(\text{after-tax}) = K_d(\text{pre-tax}) \cdot (1-t)$.
- Formula: $WACC = W_e K_e + W_p K_p + W_d K_d(\text{after-tax})$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (Choice 1):**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Question]**
> **(a)** A company is considering an investment of ₹ 5,00,000 in a project. The risk-free rate is 8%, the expected market return is 14%, and the beta of the project is 1.4. The company's capital structure consists of 60% equity and 40% debt. The pre-tax cost of debt is 10% and the tax rate is 30%. Compute the WACC using CAPM. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** Explain with a numerical illustration the Hamada equation. A firm has an unlevered beta of 0.90. If it changes its debt-equity ratio to 0.75 (with tax rate 25%), what will be its new levered beta? Compute the new cost of equity using CAPM, given $R_f = 7\%$ and $R_m = 13\%$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Cost of Equity (CAPM):
$$
K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f) = 8\% + 1.4(14\% - 8\%) = 8\% + 1.4 \times 6\% = 8\% + 8.4\% = 16.4\%
$$

After-tax cost of debt:
$$
K_d = 10\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 7\%
$$

WACC:
$$
WACC = W_e K_e + W_d K_d = (0.60)(16.4\%) + (0.40)(7\%) = 9.84\% + 2.80\% = \boxed{12.64\%}
$$

**Part (b):**

Hamada Equation:
$$
\beta_L = \beta_U \left[1 + (1-t)\frac{D}{E}\right]
$$

Given: $\beta_U = 0.90$, $D/E = 0.75$, $t = 25\%$.

$$
\beta_L = 0.90 \left[1 + (1 - 0.25)(0.75)\right] = 0.90 \left[1 + 0.5625\right] = 0.90 \times 1.5625 = \boxed{1.406}
$$

New Cost of Equity (CAPM):
$$
K_e = R_f + \beta_L(R_m - R_f) = 7\% + 1.406(13\% - 7\%) = 7\% + 1.406 \times 6\% = 7\% + 8.44\% = \boxed{15.44\%}
$$

---

> **Question B (Choice 2):**
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
> **(a)** The following data relates to ABC Ltd:
> - Sales: ₹ 10,00,000; Variable Cost: ₹ 6,00,000; Fixed Cost: ₹ 2,00,000; Interest: ₹ 50,000; Tax rate: 30%.
> - Equity shares: 1,00,000; 12% Preference shares: ₹ 2,00,000; 10% Debentures: ₹ 3,00,000.
>
> Compute **DOL, DFL, DCL**, and **WACC**. Cost of equity is 14%. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** A company has two financing alternatives:
> - **Plan X:** Equity = ₹ 10,00,000 (1,00,000 shares); Interest = ₹ 1,00,000.
> - **Plan Y:** Equity = ₹ 6,00,000 (60,000 shares); Interest = ₹ 2,00,000.
>
> EBIT = ₹ 4,00,000; Tax rate = 30%. Calculate the **indifference point** (EBIT level where EPS under both plans is equal). Which plan is better at EBIT = ₹ 6,00,000? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Compute basic figures:
$$
Contribution = Sales - VC = 10,00,000 - 6,00,000 = ₹ 4,00,000
$$

$$
EBIT = Contribution - FC = 4,00,000 - 2,00,000 = ₹ 2,00,000
$$

$$
EBT = EBIT - I = 2,00,000 - 50,000 = ₹ 1,50,000
$$

Leverages:
$$
DOL = \frac{Contribution}{EBIT} = \frac{4,00,000}{2,00,000} = 2.0
$$

$$
DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBT} = \frac{2,00,000}{1,50,000} = 1.33
$$

$$
DCL = DOL \times DFL = 2.0 \times 1.33 = 2.67
$$

WACC Computation:
- Total Capital = Equity + Preference + Debt
- Equity = ₹ 10,00,000 (assumed at face value for simplicity); Preference = ₹ 2,00,000; Debt = ₹ 3,00,000; Total = ₹ 15,00,000.

- Weights: $W_e = 10/15$, $W_p = 2/15$, $W_d = 3/15$.

- Costs: $K_e = 14\%$; $K_p = 12\%$; $K_d = 10\%(1 - 0.30) = 7\%$.

$$
WACC = \left(\frac{10}{15}\right)(14\%) + \left(\frac{2}{15}\right)(12\%) + \left(\frac{3}{15}\right)(7\%)
$$

$$
WACC = 9.33\% + 1.60\% + 1.40\% = \boxed{12.33\%}
$$

**Part (b):**

EPS Formula:
$$
EPS = \frac{(EBIT - I)(1 - t)}{N}
$$

Setting EPS of Plan X = EPS of Plan Y:

$$
\frac{(EBIT - 1,00,000)(1 - 0.30)}{1,00,000} = \frac{(EBIT - 2,00,000)(1 - 0.30)}{60,000}
$$

Since $(1 - t)$ cancels on both sides:

$$
\frac{EBIT - 1,00,000}{1,00,000} = \frac{EBIT - 2,00,000}{60,000}
$$

Cross-multiplying:
$$
60,000(EBIT - 1,00,000) = 1,00,000(EBIT - 2,00,000)
$$

$$
60,000 \cdot EBIT - 6,00,00,00,000 = 1,00,000 \cdot EBIT - 20,00,00,00,000
$$

$$
40,000 \cdot EBIT = 14,00,00,00,000
$$

$$
EBIT = ₹ 3,50,000
$$

**Indifference Point = ₹ 3,50,000**

At EBIT = ₹ 6,00,000:

Plan X EPS:
$$
EPS_X = \frac{(6,00,000 - 1,00,000)(0.70)}{1,00,000} = \frac{3,50,000}{1,00,000} = ₹ 3.50
$$

Plan Y EPS:
$$
EPS_Y = \frac{(6,00,000 - 2,00,000)(0.70)}{60,000} = \frac{2,80,000}{60,000} = ₹ 4.67
$$

**Since EBIT (₹ 6,00,000) > Indifference Point (₹ 3,50,000), Plan Y is better (higher financial leverage is favorable when operating profits are high).**

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the tax shield** in cost of debt — always multiply pre-tax $K_d$ by $(1 - t)$.
> 2. **Using book values instead of market values** for weights in WACC — KTU expects market value weights.
> 3. **Confusing beta of a firm with beta of an asset/project** — project beta must be assessed via Hamada if debt-equity differs.
> 4. **Miscounting EBIT/EBT in leverage problems** — deduct interest AFTER EBIT to get EBT.
> 5. **Skipping units and steps** in derivation — KTU marks are awarded per logical step.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Risk and Cost of Capital**

- Risk = Variability of return; Quantified by $\sigma$ (std deviation) and $\beta$ (systematic risk).
- **Systematic Risk** is undiversifiable; rewarded via CAPM.
- **Unsystematic Risk** is diversifiable; NOT rewarded.
- **CAPM formula** is the gold standard for cost of equity: $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$.
- **Beta interpretation:** $\beta > 1$ → aggressive; $\beta = 1$ → neutral; $\beta < 1$ → defensive; $\beta < 0$ → inversely correlated.
- **WACC formula** uses market-value weights and after-tax cost of debt.
- **Hamada equation** unlevered beta is used to compare firms with different capital structures.
- **DOL** measures sensitivity of EBIT to sales changes (operating leverage).
- **DFL** measures sensitivity of EPS to EBIT changes (financial leverage).
- **DCL** = DOL × DFL = Total leverage effect on EPS.
- **Indifference point** is the EBIT level where two financing plans yield identical EPS; above this, debt-heavy plan is better.
- Cost of capital is the **discount rate** in NPV/IRR — accept projects where IRR > WACC.
- In India, $R_f$ is typically proxied by the **10-year Government Security (G-Sec) yield**.
- Retained earnings have a cost equal to opportunity cost — they are not "free."
- **Marginal vs. Average Cost of Capital:** Always use **marginal cost of capital** for new project decisions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ROIC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET635 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ROIC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Return on Invested Capital (ROIC)** is a **profitability and capital-efficiency ratio** that quantifies the **after-tax operating profit (NOPAT)** a firm generates per unit of **total capital deployed** (equity + interest-bearing debt) in its core business operations. It measures the **returns earned on the productive capital base** of a firm, stripping out the effect of capital structure and non-operating distortions.

Mathematically, in its canonical KTU examination form:

$$ROIC = \frac{NOPAT}{Invested\ Capital}$$

where:
- **NOPAT** = Net Operating Profit After Tax = EBIT × (1 − t)
- **Invested Capital** = Equity + Interest-Bearing Debt − Excess Cash (or equivalently, Total Operating Assets − Non-Interest-Bearing Operating Liabilities)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** ROIC is a **core capital-allocation metric** in Financial Engineering. It is the single most important ratio for testing whether a firm's strategy actually creates **shareholder economic value** when compared against its cost of capital (WACC). The golden rule taught in KTU Module 4 is: **Value is created only when ROIC > WACC**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Tea Stall" Analogy** 🍵
>
> Imagine Ramesh sets up a tea stall with **₹2,00,000** of his own savings plus a **₹1,00,000** bank loan (so Invested Capital = ₹3,00,000). At the end of the year, his **operating profit** (after deducting milk, sugar, gas, rent, salary — but *before* paying interest on the loan) is **₹90,000**. He pays **20% tax** on it, so NOPAT = ₹90,000 × 0.8 = **₹72,000**.
>
> Therefore: **ROIC = 72,000 / 3,00,000 = 24%**.
>
> Now, if the bank loan cost him **12%** and his own money (equity) could have earned **15%** elsewhere, his **WACC ≈ 13%**. Since **24% > 13%**, Ramesh is **creating economic value** for every rupee deployed. If instead his ROIC was only **10%**, he would actually be **destroying value** — better to park the money in a bank FD.

This analogy makes the abstract notion of "invested capital" tangible: it is the **money working hard inside the business** — both borrowed and owned.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Exam Recall)

- **EBIT** = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (the firm's pure operating profit)
- **NOPAT** = Net Operating Profit After Tax (operating profit scaled for the tax shield that would exist even without debt)
- **Invested Capital (IC)** = Total capital tied up in operations
- **WACC** = Weighted Average Cost of Capital
- **EVA** = Economic Value Added = (ROIC − WACC) × Invested Capital
- **Spread** = ROIC − WACC (positive = value creation)

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always remember that NOPAT uses the *unlevered* tax rate — i.e., the tax rate that would apply if the firm were **100% equity-financed**. This is what makes ROIC comparable across firms with different debt-equity ratios.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why ROIC Exists — The Logic Chain

Financial Engineering Module 4 builds ROIC on four logical pillars. Each must be understood sequentially:

1. **Operating vs. Financing Separation:** A firm's profits come from *operations* (selling goods/services) and *financing* (debt vs. equity mix). To judge management's operating skill fairly, we strip out financing. **NOPAT does exactly this.**

2. **Capital is a Scarce Resource:** Every rupee tied up in machines, inventory, or receivables has an *opportunity cost* — it could have been used elsewhere. **ROIC measures the productivity of this scarce capital.**

3. **Comparability Across Firms and Time:** Two firms with different sizes and capital structures cannot be compared using EPS or ROE alone. **ROIC normalizes for both**, making cross-sectional and longitudinal analysis possible.

4. **Value Creation Test:** The single most important use in corporate finance. **If ROIC > WACC → firm creates value. If ROIC < WACC → firm destroys value.** This is the bedrock of capital budgeting and M&A analysis.

### 2.2 The Two Drivers of ROIC (DuPont-Style Decomposition)

The KTU syllabus frequently tests the **decomposition identity**, which splits ROIC into a *profit-margin* component and an *asset-turnover* component:

$$ROIC = \frac{NOPAT}{Revenue} \times \frac{Revenue}{Invested\ Capital} = NOPAT\ Margin \times Capital\ Turnover$$

This identity is powerful: it tells the analyst **whether** a firm earns high returns through *pricing power* (high NOPAT margin, typical of consumer staples) or through *capital efficiency* (high turnover, typical of retail).

### 2.3 NOPAT — The Building Block

NOPAT is the after-tax operating profit available to **all capital providers** (debt + equity), assuming no debt-related tax shield distortion. It can be calculated two ways:

- **Method 1 (Top-down, preferred in KTU exams):** Start from EBIT, apply tax rate
- **Method 2 (Bottom-up):** Start from Net Income, add back after-tax interest expense

$$NOPAT = EBIT \times (1 - t)$$

$$NOPAT = Net\ Income + Interest\ Expense \times (1 - t)$$

### 2.4 Invested Capital — Three Accepted Definitions

KTU examiners accept any of the following, but expect you to **state which one you are using**:

| Definition | Formula | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| Net Operating Assets | Total Assets − Non-Interest-Bearing Current Liabilities (e.g., Accounts Payable) | Operating-performance view (Tobin's Q literature) |
| Net Financial Debt | Equity + Interest-Bearing Debt − Excess Cash | Capital-provider view (most common) |
| Total Capital Employed | Shareholders' Equity + Long-term Debt | Simpler textbook view |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Do **not** subtract Accounts Payable from total assets *and* simultaneously use Net Debt = Equity + Debt − Cash. These two methods give **different** numerical answers. Pick one and stick with it.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $NOPAT = EBIT \times (1-t)$ | After-tax operating profit | Currency units (₹) |
| 2 | $IC = Equity + Debt - Excess\ Cash$ | Invested Capital | Currency units (₹) |
| 3 | $ROIC = NOPAT \div IC$ | Return on Invested Capital | Dimensionless (%) |
| 4 | $NOPAT\ Margin = NOPAT \div Revenue$ | Operating profitability per ₹ of sales | % |
| 5 | $Capital\ Turnover = Revenue \div IC$ | Sales generated per ₹ of capital | Times (x) |
| 6 | $ROIC = NOPAT\ Margin \times Capital\ Turnover$ | DuPont identity | % |
| 7 | $EVA = (ROIC - WACC) \times IC$ | Economic Value Added | Currency units (₹) |
| 8 | $Spread = ROIC - WACC$ | Value creation indicator | % |
| 9 | $Value\ Created \iff ROIC > WACC$ | Golden rule of capital allocation | Boolean |
| 10 | $Unlevered\ Cost\ of\ Equity = WACC + (WACC - r_d) \times (D/E)$ | For computing NOPAT tax adjustment | % |

> **Note on table notation:** Absolute values and conditional bars are written using `\vert` and `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity (no raw pipe symbols inside cells).

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Financial Engineering

- **Capital Budgeting:** ROIC is the *first-pass screen* for any project — its hurdle rate is the WACC, and ROIC of the new project must clear it.
- **M&A Valuation:** A target's sustainable ROIC (vs. acquirer's WACC) determines whether the deal creates or destroys value.
- **Equity Research:** Buy-side analysts use ROIC trends to identify *moats* (sustained ROIC > WACC over 5+ years signals a competitive advantage).
- **Credit Analysis:** Rating agencies (Moody's, S&P) track ROIC to assess the *quality* of a firm's earnings.
- **Performance Management:** Compensation committees increasingly use ROIC-based bonuses to align management with shareholder interests.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: NOPAT from EBIT

**Step 1 — Start with EBIT (operating profit).**
EBIT is reported directly in the income statement (or computed as Revenue − COGS − Operating Expenses).

**Step 2 — Apply the tax adjustment.**
Theoretically, if the firm had no debt, its tax bill would be $EBIT \times t$. Since we want the operating profit available to *all* capital providers on an *unlevered* basis, we deduct this hypothetical tax:

$$
NOPAT = EBIT \times (1 - t)
$$

**Step 3 — Justify the tax rate.**
The tax rate $t$ is the **effective marginal corporate tax rate** prevailing in the firm's jurisdiction (e.g., in India, the corporate tax rate is **25.17%** for domestic companies with turnover ≤ ₹400 cr, and **30%** for others, per the Finance Act). Use the rate that would apply to operating income in a debt-free scenario.

**Step 4 — Alternative bottom-up derivation.**
From Net Income, add back the after-tax interest that was deducted:

$$
NOPAT = Net\ Income + Interest\ Expense \times (1 - t)
$$

> **Why the two are equal:** $Net\ Income = (EBIT - Interest) \times (1 - t)$, so $Net\ Income + Interest \times (1 - t) = EBIT \times (1 - t) = NOPAT$. Both yield the same answer.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Invested Capital from the Balance Sheet

**Step 1 — Identify the operating-asset definition.**

$$
IC_{OA} = Total\ Assets - Non-Interest-Bearing\ Current\ Liabilities
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the financial-capital definition.**

$$
IC_{FC} = Equity + Interest-Bearing\ Debt - Excess\ Cash
$$

**Step 3 — Show equivalence (advanced).**
By the accounting identity, $Total\ Assets = Equity + Debt + Non-Operating\ Liabilities$ and $Total\ Liabilities = Interest\text{-}Bearing\ Debt + Non\text{-}Interest\text{-}Bearing\ Current\ Liabilities + Non\text{-}Operating\ Liabilities$. Substituting and simplifying yields the equality (assuming excess cash is non-operating).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: DuPont Decomposition of ROIC

**Step 1 — Multiply by Revenue/Revenue (= 1).**

$$
ROIC = \frac{NOPAT}{IC} \times \frac{Revenue}{Revenue}
$$

**Step 2 — Regroup as two ratios.**

$$
ROIC = \frac{NOPAT}{Revenue} \times \frac{Revenue}{IC}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the two components.**

$$
ROIC = NOPAT\ Margin \times Capital\ Turnover
$$

**Step 4 — Economic interpretation.**
- If NOPAT Margin is **high** (e.g., software firms at 25%+) → firm has **pricing power / brand moat**.
- If Capital Turnover is **high** (e.g., retail at 3x–5x) → firm has **superior asset utilization**.
- ROIC is the product; improving either driver lifts ROIC multiplicatively.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

**Given (Kerala Steels Ltd., FY 2023–24):**

| Item | Value (₹ crore) |
|---|---|
| Revenue | 1,200 |
| COGS | 720 |
| Operating Expenses | 240 |
| Interest Expense | 60 |
| Corporate Tax Rate (t) | 25% |
| Total Equity | 500 |
| Total Debt (interest-bearing) | 300 |
| Excess Cash & Equivalents | 50 |
| Total Assets | 1,000 |
| Accounts Payable (non-interest-bearing) | 200 |

**Step 1 — Compute EBIT.**

$$
EBIT = Revenue - COGS - Operating\ Expenses = 1200 - 720 - 240 = 240
$$

**Step 2 — Compute NOPAT.**

$$
NOPAT = 240 \times (1 - 0.25) = 240 \times 0.75 = 180
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Invested Capital (using financial-capital method).**

$$
IC = 500 + 300 - 50 = 750
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ROIC.**

$$
ROIC = \frac{180}{750} = 0.24 = 24\%
$$

**Step 5 — Decompose via DuPont.**

$$
NOPAT\ Margin = \frac{180}{1200} = 15\%
$$

$$
Capital\ Turnover = \frac{1200}{750} = 1.6\ \text{times}
$$

$$
ROIC = 15\% \times 1.6 = 24\%\ \ \checkmark
$$

**Step 6 — Compute WACC (assume cost of equity = 16%, cost of debt = 10%).**

$$
WACC = \frac{500}{800} \times 16\% + \frac{300}{800} \times 10\% \times (1 - 0.25)
$$

$$
WACC = 0.625 \times 16\% + 0.375 \times 7.5\% = 10\% + 2.8125\% = 12.8125\%
$$

**Step 7 — Value Creation Test.**

$$
Spread = 24\% - 12.8125\% = +11.1875\% \quad (> 0) \implies \text{Value Created}
$$

**Step 8 — Economic Value Added (EVA).**

$$
EVA = (ROIC - WACC) \times IC = 0.111875 \times 750 = 83.91\ \text{crore}
$$

This ₹83.91 crore is the **economic profit** Kerala Steels earned above its cost of capital in FY 2023–24 — a clean, single-number summary of value creation.

### 3.5 Summary Matrix — Interpretation Bands for Indian Context

| ROIC Range | Interpretation | Typical Industry |
|---|---|---|
| < 8% | Below cost of capital; value destruction | Heavy infrastructure, airlines |
| 8% – 12% | At or near WACC; break-even on economic basis | Mature manufacturing, utilities |
| 12% – 20% | Solid value creation | FMCG, specialty chemicals |
| 20% – 40% | Strong moat / superior capital allocation | Software, branded pharma |
| > 40% | Exceptional; verify sustainability (one-offs, accounting) | Rare; check sustainability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Convention:** A *single-period* ROIC of 15% is good; a *5-year average* ROIC of 15% is excellent. Always state the time frame.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1: ROIC Calculation Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Income Statement] --> B[Identify EBIT]
    A --> C[Identify Tax Rate t]
    B --> D[Compute NOPAT = EBIT x 1 - t]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Numerator: NOPAT]

    F[Start: Balance Sheet] --> G[Identify Equity]
    F --> H[Identify Interest-Bearing Debt]
    F --> I[Identify Excess Cash]
    G --> J[Compute Invested Capital = Equity + Debt - Excess Cash]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Denominator: Invested Capital]

    E --> L[Divide NOPAT by Invested Capital]
    K --> L
    L --> M[ROIC = Percentage Return on Capital]

    M --> N{Compare to WACC}
    N -->|ROIC greater than WACC| O[Value Created: Positive EVA]
    N -->|ROIC less than WACC| P[Value Destroyed: Negative EVA]
    N -->|ROIC equals WACC| Q[Break-even: EVA = 0]
```

### 4.2 Figure 2: DuPont Decomposition Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ROIC] --> B[NOPAT Margin]
    A --> C[Capital Turnover]
    B --> D[NOPAT divided by Revenue]
    C --> E[Revenue divided by Invested Capital]
    D --> F[Pricing Power and Cost Control]
    E --> G[Asset Utilization Efficiency]
    F --> H[Product of two drivers equals ROIC]
    G --> H
```

### 4.3 Figure 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture — ROIC in Financial Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inputs
        A1[Income Statement Data: Revenue, COGS, OpEx, EBIT]
        A2[Balance Sheet Data: Equity, Debt, Cash, Total Assets]
        A3[Market Data: Cost of Equity, Cost of Debt, Tax Rate]
    end

    subgraph Core Computations
        B1[NOPAT Engine]
        B2[Invested Capital Engine]
        B3[WACC Engine]
    end

    subgraph ROIC Output Layer
        C1[ROIC Ratio Computation]
        C2[DuPont Decomposition]
        C3[EVA Computation]
        C4[Value Creation Verdict]
    end

    subgraph Decision Support
        D1[Capital Budgeting Approval Gate]
        D2[M and A Target Screening]
        D3[Management Compensation Trigger]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    B2 --> C2
    A1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    B2 --> C3
    B3 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    B3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
    C4 --> D3
```

### 4.4 Figure 4: Time-Decision Tree — When to Trust ROIC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Computed ROIC] --> B{Consistent over 5 plus years?}
    B -->|Yes| C[High Confidence in Capital Efficiency]
    B -->|No| D{Is deviation explained by one-off event?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Adjust for one-off; use normalized ROIC]
    D -->|No| F[Investigate further: accounting changes or capital structure shift]
    E --> G[Apply to Value Creation Test]
    C --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{ROIC greater than WACC?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Reasonable to Invest / Hold]
    H -->|No| J[Avoid or Divest]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define Return on Invested Capital (ROIC). State the formula and explain why it is considered superior to Return on Equity (ROE) for cross-firm comparison.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** ROIC is the ratio of a firm's Net Operating Profit After Tax (NOPAT) to its total Invested Capital, expressed as a percentage. It measures how efficiently a firm converts the capital deployed in its operations into after-tax operating profits.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$ROIC = \frac{NOPAT}{Invested\ Capital} = \frac{EBIT \times (1 - t)}{Equity + Debt - Excess\ Cash}$$

**Superiority over ROE (1 mark):** ROE is affected by the firm's capital structure (leverage inflates ROE mechanically). ROIC neutralizes this effect by using NOPAT (which strips out the financing benefit) and dividing by *all* invested capital (debt + equity). Hence, ROIC allows fair comparison between firms with different debt-equity ratios, while ROE does not.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain the DuPont decomposition of ROIC. What does each component measure?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Identity (1.5 marks):**

$$ROIC = NOPAT\ Margin \times Capital\ Turnover = \frac{NOPAT}{Revenue} \times \frac{Revenue}{Invested\ Capital}$$

**NOPAT Margin (0.75 mark):** Measures **operating profitability per rupee of sales**, capturing pricing power, cost control, and operating efficiency. Higher margins indicate a competitive moat or product differentiation.

**Capital Turnover (0.75 mark):** Measures **sales generated per rupee of invested capital**, capturing asset-utilization efficiency. Higher turnover indicates lean operations (typical of retail and distribution businesses).

---

### Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Module 4, ESE Pattern]**
*Gamma Industries Ltd. has the following financial data for the financial year 2023–24:*

| Particulars | Amount (₹ lakh) |
|---|---|
| Total Revenue | 5,000 |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 2,800 |
| Operating Expenses | 1,200 |
| Interest Expense | 400 |
| Tax Rate | 30% |
| Total Shareholders' Equity | 3,000 |
| Long-term Debt (interest-bearing) | 2,000 |
| Cash & Cash Equivalents | 500 |
| Cost of Equity | 14% |
| Cost of Debt (pre-tax) | 9% |

**(a) Compute the NOPAT, Invested Capital, and ROIC of the firm. (7 marks)**

**(b) Compute the WACC and Economic Value Added (EVA). Comment on whether the firm is creating or destroying shareholder value. (7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Compute EBIT (1 mark):**

$$
EBIT = 5000 - 2800 - 1200 = 1000\ lakh
$$

**Step 2 — Compute NOPAT (2 marks):**

$$
NOPAT = EBIT \times (1 - t) = 1000 \times (1 - 0.30) = 1000 \times 0.70 = 700\ lakh
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Invested Capital (2 marks):**

Using the financial-capital definition:

$$
IC = Equity + Debt - Excess\ Cash = 3000 + 2000 - 500 = 4500\ lakh
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ROIC (2 marks):**

$$
ROIC = \frac{NOPAT}{IC} = \frac{700}{4500} = 0.1556 = 15.56\%
$$

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute after-tax cost of debt (1 mark):**

$$
r_d(1-t) = 9\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 6.3\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total capital weights (1 mark):**

$$
Total\ Capital = Equity + Debt = 3000 + 2000 = 5000\ lakh
$$

$$
w_e = \frac{3000}{5000} = 0.60
$$

$$
w_d = \frac{2000}{5000} = 0.40
$$

**Step 3 — Compute WACC (2 marks):**

$$
WACC = w_e \times r_e + w_d \times r_d(1-t) = 0.60 \times 14\% + 0.40 \times 6.3\%
$$

$$
WACC = 8.4\% + 2.52\% = 10.92\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Spread (1 mark):**

$$
Spread = ROIC - WACC = 15.56\% - 10.92\% = +4.64\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute EVA (1 mark):**

$$
EVA = (ROIC - WACC) \times IC = 0.0464 \times 4500 = 208.8\ lakh
$$

**Step 6 — Comment on value creation (1 mark):**

Since $ROIC = 15.56\% > WACC = 10.92\%$, and $EVA = ₹208.8\ lakh > 0$, **Gamma Industries is creating shareholder value**. The positive spread of +4.64 percentage points indicates the firm earns more than its cost of capital on every rupee deployed, generating a real economic profit of ₹208.8 lakh in FY 2023–24.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Module 4, ESE Pattern]**
*Delta Pharmaceuticals Ltd. reports the following:*

| Particulars | Amount (₹ crore) |
|---|---|
| Net Income | 480 |
| Interest Expense | 120 |
| Tax Rate | 25% |
| Revenue | 6,000 |
| Total Assets | 4,000 |
| Accounts Payable | 800 |
| Short-term non-interest-bearing liabilities | 200 |
| Cost of Equity | 13% |
| Pre-tax Cost of Debt | 8% |
| Total Equity (book) | 2,400 |
| Total Debt (interest-bearing) | 1,600 |

**(a) Compute ROIC using both the operating-asset and financial-capital definitions of Invested Capital, and verify the DuPont decomposition. (7 marks)**

**(b) If the company has identified a new project requiring an investment of ₹800 crore, expected to generate NOPAT of ₹140 crore annually, advise whether the project should be accepted using the ROIC–WACC criterion. (7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Compute NOPAT (1 mark):**

Using bottom-up method:

$$
NOPAT = Net\ Income + Interest \times (1-t) = 480 + 120 \times 0.75 = 480 + 90 = 570\ crore
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Invested Capital — Method 1 (Operating Assets) (1.5 marks):**

Non-interest-bearing current liabilities = Accounts Payable + ST non-IBL = 800 + 200 = 1000 crore

$$
IC_{OA} = 4000 - 1000 = 3000\ crore
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Invested Capital — Method 2 (Financial Capital) (1.5 marks):**

Excess cash is not separately given; assume it is embedded in the asset side. If we assume no excess cash:

$$
IC_{FC} = 2400 + 1600 - 0 = 4000\ crore
$$

*(Note: The two definitions differ because the operating-asset view excludes non-interest-bearing liabilities while the financial-capital view includes them implicitly. Students should state this caveat.)*

**Step 4 — Compute ROIC under each method (1.5 marks):**

$$
ROIC_{OA} = \frac{570}{3000} = 19.0\%
$$

$$
ROIC_{FC} = \frac{570}{4000} = 14.25\%
$$

**Step 5 — DuPont verification (1.5 marks):**

$$
NOPAT\ Margin = \frac{570}{6000} = 9.5\%
$$

$$
Capital\ Turnover_{OA} = \frac{6000}{3000} = 2.0\ x
$$

$$
ROIC_{OA} = 9.5\% \times 2.0 = 19.0\% \ \checkmark
$$

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute WACC (2 marks):**

$$
w_e = \frac{2400}{4000} = 0.60,\ \ w_d = \frac{1600}{4000} = 0.40
$$

$$
WACC = 0.60 \times 13\% + 0.40 \times 8\% \times (1 - 0.25) = 7.8\% + 2.4\% = 10.2\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute project ROIC (2 marks):**

$$
ROIC_{project} = \frac{140}{800} = 17.5\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compare (1 mark):**

$$
ROIC_{project} = 17.5\% > WACC = 10.2\% \implies Accept\ the\ project
$$

**Step 4 — Compute incremental EVA (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta EVA = (17.5\% - 10.2\%) \times 800 = 0.073 \times 800 = 58.4\ crore
$$

**Step 5 — Strategic comment (1 mark):**

The project's ROIC of 17.5% exceeds the firm's WACC of 10.2% by a comfortable spread of +7.3 percentage points, generating an incremental EVA of ₹58.4 crore annually. The project should be **accepted** because it enhances shareholder economic value. Further, the project's ROIC (17.5%) is in line with the firm's existing operating-asset ROIC (19.0%), indicating the investment is consistent with the firm's current capital-efficiency profile.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks)**
>
> 1. **Wrong tax base in NOPAT:** Using the *effective* tax rate from the income statement instead of the *marginal/statutory* corporate tax rate. KTU expects the statutory rate.
> 2. **Including interest in NOPAT:** Some students use Net Income directly. **Never do this** — it re-introduces the financing effect that ROIC is designed to strip out.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract excess cash:** Excess cash is *not* productive invested capital. Always subtract it from the financial-capital definition.
> 4. **Mixing IC definitions mid-problem:** Choose one definition (operating assets OR financial capital) and use it consistently.
> 5. **Confusing ROIC with ROE or ROA:** ROIC uses *operating* profit (EBIT-based) and *all* productive capital. ROE uses bottom-line profit. ROA uses *total* assets including non-operating ones.
> 6. **Sign error in EVA:** If ROIC < WACC, EVA is *negative* — students often write the magnitude and forget the negative sign.
> 7. **Not stating the time frame:** ROIC of 25% in one year is meaningless. State the *period* and ideally use a *5-year average* for board-exam reasoning.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **ROIC = NOPAT / Invested Capital** — the single most important ratio in Financial Engineering Module 4.
- ✅ **NOPAT = EBIT × (1 − t)** — the unlevered, after-tax operating profit.
- ✅ **Invested Capital** = Equity + Interest-Bearing Debt − Excess Cash (or equivalently, Operating Assets − Non-Interest-Bearing Operating Liabilities).
- ✅ **DuPont Identity:** $ROIC = NOPAT\ Margin \times Capital\ Turnover$.
- ✅ **Value Creation Rule:** Value is created **iff** $ROIC > WACC$. Always state this conditional explicitly in exam answers.
- ✅ **EVA = (ROIC − WACC) × Invested Capital** — the absolute rupee amount of value created (or destroyed).
- ✅ **Use statutory tax rate**, not effective tax rate, when computing NOPAT.
- ✅ **Strip out non-operating items** (e.g., one-time gains, excess cash) for a clean ROIC.
- ✅ **Time-frame matters:** Always quote ROIC over a 3–5 year window for credibility.
- ✅ **ROIC > ROE companies are often under-leveraged**; **ROE > ROIC companies are often over-leveraged** — a quick capital-structure diagnostic.
- ✅ **Indian context:** Effective corporate tax rate is **25.17%** (domestic, turnover ≤ ₹400 cr) or **30%** (others); use the applicable one.
- ✅ **Two definitions of IC** are accepted; declare which one you are using at the start of the answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Growth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET635 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Growth # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Growth (in Financial Engineering):** Growth refers to the rate at which a variable of financial interest—such as cash flow, earnings, dividends, revenue, or asset value—increases over time. In quantitative finance, it is mathematically modeled as a deterministic or stochastic function of time, embedded directly into the discount-rate framework of the time-value-of-money (TVM) engine. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Module 4 of Financial Engineering), **growth** is treated as a core input parameter to almost every discounted cash flow (DCF) model.
