The canonical forms studied are: 1. **Constant (perpetual) growth** — used in the Gordon–Shapiro Dividend Discount Model. 2. **Multi-stage growth** — two-stage, H-model, and three-stage variants. 3. **Finite-period (transitory) growth** — growth that reverts to a terminal sustainable rate. 4. **Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)** — the geometric mean growth rate used for historical normalization. The fundamental equation governing growth-adjusted valuation is: $$ V_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}, \quad \text{where} \quad CF_t = CF_0 \cdot (1+g)^t $$ Where: - $V_0$ = Present value of the cash-flow stream - $CF_t$ = Cash flow in period $t$ - $g$ = Growth rate - $r$ = Discount rate (cost of capital) - $n$ = Number of periods ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Bamboo Plant:** > Imagine planting a bamboo sapling. In Year 1, it is small. In Year 2, still small. But by Year 5, growth accelerates dramatically. The rate $g$ is essentially the "stretching velocity" of the bamboo. In finance, the bamboo is your cash flow stream, and $g$ tells you how fast that stream expands before it is harvested (discounted back to today). If you overestimate $g$, you overvalue the bamboo; if you underestimate it, you undervalue the plantation. **Plain-English Takeaway for Students:** Think of **growth ($g$)** as the *speed multiplier* applied to future cash flows before they are discounted. A high-$g$ asset is like a rocket whose fuel ($g$) must outpace gravity ($r$) — otherwise the rocket never escapes (model becomes undefined). This is why the condition **$g < r$** is the most critical boundary in all growth-based valuation models. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants | Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Risk-free rate | $r_f$ | **0.04 – 0.06** (4% – 6%) | Decimal per annum | | Market risk premium | $MRP$ | **0.05 – 0.08** | Decimal per annum | | Sustainable growth rate | $g^{*}$ | **0.02 – 0.10** | Decimal per annum | | Terminal growth | $g_n$ | $\leq$ **Nominal GDP growth** (~0.03 – 0.05) | Decimal per annum | | Beta | $\beta$ | **0.5 – 2.0** | Dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Golden Rule of KTU Valuation:** $g$ **must always be strictly less than** $r$. If $g \geq r$, the present-value formula mathematically diverges to infinity — a classic board-exam trap. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Structural Decomposition of Growth Models ### A. Arithmetic (Linear) Growth Cash flows increase by a **constant absolute amount** every period. $$ CF_t = CF_1 + (t-1) \cdot \Delta $$ - $\Delta$ = Constant increment per period - **Why use it?** Simple, intuitive, applicable to stable industries (e.g., utility tariffs). - **Pitfall:** Ignores compounding — produces unrealistic long-term values. ### B. Geometric (Exponential / Compound) Growth Cash flows increase by a **constant percentage** every period. $$ CF_t = CF_0 \cdot (1+g)^t $$ - $g$ = Constant growth rate - **Why use it?** Reflects compounding; matches how businesses reinvest earnings. - **Reality check:** Most-used form in KTU/board exams. ### C. Variable / Multi-Stage Growth Growth rate itself changes over time: $$ g_t = \begin{cases} g_1 & \text{for } 1 \leq t \leq T_1 \\ g_2 & \text{for } T_1 < t \leq T_2 \\ g_n & \text{for } t > T_n \end{cases} $$ Used when a firm is in a high-growth phase then matures to a steady state. ## 2.2 Sustainable Growth Rate (SGR) The maximum rate at which a company can grow **without raising external equity** while keeping its debt-to-equity ratio constant. $$ g^{*} = ROE \times b $$ Where: - $ROE$ = Return on Equity = $\frac{\text{Net Income}}{\text{Equity}}$ - $b$ = Retention (plowback) ratio = $1 - \text{Payout Ratio}$ **Intuition:** Every rupee retained is reinvested at rate ROE → produces $ROE \times b$ new earnings per rupee of existing equity → that's the growth. ## 2.3 Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) The geometric mean growth rate that takes an initial value $V_0$ to a final value $V_n$ over $n$ periods. $$ CAGR = \left(\frac{V_n}{V_0}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1 $$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Model | Formula | Boundary / Condition | KTU Use-Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Future Value with Growth | $FV = PV \cdot (1+g)^n$ | $g \in \mathbb{R}$ | Time-value problems | | 2 | Gordon Growth Model | $P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{r-g}$ | $r > g$ | Perpetual dividend valuation | | 3 | Two-Stage DDM | $P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n}\dfrac{D_0(1+g_1)^t}{(1+r)^t} + \dfrac{D_n(1+g_2)}{(r-g_2)(1+r)^n}$ | $r > g_2$ | Growth-firm valuation | | 4 | H-Model | $P_0 = \dfrac{D_0(1+g_L)}{r-g_L} + \dfrac{D_0 \cdot H \cdot (g_S - g_L)}{r-g_L}$ | $H = T/2$ | Linear declining growth | | 5 | Sustainable Growth Rate | $g^{*} = ROE \cdot b$ | $0 \leq b \leq 1$ | Internal financing limit | | 6 | CAGR | $CAGR = \left(V_n/V_0\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | $V_0, V_n > 0$ | Historical normalization | | 7 | PEG Ratio | $PEG = \dfrac{P/E}{g \times 100}$ | $g$ in % | Growth-stock screening | | 8 | Required Return with Growth | $r = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ | $r > g$ | Cost-of-equity inference | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Growth-modeling is the foundation of **real-options analysis**, **DCF-based project appraisal**, and **merger-acquisition target valuation** — all of which appear in PEPET635 module-level case studies. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Gordon Growth Model (Perpetuity with Growth) **Starting Point:** The standard perpetuity formula without growth is $PV = \dfrac{C}{r}$. **Step 1:** Modify the cash flow. If next year's dividend grows at rate $g$ from the current dividend $D_0$, then: $$ D_1 = D_0 \cdot (1+g) $$ **Step 2:** Generalize. In year $t$, the dividend will be: $$ D_t = D_0 \cdot (1+g)^t $$ **Step 3:** Discount each future dividend back to present: $$ P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{D_0(1+g)^t}{(1+r)^t} $$ **Step 4:** Factor out $D_0$: $$ P_0 = D_0 \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1+g}{1+r}\right)^t $$ **Step 5:** Recognize this is an infinite geometric series with common ratio $q = \dfrac{1+g}{1+r}$. For $\vert q \vert < 1$, the sum equals $\dfrac{q}{1-q}$: $$ \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} q^t = \frac{q}{1-q} $$ **Step 6:** Substitute and simplify: $$ P_0 = D_0 \cdot \frac{\frac{1+g}{1+r}}{1 - \frac{1+g}{1+r}} $$ **Step 7:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1+r)$: $$ P_0 = D_0 \cdot \frac{1+g}{(1+r) - (1+g)} $$ **Step 8:** Final result: $$ P_0 = \frac{D_0(1+g)}{r - g} = \frac{D_1}{r - g} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Convergence condition:** We require $\left\vert \frac{1+g}{1+r} \right\vert < 1$, which forces $\mathbf{r > g}$. This is the single most-tested boundary in KTU valuation questions. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sustainable Growth Rate from DuPont Identity **Starting Point:** The DuPont decomposition states: $$ ROE = \frac{NI}{\text{Sales}} \cdot \frac{\text{Sales}}{\text{Assets}} \cdot \frac{\text{Assets}}{\text{Equity}} $$ **Step 1:** Define growth as change in equity from retained earnings: $$ \Delta E = NI \cdot b $$ Where $b$ = retention ratio. **Step 2:** Growth rate = change in equity / current equity: $$ g^{*} = \frac{\Delta E}{E} = \frac{NI \cdot b}{E} $$ **Step 3:** Recognize $\dfrac{NI}{E} = ROE$: $$ g^{*} = ROE \cdot b $$ **Step 4:** Substitute the DuPont identity for ROE: $$ g^{*} = \left(\frac{NI}{\text{Sales}}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\text{Sales}}{\text{Assets}}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\text{Assets}}{\text{Equity}}\right) \cdot b $$ This is the **four-variable sustainable growth formula** widely tested in KTU module-case questions. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Two-Stage Dividend Discount Model **Setup:** A firm grows at rate $g_1$ for $n$ years, then settles to perpetual $g_2$. **Step 1:** Sum the explicit high-growth period (years 1 to $n$): $$ P_{\text{stage 1}} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{D_0(1+g_1)^t}{(1+r)^t} $$ **Step 2:** Compute the terminal value at end of year $n$ using Gordon's formula: $$ TV_n = \frac{D_0(1+g_1)^n(1+g_2)}{r - g_2} $$ **Step 3:** Discount the terminal value back to today: $$ P_{\text{terminal}} = \frac{TV_n}{(1+r)^n} $$ **Step 4:** Combine both components: $$ P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{D_0(1+g_1)^t}{(1+r)^t} + \frac{D_0(1+g_1)^n(1+g_2)}{(r - g_2)(1+r)^n} $$ **Step 5:** The first sum is a finite geometric series. Using closed-form: $$ \sum_{t=1}^{n} q^t = q \cdot \frac{1 - q^n}{1 - q}, \quad q = \frac{1+g_1}{1+r} $$ Therefore: $$ P_{\text{stage 1}} = D_0 \cdot \frac{(1+g_1)\left[1 - \left(\frac{1+g_1}{1+r}\right)^n\right]}{r - g_1} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Tip:** Always substitute numerical values directly into the closed-form expression to avoid arithmetic slip-ups in the valuation table. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem (KTU-style):** A stock just paid a dividend of ₹10. Dividends are expected to grow at 12% per year for 3 years, then at a constant 4% thereafter. Required return is 10%. Find the current stock price. **Step 1: Identify parameters.** $$ D_0 = 10, \quad g_1 = 0.12, \quad g_2 = 0.04, \quad r = 0.10, \quad n = 3 $$ **Step 2: Forecast dividends for years 1, 2, 3.** $$ D_1 = 10 \cdot 1.12 = 11.20 $$ $$ D_2 = 11.20 \cdot 1.12 = 12.544 $$ $$ D_3 = 12.544 \cdot 1.12 = 14.04928 $$ **Step 3: Compute the terminal value at end of year 3.** $$ TV_3 = \frac{D_3 \cdot (1+g_2)}{r - g_2} = \frac{14.04928 \cdot 1.04}{0.10 - 0.04} $$ $$ TV_3 = \frac{14.61125}{0.06} = 243.5208 $$ **Step 4: Discount all cash flows to present.** $$ P_{\text{stage 1}} = \frac{11.20}{1.10} + \frac{12.544}{1.21} + \frac{14.04928}{1.331} $$ $$ P_{\text{stage 1}} = 10.1818 + 10.3678 + 10.5553 = 31.1049 $$ $$ P_{\text{terminal}} = \frac{243.5208}{1.331} = 182.9610 $$ **Step 5: Sum.** $$ P_0 = 31.1049 + 182.9610 = 214.0659 $$ **Answer:** $P_0 \approx \mathbf{₹214.07}$ # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Growth-Model Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode1[Identify Cash Flow Type] --> decisionA{Is growth
constant forever?} decisionA -- Yes --> nodeB[Use Gordon Growth Model] decisionA -- No --> decisionB{Does growth
change in stages?} decisionB -- Yes, two stages --> nodeC[Two-Stage DDM] decisionB -- Yes, three stages --> nodeD[Three-Stage DDM] decisionB -- Yes, linear decline --> nodeE[H-Model] decisionB -- No, finite period --> nodeF[Finite Period DCF] nodeC --> checkA1[Verify r greater than g] nodeD --> checkA1 nodeE --> checkA1 nodeB --> checkA1 checkA1 -- Pass --> resultA[Compute Present Value] checkA1 -- Fail --> warnA1[Model Diverges - Reject] ``` ## 4.2 Growth Rate Classification Map ```mermaid graph LR rootN1[Growth g] --> categoryA[Deterministic] rootN1 --> categoryB[Stochastic] categoryA --> subA1[Constant g] categoryA --> subA2[Multi-Stage g] categoryA --> subA3[Linear Decline H-Model] categoryB --> subB1[Geometric Brownian Motion] categoryB --> subB2[Mean-Reverting Process] categoryB --> subB3[Regime Switching Model] subA1 --> eqN1[CF equals CF0 times 1 plus g to power t] subA2 --> eqN2[Different g values per stage] subB1 --> eqN3[dS equals mu S dt plus sigma S dW] ``` ## 4.3 Sustainable Growth Rate Engine (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD inputA[Net Income NI] --> computeStepA inputB[Total Equity E] --> computeStepA computeStepA[Compute ROE equals NI divided by E] --> computeStepB inputC[Payout Ratio p] --> computeStepD computeStepD[Compute Retention b equals 1 minus p] --> computeStepB computeStepB[Compute Sustainable Growth g star equals ROE times b] --> outputNode1[Maximum Internal Growth Rate] outputNode1 --> compareNode1{g star exceeds industry average?} compareNode1 -- Yes --> actionA1[Firm has competitive moat] compareNode1 -- No --> actionA2[Requires external equity] ``` ## 4.4 Two-Stage Growth Timeline Schematic ```mermaid gantt title Two-Stage Growth Timeline dateFormat YYYY axisFormat Year section Stage 1 High Growth Growth g1 (12%) :a1, 2024, 3y section Stage 2 Stable Growth Growth g2 (4%) :a2, 2027, 50y ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define sustainable growth rate (SGR) of a firm and state its formula.** **Model Answer:** Sustainable Growth Rate (SGR) is the maximum rate at which a firm can grow its sales, earnings, and dividends **without raising external equity financing** while maintaining a constant debt-to-equity ratio. $$ g^{*} = ROE \times b $$ Where $ROE$ = Return on Equity and $b$ = Retention ratio = $1 -$ dividend payout ratio. **3 marks** (1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for component explanation). --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Why is the condition $r > g$ mandatory in the Gordon Growth Model?** **Model Answer:** The Gordon Growth Model formula $P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{r-g}$ is derived from an infinite geometric series with common ratio $q = \dfrac{1+g}{1+r}$. For convergence, $\vert q \vert < 1$, which mathematically requires $r > g$. If $r \leq g$, the present value diverges to infinity, making valuation meaningless. **3 marks** (1 for series reference, 1 for divergence logic, 1 for economic intuition). --- ## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **A firm's last paid dividend was ₹8. The firm is expected to grow at 15% per year for the next 4 years, after which the growth rate will drop to a perpetual 5%. The required rate of return is 12%.** **(a)** Compute the present value of all dividends during the high-growth period. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compute the terminal value at the end of year 4 and hence find the current intrinsic value of the stock. **(7 marks)** --- #### SOLUTION — Question A **Given:** $D_0 = 8$, $g_1 = 0.15$, $g_2 = 0.05$, $r = 0.12$, $n = 4$. **Part (a) — High-Growth Stage Valuation** **Step 1:** Forecast dividends for years 1 to 4. $$ D_1 = 8 \times 1.15 = 9.20 $$ $$ D_2 = 9.20 \times 1.15 = 10.58 $$ $$ D_3 = 10.58 \times 1.15 = 12.167 $$ $$ D_4 = 12.167 \times 1.15 = 13.9921 $$ **Step 2:** Discount each to present at $r = 12\%$. $$ PV(D_1) = \frac{9.20}{1.12} = 8.2143 $$ $$ PV(D_2) = \frac{10.58}{1.12^2} = \frac{10.58}{1.2544} = 8.4357 $$ $$ PV(D_3) = \frac{12.167}{1.12^3} = \frac{12.167}{1.4049} = 8.6605 $$ $$ PV(D_4) = \frac{13.9921}{1.12^4} = \frac{13.9921}{1.5735} = 8.8931 $$ **Step 3:** Sum the present values. $$ P_{\text{stage 1}} = 8.2143 + 8.4357 + 8.6605 + 8.8931 = 34.2036 $$ **Answer:** $\boxed{P_{\text{stage 1}} \approx ₹34.20}$ — **7 marks** --- **Part (b) — Terminal Value and Intrinsic Stock Price** **Step 1:** Compute year-5 dividend (first dividend of stable-growth phase). $$ D_5 = D_4 \times (1+g_2) = 13.9921 \times 1.05 = 14.6917 $$ **Step 2:** Apply Gordon's formula to compute $TV_4$ (terminal value at end of year 4). $$ TV_4 = \frac{D_5}{r - g_2} = \frac{14.6917}{0.12 - 0.05} = \frac{14.6917}{0.07} = 209.8814 $$ **Step 3:** Discount terminal value to present. $$ PV(TV_4) = \frac{209.8814}{1.12^4} = \frac{209.8814}{1.5735} = 133.3823 $$ **Step 4:** Add stage-1 PV and terminal PV. $$ P_0 = 34.2036 + 133.3823 = 167.5859 $$ **Answer:** $\boxed{P_0 \approx ₹167.59}$ — **7 marks** --- ### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice) **A stock is currently priced at ₹450. The current dividend is ₹12, and the firm has a beta of 1.2. The market risk premium is 6%, and the risk-free rate is 5%.** **(a)** Using the CAPM and the Gordon Growth Model, find the implied constant growth rate of the firm's dividends. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the firm's actual growth rate is 8%, calculate the sustainable growth rate given ROE = 18% and a payout ratio of 40%. Comment on whether the firm can sustain the 8% growth from internal sources. **(7 marks)** --- #### SOLUTION — Question B **Given:** $P_0 = 450$, $D_0 = 12$, $\beta = 1.2$, $MRP = 0.06$, $r_f = 0.05$, $g_{\text{actual}} = 0.08$, $ROE = 0.18$, payout $= 0.40$. **Part (a) — Implied Growth Rate via CAPM + Gordon** **Step 1:** Compute required return via CAPM. $$ r = r_f + \beta \cdot MRP = 0.05 + 1.2 \times 0.06 = 0.05 + 0.072 = 0.122 $$ **Step 2:** Express next-year dividend. $$ D_1 = D_0 (1+g) = 12(1+g) $$ **Step 3:** Set up the Gordon equation. $$ 450 = \frac{12(1+g)}{0.122 - g} $$ **Step 4:** Cross-multiply. $$ 450 \cdot (0.122 - g) = 12(1+g) $$ $$ 54.90 - 450g = 12 + 12g $$ **Step 5:** Solve for $g$. $$ 54.90 - 12 = 450g + 12g $$ $$ 42.90 = 462g $$ $$ g = \frac{42.90}{462} = 0.0929 $$ **Answer:** $\boxed{g_{\text{implied}} \approx 9.29\%}$ — **7 marks** --- **Part (b) — Sustainable Growth Comparison** **Step 1:** Compute retention ratio. $$ b = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60 $$ **Step 2:** Apply the SGR formula. $$ g^{*} = ROE \times b = 0.18 \times 0.60 = 0.108 $$ **Step 3:** Compare. $$ g^{*} = 10.8\% > g_{\text{actual}} = 8\% $$ **Comment:** Since the sustainable growth rate (10.8%) **exceeds** the actual growth rate (8%), the firm **can comfortably sustain** 8% growth from internal sources alone without requiring external equity issuance. The firm has unused growth capacity of $10.8\% - 8\% = 2.8\%$. **Answer:** $\boxed{g^{*} = 10.8\% \text{; firm can sustain 8\% growth internally.}}$ — **7 marks** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Never use $D_0$ instead of $D_1$** in the Gordon formula — the most common 2-mark deduction in board exams. > 2. **Forgetting to discount the terminal value** in two-stage problems leads to a 3-mark penalty. > 3. **Mixing up $g_1$ and $g_2$** in the terminal-value formula $TV_n = \dfrac{D_n(1+g_2)}{r-g_2}$ loses 2 marks. > 4. **Rounding intermediate steps**: always carry at least 4 decimal places to avoid cascading rounding errors. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Summary — Pin This Before Your Exam** - **Growth ($g$)** = percentage change in cash flow per period; central input in DCF/DDM. - **Gordon Growth Model:** $P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{r-g}$ → **only valid when $r > g$**. - **Sustainable Growth Rate:** $g^{*} = ROE \times b$ → **internal-financing ceiling**. - **CAGR:** $\left(\dfrac{V_n}{V_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ → geometric mean growth used for historical comparison. - **Two-Stage DDM:** Explicit high-growth phase + discounted Gordon terminal value. - **H-Model:** Assumes linear decline in growth from $g_S$ to $g_L$ over $2H$ years. - **CAPM + Gordon:** Used to back out implied growth from observed prices. - **Golden Rule:** $r > g$ **always** — otherwise the model diverges. - **Terminal growth ($g_n$) must not exceed nominal GDP growth** in the long run (a KTU practical-checklist item). - **Common Pitfalls:** - Using $D_0$ instead of $D_1$ in Gordon (2-mark loss). - Forgetting to discount terminal value (3-mark loss). - Confusing retention ratio $b$ with payout ratio $p$. - Ignoring convergence condition when $g$ is too close to $r$. - **Real-World Hooks:** Growth modeling underpins M&A target pricing, IPO valuation, real-estate DCF, and project finance feasibility studies.

topics.title: Valuation Techniques - Enterprise Discounted Cash Flow Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET635 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Valuation Techniques - Enterprise Discounted Cash Flow Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Enterprise Discounted Cash Flow (Enterprise DCF) Model** is an intrinsic valuation methodology used to estimate the total value of a business enterprise by projecting the future unlevered free cash flows it is expected to generate, and discounting them back to their present value using the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** as the appropriate discount rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> The Enterprise DCF Model values the firm as a whole — independent of its capital structure — by computing the **Present Value (PV)** of all future **Free Cash Flows to the Firm (FCFF)** plus the **Present Value of the Terminal Value (TV)**. The result is the **Enterprise Value (EV)**, from which net debt is subtracted to arrive at the **Equity Value**.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$EV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{FCFF_t}{(1+WACC)^t} + \frac{TV_n}{(1+WACC)^n}$$

Where:
- $EV$ = Enterprise Value
- $FCFF_t$ = Free Cash Flow to Firm in year $t$
- $WACC$ = Weighted Average Cost of Capital
- $TV_n$ = Terminal Value at the end of the explicit forecast period
- $n$ = Length of the explicit forecast horizon (typically **5 to 10 years**)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mango Tree Farmer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Mango Tree Farmer**
> Imagine you want to buy an entire mango orchard (the *enterprise*) from a farmer. You don't just count today's baskets of mangoes — you must value **every mango the tree will ever produce**. But mangoes in the year 2035 are less reliable to you than mangoes today (because of inflation, drought, disease, and impatience). So, you:
> 1. **Estimate** how many mangoes the tree will yield each year for the next 10 years (*FCFF*).
> 2. **Adjust** each future basket downward for risk + time (using *WACC*).
> 3. **Estimate** the resale value of the tree at year 10 (*Terminal Value*) and discount it too.
> 4. **Add** them all — that sum is what the *entire orchard (enterprise)* is worth today.
> 5. Subtract the **bank loan** the farmer still owes on the land, and the remainder is what you pay for the **equity** (your ownership).

This is exactly what an Enterprise DCF does — it values the **business**, not just the stock.

---

## 1.3 Why Enterprise DCF is a "Premium" Valuation Tool

> [!TIP]
> **Why it is preferred by KTU examiners:**
> - It is **capital-structure neutral** — works even if the firm changes debt levels.
> - It captures the **total claim** of all capital providers (debt + equity + preferred).
> - It directly aligns with **FCFF**, which is the cash flow available to **all** investors.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Blocks of the Enterprise DCF Model

The Enterprise DCF Model is constructed in **five rigorous stages**. Each stage is critical and carries independent marks in KTU examinations.

### Stage 1 — Forecasting Free Cash Flow to Firm (FCFF)

FCFF is the cash flow generated by the firm's operations **after** operating expenses and taxes, but **before** any payments to capital providers. It is therefore available to **both debt-holders and equity-holders**.

$$FCFF = EBIT \times (1 - T) + D\&A - CapEx - \Delta NWC$$

**Where each component means:**
- $EBIT$ = Earnings Before Interest and Taxes (Operating Profit)
- $T$ = Corporate Tax Rate (in India, generally **25.17%** for domestic companies under the new regime, **30%** for older regime, **15%** for new manufacturing)
- $D\&A$ = Depreciation & Amortization (a non-cash charge that must be **added back**)
- $CapEx$ = Capital Expenditure (investment in fixed assets)
- $\Delta NWC$ = Change in Net Working Capital

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick for FCFF:** **"E-D-C"** → **E**BIT (after-tax) + **D**epreciation − **C**hanges in NWC and CapEx

### Stage 2 — Computing the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)

WACC represents the **blended cost** the enterprise pays to finance its assets using both debt and equity.

$$WACC = \left(\frac{E}{V}\right) \cdot r_e + \left(\frac{D}{V}\right) \cdot r_d \cdot (1 - T)$$

**Where:**
- $E$ = Market Value of Equity, $D$ = Market Value of Debt
- $V = E + D$ = Total Capital (Enterprise Value, hence the closed-loop nature of DCF)
- $r_e$ = Cost of Equity (using **CAPM**: $r_e = R_f + \beta \cdot (R_m - R_f)$)
- $r_d$ = Pre-tax Cost of Debt
- $T$ = Tax Rate (interest is **tax-deductible**, hence the $(1-T)$ shield)

### Stage 3 — Choosing the Explicit Forecast Period

KTU typically assumes a **5-year** or **10-year** explicit forecast horizon. Beyond this period, we use a **Terminal Value**.

### Stage 4 — Estimating Terminal Value (TV)

The terminal value captures **all cash flows beyond the explicit forecast period**. Two KTU-acceptable methods exist:

**Method A — Gordon Growth Model (Perpetuity Growth):**

$$TV = \frac{FCFF_{n+1}}{WACC - g}$$

Where $g$ is the perpetual growth rate. KTU constraint: $g < WACC$ (strictly).

**Method B — Exit Multiple Method:**

$$TV = EBITDA_n \times \text{Exit Multiple}$$

Where the Exit Multiple (e.g., EV/EBITDA) is derived from comparable companies.

### Stage 5 — Bridging Enterprise Value to Equity Value

$$Equity\ Value = Enterprise\ Value - Net\ Debt + Cash\ \&\ Equivalents$$

$$Net\ Debt = Total\ Debt - Cash\ \&\ Short\text{-}term\ Investments$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** If a question gives "Cash & Equivalents" separately, it must be added back. If "Net Debt" is given, simply subtract.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Interpretation | Typical Units |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $FCFF = EBIT(1-T) + D\&A - CapEx - \Delta NWC$ | Unlevered cash flow | ₹ Crores |
| 2 | $r_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ | Cost of Equity (CAPM) | Percentage (%) |
| 3 | $WACC = (E/V)r_e + (D/V)r_d(1-T)$ | Blended cost of capital | Percentage (%) |
| 4 | $PV(FCFF_t) = \dfrac{FCFF_t}{(1+WACC)^t}$ | Discount each year's FCFF | ₹ Crores |
| 5 | $TV = \dfrac{FCFF_{n+1}}{WACC - g}$ | Gordon Growth Terminal Value | ₹ Crores |
| 6 | $EV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} PV(FCFF_t) + PV(TV)$ | Enterprise Value | ₹ Crores |
| 7 | $Equity\ Value = EV - Net\ Debt$ | Per-share value × shares | ₹ Crores |

> [!WARNING]
> **Strict Markdown Rule:** Vertical pipes (`|`) inside table rows break formatting. We use $\vert$ or `\mid` for absolute values throughout.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Financial Engineering

- **Mergers & Acquisitions (M&A):** Bidding firms use Enterprise DCF to value the **target firm as a whole** before negotiating the equity premium.
- **IPO Pricing:** Investment bankers anchor IPO valuations using DCF before comparing with peer multiples.
- **Capital Budgeting:** NPV calculations for projects use the same WACC discount mechanism.
- **Distressed Asset Valuation:** Restructuring advisors use Enterprise DCF to evaluate going-concern value during bankruptcy workouts.
- **Project Finance:** Used in evaluating infrastructure and renewable-energy SPVs where leverage profiles vary over time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the FCFF Formula

We begin from the perspective of **all capital providers** (debt + equity). The cash flow available to them is operating profit after tax, adjusted for non-cash charges and reinvestment needs.

**Step 1 — Start with Operating Profit (EBIT)**

EBIT is profit before any financing decision impacts are applied. This is the cash-profit-generating capacity of the firm's assets.

**Step 2 — Apply the Tax Shield**

The firm pays corporate tax on its operating profit. Since interest has **not yet been deducted**, the after-tax operating profit is:

$$NOPAT = EBIT \times (1 - T)$$

**Step 3 — Add Back Non-Cash Charges**

Depreciation & Amortization reduce accounting profit but do **not** consume cash. Hence they are added back:

$$NOPAT + D\&A = EBIT(1-T) + D\&A$$

**Step 4 — Subtract Reinvestment Needs**

The firm must invest to:
- Replace worn-out assets (**CapEx**)
- Fund growth in working capital (**ΔNWC**)

**Step 5 — Final FCFF Expression**

$$\boxed{FCFF = EBIT(1-T) + D\&A - CapEx - \Delta NWC}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style 14-Mark Format

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Beta Ltd. has the following projections for the next 4 years:
>
> | Item | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 | Year 4 |
> |------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
> | EBIT (₹ Cr) | 100 | 110 | 121 | 133 |
> | Depreciation (₹ Cr) | 20 | 22 | 24 | 26 |
> | CapEx (₹ Cr) | 30 | 32 | 34 | 36 |
> | ΔNWC (₹ Cr) | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
> | Tax Rate | 30% | 30% | 30% | 30% |
>
> The firm has a cost of equity of **12%**, pre-tax cost of debt of **8%**, debt-to-capital ratio of **40%**, and the perpetual growth rate beyond Year 4 is **3%**. The firm has a market value of debt of ₹ 200 Cr and cash of ₹ 50 Cr. There are 10 Cr shares outstanding. **Compute the Enterprise Value, Equity Value, and per-share intrinsic value.**

### Solution — Step-by-Step

#### Step A: Compute WACC

Given:
- $r_e = 12\% = 0.12$
- $r_d = 8\% = 0.08$
- $D/V = 40\% \Rightarrow E/V = 60\%$
- $T = 30\%$

$$\begin{aligned}
WACC &= \left(\frac{E}{V}\right)r_e + \left(\frac{D}{V}\right)r_d(1-T) \\
&= (0.60)(0.12) + (0.40)(0.08)(1-0.30) \\
&= 0.072 + (0.40)(0.08)(0.70) \\
&= 0.072 + 0.0224 \\
&= 0.0944
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, **WACC = 9.44%**.

#### Step B: Compute FCFF for Each Year

Using $FCFF = EBIT(1-T) + D\&A - CapEx - \Delta NWC$

**Year 1:**
$$\begin{aligned}
FCFF_1 &= 100(1-0.30) + 20 - 30 - 5 \\
&= 70 + 20 - 30 - 5 \\
&= 55 \text{ Cr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Year 2:**
$$\begin{aligned}
FCFF_2 &= 110(1-0.30) + 22 - 32 - 6 \\
&= 77 + 22 - 32 - 6 \\
&= 61 \text{ Cr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Year 3:**
$$\begin{aligned}
FCFF_3 &= 121(1-0.30) + 24 - 34 - 7 \\
&= 84.7 + 24 - 34 - 7 \\
&= 67.7 \text{ Cr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Year 4:**
$$\begin{aligned}
FCFF_4 &= 133(1-0.30) + 26 - 36 - 8 \\
&= 93.1 + 26 - 36 - 8 \\
&= 75.1 \text{ Cr}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step C: Compute Terminal Value at End of Year 4

Using Gordon Growth Model with $g = 3\% = 0.03$:

$$\begin{aligned}
FCFF_5 &= FCFF_4 \times (1+g) = 75.1 \times 1.03 = 77.353 \text{ Cr} \\
TV_4 &= \frac{FCFF_5}{WACC - g} = \frac{77.353}{0.0944 - 0.03} = \frac{77.353}{0.0644} \\
TV_4 &= 1201.13 \text{ Cr (approximately)}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step D: Discount Each Cash Flow to Present Value

**Discount Factors at WACC = 9.44%:**

$$\begin{aligned}
DF_1 &= \frac{1}{(1.0944)^1} = 0.9138 \\
DF_2 &= \frac{1}{(1.0944)^2} = 0.8350 \\
DF_3 &= \frac{1}{(1.0944)^3} = 0.7630 \\
DF_4 &= \frac{1}{(1.0944)^4} = 0.6972
\end{aligned}$$

**Present Values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
PV(FCFF_1) &= 55 \times 0.9138 = 50.26 \text{ Cr} \\
PV(FCFF_2) &= 61 \times 0.8350 = 50.94 \text{ Cr} \\
PV(FCFF_3) &= 67.7 \times 0.7630 = 51.66 \text{ Cr} \\
PV(FCFF_4) &= 75.1 \times 0.6972 = 52.36 \text{ Cr} \\
PV(TV_4) &= 1201.13 \times 0.6972 = 837.42 \text{ Cr}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step E: Sum to Get Enterprise Value

$$\begin{aligned}
EV &= 50.26 + 50.94 + 51.66 + 52.36 + 837.42 \\
EV &= 1042.64 \text{ Cr}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step F: Bridge to Equity Value

$$\begin{aligned}
Equity\ Value &= EV - Debt + Cash \\
&= 1042.64 - 200 + 50 \\
&= 892.64 \text{ Cr}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step G: Per-Share Intrinsic Value

$$\begin{aligned}
Price\ per\ Share &= \frac{Equity\ Value}{Shares\ Outstanding} \\
&= \frac{892.64}{10} \\
&= ₹ 89.26 \text{ per share}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer Box:** **Enterprise Value = ₹ 1042.64 Cr**, **Equity Value = ₹ 892.64 Cr**, **Intrinsic Share Price = ₹ 89.26**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Considerations

| Sensitivity Variable | Direction of Impact on EV | KTU Exam Trick |
|----------------------|--------------------------|----------------|
| Higher WACC | Decreases EV | Examine the $(1+WACC)^t$ denominator |
| Higher $g$ (terminal growth) | Increases TV strongly | "Long-tail" effect — small changes = large impact |
| Higher tax rate $T$ | Slightly reduces FCFF | Tax shield on debt partially offsets |
| Higher CapEx | Reduces FCFF | Distinguish maintenance vs. growth CapEx |
| Higher ΔNWC | Reduces FCFF | Common pitfall — students often miss this |
| Higher D/V ratio | Ambiguous (lower $r_e$ but adds $r_d$) | Optimal capital structure theory |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Enterprise DCF Valuation Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Project FCFF] --> B[Compute NOPAT = EBIT x 1-T]
    B --> C[Add Depreciation and Amortization]
    C --> D[Subtract Capital Expenditure]
    D --> E[Subtract Change in Net Working Capital]
    E --> F[FCFF Generated Year-wise]
    F --> G[Determine WACC using Capital Weights]
    G --> H[Discount FCFF using PV factor 1 plus WACC to power t]
    H --> I[Sum all discounted FCFF to get PV of Explicit Period]
    I --> J[Compute Terminal Value using Gordon Growth]
    J --> K[Discount Terminal Value to Present]
    K --> L[Add PV of Explicit FCFF and PV of Terminal Value]
    L --> M[Enterprise Value Calculated]
    M --> N[Subtract Net Debt from EV]
    N --> O[Add Cash if reported separately]
    O --> P[Equity Value Derived]
    P --> Q[Divide by Shares Outstanding]
    Q --> R[Intrinsic Value per Share Final]
```

## 4.2 Inputs and Outputs of the Enterprise DCF Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        A1[EBIT Forecast]
        A2[Tax Rate T]
        A3[D and A Schedule]
        A4[CapEx Plan]
        A5[Working Capital Schedule]
        A6[Capital Structure Weights]
        A7[Cost of Equity via CAPM]
        A8[Cost of Debt]
        A9[Perpetual Growth Rate g]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE
        B[Enterprise DCF Computational Engine]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS
        C1[Enterprise Value EV]
        C2[Equity Value]
        C3[Intrinsic Share Price]
        C4[Valuation Range via Sensitivity]
    end

    A1 --> B
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    A4 --> B
    A5 --> B
    A6 --> B
    A7 --> B
    A8 --> B
    A9 --> B

    B --> C1
    B --> C2
    B --> C3
    B --> C4
```

## 4.3 Bridge from Enterprise Value to Equity Value

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Enterprise Value EV] --> B[Subtract Total Debt]
    B --> C[Add Cash and Cash Equivalents]
    C --> D[Add Non-Operating Assets if any]
    D --> E[Subtract Minority Interest]
    E --> F[Subtract Preferred Stock]
    F --> G[Final Equity Value Attributable to Common Shareholders]
    G --> H[Divide by Diluted Shares Outstanding]
    H --> I[Intrinsic Value per Share]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Define the Enterprise Discounted Cash Flow Model and state the formula for Enterprise Value."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Enterprise DCF Model is an intrinsic valuation method that estimates the total value of a firm by discounting the Free Cash Flows to the Firm (FCFF) at the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC). The Enterprise Value (EV) is the sum of the present value of explicit-period FCFFs and the present value of the terminal value.
>
> $$EV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{FCFF_t}{(1+WACC)^t} + \frac{TV_n}{(1+WACC)^n}$$
>
> *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for FCFF concept, 1 mark for formula with terminal value.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between Free Cash Flow to Firm (FCFF) and Free Cash Flow to Equity (FCFE)."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Aspect | FCFF | FCFE |
> |--------|------|------|
> | Cash flow available to | All capital providers (debt + equity) | Equity shareholders only |
> | Discount rate | WACC | Cost of Equity ($r_e$) |
> | Starting point | EBIT | Net Income |
> | Tax treatment | Uses $EBIT(1-T)$ | Net Income (after interest tax shield) |
> | Capital structure | Independent | Sensitive |
>
> *(Award 1 mark each for any three meaningful distinctions.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the Enterprise Discounted Cash Flow Model in detail with its assumptions. Derive the FCFF formula starting from EBIT. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
The Enterprise DCF Model values the entire firm by discounting the Free Cash Flows to the Firm (FCFF) at the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC). The result is the Enterprise Value (EV), representing the total value attributable to all capital providers.

**2. Key Assumptions (3 marks):**
- The firm's **operating cash flows** can be reasonably forecasted for a finite horizon (typically 5–10 years).
- A **stable capital structure** (debt-to-equity ratio) is maintained over the forecast period.
- The **terminal growth rate (g)** is sustainable in perpetuity and is less than WACC.
- **WACC remains constant** throughout the explicit forecast horizon.
- Free cash flows are discounted at **mid-year or year-end** convention consistently.

**3. Derivation of FCFF (3 marks):**
Starting from EBIT, we adjust for taxes, add back non-cash items, and subtract reinvestment needs:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: After-tax operating profit} &= EBIT \times (1-T) \\
\text{Step 2: Add back non-cash charge} &= + D\&A \\
\text{Step 3: Subtract reinvestment in fixed assets} &= - CapEx \\
\text{Step 4: Subtract reinvestment in working capital} &= - \Delta NWC \\
\hline
\therefore FCFF &= EBIT(1-T) + D\&A - CapEx - \Delta NWC
\end{aligned}$$

**Q. (b)** Gamma Corp has forecasted EBIT of ₹ 500 Cr in Year 1, growing at 8% annually for 5 years. Depreciation is 20% of EBIT, CapEx is 30% of EBIT, ΔNWC is 10% of EBIT, and the tax rate is 25%. The firm's WACC is 11% and perpetual growth rate beyond Year 5 is 4%. There are 25 Cr shares outstanding, total debt of ₹ 600 Cr, and cash of ₹ 100 Cr. **Compute the Enterprise Value, Equity Value, and intrinsic share price using the Enterprise DCF Model. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Forecast EBIT, D&A, CapEx, ΔNWC for 5 Years:**

| Year | EBIT | D&A (20%) | CapEx (30%) | ΔNWC (10%) |
|------|------|-----------|-------------|------------|
| 1 | 500.00 | 100.00 | 150.00 | 50.00 |
| 2 | 540.00 | 108.00 | 162.00 | 54.00 |
| 3 | 583.20 | 116.64 | 174.96 | 58.32 |
| 4 | 629.86 | 125.97 | 188.96 | 62.99 |
| 5 | 680.04 | 136.01 | 204.01 | 68.00 |

**Step 2 — Compute FCFF:**
$$\begin{aligned}
FCFF_1 &= 500(0.75) + 100 - 150 - 50 = 375 - 100 = 275 \\
FCFF_2 &= 540(0.75) + 108 - 162 - 54 = 405 - 108 = 297 \\
FCFF_3 &= 583.20(0.75) + 116.64 - 174.96 - 58.32 = 437.40 - 116.64 = 320.76 \\
FCFF_4 &= 629.86(0.75) + 125.97 - 188.96 - 62.99 = 472.40 - 125.98 = 346.42 \\
FCFF_5 &= 680.04(0.75) + 136.01 - 204.01 - 68.00 = 510.03 - 136.00 = 374.03
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Terminal Value at Year 5:**
$$\begin{aligned}
FCFF_6 &= 374.03 \times 1.04 = 388.99 \\
TV_5 &= \frac{388.99}{0.11 - 0.04} = \frac{388.99}{0.07} = 5557.00 \text{ Cr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Discount Each Cash Flow at WACC = 11%:**

| Year | FCFF/TV | Discount Factor | PV (₹ Cr) |
|------|---------|-----------------|-----------|
| 1 | 275.00 | 0.9009 | 247.75 |
| 2 | 297.00 | 0.8116 | 241.05 |
| 3 | 320.76 | 0.7312 | 234.54 |
| 4 | 346.42 | 0.6587 | 228.18 |
| 5 | 374.03 | 0.5935 | 221.99 |
| 5 | 5557.00 | 0.5935 | 3298.09 |

**Step 5 — Enterprise Value:**
$$EV = 247.75 + 241.05 + 234.54 + 228.18 + 221.99 + 3298.09 = 6471.60 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 6 — Equity Value:**
$$Equity\ Value = 6471.60 - 600 + 100 = 5971.60 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 7 — Per-Share Value:**
$$Price = \frac{5971.60}{25} = ₹ 238.86 \text{ per share}$$

> **Final Answer:** EV = ₹ 6471.60 Cr; Equity Value = ₹ 5971.60 Cr; Share Price = **₹ 238.86**

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Discuss the role of Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) as the appropriate discount rate in the Enterprise DCF Model. Explain its components with formulas. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Role of WACC (2 marks):**
WACC represents the **minimum required rate of return** that the firm must earn on its existing asset base to satisfy all capital providers — equity shareholders, debenture holders, and preference shareholders. Since FCFF is the cash flow available to **all** capital providers, it is logically and mathematically consistent to discount FCFF at WACC. Using any other rate (e.g., cost of equity alone) would either over-value or under-value the firm.

**2. Components of WACC (4 marks):**

$$WACC = \left(\frac{E}{V}\right) r_e + \left(\frac{D}{V}\right) r_d (1-T) + \left(\frac{P}{V}\right) r_p$$

- **Cost of Equity ($r_e$)** — typically computed using CAPM:
$$r_e = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f)$$
- **After-tax Cost of Debt ($r_d (1-T)$)** — debt is cheaper because interest is tax-deductible.
- **Cost of Preferred Stock ($r_p$)** — applicable if the firm has preference shares.

Where $V = E + D + P$ (total capitalization).

**3. Practical Computation Insight (1 mark):**
In practice, **market values** (not book values) of debt and equity are used in weights to reflect investor opportunity costs. WACC must be re-estimated whenever the firm's capital structure, tax rate, or risk profile changes materially.

**Q. (b)** Theta Industries has the following data: Market value of equity = ₹ 1200 Cr, Market value of debt = ₹ 800 Cr, Cost of equity = 14%, Pre-tax cost of debt = 9%, Tax rate = 30%, and the firm expects perpetual FCFF of ₹ 200 Cr with a growth rate of 5%. **Compute the Enterprise Value using the perpetuity growth method and the per-share intrinsic value if there are 40 Cr equity shares outstanding. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute WACC:**
$$\begin{aligned}
WACC &= \left(\frac{1200}{2000}\right)(0.14) + \left(\frac{800}{2000}\right)(0.09)(1-0.30) \\
&= (0.60)(0.14) + (0.40)(0.09)(0.70) \\
&= 0.084 + 0.0252 \\
&= 0.1092 \text{ or } 10.92\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Terminal Value (Perpetuity Formula):**

Since FCFF grows perpetually at $g = 5\%$ and the firm is a perpetuity:

$$\begin{aligned}
EV &= \frac{FCFF_1}{WACC - g} = \frac{200}{0.1092 - 0.05} = \frac{200}{0.0592} \\
EV &= 3378.38 \text{ Cr}
\end{aligned}$$

*(Note: Since there is no explicit forecast period, the FCFF given is already Year 1 cash flow, so TV = EV directly.)*

**Step 3 — Equity Value:**
$$\begin{aligned}
Equity\ Value &= EV - Debt + Cash \\
&= 3378.38 - 800 + 0 \quad (\text{no cash mentioned}) \\
&= 2578.38 \text{ Cr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Per-Share Value:**
$$Price = \frac{2578.38}{40} = ₹ 64.46 \text{ per share}$$

> **Final Answer:** EV = ₹ 3378.38 Cr; Per-Share Value = **₹ 64.46**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Tax Shield on Debt:** Students often compute WACC using pre-tax cost of debt only. Always apply $(1-T)$ to debt cost.
> 2. **Mixing FCFF and FCFE:** Using Cost of Equity ($r_e$) to discount FCFF is a **fatal conceptual error**. FCFF → WACC; FCFE → $r_e$.
> 3. **Book Value Weights:** Using book values of debt and equity instead of market values.
> 4. **Terminal Growth Logic:** Forgetting to compute $FCFF_{n+1} = FCFF_n \times (1+g)$ before plugging into Gordon's formula.
> 5. **Sign Errors in ΔNWC:** A positive change in NWC reduces FCFF. Reversing this loses full marks.
> 6. **Discount Factor Precision:** Rounding off WACC too aggressively (e.g., 9.44% → 9%) compounds large valuation errors.
> 7. **Forgetting to Bridge to Equity Value:** Many students stop at EV and never compute Equity Value or share price.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Enterprise DCF Model:**

- ✅ **FCFF** = $EBIT(1-T) + D\&A - CapEx - \Delta NWC$ — must always be unlevered (pre-interest).
- ✅ **WACC** = $(E/V)r_e + (D/V)r_d(1-T)$ — always use **market values** and the **after-tax cost of debt**.
- ✅ **Cost of Equity** = $R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ — derived from **CAPM**, the KTU default.
- ✅ **Discount FCFF at WACC** — never at $r_e$ alone; that would be a value for **equity**, not enterprise.
- ✅ **Terminal Value** = $FCFF_{n+1}/(WACC - g)$, with the **strict constraint** $g < WACC$.
- ✅ **Enterprise Value (EV)** = $\sum PV(FCFF_t) + PV(TV)$ — total claim on the firm.
- ✅ **Equity Value** = $EV - Net\ Debt$ — only the residual belongs to shareholders.
- ✅ **Intrinsic Share Price** = $Equity\ Value / Shares\ Outstanding$ — the final KTU deliverable.
- ✅ **Capital Structure Neutrality** — Enterprise DCF is unaffected by debt-equity mix changes.
- ✅ **Sensitivity Risks** — Terminal value typically contributes **60–80%** of EV; small $g$ errors cause large valuation swings.
- ✅ **Indian Tax Rates** — Domestic company (old regime): 30% + cess = 31.2%; (new regime): 25.17%; New manufacturing: 15%.
- ✅ **Five-Year vs Ten-Year Horizon** — KTU favors 5-year explicit periods; ten years permitted for long-cycle industries.
- ✅ **Mid-year Convention** — Optional refinement; default is year-end discounting.
- ✅ **Why WACC, not $r_e$?** — Because FCFF belongs to **all** capital providers; $r_e$ only compensates equity holders.
- ✅ **Net Debt Definition** = $Total\ Debt - Cash\ \&\ Equivalents$; minority interest and preferred stock are also subtracted in some KTU problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economic Profit-Based Valuation Models
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Economic Profit-Based Valuation Models

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Economic Profit-Based Valuation** is an alternative discounted cash flow methodology that values a firm by separating its operations into two distinct components: the **book value of invested capital** (the capital already deployed in the business) and the **present value of future economic profits** (the value created by management above the cost of capital). Unlike traditional FCFE/FCFF models that discount all future cash flows, economic profit models focus only on the *incremental value created above the required return* demanded by capital providers.

The cornerstone metric of this framework is **Economic Value Added (EVA)**, a registered trademark of Stern Stewart & Co., defined formally as:

> **Economic Value Added (EVA)** = Net Operating Profit After Tax (NOPAT) − (Weighted Average Cost of Capital × Invested Capital)

Mathematically expressed:

$$EVA_t = NOPAT_t - (WACC \times IC_{t-1})$$

Where:
- $NOPAT_t$ = Net Operating Profit After Tax in year $t$
- $WACC$ = Weighted Average Cost of Capital
- $IC_{t-1}$ = Invested Capital at the beginning of year $t$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: The Economic Profit Model is mathematically equivalent to the Discounted Free Cash Flow Model. Both approaches must yield **identical firm valuations** when applied consistently with identical assumptions. This equivalence is a frequently tested concept in KTU examinations.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a landlord who owns a rental property worth ₹50,00,000. The bank charges you 8% interest on the mortgage, costing you ₹4,00,000 per year just to maintain the capital. Now, suppose the property generates ₹5,50,000 in net rental income after expenses.

- **Traditional Cash Flow View**: "Great! I made ₹5,50,000 in profit."
- **Economic Profit View**: "Wait. I needed ₹4,00,000 just to service my capital cost. My *real* economic profit is only ₹1,50,000 — the surplus above what my investors demand."

This ₹1,50,000 is the **Economic Profit**. The property is genuinely valuable not because it generates cash, but because it generates cash *in excess of what its capital costs*. Similarly, a company's worth is determined by how much profit it produces beyond the opportunity cost of its invested capital.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition - Invested Capital (IC)**: The total amount of capital invested in the business by both **equity holders and debt holders**, calculated as the sum of equity (book value) and interest-bearing debt, OR equivalently, as total operating assets minus non-interest-bearing current liabilities.

### Standard Metrics in Bold

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Description |
|--------|----------------|-------------|
| **Net Operating Profit After Tax** | **NOPAT** | EBIT × (1 − Tax Rate) |
| **Weighted Average Cost of Capital** | **WACC** | Blended cost of debt and equity |
| **Invested Capital** | **IC** | Capital deployed in operations |
| **Return on Invested Capital** | **ROIC** | NOPAT / IC |
| **Economic Value Added** | **EVA** | NOPAT − WACC × IC |
| **Spread** | **ROIC − WACC** | Value-creation spread |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Theoretical Framework

The Economic Profit model rests on a powerful financial insight: **Value is created only when a company earns a return on its invested capital that exceeds its cost of capital.** This is conceptually distinct from accounting profit, which can be positive even while destroying shareholder value.

### The Logic Chain — Step-by-Step Decomposition

1. **Step 1: Identify Invested Capital**
   Capital is the cumulative funds committed to the business by providers of financing (both debt and equity). It is measured at the **beginning of the period** to match against the period's operating profits.

2. **Step 2: Compute NOPAT**
   NOPAT represents the operating earnings available to all capital providers (debt + equity), after tax but before financing costs. The formula:
   
   $$NOPAT = EBIT \times (1 - T)$$
   
   Where $T$ is the marginal tax rate. This figure is independent of the firm's financing mix.

3. **Step 3: Calculate the Capital Charge**
   The capital charge represents the minimum return required by capital providers:
   
   $$Capital\ Charge = WACC \times IC$$
   
   This is the **opportunity cost** of capital — what investors could earn elsewhere at comparable risk.

4. **Step 4: Determine Economic Profit**
   The surplus (or deficit) after covering the opportunity cost:
   
   $$EP = NOPAT - Capital\ Charge$$

5. **Step 5: Discount and Aggregate**
   Future economic profits are discounted to present value at the WACC, then added to current invested capital:
   
   $$Firm\ Value = IC_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{EP_t}{(1 + WACC)^t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Single-Period Value Driver**: Since $EP = (ROIC - WACC) \times IC$, value creation depends on two levers: the **spread** (ROIC − WACC) and the **scale** (Invested Capital). Companies can create value either by widening the spread (improving operational efficiency) or by growing invested capital in projects where ROIC > WACC.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Purpose |
|---------|-----------|---------|
| NOPAT | $NOPAT = EBIT \times (1 - T)$ | Operating profit after tax |
| Invested Capital (Asset Side) | $IC = Total\ Operating\ Assets - Non\text{-}interest\ bearing\ CL$ | Capital base for value creation |
| Invested Capital (Financing Side) | $IC = Equity_{BV} + Debt_{BV} - Excess\ Cash$ | Alternative calculation |
| Capital Charge | $CC = WACC \times IC$ | Required return on capital |
| Economic Profit | $EP = NOPAT - WACC \times IC$ | Value created in period |
| Economic Profit (Spread Form) | $EP = (ROIC - WACC) \times IC$ | Value driver decomposition |
| ROIC | $ROIC = NOPAT / IC$ | Return earned on capital |
| Firm Value | $V_0 = IC_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{EP_t}{(1 + WACC)^t}$ | Total enterprise value |
| Market Value Added | $MVA = Market\ Value\ of\ Firm - IC_0$ | Cumulative value created |
| Steady-State EP | $EP_{\infty} = NOPAT \times \frac{(ROIC - WACC)}{ROIC - g}$ | Perpetuity value driver |
| EP Discount Factor | $DF_t = \frac{1}{(1 + WACC)^t}$ | Standard WACC discount factor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition**: When $ROIC = WACC$, Economic Profit equals zero, and the firm's value equals exactly its invested capital (no value created, no value destroyed). This is the **no-growth, no-value-creation benchmark**.

### Real-World Engineering and Financial Utility

Economic Profit models are widely used in:
- **Corporate Performance Management**: Companies like Coca-Cola, Siemens, and Tata Consultancy Services use EVA-based incentive compensation systems.
- **Capital Budgeting**: Evaluating whether new projects create or destroy shareholder value at the project level.
- **M&A Valuation**: Determining the standalone value of acquisition targets versus their acquisition price.
- **Strategic Decision-Making**: Guiding capital allocation toward business units with the highest ROIC − WACC spread.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Equivalence of Economic Profit and DCF Valuation

**Statement**: The Economic Profit model yields the same firm value as the standard Free Cash Flow model.

**Starting Point — The Free Cash Flow Identity**:

$$FCFF_t = NOPAT_t - \Delta IC_t$$

This expresses free cash flow as the operating profit after tax minus the additional capital reinvested in the business.

**Step 1**: Substitute this identity into the standard DCF valuation formula:

$$V_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{FCFF_t}{(1 + WACC)^t}$$

$$V_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{NOPAT_t - \Delta IC_t}{(1 + WACC)^t}$$

**Step 2**: Separate the summation into two components:

$$V_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{NOPAT_t}{(1 + WACC)^t} - \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{\Delta IC_t}{(1 + WACC)^t}$$

**Step 3**: Recognize that the second summation telescopes. Using $\Delta IC_t = IC_t - IC_{t-1}$:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{IC_t - IC_{t-1}}{(1 + WACC)^t}$$

**Step 4**: Apply the algebraic identity that this telescopes to $IC_0$ (subtracting future IC from initial IC):

$$\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{\Delta IC_t}{(1 + WACC)^t} = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \frac{IC_t}{(1 + WACC)^t} - \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \frac{IC_t}{(1 + WACC)^{t+1}} = IC_0$$

**Step 5**: Substitute back into the original valuation:

$$V_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{NOPAT_t}{(1 + WACC)^t} - IC_0$$

**Step 6**: Add and subtract the capital charge term:

$$V_0 = IC_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{NOPAT_t - WACC \times IC_{t-1}}{(1 + WACC)^t}$$

**Step 7**: Recognize the numerator as the Economic Profit definition:

$$V_0 = IC_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{EP_t}{(1 + WACC)^t}$$

**Final Result**: The valuation decomposes into current invested capital plus the present value of all future economic profits. This proves mathematical equivalence with the FCFF approach.

## Derivation 2: Economic Profit in Spread Form

Starting with the basic definition:

$$EP_t = NOPAT_t - WACC \times IC_{t-1}$$

**Step 1**: Factor out $IC_{t-1}$ from both terms:

$$EP_t = IC_{t-1} \times \left( \frac{NOPAT_t}{IC_{t-1}} - WACC \right)$$

**Step 2**: Recognize the first term inside parentheses as ROIC:

$$EP_t = IC_{t-1} \times (ROIC_t - WACC)$$

**Step 3**: Conclusion — Economic Profit equals the product of capital base and the value-creation spread.

## Numerical Example — Worked Problem

**Problem**: A firm has the following data:
- Current Invested Capital: ₹10,00,000
- Expected NOPAT for next 3 years: ₹1,50,000; ₹1,70,000; ₹1,90,000
- WACC = 10%
- Terminal Economic Profit (Year 4 onwards) = ₹80,000 growing at 3% perpetually

**Step 1**: Compute Economic Profit for each year (assuming IC remains ₹10,00,000):

$$EP_1 = 1,50,000 - (0.10 \times 10,00,000) = 1,50,000 - 1,00,000 = 50,000$$

$$EP_2 = 1,70,000 - 1,00,000 = 70,000$$

$$EP_3 = 1,90,000 - 1,00,000 = 90,000$$

**Step 2**: Discount explicit-period economic profits:

$$PV(EP_1) = \frac{50,000}{1.10} = 45,454.55$$

$$PV(EP_2) = \frac{70,000}{1.10^2} = 57,851.24$$

$$PV(EP_3) = \frac{90,000}{1.10^3} = 67,596.45$$

**Step 3**: Compute terminal value of economic profits at end of Year 3:

$$TV = \frac{EP_4}{WACC - g} = \frac{80,000}{0.10 - 0.03} = \frac{80,000}{0.07} = 11,42,857.14$$

**Step 4**: Discount terminal value to present:

$$PV(TV) = \frac{11,42,857.14}{1.10^3} = 8,58,128.79$$

**Step 5**: Total firm value:

$$V_0 = IC_0 + \sum PV(EP) + PV(TV)$$

$$V_0 = 10,00,000 + 45,454.55 + 57,851.24 + 67,596.45 + 8,58,128.79$$

$$V_0 = 20,29,031.03$$

**Step 6**: Compute Market Value Added (MVA):

$$MVA = V_0 - IC_0 = 20,29,031.03 - 10,00,000 = 10,29,031.03$$

This positive MVA indicates the firm has created value above its capital base.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Economic Profit Decomposition Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Firm Operating Assets"] --> B["EBIT Earnings Before Interest and Tax"]
    B --> C["NOPAT Equals EBIT times One minus T"]
    D["Invested Capital Base"] --> E["Capital Charge Equals WACC times IC"]
    C --> F["Economic Profit"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Discount at WACC"]
    D --> H["Current Firm Value"]
    G --> I["PV of Future Economic Profits"]
    H --> J["Total Enterprise Value"]
    I --> J
    C --> K["ROIC Indicator"]
    D --> K
    K --> L["Spread ROIC minus WACC"]
    L --> F
```

## Diagram 2: Value Driver Tree (EVA Logic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["Firm Value"] --> ic["Invested Capital"]
    root --> pvep["PV of Future EP"]
    pvep --> spread["ROIC minus WACC Spread"]
    pvep --> scale["Capital Growth Rate"]
    spread --> roic["ROIC Components"]
    spread --> wacc["WACC Components"]
    roic --> margin["Operating Margin"]
    roic --> turnover["Asset Turnover"]
    roic --> tax["Tax Effect"]
    roic --> leverage["Financial Leverage"]
    wacc --> re["Cost of Equity"]
    wacc --> rd["After-tax Cost of Debt"]
    wacc --> weights["Capital Weights"]
    scale --> rets["Reinvestment Rate"]
    scale --> newinv["New Investment"]
```

## Diagram 3: Economic Profit Lifecycle Stages

```mermaid
graph LR
    phaseA["Expansion Phase"] --> phaseB["Maturity Phase"]
    phaseB --> phaseC["Steady State Phase"]
    phaseA --> descA["High investment, low EP, strong growth"]
    phaseB --> descB["EP rises, ROIC exceeds WACC"]
    phaseC --> descC["Constant spread, stable EP"]
    descA --> formulaA["ROIC may be less than WACC temporarily"]
    descB --> formulaB["ROIC greater than WACC sustained"]
    descC --> formulaC["EP grows at constant rate g"]
```

## Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture of EVA Calculation

```mermaid
graph TD
    fin["Financial Statements Input"] --> calc1["EBIT Calculation Block"]
    fin --> calc2["Invested Capital Block"]
    fin --> calc3["WACC Computation Block"]
    calc1 --> nopat["NOPAT Output"]
    calc2 --> icout["IC Output"]
    calc3 --> waccout["WACC Output"]
    nopat --> eva["EVA Calculation Engine"]
    icout --> eva
    waccout --> eva
    eva --> out1["Annual EVA Series"]
    out1 --> val["Valuation Engine"]
    val --> out2["Enterprise Value"]
    val --> out3["MVA Output"]
    val --> out4["Value per Share"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Diagram Note**: The above schematics represent the functional flow of inputs, computations, and outputs in an Economic Profit-based valuation. Each block performs a discrete operation; data flows top-down through the architecture, producing the final valuation outputs.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Short-Answer

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define Economic Value Added (EVA) and explain its significance in firm valuation.

**Model Answer**:

Economic Value Added (EVA) is a financial performance metric representing the residual wealth generated by a firm after deducting the cost of all capital employed. It is calculated as:

$$EVA_t = NOPAT_t - (WACC \times IC_{t-1})$$

Where NOPAT is the Net Operating Profit After Tax, WACC is the Weighted Average Cost of Capital, and IC is the Invested Capital. EVA's significance lies in its ability to measure **true economic profit** rather than accounting profit — it captures whether a firm's operational earnings exceed the minimum return required by capital providers. A positive EVA indicates value creation, while a negative EVA signals value destruction. In valuation, the firm's enterprise value equals current invested capital plus the present value of all future EVAs discounted at the WACC.

### Question 2: Definition Question

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Distinguish between Economic Profit and Accounting Profit with a suitable example.

**Model Answer**:

| Aspect | Accounting Profit | Economic Profit |
|--------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Definition | Revenue minus explicit costs (including interest) | NOPAT minus opportunity cost of capital |
| Cost of Equity | Not deducted | Implicitly deducted via WACC |
| Decision Use | Statutory reporting | Value-based management |
| Value Implication | No direct link to value creation | Direct measure of value creation |

**Example**: A company reports ₹2,00,000 in net profit after interest expense. However, its shareholders could earn ₹1,50,000 by investing in comparable-risk securities. The economic profit would be ₹50,000 (₹2,00,000 − ₹1,50,000 opportunity cost). Although accounting profit appears healthy, true economic surplus is only ₹50,000.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Set A]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between Economic Profit model and Free Cash Flow model, proving their mathematical equivalence. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

The derivation begins with the Free Cash Flow identity:

$$FCFF_t = NOPAT_t - \Delta IC_t$$

Step 1: Express firm value via standard DCF:

$$V_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{NOPAT_t - \Delta IC_t}{(1 + WACC)^t}$$

Step 2: Separate the summations:

$$V_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{NOPAT_t}{(1 + WACC)^t} - \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{\Delta IC_t}{(1 + WACC)^t}$$

Step 3: Show that the capital change summation telescopes to $IC_0$ using the identity that invested capital at infinity approaches zero in present value terms.

Step 4: Rewrite as:

$$V_0 = IC_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{NOPAT_t - WACC \times IC_{t-1}}{(1 + WACC)^t}$$

Step 5: Recognize the bracketed term as Economic Profit:

$$V_0 = IC_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{EP_t}{(1 + WACC)^t}$$

This proves that **Economic Profit valuation and FCFF valuation are mathematically equivalent**. Both yield identical firm values when consistent assumptions are used.

**(b)** A firm has the following data: Invested Capital = ₹50,00,000; WACC = 12%; NOPAT projections: Year 1 = ₹6,00,000; Year 2 = ₹7,00,000; Year 3 = ₹8,00,000. From Year 4 onwards, economic profit grows at 5% perpetually with Year 4 EP = ₹1,20,000. Calculate the firm value. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1**: Compute Economic Profit for Years 1–3:

Capital Charge each year = 0.12 × 50,00,000 = ₹6,00,000

$$EP_1 = 6,00,000 - 6,00,000 = 0$$

$$EP_2 = 7,00,000 - 6,00,000 = 1,00,000$$

$$EP_3 = 8,00,000 - 6,00,000 = 2,00,000$$

**Step 2**: Compute Terminal Value at end of Year 3:

$$TV = \frac{EP_4}{WACC - g} = \frac{1,20,000}{0.12 - 0.05} = \frac{1,20,000}{0.07} = 17,14,285.71$$

**Step 3**: Discount all economic profits and terminal value:

$$PV(EP_1) = \frac{0}{1.12} = 0$$

$$PV(EP_2) = \frac{1,00,000}{1.12^2} = \frac{1,00,000}{1.2544} = 79,719.39$$

$$PV(EP_3) = \frac{2,00,000}{1.12^3} = \frac{2,00,000}{1.404928} = 1,42,356.05$$

$$PV(TV) = \frac{17,14,285.71}{1.404928} = 12,20,193.50$$

**Step 4**: Total firm value:

$$V_0 = 50,00,000 + 0 + 79,719.39 + 1,42,356.05 + 12,20,193.50$$

$$V_0 = 64,42,268.94$$

**Final Answer**: The firm value is approximately **₹64,42,269**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Market Value Added (MVA) and its relationship with Economic Value Added (EVA). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Market Value Added (MVA)** is the difference between the total market value of a firm (equity market capitalization plus debt market value minus excess cash) and the total capital invested in the firm by both equity and debt holders.

$$MVA = Market\ Value\ of\ Firm - Invested\ Capital$$

**Relationship with EVA**: MVA represents the **cumulative discounted sum** of all future expected EVAs. The mathematical relationship is:

$$MVA_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{EVA_t}{(1 + WACC)^t}$$

This means:
- If a firm consistently generates **positive EVA**, the market will value it above its invested capital, producing positive MVA.
- If a firm consistently generates **negative EVA**, the market will value it below its invested capital, producing negative MVA.

MVA is therefore the **stock measure** (cumulative wealth created to date), while EVA is the **flow measure** (value created during a single period). MVA reflects market expectations of future EVAs, while EVA reflects actual current-period performance.

**(b)** A company reports: Total Operating Assets = ₹80,00,000; Non-interest-bearing current liabilities = ₹10,00,000; Equity book value = ₹45,00,000; Debt book value = ₹25,00,000; EBIT = ₹9,00,000; Tax rate = 30%; WACC = 11%; ROIC expected in perpetuity = 14%. Calculate the firm value using the Economic Profit model. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1**: Compute Invested Capital (Asset Side):

$$IC = 80,00,000 - 10,00,000 = 70,00,000$$

(Verification from financing side: ₹45,00,000 + ₹25,00,000 = ₹70,00,000 ✓)

**Step 2**: Compute NOPAT:

$$NOPAT = 9,00,000 \times (1 - 0.30) = 9,00,000 \times 0.70 = 6,30,000$$

**Step 3**: Compute perpetual Economic Profit:

$$EP = NOPAT - WACC \times IC = 6,30,000 - (0.11 \times 70,00,000)$$

$$EP = 6,30,000 - 7,70,000 = -1,40,000$$

Since ROIC (14%) > WACC (11%), spread is positive, EP should be positive. Let us recompute using beginning-of-period convention more carefully:

$$ROIC = 6,30,000 / 70,00,000 = 9\%$$

This ROIC (9%) is less than WACC (11%), indicating the firm's current operations destroy value. The perpetual economic profit is negative ₹1,40,000.

**Step 4**: Compute PV of perpetual Economic Profit:

$$PV(EP) = \frac{-1,40,000}{0.11} = -12,72,727.27$$

**Step 5**: Total Firm Value:

$$V_0 = IC_0 + PV(EP) = 70,00,000 + (-12,72,727.27)$$

$$V_0 = 57,27,272.73$$

**Final Answer**: The firm value is approximately **₹57,27,273**, less than its invested capital, indicating that the market discounts the firm by approximately ₹12,72,727 due to value destruction in operations.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Economic Profit Valuation**:
> 
> 1. **Invested Capital Timing Error**: Students frequently compute EP using end-of-period capital instead of beginning-of-period capital. The correct convention is **beginning-of-period** capital. Using end-of-period IC will distort EP by one period's investment decision.
> 
> 2. **Discount Factor Confusion**: Economic profits must be discounted at **WACC** (not cost of equity). Confusion with FCFE/FCFF discount rates is a frequent source of errors.
> 
> 3. **Confusing Accounting NOPAT with True NOPAT**: Accounting NOPAT may include non-operating items. True NOPAT excludes interest income and other non-operating gains.
> 
> 4. **Omitting Initial Capital**: A very common mistake is forgetting to add $IC_0$ to the present value of future economic profits. The Economic Profit model explicitly requires this addition.
> 
> 5. **Miscalculating Invested Capital**: Mixing book values with market values of debt/equity creates internal inconsistency. Use book value of debt plus book value of equity (less excess cash), or use total operating assets minus non-interest-bearing current liabilities.
> 
> 6. **Growth Rate in Perpetuity**: When applying the perpetuity formula to economic profit, use the sustainable growth rate that is **less than WACC** to avoid mathematical inconsistency.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Economic Profit-Based Valuation**:

- **Definition of EVA**: Residual profit after capital charge = $NOPAT - WACC \times IC$
- **NOPAT Formula**: $EBIT \times (1 - T)$ — operating profit after tax, financing-independent
- **Invested Capital**: Total operating assets minus non-interest-bearing current liabilities
- **Spread Decomposition**: $EP = (ROIC - WACC) \times IC$ — separates scale from efficiency
- **Valuation Equation**: $V_0 = IC_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{EP_t}{(1 + WACC)^t}$
- **Discount Rate**: WACC (always, for both components)
- **Equivalence Theorem**: EP model = FCFF model under identical assumptions (tested frequently)
- **MVA Interpretation**: $MVA = \sum PV(Future\ EVA)$ — cumulative value creation measure
- **Steady-State Perpetuity**: $PV(EP_\infty) = \frac{EP_4}{WACC - g}$, where $g < WACC$
- **Break-Even Point**: When $ROIC = WACC$, firm value equals invested capital exactly
- **Value Creation Rule**: Positive EVA ⟹ Value creation; Negative EVA ⟹ Value destruction
- **Capital Timing Convention**: Always use **beginning-of-period** invested capital
- **Two Value Drivers**: (1) ROIC − WACC spread, (2) Investment growth rate
- **Numerical Priority**: Always show IC, NOPAT, Capital Charge, EP, then PV computations in order

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adjusted-Present-Value Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET635 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adjusted-Present-Value Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Adjusted Present Value (APV) Model** is a discounted cash flow valuation technique introduced by **Stewart C. Myers (1974)**. It decomposes a firm's value into two independent components: the value of the project as if it were **unlevered (all-equity financed)**, plus the present value of the **financing side effects** arising from the capital structure decisions.

Mathematically, the APV equation is:

$$APV = \text{Base NPV} + \text{PV of Financing Side Effects}$$

Where the **Base NPV** is computed by discounting the **unlevered free cash flows (UFCF)** of the project at the **unlevered cost of equity (r_U)** — also called the cost of capital for an all-equity firm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"The Adjusted Present Value approach values a project by first computing its base value assuming all-equity financing (using unlevered cost of capital), and then adding the present value of all side effects of debt, including tax shields, bankruptcy costs, and subsidies, each discounted at a rate appropriate to the risk of that specific cash flow."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Part Cake" Intuition

Imagine you want to buy a piece of land to build a rental apartment. The land itself has a **standalone value** based purely on how much rent it can generate — *this is the Base NPV*.

Now, suppose the bank offers you a **home loan at a subsidized rate**, and the government also gives you a **tax break** on the interest you pay. These are *extra benefits* you wouldn't get if you paid all cash. The APV model says:

> 💡 **"First, value the land as if no loan existed. Then, add the present value of all the loan-related perks (and subtract the loan-related costs)."**

The intuition is that **a project's operational value** and its **financing structure value** are conceptually independent streams of cash — so we should discount them **separately** using **different discount rates** that match the risk of each stream.

## 1.3 Why APV Exists — The Limitation of WACC

The traditional **WACC method** blends debt and equity costs into a single discount rate, assuming a **constant capital structure**. But many real-world situations violate this:

- **LBO (Leveraged Buyouts)** — debt ratios change dramatically over time.
- **Project finance** — debt is often paid down in a scheduled manner (non-constant leverage).
- **Startups / distressed firms** — capital structure shifts year-to-year.
- **Valuing tax shields** when debt is risky.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** APV is preferred when the **debt-to-equity ratio changes over the project's life**, when debt is **risk-free vs. risky**, or when there are **multiple distinct financing side effects** that need different discount rates.

## 1.4 Core Components of APV — The "Side Effects" Inventory

| Side Effect | Sign | Typical Discount Rate |
|-------------|------|----------------------|
| **Interest Tax Shield (ITS)** | + | Cost of debt (r_D) |
| **Subsidies / Grants** | + | Risk-free rate (r_f) |
| **Costs of Financial Distress** | − | Unlevered cost of equity (r_U) |
| **Debt Issuance Costs** | − | Unlevered cost of equity (r_U) |
| **Net Debt Effect** | + or − | Blend |

Explicitly stated in KTU standard notation:

$$APV = NPV_{\text{unlevered}} + PV(ITS) + PV(\text{Subsidies}) - PV(\text{Financial Distress}) - PV(\text{Issuance Costs})$$

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlighted)

- **Unlevered cost of equity (r_U)** — typically computed using the **Modigliani-Miller Proposition II** (with Hamada adjustment) or the **Hamada equation**.
- **Risk-free rate (r_f)** — standard reference: **10-Year Government Securities (G-Sec) yield**; in KTU problems commonly given directly.
- **Corporate tax rate (T_c)** — standard Indian rate cited: **25.17% (domestic)** or **30% (old regime)**.
- **Debt-to-equity ratio (D/E)** — observed capital structure parameter.
- **Beta of levered firm (β_L)** and **unlevered beta (β_U)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Stage Logic of APV Computation

### **Stage 1 — Compute the Base NPV (Unlevered Project Value)**

The project is hypothetically financed **100% by equity**. Therefore:

- Free cash flows are computed **ignoring interest expense**.
- These are called **Unlevered Free Cash Flows (UFCF)**.
- Discounted at the **Unlevered Cost of Equity (r_U)**, which reflects only the **business risk** of the project (no financial risk).

### **Stage 2 — Add the PV of Financing Side Effects**

Each side effect is identified, its magnitude is estimated, and then **discounted at a rate that matches its own risk level**:

- **Tax shields on debt** are discounted at the **pre-tax cost of debt (r_D)**, because the cash flows (interest payments) are contractual and low-risk.
- **Subsidies and grants** are discounted at the **risk-free rate (r_f)** because they are government-backed.
- **Costs of distress** are discounted at the **unlevered cost of equity (r_U)** because they rise and fall with operating performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why different discount rates?** The WACC method averages risks; APV **disaggregates** them. A tax shield has lower risk than operating cash flow, so it deserves a lower discount rate — which makes the APV more accurate.

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $APV = NPV_{\text{unlevered}} + PV(\text{Financing Side Effects})$ | Master APV equation |
| 2 | $NPV_{\text{unlevered}} = \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{UFCF_t}{(1+r_U)^t} - I_0$ | Base NPV at unlevered cost |
| 3 | $r_U = r_E + (r_E - r_D) \cdot \dfrac{D}{E} \cdot (1 - T_c)$ | MM Prop II — Unlevered cost of equity |
| 4 | $r_U = \dfrac{r_E \cdot E + r_D \cdot D \cdot (1 - T_c)}{E + D}$ | Pre-tax WACC formula rearranged |
| 5 | $PV(ITS_{\text{perpetual}}) = \dfrac{T_c \cdot r_D \cdot D}{r_D} = T_c \cdot D$ | Perpetual debt tax shield |
| 6 | $PV(ITS_{\text{finite}}) = \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{T_c \cdot I_t}{(1+r_D)^t}$ | Finite-life debt tax shield |
| 7 | $\beta_U = \dfrac{\beta_L}{1 + (1-T_c) \cdot \dfrac{D}{E}}$ | **Hamada Equation** |
| 8 | $r_U = r_f + \beta_U \cdot (r_m - r_f)$ | CAPM for unlevered firm |
| 9 | $PV(\text{Subsidy}) = \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{S_t}{(1+r_f)^t}$ | PV of government subsidy |
| 10 | $APV_{\text{risk-free debt}} = NPV_{\text{unlevered}} + T_c \cdot D$ | Risk-free debt case (MM Prop I) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Never use `|` (vertical pipe) in KTU formula tables — use `\vert` or `\mid`. Avoid mixing WACC and APV concepts in a single problem.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering / Finance Utility

The APV model is the **gold standard** in:

- **Project finance** (infrastructure, roads, power plants) — debt is **sculpted** (matched to cash flows), making WACC inappropriate.
- **Mergers & Acquisitions (M&A)** — for LBOs and management buyouts.
- **Valuing tax shields** of risky debt where WACC over- or under-discounts.
- **Public-Private Partnership (PPP) projects** in India (NHAI, metro rail) — where **Viability Gap Funding (VGF)** subsidies are critical.
- **Start-up valuation** when capital structure is non-stationary.

## 2.4 Tax Shield — Risk-Free vs. Risky Debt

This is a **classic KTU exam trap**.

**Case A: Risk-Free Debt (MM Proposition I assumption)**

The tax shield is **certain** (the firm will always pay interest, debt is safe). Therefore:

$$PV(ITS) = T_c \cdot D$$

This is independent of r_D and r_U.

**Case B: Risky Debt (Default Possible)**

The tax shield is **contingent** — only paid if the firm has profits. The standard formula:

$$PV(ITS) = T_c \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{r_D \cdot D_t \cdot p_t}{(1 + r_D \cdot p_t)^t}$$

where $p_t$ is the probability that the firm survives (and pays tax) in year $t$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If the problem says "perpetual debt with constant risk" → use the simple formula $T_c \cdot D$. If it says "debt has a probability of default" → use the **expected tax shield** formula. Marks are routinely lost here.

## 2.5 Subsidies and Other Side Effects

For Indian project finance, the **Viability Gap Funding (VGF)** is a Union Government grant up to **20% of project cost** (or 40% in special sectors) provided to PPP projects. Other side effects include:

- **Tax holidays** (Section 80-IA) — valued as **NPV × (1 − effective tax rate over the period)**.
- **Loan at concessional rates** (e.g., from NABARD, SIDBI) — valued as the **spread saved** × debt outstanding.

## 2.6 Comparison: APV vs. WACC Method

| Feature | WACC | APV |
|---------|------|-----|
| Discount rate | Single blended rate | Multiple rate, per cash flow |
| Capital structure | Assumed **constant** | Can vary over time |
| Best for | Mature firms, constant leverage | LBOs, project finance, M&A |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Accuracy for tax shields | Approximate | High (uses r_D) |
| Exam use | Common in 2-mark questions | Common in 14-mark derivations |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the APV Identity (from First Principles)

We start from the **Modigliani-Miller (1958) Proposition I** in a world with taxes:

$$V_L = V_U + PV(ITS)$$

Where:
- $V_L$ = Value of levered firm
- $V_U$ = Value of unlevered firm
- $PV(ITS)$ = Present value of interest tax shields

**Step 1:** The unlevered firm value, $V_U$, is the present value of unlevered free cash flows discounted at r_U:

$$V_U = \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{UFCF_t}{(1+r_U)^t}$$

**Step 2:** For a project (not a going concern), we subtract the initial investment $I_0$:

$$NPV_{\text{unlevered}} = \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{UFCF_t}{(1+r_U)^t} - I_0$$

**Step 3:** The tax shield in year $t$ equals the **interest paid** × **tax rate**:

$$ITS_t = T_c \cdot I_t = T_c \cdot r_D \cdot D_t$$

**Step 4:** Discounting each tax shield at the cost of debt r_D (matching its risk):

$$PV(ITS) = \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{T_c \cdot r_D \cdot D_t}{(1+r_D)^t}$$

**Step 5:** Combine the two streams — this is the **APV identity**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
APV &= NPV_{\text{unlevered}} + PV(ITS) + PV(\text{Other Side Effects}) \\
&= \left[\sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{UFCF_t}{(1+r_U)^t} - I_0\right] + \left[\sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{T_c \cdot r_D \cdot D_t}{(1+r_D)^t}\right] + \text{etc.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why add the side effects and not subtract?** The "Base NPV" assumes **no debt**. The side effects measure the *change* in value due to the chosen debt policy. Tax shields increase value (+), distress costs decrease value (−).
