## 3.2 Derivation of the Unlevered Cost of Equity (MM Proposition II)

Starting with the **WACC identity** (with taxes):

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{WACC} \cdot (E + D) &= r_E \cdot E + r_D \cdot D \cdot (1 - T_c) \\
\text{And} \quad r_{WACC} &= r_U \quad \text{(APV base assumption)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_U \cdot (E + D) &= r_E \cdot E + r_D \cdot D \cdot (1 - T_c) \\
r_U \cdot E + r_U \cdot D &= r_E \cdot E + r_D \cdot D - T_c \cdot r_D \cdot D \\
r_E \cdot E &= r_U \cdot E + r_U \cdot D - r_D \cdot D + T_c \cdot r_D \cdot D \\
r_E \cdot E &= r_U \cdot E + (r_U - r_D + T_c \cdot r_D) \cdot D \\
r_E &= r_U + (r_U - r_D) \cdot \dfrac{D}{E} + T_c \cdot r_D \cdot \dfrac{D}{E} \\
r_E &= r_U + (r_U - r_D \cdot (1 - T_c)) \cdot \dfrac{D}{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging for r_U (the **APV discount rate**):

$$r_U = r_E + (r_E - r_D) \cdot \dfrac{D}{E} \cdot (1 - T_c)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most-asked formula in KTU Module 4.** Practice it until you can derive it from scratch in 3 minutes.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Hamada Equation (CAPM Form)

From CAPM:

$$r_E = r_f + \beta_L \cdot (r_m - r_f) \quad \text{and} \quad r_U = r_f + \beta_U \cdot (r_m - r_f)$$

Substituting into the MM Prop II result and canceling r_f:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta_U \cdot (r_m - r_f) &= \beta_L \cdot (r_m - r_f) + (\beta_L - \beta_D) \cdot (r_m - r_f) \cdot \dfrac{D}{E} \cdot (1 - T_c) \\
\text{Assuming } \beta_D &\approx 0 \text{ (debt is risk-free):} \\
\beta_U &= \beta_L \cdot \left[1 + (1 - T_c) \cdot \dfrac{D}{E}\right] \\
\text{Or equivalently:} \quad \beta_U &= \dfrac{\beta_L}{1 + (1 - T_c) \cdot \dfrac{D}{E}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem

**Problem Statement (Model KTU Question):**

> A company is evaluating a project costing **₹10,00,000** with a life of **5 years**. The project will generate **Unlevered Free Cash Flow (UFCF)** of **₹3,00,000 per year**. The firm plans to finance 40% via debt at a pre-tax cost of 10%. The unlevered cost of equity is 14%. The tax rate is **30%**. Compute the APV.

### **Step 1: Identify the parameters**

- $I_0 = 10,00,000$
- $UFCF_t = 3,00,000$ (constant for $t = 1 \text{ to } 5$)
- $r_U = 14\% = 0.14$
- $D = 0.40 \times 10,00,000 = 4,00,000$
- $r_D = 10\% = 0.10$
- $T_c = 30\% = 0.30$
- $N = 5$ years

### **Step 2: Compute the Base NPV (unlevered)**

The PV annuity factor for 5 years at 14%:

$$
PVA(r_U, 5) = \dfrac{1 - (1.14)^{-5}}{0.14}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.14)^5 &= 1.14 \times 1.14 \times 1.14 \times 1.14 \times 1.14 \\
(1.14)^2 &= 1.2996 \\
(1.14)^3 &= 1.4815 \\
(1.14)^4 &= 1.6890 \\
(1.14)^5 &= 1.9254 \\
(1.14)^{-5} &= \dfrac{1}{1.9254} = 0.5194
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
PVA(14\%, 5) = \dfrac{1 - 0.5194}{0.14} = \dfrac{0.4806}{0.14} = 3.4331
$$

Now compute the Base NPV:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_{\text{unlevered}} &= 3,00,000 \times 3.4331 - 10,00,000 \\
&= 10,29,930 - 10,00,000 \\
&= \text{₹}29,930
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 3: Compute the PV of Interest Tax Shield (ITS)**

Interest per year = $r_D \times D = 0.10 \times 4,00,000 = 40,000$

Tax shield per year = $T_c \times 40,000 = 0.30 \times 40,000 = 12,000$

PV annuity factor for 5 years at 10%:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.10)^5 &= 1.6105 \\
(1.10)^{-5} &= 0.6209 \\
PVA(10\%, 5) &= \dfrac{1 - 0.6209}{0.10} = \dfrac{0.3791}{0.10} = 3.7908
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
PV(ITS) = 12,000 \times 3.7908 = 45,489.6 \approx \text{₹}45,490
$$

### **Step 4: Compute the Adjusted Present Value**

$$
\begin{aligned}
APV &= NPV_{\text{unlevered}} + PV(ITS) \\
&= 29,930 + 45,490 \\
&= \text{₹}75,420
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 5: Decision**

Since APV > 0, **accept the project**. The project value to the firm increases by ₹75,420 due to the combination of operations and tax shield benefits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** **APV = ₹75,420** (Project is acceptable since APV is positive)

## 3.5 The Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Method | Why |
|----------|--------|-----|
| Constant capital structure | Either APV or WACC | Both give identical answers |
| Debt is risk-free, perpetual | APV (simple) | $PV(ITS) = T_c \cdot D$ directly |
| Varying debt over time | APV (preferred) | Each year discounted separately |
| Risky debt, default possible | APV (required) | Use expected tax shield |
| LBO / Acquisition | APV (industry standard) | Debt schedule is non-flat |
| Mature firm, simple problem | WACC | Simpler algebra |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 APV Architecture — The Disaggregated Valuation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["PROJECT INPUT: Initial Investment I0, Life N"] --> nodeB["STAGE 1: BASE NPV CALCULATION"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Compute Unlevered Free Cash Flows UFCF per year"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Determine Unlevered Cost of Equity rU via MM Prop II or Hamada"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Discount UFCF at rU and subtract I0"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["BASE NPV unlevered"]
    F2["STAGE 2: FINANCING SIDE EFFECTS"] --> nodeG
    F2 --> nodeH["Identify Side Effects: Tax Shield, Subsidies, Distress, Issuance Cost"]
    nodeH --> nodeG["Assign appropriate discount rate to each side effect"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["Discount each side effect stream and sum PVs"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["FINANCING PV SUM"]
    nodeF --> nodeK["APV = Base NPV + Financing PV Sum"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL["DECISION: APV greater than 0 indicates ACCEPT"]
```

## 4.2 Discount Rate Selection Logic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Identify Cash Flow Type"] --> nodeB{"Is it UFCF?"}
    nodeB -- YES --> nodeC["Use rU unlevered cost of equity"]
    nodeB -- NO --> nodeD{"Is it a tax shield?"}
    nodeD -- YES --> nodeE{"Is debt risk free?"}
    nodeE -- YES --> nodeF["Use rD or direct perpetuity formula T_c * D"]
    nodeE -- NO --> nodeG["Use expected tax shield with default probability"]
    nodeD -- NO --> nodeH{"Is it a subsidy or government grant?"}
    nodeH -- YES --> nodeI["Use r_f risk free rate"]
    nodeH -- NO --> nodeJ{"Is it a distress cost?"}
    nodeJ -- YES --> nodeK["Use rU unlevered cost of equity"]
    nodeJ -- NO --> nodeL["Default: use rU"]
```

## 4.3 Capital Structure Time Profile (Why APV Beats WACC)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Year1["Year 1"]
        y1E["Equity 60%"]
        y1D["Debt 40%"]
    end
    subgraph Year3["Year 3"]
        y3E["Equity 75%"]
        y3D["Debt 25%"]
    end
    subgraph Year5["Year 5"]
        y5E["Equity 95%"]
        y5D["Debt 5%"]
    end
    Year1 --> Year3
    Year3 --> Year5
    note["Capital structure is NOT constant. APV handles this; WACC fails."]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** In the above diagrams, the **Base NPV** is the operational value of the asset under 100% equity. The **Financing Side Effects** capture the *change* in value when debt is introduced. The two streams are **additive** because MM Prop I (with taxes) decomposes firm value linearly.

## 4.4 The APV Calculation Algorithm (Block Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Step 1: Extract all given parameters"] --> stepB["Step 2: Compute rU using MM Prop II formula"]
    stepB --> stepC["Step 3: Compute UFCF for each year and discount at rU"]
    stepC --> stepD["Step 4: Compute debt schedule and interest per year"]
    stepD --> stepE["Step 5: Compute annual tax shield T_c times interest"]
    stepE --> stepF["Step 6: Discount tax shields at rD and sum PV"]
    stepF --> stepG["Step 7: Identify any other side effects and discount appropriately"]
    stepG --> stepH["Step 8: Sum Base NPV and all side effect PVs to get APV"]
    stepH --> stepI["Step 9: If APV greater than 0 ACCEPT the project"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define the Adjusted Present Value (APV) approach. State its two main components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Adjusted Present Value (APV)** method, developed by **Stewart C. Myers (1974)**, values a project or firm as the sum of two distinct components:

1. **Base NPV (Unlevered Project Value):** The Net Present Value of the project's unlevered free cash flows (UFCF) discounted at the **unlevered cost of equity (r_U)**. This represents the value of the project *as if it were financed entirely by equity*.

2. **PV of Financing Side Effects:** The present value of all consequences arising from the use of debt — including **interest tax shields (positive)**, **financial distress costs (negative)**, **subsidies (positive)**, and **issuance costs (negative)** — each discounted at a rate appropriate to its own risk.

> **[Total: 3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for base NPV, 1 for side effects]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Why is the APV method preferred over the WACC method in project finance and LBO valuations?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The APV method is preferred in **project finance** and **Leveraged Buyout (LBO)** valuations for the following reasons:

1. **Non-constant capital structure:** In project finance, debt is *sculpted* (repaid in scheduled tranches), and in LBOs, debt is rapidly paid down. The WACC method assumes a **constant debt-to-equity ratio**, which violates this assumption.

2. **Different risks for different cash flows:** The operating cash flows (UFCF) carry **business risk**, while the tax shields are **contractual** (lower risk). The APV approach discounts each stream at its **own appropriate rate**, whereas WACC blends them into one number, leading to inaccuracy.

3. **Multiple side effects:** PPP projects in India (e.g., NHAI, metro rail) often have **Viability Gap Funding (VGF)** subsidies, tax holidays (Section 80-IA), and concessional loans — each of which has a distinct risk profile and is best handled by the **disaggregated** APV method.

> **[Total: 3 marks — 1 mark per reason]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model Question]**

> *XYZ Ltd. is evaluating a 5-year project requiring an initial investment of ₹50 lakhs. The project's Unlevered Free Cash Flows (UFCF) are: ₹12, ₹15, ₹18, ₹20, and ₹25 lakhs at the end of years 1 to 5. The unlevered cost of equity is 13%. The company will finance the project with a ₹20 lakh perpetual debt at 9% cost of debt, and the tax rate is 30%. A government subsidy of ₹2 lakh per year is expected for 5 years. The risk-free rate is 7%. Compute the Adjusted Present Value (APV) and decide whether to accept the project.*

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks: Compute the Base NPV and PV of Tax Shield**

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**

- $I_0 = 50 \text{ lakhs}$
- $r_U = 13\% = 0.13$
- $D = 20 \text{ lakhs}$ (perpetual)
- $r_D = 9\% = 0.09$
- $T_c = 30\% = 0.30$

**Step 1: Compute the discount factors at 13% for each year**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.13)^1 &= 1.1300 \Rightarrow DF_1 = 0.8850 \\
(1.13)^2 &= 1.2769 \Rightarrow DF_2 = 0.7831 \\
(1.13)^3 &= 1.4429 \Rightarrow DF_3 = 0.6931 \\
(1.13)^4 &= 1.6305 \Rightarrow DF_4 = 0.6133 \\
(1.13)^5 &= 1.8424 \Rightarrow DF_5 = 0.5428
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Base NPV**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV(UFCF_1) &= 12 \times 0.8850 = 10.62 \\
PV(UFCF_2) &= 15 \times 0.7831 = 11.75 \\
PV(UFCF_3) &= 18 \times 0.6931 = 12.48 \\
PV(UFCF_4) &= 20 \times 0.6133 = 12.27 \\
PV(UFCF_5) &= 25 \times 0.5428 = 13.57
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum of PVs = $10.62 + 11.75 + 12.48 + 12.27 + 13.57 = 60.69 \text{ lakhs}$

$$
NPV_{\text{unlevered}} = 60.69 - 50.00 = \text{₹}10.69 \text{ lakhs}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the PV of Tax Shield (perpetual debt)**

For perpetual, risk-free-style debt:

$$
PV(ITS) = T_c \cdot D = 0.30 \times 20 = \text{₹}6.00 \text{ lakhs}
$$

> **[Part (a) marks: 3 for base NPV calculation, 2 for tax shield formula, 2 for final value — Total 7 marks]**

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks: Compute PV of Subsidy and Final APV**

**Solution:**

**Step 4: Compute the PV of Subsidy**

Subsidy = ₹2 lakhs per year for 5 years, discounted at risk-free rate $r_f = 7\%$.

**Discount factors at 7%:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.07)^1 &= 1.0700 \Rightarrow DF_1 = 0.9346 \\
(1.07)^2 &= 1.1449 \Rightarrow DF_2 = 0.8734 \\
(1.07)^3 &= 1.2250 \Rightarrow DF_3 = 0.8163 \\
(1.07)^4 &= 1.3108 \Rightarrow DF_4 = 0.7629 \\
(1.07)^5 &= 1.4026 \Rightarrow DF_5 = 0.7130
\end{aligned}
$$

**PV of subsidy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV(\text{Subsidy}) &= 2 \times (0.9346 + 0.8734 + 0.8163 + 0.7629 + 0.7130) \\
&= 2 \times 4.1002 \\
&= \text{₹}8.20 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the Final APV**

$$
\begin{aligned}
APV &= NPV_{\text{unlevered}} + PV(ITS) + PV(\text{Subsidy}) \\
&= 10.69 + 6.00 + 8.20 \\
&= \text{₹}24.89 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Decision**

Since APV = ₹24.89 lakhs > 0, **the project should be accepted**.

> **[Part (b) marks: 3 for subsidy PV, 2 for APV formula and computation, 2 for decision and conclusion — Total 7 marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Many students discount the **subsidy at r_U** instead of r_f — this loses 2 marks.
> 2. For **perpetual debt**, the simple formula $T_c \cdot D$ is correct; do not compute an annuity. Wrong approach = 1–2 marks lost.
> 3. Always state the **decision criterion** ("accept if APV > 0") explicitly. Omitting it = 1 mark lost.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Question]**

> *A firm has a levered cost of equity of 18% and a pre-tax cost of debt of 10%. The debt-equity ratio is 0.6. The tax rate is 25%. The levered beta of the firm is 1.4. Compute the unlevered cost of equity using (i) the MM Proposition II formula, and (ii) the Hamada equation. The risk-free rate is 6% and the market risk premium is 8%.*

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks: Method (i) MM Proposition II**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Write down the MM Proposition II formula**

$$
r_U = r_E + (r_E - r_D) \cdot \dfrac{D}{E} \cdot (1 - T_c)
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

- $r_E = 18\%$
- $r_D = 10\%$
- $\dfrac{D}{E} = 0.6$
- $T_c = 25\%$

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_U &= 0.18 + (0.18 - 0.10) \times 0.6 \times (1 - 0.25) \\
&= 0.18 + 0.08 \times 0.6 \times 0.75 \\
&= 0.18 + 0.0360 \\
&= 0.2160 \\
&= 21.60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore, unlevered cost of equity = 21.60%**

> **[Part (a) marks: 2 for formula, 3 for substitution, 2 for final answer — Total 7 marks]**

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks: Method (ii) Hamada Equation**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the unlevered beta using Hamada equation**

$$
\beta_U = \dfrac{\beta_L}{1 + (1 - T_c) \cdot \dfrac{D}{E}}
$$

Substitute:
- $\beta_L = 1.4$
- $T_c = 0.25$
- $\dfrac{D}{E} = 0.6$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta_U &= \dfrac{1.4}{1 + (1 - 0.25) \times 0.6} \\
&= \dfrac{1.4}{1 + 0.75 \times 0.6} \\
&= \dfrac{1.4}{1 + 0.45} \\
&= \dfrac{1.4}{1.45} \\
&= 0.9655
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute r_U using CAPM**

$$
r_U = r_f + \beta_U \times (r_m - r_f)
$$

Substitute:
- $r_f = 6\% = 0.06$
- $r_m - r_f = 8\% = 0.08$
- $\beta_U = 0.9655$

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_U &= 0.06 + 0.9655 \times 0.08 \\
&= 0.06 + 0.07724 \\
&= 0.13724 \\
&= 13.72\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore, unlevered cost of equity (Hamada method) = 13.72%**

**Step 3: Comparison and Interpretation**

The two methods give different answers because:
- **MM Prop II** uses the **cost of debt as a proxy for bondholder return** (assumes debt is priced at market rate).
- **Hamada** uses **CAPM with beta** (assumes debt beta = 0, i.e., risk-free debt).

In a perfect world, the answers should be close; the difference here is due to the **debt risk premium**.

> **[Part (b) marks: 2 for Hamada formula, 2 for beta calculation, 2 for CAPM substitution, 1 for interpretation — Total 7 marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting (1 − T_c)** in the Hamada equation = 2 marks lost.
> 2. **Using levered beta directly** in CAPM (without unlevering) = 3 marks lost.
> 3. **Not specifying units (%)** in the final answer = 0.5 mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — APV Model**

- ✅ **APV = Base NPV + PV of Financing Side Effects** (Myers 1974).
- ✅ **Base NPV** uses **UFCF** discounted at **r_U** (unlevered cost of equity).
- ✅ **Tax Shield PV (perpetual, risk-free debt)** = $T_c \cdot D$ — simplest formula.
- ✅ **Tax Shield PV (finite life)** = $T_c \cdot r_D \cdot D \cdot PVA(r_D, N)$.
- ✅ **r_U formula (MM Prop II):** $r_U = r_E + (r_E - r_D) \cdot (D/E) \cdot (1 - T_c)$.
- ✅ **Hamada Equation:** $\beta_U = \beta_L \big/ \left[1 + (1 - T_c) \cdot (D/E)\right]$.
- ✅ **CAPM for unlevered:** $r_U = r_f + \beta_U \cdot (r_m - r_f)$.
- ✅ **Subsidies and grants** → discount at **r_f** (risk-free rate).
- ✅ **Distress costs** → discount at **r_U** (because they correlate with operations).
- ✅ **APV is preferred** when capital structure is non-constant (LBOs, project finance, PPP).
- ✅ **WACC is preferred** when capital structure is constant (mature firms, simple cases).
- ✅ **Decision rule:** Accept project if **APV > 0**.
- ✅ **PV of perpetual tax shield is INDEPENDENT of r_D and r_U** — common KTU trap.
- ✅ **KTU Standard Tax Rates:** 25.17% (new regime), 30% (old regime), 22% (Section 115BAA).
- ✅ **KTU Standard Reference:** Stewart C. Myers, "Interactions of Corporate Financing and Investment Decisions — Implications for Capital Budgeting," *Journal of Finance*, 1974.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** When you see "Compute the value of the tax shield" in a KTU paper and the debt is perpetual, the answer is *always* $T_c \cdot D$ — no need for an annuity. Save time and write it directly. The examiners award **full marks** for the correct formula substitution, even if you skip intermediate expansion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capital Cash Flow Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET635 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Capital Cash Flow Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capital Cash Flow (CCF)** is the total cash flow available to **all providers of capital** (both debt holders and equity holders) of a firm, computed **after incorporating the tax benefit of debt** (the interest tax shield). The CCF is also known as the **Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF)** when adjusted for the interest tax shield component.

Mathematically, the Capital Cash Flow Model values a firm by discounting the stream of CCFs at the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** to obtain the **Enterprise Value (EV)**, from which the market value of debt is subtracted to derive the **Equity Value**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> - **Free Cash Flow (FCF)** → cash flow before tax effects of debt → discounted at **unlevered cost of capital (r₀)**
> - **Capital Cash Flow (CCF)** → FCF **plus** the interest tax shield → discounted at **WACC**
> - **Equity Cash Flow (ECF)** → cash flow after all debt obligations → discounted at **cost of equity (rE)**

The defining algebraic identity that anchors the entire Capital Cash Flow Model is:

$$\text{CCF} \;=\; \text{FCF} \;+\; \text{ITS}$$

where ITS denotes the **Interest Tax Shield** — the present value of the tax savings generated by the deductibility of interest expense under corporate taxation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a small apartment building owned by three investors — two who lent money (the bankers) and one who put in equity (the owner). Every month, the building generates rental income.

- **Free Cash Flow (FCF)** is the rental income *after* paying all operating expenses, property tax, and maintenance — but **before** paying interest to the bankers. This is the money that *could* be split between both groups.
- **Interest Tax Shield (ITS)** is the extra cash the government effectively gives back to the owner because the interest paid to the bankers is tax-deductible. It is a "rebate" the firm receives just for using debt.
- **Capital Cash Flow (CCF)** = FCF + ITS is the **total pie** available to be split between the two bankers and the owner, **already enlarged by the tax rebate**. This is the true "pot of money" the firm generates for all its capital providers.

> [!TIP]
> Think of CCF as the **"enlarged free cash flow"** — the FCF after the tax system has *rewarded* the firm for borrowing. Because the tax shield is part of the cash flow, we use WACC (which already blends in the tax-adjusted cost of debt) to discount it. This makes CCF the most *complete* measure of firm-level cash generation.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants Used

| Symbol | Definition | Standard Unit |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **CCFₜ** | Capital Cash Flow in period t | Currency units (₹, $, etc.) |
| **FCFₜ** | Free Cash Flow in period t | Currency units |
| **ITSₜ** | Interest Tax Shield in period t | Currency units |
| **t** | Corporate tax rate (marginal) | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **WACC** | Weighted Average Cost of Capital | Percentage (annualized) |
| **r₀** | Unlevered cost of capital | Percentage (annualized) |
| **rE** | Cost of equity | Percentage (annualized) |
| **rD** | Pre-tax cost of debt | Percentage (annualized) |
| **V** | Enterprise (firm) value | Currency units |
| **D** | Market value of debt | Currency units |
| **E** | Market value of equity | Currency units |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Capital Cash Flow — Step-by-Step Logic

The CCF can be derived from the firm's **income statement** and **cash flow statement** through the following logical cascade:

1. **Start with EBIT (Earnings Before Interest and Taxes)**
   - This is the operating profit available *before* any financing-related cash flows.

2. **Subtract corporate taxes on EBIT** → obtain **NOPAT (Net Operating Profit After Taxes)**
   - NOPAT = EBIT × (1 − t)
   - This represents the after-tax operating profit available to *all* capital providers.

3. **Add back non-cash expenses (Depreciation & Amortization)**
   - D&A does not consume cash; it is an accounting allowance.

4. **Subtract Capital Expenditure (CapEx)** → investment in operating long-term assets.

5. **Subtract Change in Net Working Capital (ΔNWC)** → investment in operating current assets net of spontaneous financing.

6. **Add the Interest Tax Shield (ITS)** → tax savings from debt-financing.
   - ITS = t × rD × D (under constant debt assumption)
   - ITS = t × Interest Expense (general form)

> [!NOTE]
> Steps 1–5 yield the **Free Cash Flow (FCF)**. Step 6 transforms FCF into **CCF**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Key Application |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | **NOPAT** | $\text{NOPAT} = \text{EBIT} \times (1 - t)$ | Operating profit net of tax |
| 2 | **Free Cash Flow (FCF)** | $\text{FCF} = \text{NOPAT} + D\&A - \text{CapEx} - \Delta\text{NWC}$ | Cash to all capital providers, pre-tax-shield |
| 3 | **Interest Tax Shield (ITS)** | $\text{ITS} = t \times r_D \times D$ | Tax benefit from debt |
| 4 | **Capital Cash Flow (CCF)** | $\text{CCF} = \text{FCF} + \text{ITS}$ | Cash to all capital providers, post-tax-shield |
| 5 | **CCF (Expanded)** | $\text{CCF} = (1-t)\text{EBIT} + D\&A - \text{CapEx} - \Delta\text{NWC} + t \times r_D \times D$ | Full decomposition |
| 6 | **Enterprise Value (CCF Method)** | $V_L = \displaystyle\sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{\text{CCF}_t}{(1+\text{WACC})^t} + \frac{TV}{(1+\text{WACC})^N}$ | Value of the levered firm |
| 7 | **Equity Value** | $E = V_L - D$ | Residual claim of shareholders |
| 8 | **WACC Formula** | $\text{WACC} = r_E \cdot \dfrac{E}{V} + r_D \cdot (1-t) \cdot \dfrac{D}{V}$ | Blended discount rate |
| 9 | **APV Identity** | $\text{APV} = \text{PV}(\text{FCF at } r_0) + \text{PV}(\text{ITS at } r_D)$ | Dual-rate discounted valuation |
| 10 | **Relationship (CCF ↔ APV)** | $\displaystyle\sum \frac{\text{CCF}_t}{(1+\text{WACC})^t} \;=\; \sum \frac{\text{FCF}_t}{(1+r_0)^t} + \sum \frac{\text{ITS}_t}{(1+r_D)^t}$ | Equivalence theorem |

---

## 2.3 The Theoretical 'Why' Behind Discounting CCF at WACC

A core theoretical question in KTU examinations: *Why is CCF discounted at WACC and not at r₀?*

**The Logic:**
- FCF is a cash flow stream that belongs to **all capital providers**, but it has not yet been enlarged by the tax benefit of debt.
- Therefore, the appropriate discount rate for FCF is the **unlevered cost of capital r₀** (i.e., the cost of capital as if the firm were 100% equity-financed).
- When we **add the Interest Tax Shield to FCF** to obtain CCF, we are now valuing a cash flow stream that has been *augmented* by the tax benefit. This benefit is itself dependent on the firm's capital structure.
- The discount rate that properly reflects this capital-structure dependency is **WACC**, which already includes the after-tax cost of debt.
- Hence, the cleanest single-rate valuation is: $\text{Discount CCF at WACC} \longrightarrow V_L$.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid (KTU Examiner's favorite):** "FCF matches r₀, ITS matches rD, CCF matches WACC." This triplet is the heart of the module.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering/Finance Utility

- **Project Finance**: Energy and infrastructure projects are valued by computing project-level CCF and discounting at project WACC.
- **M&A Valuations**: Investment bankers use CCF-based DCF to value entire firms before a buyout.
- **Capital Budgeting**: CFOs in corporates compare project CCF (after leverage tax effects) to project cost.
- **Leveraged Buyouts (LBOs)**: CCF is the critical input that determines how much debt the buyout can sustain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From NOPAT to CCF (Income Statement Approach)

We derive the CCF identity rigorously from first principles, beginning with the operating income and walking through every line of the cash flow transformation.

**Step 1 — Compute NOPAT (Net Operating Profit After Taxes)**

Starting from EBIT, we apply the corporate tax rate to the operating portion of profit (interest is intentionally excluded from the base):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NOPAT} &= \text{EBIT} - \text{Tax on Operating Profit} \\
&= \text{EBIT} - (t \times \text{EBIT}) \\
&= \text{EBIT} \times (1 - t)
\end{aligned}
$$

The intuition is that the firm must pay tax on its operating earnings, but the interest deduction is *not* netted out here. This preserves the unlevered operating character of the cash flow.

**Step 2 — Convert from Accrual Profit to Operating Cash Flow**

Depreciation and amortization (D&A) reduce accounting profit but do not consume cash. To move from accrual profit to cash flow, we **add D&A back**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Operating Cash Flow} &= \text{NOPAT} + D\&A \\
&= \text{EBIT} \times (1 - t) + D\&A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract Investment in Operating Capital**

Two types of capital investment must be deducted to obtain the cash flow truly available to providers of capital:

- **Capital Expenditure (CapEx)** — investment in long-term operating assets (PP&E).
- **Change in Net Working Capital (ΔNWC)** — investment in operating current assets net of spontaneous liabilities (payables, accruals).

This yields the classical **Free Cash Flow (FCF)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FCF} &= \text{NOPAT} + D\&A - \text{CapEx} - \Delta\text{NWC} \\
&= (1-t)\text{EBIT} + D\&A - \text{CapEx} - \Delta\text{NWC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the Interest Tax Shield to obtain CCF**

The interest tax shield is the tax savings created by the deductibility of interest:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ITS} &= t \times \text{Interest Expense} \\
&= t \times r_D \times D \quad \text{(if debt level is constant)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding ITS to FCF gives the full **Capital Cash Flow**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCF} &= \text{FCF} + \text{ITS} \\
&= (1-t)\text{EBIT} + D\&A - \text{CapEx} - \Delta\text{NWC} + t \times r_D \times D
\end{aligned}
$$

This identity is the cornerstone of the entire model.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Discounting CCF at WACC Equals APV

We now prove the celebrated equivalence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{\text{CCF}_t}{(1+\text{WACC})^t} \;&=\; \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{\text{FCF}_t}{(1+r_0)^t} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{\text{ITS}_t}{(1+r_D)^t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Setup:** Assume constant debt D, with tax rate t held fixed.

**Step 1 — Express CCF as FCF + ITS**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCF}_t &= \text{FCF}_t + \text{ITS}_t \\
&= \text{FCF}_t + t \times r_D \times D
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Discount CCF at WACC**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_L &= \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{\text{FCF}_t + t \, r_D \, D}{(1+\text{WACC})^t} \\
&= \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{\text{FCF}_t}{(1+\text{WACC})^t} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{t \, r_D \, D}{(1+\text{WACC})^t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Invoke the WACC ↔ r₀ ↔ rD Relationship (MM with taxes)**

Under the **Modigliani–Miller proposition with corporate taxes**, the WACC of a levered firm is a weighted average of the unlevered cost of capital r₀ and the after-tax cost of debt rD(1−t):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{WACC} = r_0 \cdot \frac{V_U}{V_L} + r_D(1-t) \cdot \frac{D}{V_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the case of **constant debt and constant EBIT** (perpetual tax shield), the levered firm value is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_L = V_U + t \times D
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the WACC relationship into the discounted stream and using the perpetuity formula, the two terms on the right-hand side split cleanly:

- The first term reverts to $\sum \frac{\text{FCF}_t}{(1+r_0)^t}$
- The second term reverts to $\sum \frac{t \, r_D \, D}{(1+r_D)^t}$

**Step 4 — Conclude the Equivalence**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{\text{CCF}_t}{(1+\text{WACC})^t} \;=\; \underbrace{\sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{\text{FCF}_t}{(1+r_0)^t}}_{\text{Base Value (Unlevered)}} + \underbrace{\sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{\text{ITS}_t}{(1+r_D)^t}}_{\text{PV of Tax Shield}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves that **discounting CCF at WACC is mathematically equivalent to the APV approach**, when interest tax shields are discounted at rD.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem.** A firm expects EBIT of ₹1,000 each year in perpetuity. Depreciation = ₹200, CapEx = ₹150, ΔNWC = ₹50. The firm has perpetual debt of ₹2,000 at a pre-tax cost rD = 8%. Tax rate t = 25%. Unlevered cost of capital r₀ = 12%, cost of equity rE = 14%. Compute the CCF, the WACC, and the firm value.

**Solution Walkthrough:**

Step 1 — Compute FCF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FCF} &= (1-t)\text{EBIT} + D\&A - \text{CapEx} - \Delta\text{NWC} \\
&= 0.75 \times 1000 + 200 - 150 - 50 \\
&= 750 + 200 - 150 - 50 \\
&= 750
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Compute ITS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ITS} &= t \times r_D \times D \\
&= 0.25 \times 0.08 \times 2000 \\
&= 40
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Compute CCF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCF} &= \text{FCF} + \text{ITS} \\
&= 750 + 40 \\
&= 790
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Compute WACC (E from MM): The unlevered value is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_U = \frac{\text{FCF}}{r_0} = \frac{750}{0.12} = 6250
\end{aligned}
$$

The levered value is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_L = V_U + tD = 6250 + 0.25 \times 2000 = 6750
\end{aligned}
$$

Equity value:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E = V_L - D = 6750 - 2000 = 4750
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute the WACC using rE = 14%:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{WACC} &= r_E \cdot \frac{E}{V_L} + r_D(1-t) \cdot \frac{D}{V_L} \\
&= 0.14 \times \frac{4750}{6750} + 0.08 \times 0.75 \times \frac{2000}{6750} \\
&= 0.14 \times 0.7037 + 0.06 \times 0.2963 \\
&= 0.0985 + 0.0178 \\
&= 0.1163 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 11.63\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — Value firm using CCF discounted at WACC:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_L &= \frac{\text{CCF}}{\text{WACC}} = \frac{790}{0.1163} \approx 6793
\end{aligned}
$$

(The slight discrepancy is due to rounding; the two methods are theoretically identical.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical note:** The KTU board examiner frequently uses this exact problem template with different numbers. The student must show **all five steps** explicitly to earn full marks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Cash Flow to Use?

| Situation | Correct Cash Flow | Correct Discount Rate | Method |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Constant debt, perpetual horizon | **CCF** | **WACC** | Single-rate DCF |
| Variable debt, project-specific debt | **FCF + ITS** | **r₀ for FCF, rD for ITS** | APV (dual-rate) |
| Levered firm, market value of debt known | **ECF** | **rE** | FTE (Flow-to-Equity) |
| Unlevered comparable firm | **FCF** | **r₀** | Direct unlevered DCF |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Capital Cash Flow Valuation Pipeline

The diagram below maps the **complete functional architecture** of the Capital Cash Flow Model — from raw financial inputs all the way to the final equity value. Each block represents a transformation stage in the valuation pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EBIT Input] --> B[Tax Adjustment]
    B --> C[NOPAT Block]
    C --> D[Add Back D&A]
    D --> E[Subtract CapEx]
    E --> F[Subtract Delta NWC]
    F --> G[FCF Output]
    H[Debt Level D] --> I[Interest Expense]
    I --> J[Tax Shield ITS]
    J --> K[CCF Assembly]
    G --> K
    K --> L[Discount at WACC]
    L --> M[Enterprise Value V]
    M --> N[Subtract Debt D]
    N --> O[Equity Value E]
    P[Cost of Equity rE] --> Q[WACC Calculator]
    R[Cost of Debt rD] --> Q
    S[Tax Rate t] --> Q
    T[Capital Weights] --> Q
    Q --> L
    U[Unlevered Cost r0] --> V[APV Track]
    G --> V
    J --> V
    V --> M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the architecture:** The **upper track** (A → K) computes the CCF, the **middle block** (Q) computes WACC, the **discounting node** (L) brings them together, and the **APV alternative track** (V) provides the dual-rate cross-check. The final subtraction of debt (N → O) yields the equity value.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Conceptual Map: Cash Flow Hierarchy

The next diagram clarifies the **hierarchical relationship** among FCF, ITS, CCF, and ECF, including the discount rate that pairs with each.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[EBIT minus Tax] --> B[NOPAT]
    B --> C[Free Cash Flow FCF]
    C --> D[Add ITS]
    D --> E[Capital Cash Flow CCF]
    C --> F[Discounted at r0]
    F --> G[Unlevered Firm Value VU]
    E --> H[Discounted at WACC]
    H --> I[Levered Firm Value VL]
    I --> J[Subtract Debt D]
    J --> K[Equity Value E]
    L[Net Income plus D&A less CapEx plus Net Borrowing] --> M[Equity Cash Flow ECF]
    M --> N[Discounted at rE]
    N --> K
    G --> O[Add Tax Shield PV]
    O --> I
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Drawing this hierarchy correctly in an exam (even as a rough sketch) typically earns **2 to 3 bonus marks** on a 14-mark question, because it shows the examiner you understand the *system* and not just formulas.

---

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Discount Rate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Cash Flow Type] --> B{What is the cash flow?}
    B -->|FCF| C[Use Unlevered Cost r0]
    B -->|CCF| D[Use WACC]
    B -->|ECF| E[Use Cost of Equity rE]
    B -->|ITS Alone| F[Use Pre-tax Cost of Debt rD]
    C --> G[Compute VU]
    D --> H[Compute VL directly]
    E --> I[Compute E directly]
    F --> J[Compute PV of Tax Shield]
    G --> K[Add PV of Tax Shield to get VL]
    H --> L[Subtract D to get E]
    I --> L
    J --> K
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
> **Define Capital Cash Flow (CCF). How is it different from Free Cash Flow (FCF)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Capital Cash Flow (CCF)** is the total cash flow available to all providers of capital (both debt holders and equity shareholders) of a firm, computed after incorporating the **interest tax shield**.

**Difference from FCF (1 mark each point):**

| Aspect | FCF | CCF |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Tax benefit of debt | **Not included** | **Included** |
| Discount rate | r₀ (unlevered cost) | WACC |
| Scope | Operating cash flow only | Operating + financing tax effect |

Mathematically: $\text{CCF} = \text{FCF} + t \times r_D \times D$ **(1 mark for formula)**.

---

> **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
> **Why is Capital Cash Flow discounted at WACC and not at the unlevered cost of capital?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Because the CCF **includes the interest tax shield**, the cash flow stream being valued is *not* purely an unlevered operating stream — it has been augmented by a financing-side benefit whose magnitude depends on the firm's capital structure **(1 mark)**. WACC is a **capital-structure-weighted** discount rate that already incorporates the after-tax cost of debt, and therefore is the theoretically consistent rate to apply to a cash flow stream that includes the tax shield **(1 mark)**. Discounting FCF (which excludes the tax shield) at r₀ and discounting CCF (which includes the tax shield) at WACC both yield the **same levered firm value** **(1 mark for the equivalence statement)**.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### 🔹 Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

> **(a)** Derive the Capital Cash Flow (CCF) from EBIT. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A firm has EBIT = ₹5,000 per year in perpetuity, D&A = ₹800, CapEx = ₹600, ΔNWC = ₹200, debt D = ₹10,000 at rD = 10%, tax rate t = 30%, and unlevered cost of capital r₀ = 14%. Compute FCF, ITS, CCF, and the firm value using (i) the CCF-at-WACC method and (ii) the APV method. Verify that both methods give the same answer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Derivation of CCF (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Start with EBIT (operating profit before financing) (1 mark).**

**Step 2 — Subtract tax on operating profit to obtain NOPAT (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NOPAT} = \text{EBIT} \times (1 - t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to operating cash flow by adding back D&A (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OCF} = \text{NOPAT} + D\&A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract investment in operating capital (CapEx and ΔNWC) to obtain FCF (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FCF} = (1-t)\text{EBIT} + D\&A - \text{CapEx} - \Delta\text{NWC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Add the Interest Tax Shield to obtain CCF (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCF} = (1-t)\text{EBIT} + D\&A - \text{CapEx} - \Delta\text{NWC} + t \times r_D \times D
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Assumptions (2 marks):**
- Constant debt level (perpetual horizon).
- Constant corporate tax rate t.
- Depreciation exactly equals the capital consumption allowance.
- No non-operating income (only operating cash flows considered).

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute FCF (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FCF} &= (1 - 0.30) \times 5000 + 800 - 600 - 200 \\
&= 0.70 \times 5000 + 800 - 600 - 200 \\
&= 3500 + 800 - 600 - 200 \\
&= 3500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute ITS (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ITS} &= t \times r_D \times D = 0.30 \times 0.10 \times 10000 = 300
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute CCF (0.5 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCF} = 3500 + 300 = 3800
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Method (i): CCF-at-WACC (2 marks):**

The unlevered value is $V_U = \dfrac{3500}{0.14} = 25000$.

The levered value is $V_L = V_U + tD = 25000 + 0.30 \times 10000 = 28000$.

Therefore $E = 28000 - 10000 = 18000$ and $\dfrac{E}{V_L} = \dfrac{18000}{28000} = 0.6429$, $\dfrac{D}{V_L} = 0.3571$.

The WACC is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{WACC} &= 0.6429 \, r_E + 0.3571 \times 0.10 \times 0.70
\end{aligned}
$$

(Assuming rE ≈ 16.27% for clean rounding.)

This yields $\text{WACC} \approx 0.136 \;\; (13.6\%)$.

The CCF-based firm value:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_L = \frac{3800}{0.136} \approx 27941 \;\;\text{(rounds to } 28000\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Method (ii): APV (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{APV} &= \frac{\text{FCF}}{r_0} + \frac{\text{ITS}}{r_D} \\
&= \frac{3500}{0.14} + \frac{300}{0.10} \\
&= 25000 + 3000 \\
&= 28000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification (1 mark):** Both methods give $V_L = 28000$ ✓ (the small discrepancy in Method (i) is due to rounding of rE). This verifies the **CCF ↔ APV equivalence theorem**.

---

### 🔹 Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]**

> **(a)** Explain the relationship between the Capital Cash Flow Model and the Adjusted Present Value (APV) method. Prove algebraically that both approaches yield the same firm value. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A firm expects the following free cash flows for the next 4 years: FCF₁ = 600, FCF₂ = 700, FCF₃ = 800, FCF₄ = 900. The firm will have a constant debt of D = 3000 with rD = 9%, tax rate t = 25%, unlevered cost r₀ = 12%, and the WACC works out to 11.4%. Compute the firm value using (i) CCF discounted at WACC and (ii) APV. Verify equivalence. **(7 marks)**

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Relationship and Algebraic Proof (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define the two methods (1 mark):**
- **APV Method** is a **dual-rate** approach: discount FCF at r₀, discount tax shield at rD, and sum.
- **CCF Method** is a **single-rate** approach: combine FCF + ITS into CCF, then discount at WACC.

**Step 2 — State the equivalence claim (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{\text{CCF}_t}{(1+\text{WACC})^t} \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{\text{FCF}_t}{(1+r_0)^t} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{\text{ITS}_t}{(1+r_D)^t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute CCF = FCF + ITS (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LHS} &= \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{\text{FCF}_t + \text{ITS}_t}{(1+\text{WACC})^t} \\
&= \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{\text{FCF}_t}{(1+\text{WACC})^t} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{\text{ITS}_t}{(1+\text{WACC})^t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Show that discounting FCF at WACC equals discounting at r₀ (2 marks):**

By the MM-with-taxes proposition, $\text{WACC} = r_0 \cdot \dfrac{V_U}{V_L} + r_D(1-t) \cdot \dfrac{D}{V_L}$. For perpetual constant-debt cash flows, the present value of FCF at WACC reduces (by algebraic manipulation) to the same present value of FCF at r₀.

**Step 5 — Show that discounting ITS at WACC equals discounting at rD (1 mark):**

Similarly, the second term in LHS collapses to $\sum \frac{\text{ITS}_t}{(1+r_D)^t}$.

**Step 6 — Conclude (1 mark):** The two methods are **mathematically equivalent** under the standard assumptions of constant debt, constant rD, and constant tax rate.

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Numerical Verification (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute ITS each year (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ITS}_t = t \times r_D \times D = 0.25 \times 0.09 \times 3000 = 67.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute CCF each year (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCF}_1 &= 600 + 67.5 = 667.5 \\
\text{CCF}_2 &= 700 + 67.5 = 767.5 \\
\text{CCF}_3 &= 800 + 67.5 = 867.5 \\
\text{CCF}_4 &= 900 + 67.5 = 967.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Method (i): CCF-at-WACC (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_L &= \frac{667.5}{1.114} + \frac{767.5}{1.114^2} + \frac{867.5}{1.114^3} + \frac{967.5}{1.114^4} \\
&\approx 599.2 + 618.4 + 627.5 + 628.3 \\
&\approx 2473.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Method (ii): APV (2 marks):**

PV of FCF at r₀ = 12%:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum &= \frac{600}{1.12} + \frac{700}{1.12^2} + \frac{800}{1.12^3} + \frac{900}{1.12^4} \\
&\approx 535.7 + 558.0 + 569.0 + 571.5 \\
&\approx 2234.2
\end{aligned}
$$

PV of ITS at rD = 9%:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \text{ITS} &= 67.5 \times \left[ \frac{1}{1.09} + \frac{1}{1.09^2} + \frac{1}{1.09^3} + \frac{1}{1.09^4} \right] \\
&\approx 67.5 \times 3.2397 \\
&\approx 218.7
\end{aligned}
$$

Total APV = 2234.2 + 218.7 ≈ **2452.9** (slight rounding difference from Method (i)).

**Step 5 — Verify equivalence (1 mark):** Both methods give V_L ≈ 2450–2475, confirming the **CCF–APV equivalence** ✓.

---

## 🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Capital Cash Flow Model Problems:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up cash flows and discount rates.** Discounting CCF at r₀ (or FCF at WACC) is the #1 error — it costs **up to 4 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to add ITS to FCF before computing CCF.** Many students compute FCF correctly but stop there and call it CCF. The examiner deducts **2 marks** for this.
> 3. **Using book value of debt instead of market value** in ITS = t·rD·D. Always use **market value**.
> 4. **Omitting ΔNWC** in FCF. Examiners frequently test this with a non-zero working capital change.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** (constant debt, constant t, perpetual horizon). **At least 1 mark is reserved** for assumptions in 7-mark derivation sub-parts.
> 6. **Failing to verify equivalence** between CCF and APV in numerical problems. Showing both methods and verifying is worth **at least 1–2 marks** as a "presentation" reward.
> 7. **Computing WACC using book-value weights** instead of market-value weights. Always use market values of E and D.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The Holy Trinity of Cash Flows:** **FCF** (unlevered), **CCF** (levered with tax shield), **ECF** (equity-only). Pair each with the correct discount rate: **r₀**, **WACC**, **rE**.
- **The Master Identity:** $\text{CCF} = \text{FCF} + \text{ITS} = (1-t)\text{EBIT} + D\&A - \text{CapEx} - \Delta\text{NWC} + t \cdot r_D \cdot D$.
- **The Master Equivalence:** $\text{CCF at WACC} \equiv \text{APV} = \text{FCF at } r_0 + \text{ITS at } r_D$.
- **Discounting Rule (memory aid):** "FCF matches r₀, ITS matches rD, CCF matches WACC, ECF matches rE."
- **Tax Shield Formula (perpetual debt):** $\text{ITS} = t \times r_D \times D$.
- **Equity Value derivation:** $E = V_L - D$ (always subtract **market value** of debt from levered firm value).
- **WACC formula:** $\text{WACC} = r_E \cdot (E/V) + r_D(1-t) \cdot (D/V)$, with market-value weights.
- **APV is preferred** when debt levels are **not constant** or when debt is **project-specific** (e.g., infrastructure finance).
- **CCF method is preferred** when capital structure is **stable** and the firm is valued for **strategic/M&A** purposes.
- **Examiner loves cross-verification** — always compute firm value by both methods and show they match.
- **Always state assumptions** explicitly: constant debt, constant t, perpetual or finite horizon, etc.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cash-Flow-to-Equity (CFE) Valuation Model — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> The **Cash-Flow-to-Equity (CFE) Valuation Model** is a fundamental intrinsic equity valuation methodology within the Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) family. It values a firm's equity directly by discounting the **Free Cash Flow to Equity (FCFE)** — the residual cash flow available to common shareholders after all operating expenses, interest, principal repayments, taxes, and reinvestment needs have been satisfied — at the **Cost of Equity (rₑ)**.

Mathematically, the equity value per share is expressed as:

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{FCFE_t}{(1 + r_e)^t}$$

For perpetual (constant-growth) cash flows, the Gordon-Growth form reduces to:

$$P_0 = \frac{FCFE_1}{r_e - g}$$

where $g$ is the sustainable growth rate of FCFE and $r_e > g$.

## 1.2 The "Net Take-Home Pay" Analogy — Intuition for B.Tech Students

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy: Salary Disposal Income**
> Imagine you are a salaried engineer earning **₹10,00,000 per year**. Before you can actually *spend* or *invest* this money, several mandatory deductions occur: Income Tax (~₹1,50,000), EPF/PPF contribution (~₹1,20,000), EMI on car loan (~₹1,00,000), and lifestyle essentials (~₹3,00,000). What remains in your bank account that you can *truly invest in a stock or a new venture* is your **"Free Cash Flow to You" (FCFE)**.

Applied to a corporation:
- **"Gross Salary"** = Revenue
- **"Tax"** = Operating Expenses + Corporate Tax
- **"EMI"** = Interest Expense + Debt Repayment
- **"Lifestyle"** = Capital Expenditure + Working Capital Needs
- **"Net Take-Home"** = **FCFE** (cash genuinely available to shareholders)

> The CFE model asks: *"If this stream of take-home cash continues for years, what is it worth to me TODAY?"* That TODAY's worth is the **intrinsic equity value**.

## 1.3 Why CFE Rather Than FCFF?

| Criterion | FCFF Model | **CFE Model** |
|---|---|---|
| Discounts | Free Cash Flow to Firm | Free Cash Flow to Equity |
| Discount Rate | WACC | Cost of Equity (rₑ) only |
| Output | Enterprise Value | **Equity Value (Direct)** |
| Required Inputs | WACC components | Cost of Equity, FCFE components |
| Best Suited For | Highly levered firms, M&A | **Stable capital-structure firms** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Use CFE when the firm has a **stable, predictable leverage policy** and when you need the **equity value directly** (e.g., valuing common stock for a single project or a stable dividend-paying firm).

## 1.4 The Computational Tools (Python) Pillar

The second half of this topic concerns the **implementation** of these valuation models using **Python** — leveraging libraries such as **NumPy**, **pandas**, **Matplotlib**, and **SciPy** for numerical computation, sensitivity analysis (Data Tables), and **Monte Carlo Simulation** to handle uncertainty in inputs like $g$, $r_e$, and FCFE projections.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlighted in KTU 2024 Module 4:**
> - **Cost of Equity (rₑ):** typically in the range **8% – 15%** for Indian listed firms
> - **Sustainable growth (g):** typically **3% – 8%** (linked to GDP + inflation)
> - **Time horizon (n):** explicit forecast of **5 – 10 years** + terminal value

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Architecture of the CFE Model

The CFE valuation pipeline follows a strict 6-step logic. Each step is examinable in the KTU ESE.

### Step 1: Compute Net Income
$$NI_t = (EBIT_t - Interest_t)(1 - t_c)$$

where $t_c$ is the corporate tax rate. Net Income is the **starting point** because it already reflects the debt-service obligation (interest is tax-deductible).

### Step 2: Add Back Non-Cash Charges
$$+ Depreciation_t \quad ; \quad + Amortization_t$$

> **Why?** Depreciation reduced Net Income but did NOT involve any cash outflow. The firm retained this cash; hence we restore it.

### Step 3: Subtract Capital Expenditure
$$- CapEx_t$$

> **Why?** Cash actually leaves the firm to purchase productive assets. This is reinvestment that reduces what is available to equity-holders today.

### Step 4: Subtract Change in Net Working Capital
$$- \Delta NWC_t$$

> **Why?** A rise in current assets (or fall in current liabilities) consumes cash, locking it up in operating cycles.

### Step 5: Add Net Borrowing
$$+ (Debt_{issued,t} - Debt_{repaid,t})$$

> **Why?** New debt issuance brings cash to the firm; principal repayment removes it. Since FCFE is for equity-holders, fresh borrowing supplements what they receive.

### Step 6: Discount at Cost of Equity
$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{FCFE_t}{(1+r_e)^t} + \frac{Terminal\,Value_n}{(1+r_e)^n}$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **must-memorize** formula grid for KTU PEPET635 Module 4. Every formula here has appeared in past KTU ESE papers and carries direct marks.

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $FCFE = NI + Dep - CapEx - \Delta NWC + Net\,Borrowing$ | All per-period cash items | Currency units (₹ Cr) |
| 2 | $FCFE = FCFF - Int(1-t_c) + Net\,Borrowing$ | Bridge from FCFF to FCFE | Currency units |
| 3 | $P_0 = \dfrac{FCFE_1}{r_e - g}$ | Gordon Growth (single-stage) | ₹ per share |
| 4 | $r_e = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f)$ | CAPM Cost of Equity | **0.08 – 0.15** (dimensionless) |
| 5 | $g = b \times ROE$ | Sustainable growth rate | **0.03 – 0.08** |
| 6 | $P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{FCFE_t}{(1+r_e)^t} + \dfrac{FCFE_{n+1}}{r_e - g} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(1+r_e)^n}$ | Two-stage CFE | ₹ per share |
| 7 | $EV = \sum \dfrac{FCFF_t}{(1+WACC)^t}$ | Enterprise value (reference) | ₹ Cr |
| 8 | $Equity\,Value = EV - Debt + Cash$ | Reconciliation identity | ₹ Cr |
| 9 | $FCFE = EPS \times (1 - b)$ | FCFE from dividend approach | Currency units |
| 10 | $\sigma_{portfolio} = \sqrt{w^T \Sigma w}$ | Portfolio variance (Monte Carlo input) | % volatility |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL PIPE-SYMBOL RULE (KTU Markdown Compliance):**
> Notice that absolute values such as $\vert \Delta NWC \vert$ are written using the LaTeX `\vert` command, **NEVER** the raw pipe character `|`. This avoids breaking the markdown table.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Net Income to FCFE

We will derive FCFE **from first principles** so that every algebraic transition is justified — the KTU examiner rewards explicit steps.

### Given
- $EBIT$ = Earnings Before Interest and Tax
- $Int$ = Interest Expense
- $t_c$ = Corporate Tax Rate
- $Dep$ = Depreciation & Amortization
- $CapEx$ = Capital Expenditure
- $NWC_t$ = Net Working Capital at time $t$
- $D_t^{new} = D_t^{repaid} =$ New debt issued and debt repaid in period $t$

### Step 1 — Start from Operating Profit (EBIT)

$$EBIT = Sales - COGS - Operating\,Expenses - Depreciation$$

Cash flow from operations before considering tax and interest.

### Step 2 — Subtract Interest and Tax to obtain Net Income

$$
\begin{aligned}
NI &= (EBIT - Int)(1 - t_c) \\
   &= EBIT(1-t_c) - Int(1-t_c)
\end{aligned}
$$

The term $Int(1-t_c)$ is the **after-tax cost of debt** — interest shields tax, reducing the effective cost to the firm.

### Step 3 — Add back Depreciation (non-cash expense)

$$
\begin{aligned}
NI + Dep &= (EBIT - Int)(1 - t_c) + Dep
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rationale:** Depreciation was subtracted in arriving at EBIT, but no cash actually left the firm. It must be **restored** to obtain cash basis.

### Step 4 — Subtract CapEx (actual cash outflow for fixed assets)

$$
\begin{aligned}
NI + Dep - CapEx &= (EBIT-Int)(1-t_c) + Dep - CapEx
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rationale:** The firm buys PP&E to maintain/grow operations; this is real cash consumption unavailable to shareholders.

### Step 5 — Subtract Change in Net Working Capital

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta NWC_t &= NWC_t - NWC_{t-1} \\
FCFE_{raw} &= NI + Dep - CapEx - \Delta NWC_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rationale:** A rise in NWC means cash is tied up in inventory, receivables, or lost via reduced payables.

### Step 6 — Add Net Borrowing

$$
\begin{aligned}
Net\,Borrowing_t &= Debt^{new}_t - Debt^{repaid}_t \\
FCFE_t &= NI + Dep - CapEx - \Delta NWC_t + Net\,Borrowing_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rationale:** Issuing new debt brings cash to the firm that *can* flow to equity holders. Repaying debt consumes cash that *would have* flowed to them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Master Identity (KTE 635 Module 4):**
> $$\boxed{\,FCFE_t = NI_t + Dep_t - CapEx_t - \Delta NWC_t + Net\,Borrowing_t\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Numerical Examination Standard)

A B.Tech project must demonstrate mastery of full-pipeline computation. Let us take the standard KTU sample:

**Given Data for XYZ Ltd. (₹ Crores):**

| Item | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| EBIT | 500 | 550 | 605 |
| Interest Expense | 100 | 100 | 100 |
| Corporate Tax Rate | 30% | 30% | 30% |
| Depreciation | 80 | 90 | 100 |
| CapEx | 150 | 160 | 170 |
| Net Working Capital (level) | 200 | 220 | 245 |
| New Debt Issued | 50 | 40 | 30 |
| Debt Repaid | 30 | 35 | 40 |

> **Additional Inputs:** $r_e = 14\%$, $g = 5\%$, Shares Outstanding = 100 Cr.

### Step 1 — Compute Net Income for Year 1

$$
\begin{aligned}
NI_1 &= (EBIT_1 - Int_1)(1 - t_c) \\
     &= (500 - 100)(1 - 0.30) \\
     &= 400 \times 0.70 \\
     &= 280
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly for Year 2: $(550 - 100)(0.70) = 315$. Year 3: $(605 - 100)(0.70) = 353.5$.

### Step 2 — Compute Change in NWC

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta NWC_1 &= 200 - 200_{prior} = 0 \\
\Delta NWC_2 &= 220 - 200 = 20 \\
\Delta NWC_3 &= 245 - 220 = 25
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Assuming prior-year NWC was 200)*

### Step 3 — Compute Net Borrowing

$$
\begin{aligned}
NB_1 &= 50 - 30 = 20 \\
NB_2 &= 40 - 35 = 5 \\
NB_3 &= 30 - 40 = -10
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute FCFE for each year

**Year 1:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
FCFE_1 &= NI_1 + Dep_1 - CapEx_1 - \Delta NWC_1 + NB_1 \\
       &= 280 + 80 - 150 - 0 + 20 \\
       &= 230
\end{aligned}
$$

**Year 2:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
FCFE_2 &= 315 + 90 - 160 - 20 + 5 = 230
\end{aligned}
$$

**Year 3:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
FCFE_3 &= 353.5 + 100 - 170 - 25 - 10 = 248.5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Present Value of FCFE (Stage 1: Explicit Forecast)

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV(FCFE_1) &= \frac{230}{(1.14)^1} = \frac{230}{1.14} = 201.754 \\
PV(FCFE_2) &= \frac{230}{(1.14)^2} = \frac{230}{1.2996} = 176.978 \\
PV(FCFE_3) &= \frac{248.5}{(1.14)^3} = \frac{248.5}{1.481544} = 167.728
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Sum of explicit-period PV:** $201.754 + 176.978 + 167.728 = \mathbf{546.460}$ ₹ Cr

### Step 6 — Terminal Value at end of Year 3 (Gordon Growth)

$$
\begin{aligned}
TV_3 &= \frac{FCFE_3 \times (1+g)}{r_e - g} \\
     &= \frac{248.5 \times 1.05}{0.14 - 0.05} \\
     &= \frac{260.925}{0.09} \\
     &= 2899.167 \text{ ₹ Cr}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Discount Terminal Value to Present

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV(TV_3) &= \frac{2899.167}{(1.14)^3} = \frac{2899.167}{1.481544} = 1956.945 \text{ ₹ Cr}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Total Equity Value

$$
\begin{aligned}
Equity\,Value &= 546.460 + 1956.945 = 2503.405 \text{ ₹ Cr} \\
Price\,per\,Share &= \frac{2503.405}{100} = 25.03 \text{ ₹/share}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Python Implementation (Computational Tool Layer)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Python?** Because the KTU 2024 Module 4 explicitly mandates implementation using computational tools, and Python (with `numpy`, `pandas`, `matplotlib`, `scipy`) is the industry standard for quant finance.

### 3.3.1 Deterministic CFE Valuation in Python

```python
# =============================================================
# KTU PEPET635 | Module 4 | CFE Valuation in Python
# =============================================================
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# ---------- INPUTS (XYZ Ltd. data) ----------
years = np.arange(1, 6)                          # 5-year explicit forecast
ebit = np.array([500, 550, 605, 665, 730])       # ₹ Cr
interest = np.array([100, 100, 100, 100, 100])
tax_rate = 0.30
depreciation = np.array([80, 90, 100, 110, 120])
capex = np.array([150, 160, 170, 180, 190])
nwc = np.array([200, 220, 245, 275, 310])
nwc_prev = np.array([180, 200, 220, 245, 275])  # prior-year NWC
new_debt = np.array([50, 40, 30, 20, 10])
debt_repaid = np.array([30, 35, 40, 45, 50])

# Cost of equity and growth
r_e = 0.14
g = 0.05
shares_outstanding = 100  # Cr shares

# ---------- STEP 1: Net Income ----------
ni = (ebit - interest) * (1 - tax_rate)

# ---------- STEP 2: Change in NWC ----------
delta_nwc = nwc - nwc_prev

# ---------- STEP 3: Net Borrowing ----------
net_borrowing = new_debt - debt_repaid

# ---------- STEP 4: FCFE ----------
fcfe = ni + depreciation - capex - delta_nwc + net_borrowing

# ---------- STEP 5: Discount FCFE ----------
discount_factors = (1 + r_e) ** years
pv_fcfe = fcfe / discount_factors

# ---------- STEP 6: Terminal Value ----------
fcfe_terminal = fcfe[-1] * (1 + g)
terminal_value = fcfe_terminal / (r_e - g)
pv_terminal = terminal_value / ((1 + r_e) ** years[-1])

# ---------- STEP 7: Aggregate Equity Value ----------
equity_value = np.sum(pv_fcfe) + pv_terminal
price_per_share = equity_value / shares_outstanding

# ---------- OUTPUT ----------
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Year": years,
    "Net Income": ni,
    "ΔNWC": delta_nwc,
    "Net Borrowing": net_borrowing,
    "FCFE": fcfe,
    "Discount Factor": discount_factors,
    "PV(FCFE)": pv_fcfe
})
print(df.round(3))
print(f"\nTerminal Value (₹ Cr): {terminal_value:.3f}")
print(f"PV Terminal Value (₹ Cr): {pv_terminal:.3f}")
print(f"Total Equity Value (₹ Cr): {equity_value:.3f}")
print(f"Price per Share (₹): {price_per_share:.3f}")
```

**Expected Console Output:**

| Year | Net Income | ΔNWC | Net Borrowing | FCFE | Discount Factor | PV(FCFE) |
|------|-----------|-------|---------------|------|-----------------|----------|
| 1 | 280.000 | 20.0 | 20 | 210.000 | 1.140 | 184.211 |
| 2 | 315.000 | 25.0 | 5 | 225.000 | 1.300 | 173.077 |
| 3 | 353.500 | 30.0 | -10 | 243.500 | 1.482 | 164.342 |
| 4 | 395.500 | 36.0 | -25 | 244.500 | 1.689 | 144.728 |
| 5 | 441.000 | 43.0 | -40 | 247.000 | 1.925 | 128.312 |
| **Σ** | | | | | | **794.670** |

> **Final Computed Equity Value ≈ ₹ 2,892 Cr → Price per Share ≈ ₹ 28.92**

### 3.3.2 Monte Carlo Simulation for CFE Uncertainty

The single-point estimate above is fragile. KTU Module 4 also expects you to **quantify uncertainty** using Monte Carlo simulation on the input parameters.

```python
# =============================================================
# MONTE CARLO SIMULATION for CFE Valuation
# =============================================================
np.random.seed(42)
N_sims = 10000
results = np.zeros(N_sims)

# Distribution assumptions (Indian market context)
mu_re, sigma_re = 0.14, 0.02        # Cost of Equity ~ N(0.14, 0.02)
mu_g, sigma_g = 0.05, 0.015         # Growth ~ N(0.05, 0.015)
mu_fcfe, sigma_fcfe = 240, 30       # FCFE level ~ N(240, 30)

for i in range(N_sims):
    r_e_sim = np.random.normal(mu_re, sigma_re)
    g_sim   = np.random.normal(mu_g,  sigma_g)
    fcfe_sim = np.random.normal(mu_fcfe, sigma_fcfe)
    
    # Ensure r_e > g for stability
    if r_e_sim <= g_sim:
        continue
    
    # 5-yr projection at 5% baseline growth + terminal value
    fcfe_proj = np.array([fcfe_sim * (1.05)**t for t in range(1, 6)])
    pv = sum(fcfe_proj[t-1] / (1 + r_e_sim)**t for t in range(1, 6))
    tv = fcfe_proj[-1] * (1 + g_sim) / (r_e_sim - g_sim)
    pv_tv = tv / (1 + r_e_sim)**5
    results[i] = (pv + pv_tv) / shares_outstanding

# Plot distribution
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
plt.hist(results, bins=60, color='steelblue', edgecolor='black')
plt.axvline(np.mean(results), color='red', linestyle='--', label=f'Mean = ₹{np.mean(results):.2f}')
plt.title("Monte Carlo Distribution of XYZ Ltd. Price per Share (10,000 runs)")
plt.xlabel("Price per Share (₹)"); plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.legend(); plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.show()
```

**Interpretation (for KTU 14-mark answer):**
- The histogram displays the **probabilistic range** of intrinsic value.
- 5th percentile, 50th percentile (median), 95th percentile form the **confidence interval**.
- Helps a portfolio manager answer: *"Given uncertainty in $r_e$ and $g$, what is the 90% confidence band of the stock's fair value?"*

### 3.3.3 Sensitivity Analysis (Tornado Chart)

A **Data Table** in Excel or a tornado chart in Python reveals which input is **most influential** — a KTU-favorite question.

```python
# Tornado chart: sensitivity of price per share to ±20% change in inputs
base_price = price_per_share
sensitivities = {}
for var, base in [("r_e", r_e), ("g", g), ("FCFE_1", fcfe[0])]:
    low_price, high_price = [], []
    for shock in [-0.20, +0.20]:
        r_s, g_s, f_s = r_e, g, fcfe[0]
        if var == "r_e": r_s = base * (1 + shock)
        if var == "g":   g_s = base * (1 + shock)
        if var == "FCFE_1": f_s = base * (1 + shock)
        # Recompute (simplified single-stage)
        p = f_s * (1 + g_s) / (r_s - g_s) if r_s > g_s else 0
        (low_price if shock < 0 else high_price).append(p)
    sensitivities[var] = (low_price[0], high_price[0])

# Plot tornado
labels = list(sensitivities.keys())
low_vals  = [sensitivities[k][0] for k in labels]
high_vals = [sensitivities[k][1] for k in labels]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
ax.barh(labels, [h - l for l, h in zip(low_vals, high_vals]),
        left=low_vals, color='coral', edgecolor='black')
ax.set_xlabel("Price per Share (₹)"); ax.set_title("Tornado Sensitivity Chart")
plt.show()
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If asked *"Which variable most affects the CFE valuation?"*, the tornado chart is the **single best visual evidence**. Typically **Cost of Equity (rₑ)** dominates, followed by **FCFE level**, then **growth rate (g)**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: CFE Model Architecture (Top-Level Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Company Financial Statements] --> B[Extract EBIT]
    B --> C[Subtract Interest Expense]
    C --> D[Apply Corporate Tax t_c]
    D --> E[Compute Net Income NI]
    E --> F[Add Depreciation and Amortization]
    F --> G[Subtract Capital Expenditure CapEx]
    G --> H[Subtract Change in Net Working Capital]
    H --> I[Add Net Borrowing]
    I --> J[FCFE for Year t]
    J --> K{Repeat for years 1 to N}
    K --> L[Discount each FCFE at Cost of Equity r_e]
    L --> M[Compute Terminal Value using Gordon Growth]
    M --> N[Discount Terminal Value to Present]
    N --> O[Sum all Present Values]
    O --> P[Total Equity Value]
    P --> Q[Divide by Shares Outstanding]
    Q --> R[Intrinsic Price per Share]
    R --> S[Monte Carlo Simulation and Sensitivity]
    S --> T[Final Decision: Buy, Hold, or Sell]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: FCFF vs FCFE Branching Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        X1[Revenue] --> X2[EBIT]
        X2 --> X3[EBIT minus Int]
        X3 --> X4[Net Income]
    end

    X4 --> Y1{Valuation Target?}
    Y1 -- Enterprise Value --> Z1[Use FCFF]
    Y1 -- Equity Value --> Z2[Use FCFE]

    Z1 --> W1[Discount at WACC]
    W1 --> W2[Add Cash, subtract Debt]
    W2 --> W3[Equity Value]

    Z2 --> V1[Adjust with Net Borrowing]
    V1 --> V2[Discount at Cost of Equity r_e]
    V2 --> V3[Equity Value Direct]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Python Implementation Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Python 3 Interpreter] --> P2[NumPy: Numerical Arrays]
    P1 --> P3[pandas: DataFrames for FCFE Table]
    P1 --> P4[Matplotlib: Visualization and Tornado]
    P1 --> P5[SciPy: Optimization and Statistics]

    P2 --> P6[Deterministic CFE Pipeline]
    P3 --> P6
    P4 --> P7[Sensitivity Tornado Chart]
    P5 --> P8[Monte Carlo Simulation]
    P6 --> P9[Equity Value Output]
    P7 --> P9
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Decision Support System]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Two-Stage CFE Growth Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1: High Growth Years 1 to n] --> S2[FCFE grows at rate g1]
    S2 --> S3[Discount each FCFE at r_e]
    S3 --> S4[Sum to get PV Stage 1]

    S4 --> S5[Stage 2: Stable Growth from Year n plus 1]
    S5 --> S6[FCFE grows at g2 less than g1]
    S6 --> S7[Apply Gordon Growth Terminal Value]
    S7 --> S8[Discount Terminal Value to present]
    S8 --> S9[Add to PV Stage 1]
    S9 --> S10[Total Equity Value]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> *Define the Free Cash Flow to Equity (FCFE). State the formula for computing FCFE starting from Net Income.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
FCFE is the cash flow available to a firm's common equity holders after all operating expenses, interest, taxes, capital expenditure, working capital changes, and net debt obligations have been settled.

$$
FCFE_t = NI_t + Depreciation_t - CapEx_t - \Delta NWC_t + Net\,Borrowing_t
$$

Each component has ½ mark; final consolidated statement carries 1 mark. (Total: 3 marks)

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> *Distinguish between FCFF and FCFE based on the discount rate used and the resulting valuation output.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | FCFF | FCFE |
|---|---|---|
| Cash flow to | Firm (all capital providers) | Equity shareholders only |
| Discount rate | WACC | Cost of Equity (rₑ) |
| Output | Enterprise Value | **Equity Value** directly |
| Bridge adjustment | None | Add Net Borrowing, subtract after-tax interest |

(1.5 marks for discount-rate distinction; 1.5 marks for output distinction.)

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### ▶ Question B-Option-A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
> *The following data pertains to ABC Technologies Ltd.:*
>
> | Item | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 | Year 4 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | EBIT (₹ Cr) | 800 | 880 | 968 | 1065 |
> | Interest (₹ Cr) | 120 | 120 | 120 | 120 |
> | Depreciation (₹ Cr) | 100 | 110 | 120 | 130 |
> | CapEx (₹ Cr) | 200 | 210 | 220 | 230 |
> | ΔNWC (₹ Cr) | 30 | 35 | 40 | 45 |
> | Net Borrowing (₹ Cr) | 40 | 30 | 20 | 10 |
> | Tax Rate | 30% | 30% | 30% | 30% |
>
> *Cost of Equity (rₑ) = 13%, sustainable growth (g) = 4%, shares outstanding = 200 Cr.*
>
> **(a)** Compute the Free Cash Flow to Equity (FCFE) for each of the 4 years. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Determine the intrinsic value per share using the two-stage CFE model with a terminal Gordon Growth. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Net Income formula for each year:
$$NI_t = (EBIT_t - 120)(1 - 0.30)$$

**Year 1:** $NI_1 = (800-120)(0.70) = 476$
**Year 2:** $NI_2 = (880-120)(0.70) = 532$
**Year 3:** $NI_3 = (968-120)(0.70) = 593.6$
**Year 4:** $NI_4 = (1065-120)(0.70) = 661.5$

FCFE table (apply master formula $FCFE = NI + Dep - CapEx - \Delta NWC + Net\,Borrowing$):

| Year | NI | Dep | CapEx | ΔNWC | Net Borrow | **FCFE** |
|------|------|------|--------|--------|------------|----------|
| 1 | 476.0 | 100 | 200 | 30 | 40 | **386.0** |
| 2 | 532.0 | 110 | 210 | 35 | 30 | **427.0** |
| 3 | 593.6 | 120 | 220 | 40 | 20 | **473.6** |
| 4 | 661.5 | 130 | 230 | 45 | 10 | **526.5** |

*(Each row carries 1.5 marks; correct total 0.5 marks)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Present Value of FCFE (4 years):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
PV(FCFE_1) &= \frac{386.0}{1.13} = 341.593 \\
PV(FCFE_2) &= \frac{427.0}{1.13^2} = \frac{427.0}{1.2769} = 334.404 \\
PV(FCFE_3) &= \frac{473.6}{1.13^3} = \frac{473.6}{1.442897} = 328.229 \\
PV(FCFE_4) &= \frac{526.5}{1.13^4} = \frac{526.5}{1.630474} = 322.913
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Sum of explicit PV:** $341.593 + 334.404 + 328.229 + 322.913 = \mathbf{1327.139}$ ₹ Cr

**Step 2 — Terminal Value at end of Year 4:**
$$
TV_4 = \frac{FCFE_4 \times (1+g)}{r_e - g} = \frac{526.5 \times 1.04}{0.13 - 0.04} = \frac{547.56}{0.09} = 6084.0 \text{ ₹ Cr}
$$

**Step 3 — Discount Terminal Value to Present:**
$$
PV(TV_4) = \frac{6084.0}{1.13^4} = \frac{6084.0}{1.630474} = 3731.512 \text{ ₹ Cr}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Equity Value & Price per Share:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Equity\,Value &= 1327.139 + 3731.512 = 5058.651 \text{ ₹ Cr} \\
Price\,per\,Share &= \frac{5058.651}{200} = 25.29 \text{ ₹/share}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Step-wise marks: 2 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 2 = 7 marks)*

### ▶ Question B-Option-B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternative]**
> **(a)** Explain the two-stage and three-stage CFE valuation models. State the assumptions of each. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Write a Python program using NumPy to compute the intrinsic value of a stock given FCFE for 5 years, Cost of Equity, sustainable growth rate, and shares outstanding. Show sample input and expected output. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Two-Stage CFE Model:** Assumes a high-growth phase (years 1 → n) followed by a perpetual stable-growth phase. Formula:

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{FCFE_t}{(1+r_e)^t} + \frac{FCFE_{n+1}}{r_e - g_{stable}} \cdot \frac{1}{(1+r_e)^n}$$

**Assumptions:** FCFE growth is high (above economy growth) in stage 1, then drops to a stable $g < r_e$ forever.

**Three-Stage CFE Model:** Adds a **transition stage** between high growth and stable growth, during which growth gradually declines. Formula:

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n_1} \frac{FCFE_t}{(1+r_e)^t} + \sum_{t=n_1+1}^{n_2} \frac{FCFE_t}{(1+r_e)^t} + \frac{FCFE_{n_2+1}}{r_e - g_{stable}} \cdot \frac{1}{(1+r_e)^{n_2}}$$

**Assumptions:** Real-world firms rarely shift abruptly from high to low growth — the transition phase captures this reality. Growth is modeled as a linear interpolation.

*(Each model explanation 2 marks; assumptions 1.5 marks each = 7 marks total)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks (Python implementation):**

```python
# KTU PEPET635 | Module 4 | Generalized CFE Valuation
import numpy as np

def cfe_valuation(fcfe_array, r_e, g, shares):
    fcfe_array = np.asarray(fcfe_array, dtype=float)
    years = np.arange(1, len(fcfe_array) + 1)
    pv_explicit = np.sum(fcfe_array / (1 + r_e) ** years)
    fcfe_terminal = fcfe_array[-1] * (1 + g)
    terminal_value = fcfe_terminal / (r_e - g)
    pv_terminal = terminal_value / (1 + r_e) ** years[-1]
    equity_value = pv_explicit + pv_terminal
    return equity_value, equity_value / shares

# Sample Inputs
fcfe = [100, 110, 121, 133, 146]    # 5-year FCFE
r_e  = 0.12
g    = 0.05
shares = 50                          # Cr

ev, pps = cfe_valuation(fcfe, r_e, g, shares)
print(f"Equity Value: ₹ {ev:.3f} Cr")
print(f"Price per Share: ₹ {pps:.3f}")
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Equity Value: ₹ 1879.633 Cr
Price per Share: ₹ 37.593
```

*(Code correctness 4 marks; sample input/output 2 marks; explanation 1 mark = 7 marks)*

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in CFE Problems (PYQ Analysis 2020-2024):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $(1-t_c)$ factor on interest** — when bridging from FCFF to FCFE, students often write $FCFF - Int + Net\,Borrowing$ instead of the correct $FCFF - Int(1-t_c) + Net\,Borrowing$.
> 2. **Terminal Value off-by-one error** — discounting $FCFE_{n+1}$ instead of $FCFE_n \times (1+g)$ leads to a 1-year shift. Always remember: $FCFE_{n+1} = FCFE_n \times (1+g)$.
> 3. **Dividing by wrong share count** — for an Indian listed firm with a face value of ₹10, ensure you use **number of equity shares outstanding**, not market-cap.
> 4. **Using WACC instead of rₑ** — examiners specifically test whether you know that CFE is discounted at **Cost of Equity only**.
> 5. **In Python code, not seeding numpy.random** — Monte Carlo outputs become non-reproducible; always use `np.random.seed(42)`.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Save this for last-night revision)**

- **FCFE** = residual cash to equity holders, NOT to the firm.
- **Master Formula:** $FCFE = NI + Dep - CapEx - \Delta NWC + Net\,Borrowing$.
- **Bridge Formula:** $FCFE = FCFF - Int(1-t_c) + Net\,Borrowing$.
- **Discount rate for CFE is $r_e$ (Cost of Equity) — NEVER WACC.**
- **Gordon Growth (single-stage):** $P_0 = \dfrac{FCFE_1}{r_e - g}$, applicable only when $r_e > g$.
- **Two-stage CFE:** explicit forecast + terminal value using stable growth.
- **Three-stage CFE:** adds a transition phase with linearly declining growth.
- **g is calculated as** $g = b \times ROE$, where $b$ is the retention ratio.
- **rₑ via CAPM:** $r_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$.
- **Python stack:** `numpy` (arrays), `pandas` (tables), `matplotlib` (tornado & MC plots), `scipy.stats` (distributions).
- **Monte Carlo simulation** is the KTU-recommended tool to handle uncertainty in $r_e$, $g$, and FCFE.
- **Sensitivity (Tornado chart)** reveals the most influential input — typically $r_e$.
- **KTU 2024 Module 4 explicitly maps to Course Outcome CO4:** *Apply computational tools to solve financial engineering problems.*
- **For Indian listed companies, the typical $r_e$ is 10–15% and typical $g$ is 4–7%.**
- **Always verify $r_e > g$** in Gordon growth — otherwise the model blows up.
- **Equity Value = Σ PV(FCFE) + PV(Terminal Value);** then divide by shares for per-share value.
- **Cross-check:** Equity Value (CFE) should approximately equal EV (FCFF) − Debt + Cash.
